topics.title: CE Configuration Using Small Signal Hybrid-Pi Model for Mid Frequency and Low Frequency (Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CE Configuration Using Small Signal Hybrid-Pi Model for Mid Frequency and Low Frequency (Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CE (Common Emitter) Configuration using Small-Signal Hybrid-π Model**: The Common Emitter amplifier is the most widely used BJT configuration in analog electronics, in which the input signal is applied between the **Base** and **Emitter**, and the amplified output is taken between the **Collector** and **Emitter**, with the Emitter terminal being common to both input and output ports. The **Hybrid-π Model** is a small-signal equivalent circuit of the BJT that is valid for **high-frequency analysis** (above audio range) and represents the transistor as a **voltage-controlled current source (gm·v′π)** with associated parasitic resistances (rπ, ro) and capacitances (Cπ, Cμ).

For the frequency domain study, the amplifier response is divided into three regions:

| Region | Frequency | Behavior of Coupling/Bypass Capacitors | Behavior of Internal Cπ, Cμ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mid-Frequency** | 100 Hz – 10 kHz (approx.) | **Short Circuit** (negligible reactance) | **Open Circuit** (negligible admittance) |
| **Low-Frequency** | < lower cutoff fL | Significant reactance (Xc rises) | Open Circuit |
| **High-Frequency** | > upper cutoff fH | Short Circuit | Significant admittance (Miller effect) |

This module focuses on the **Mid-Frequency** and **Low-Frequency** response of the CE amplifier.

## 1.2 The Hybrid-π Small-Signal Model — Parameters

The intrinsic Hybrid-π model of a BJT in the **active region** consists of the following parameters, all of which are bias-dependent (i.e., depend on the DC operating point Q-point):

- **gm (Transconductance)** — Forward transfer parameter: $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$, where $V_T \approx \mathbf{26~mV}$ at 27 °C.
- **rπ (Base-emitter dynamic resistance)** — $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{V_T}{I_B}$.
- **ro (Output resistance of transistor)** — $r_o = \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$, where $V_A$ is the Early voltage (typically **50 V – 200 V**).
- **Cπ (Base-emitter diffusion + depletion capacitance)** — typically **10 pF – 100 pF**.
- **Cμ (Base-collector feedback capacitance, Miller capacitance)** — typically **1 pF – 10 pF**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Origin of Hybrid-π Name**: The model is called *hybrid-π* because the small-signal equivalent circuit of the intrinsic BJT resembles the Greek letter **π (π)**, and the parameters (gm, rπ, ro) are *hybrid* (mixed) — they cannot be grouped purely as h-parameters, y-parameters, or z-parameters, but are derived naturally from the basic device physics.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Audio Mixing Console

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a BJT as a high-end audio mixing console at a recording studio:**
> - The **input slider (v′π)** is the base-emitter control voltage that the mixing engineer moves.
> - **rπ** is the **mechanical friction** of the slider — the engineer feels resistance when pushing it. (This is why the source sees a finite input impedance.)
> - The **amplifier gain knob (gm)** is the transconductance — it converts the slider movement (voltage) into the output audio level (current ic).
> - The **output current ic** is the amplified audio signal that flows into the speakers.
> - **ro** is the internal resistance of the cable connecting the mixer to the speaker — limiting how cleanly current can be delivered.
> - **Cμ** is the **unwanted acoustic feedback** from the speaker back into the microphone (it tries to feed output back to the input — this is the **Miller effect**).

The studio engineer adjusts the DC bias to set the slider in the middle of its travel (linear region) — this is exactly what we do with the Q-point in transistor biasing. The small-signal model is valid only when the slider stays in the linear region (i.e., the BJT remains in the active region).

## 1.4 Why Mid-Frequency and Low-Frequency Analysis?

- **Mid-Frequency Gain** ($A_{v(mid)}$) is the **maximum, flat** voltage gain of the amplifier. It is the figure of merit used in most design calculations and is independent of frequency.
- **Low-Frequency Response** is governed by the **external coupling capacitors** (Cs at input, CC at output) and the **emitter bypass capacitor CE**. As frequency decreases, these capacitors develop significant reactance, and the gain rolls off.
- The **lower cutoff frequency fL** is defined as the frequency at which the gain drops to **0.707** (i.e., **−3 dB**) of its mid-band value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CE Amplifier with All Capacitors (Complete Schematic)

A typical voltage-divider biased CE amplifier with all reactive elements has:

- **Cs** — Input coupling capacitor (Source → Amplifier)
- **CC** — Output coupling capacitor (Amplifier → Load)
- **CE** — Emitter bypass capacitor (parallel to RE)
- **R1, R2** — Voltage divider biasing resistors
- **RC** — Collector load resistor
- **RE** — Emitter resistor (for bias stability)
- **RL** — External load resistance

In **mid-frequency** analysis, all three capacitors (Cs, CC, CE) are **replaced by short circuits**, and internal capacitances (Cπ, Cμ) are **replaced by open circuits**. The result is a purely resistive small-signal model.

In **low-frequency** analysis, the three external capacitors are **retained**, and we derive the gain as a function of ω.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Strategy for Mid-Frequency Gain

### Step 1 — Replace the BJT by its Hybrid-π equivalent
- Replace VBE with **v′π** (small-signal control voltage).
- Insert a current source $g_m v'_\pi$ from collector to emitter.
- Insert **rπ** between base and emitter, and **ro** between collector and emitter.

### Step 2 — Replace all capacitors with their mid-frequency model
- **Cs, CC, CE** → **Short circuit**
- **Cπ, Cμ** → **Open circuit**

### Step 3 — Simplify the input loop
- The biasing resistors R1 and R2 appear in parallel at the base node.
- Define **R_B = R1 ∥ R2**.
- The total input resistance looking into the base is **rπ ∥ [external source resistance Rs + ...]** — but more commonly, $R_{in(base)} = r_\pi$.

### Step 4 — Apply Miller's Theorem (for gain calculation)
- Since the gain $A_v$ is large and negative, the **Miller effect** of Cμ (and rμ if present) is significant.
- For the mid-frequency analysis with purely resistive rμ (or omitted Cμ), Miller's theorem converts the feedback element **Rμ** (or 1/gμ) at the input side to $\dfrac{R_\mu}{1 - A_v} \approx \dfrac{R_\mu}{1 + \vert A_v \vert}$ (very small) and at the output side to $R_\mu(1 - A_v) \approx R_\mu \cdot \vert A_v \vert$ (very large, can often be ignored).

### Step 5 — Compute Output Voltage
- The output current $i_c = g_m v'_\pi$ flows through the parallel combination $R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$.
- $v_{out} = -g_m v'_\pi (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$.

### Step 6 — Compute the Voltage Gain
- $A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}}$, where $v_{in}$ is the source signal vs (or base-emitter voltage).

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Analytical Strategy for Low-Frequency Gain

The low-frequency response of a CE amplifier involves **three high-pass RC networks** formed by the three external capacitors:

1. **Input high-pass network**: Cs with the Thevenin resistance seen by it → $R_{S}' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$.
2. **Emitter bypass network**: CE with the resistance seen by it → $R_E' = R_E \parallel \left( r_e + \dfrac{R_S'}{\beta+1} \right)$, where $r_e = 1/g_m$.
3. **Output high-pass network**: CC with the resistance seen by it → $R_C' = R_C + R_L$ (or Thevenin equivalent).

For each network, the transfer function is of the form:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_i}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega/\omega_L}{1 + j\omega/\omega_L}$$

This is a **high-pass** response with cutoff frequency:

$$\omega_L = \frac{1}{R_{eq} C_{eq}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all essential formulas** for CE amplifier mid-frequency and low-frequency analysis. **Memorize these for KTU exams.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{\vert I_C \vert}{26~\text{mV}}$ | Siemens (S) or A/V |
| 2 | Base-emitter resistance | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{V_T}{I_B}$ | Ohms (Ω) |
| 3 | Output resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$ | Ohms (Ω), often ignored if $r_o \gg R_C$ |
| 4 | Mid-band voltage gain (with Rs) | $A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o) \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_S' + r_\pi}$ | Dimensionless, with $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B$ |
| 5 | Mid-band voltage gain (ideal source) | $A_{v(mid)} \approx -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | When $R_S \to 0$ and $r_o \to \infty$ |
| 6 | Input resistance (looking into base) | $R_{ib} = r_\pi$ | Ω |
| 7 | Input resistance (at amplifier terminals) | $R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi$ | Ω |
| 8 | Output resistance (looking into amplifier) | $R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o \approx R_C$ | Ω |
| 9 | Source-gain (overall from source vs) | $A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in}}$ | Includes source loading |
| 10 | fLS — input coupling capacitor | $f_{LS} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi) C_S}$ | Hz |
| 11 | fLE — emitter bypass capacitor | $f_{LE} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_E' C_E}$ where $R_E' = R_E \parallel \left( r_e + \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{\beta+1} \right)$ | Hz |
| 12 | fLC — output coupling capacitor | $f_{LC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_C}$ | Hz |
| 13 | Overall lower cutoff | $f_L \approx f_{LS} + f_{LE} + f_{LC}$ (approx.) or $f_L \approx \max(f_{LS}, f_{LE}, f_{LC})$ | Hz |
| 14 | Current gain | $A_i = -g_m \cdot \beta_{ac} \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_B \parallel r_\pi}$ | A/A |
| 15 | Power gain | $A_P = \vert A_v \vert \cdot A_i$ | Dimensionless (W/W) |
| 16 | Miller input capacitance | $C_{M} = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Farads (used in high-freq) |
| 17 | Miller input resistance | $R_{M(in)} = \dfrac{R_\mu}{1 + \vert A_v \vert}$ | Ohms |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

The CE amplifier is the **workhorse of analog signal processing**. Its applications include:

- **Audio pre-amplifiers** in microphones, hearing aids, and mixing consoles.
- **RF and IF stages** in radio receivers (with appropriate biasing).
- **Sensor signal conditioning** circuits (for thermocouples, photodiodes, strain gauges).
- **Switching and digital logic** (when overdriven, the CE configuration becomes a digital inverter).
- **Oscillators and waveform generators** (used as the gain element in tuned amplifiers).

The mid-band gain determines the **signal amplification factor**, while the lower cutoff frequency $f_L$ defines the **usable bandwidth** for AC signals. A properly designed CE amplifier for audio applications should have $f_L < 20~\text{Hz}$ and $f_H > 20~\text{kHz}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mid-Frequency Voltage Gain $A_{v(mid)}$

**Given**: A voltage-divider biased CE amplifier with source $v_s$, source resistance $R_S$, biasing resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$, collector resistor $R_C$, emitter resistor $R_E$ (fully bypassed by $C_E$), and load $R_L$.

**Step 1 — Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit (mid-frequency)**

In mid-frequency, replace all capacitors with short circuits and replace the BJT with the simplified hybrid-π model (with $r_o$ ignored for simplicity). The equivalent circuit consists of:
- Source $v_s$ in series with $R_S$
- $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2$ from base to ground
- $r_\pi$ from base to emitter
- Current source $g_m v'_\pi$ from collector to emitter
- $R_C \parallel R_L$ from collector to ground

**Step 2 — Calculate the Thevenin equivalent at the base**

The Thevenin resistance seen at the base (with the source looking in) is:

$$R_B' = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 = R_S \parallel R_B$$

The Thevenin voltage at the base is:

$$v_b = v_s \cdot \dfrac{R_B}{R_S + R_B}$$

**Step 3 — Write the KVL/KCL for the input loop**

The base current $i_b$ flows from the source through $R_S$, $R_B$, and into $r_\pi$:

$$v_b = i_b \cdot (R_S \parallel R_B) + i_b \cdot r_\pi = i_b \cdot (R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi)$$

Therefore:

$$i_b = \dfrac{v_b}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Find the control voltage $v'_\pi$**

The voltage across $r_\pi$ is:

$$v'_\pi = i_b \cdot r_\pi = v_b \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Find the output current**

The dependent current source delivers:

$$i_c = g_m \cdot v'_\pi = g_m \cdot v_b \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}$$

**Step 6 — Find the output voltage**

The output current flows through the parallel combination of $R_C$ and $R_L$ (with a negative sign because the current direction is from collector to emitter, creating a voltage drop opposite to $v_{out}$ convention):

$$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) = -g_m \cdot v_b \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 7 — Form the voltage gain ratio $A_v = v_{out} / v_s$**

$$A_v = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_s} = \dfrac{v_b}{v_s} \cdot (-g_m) \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}$$

Substitute $\dfrac{v_b}{v_s} = \dfrac{R_B}{R_S + R_B}$ and $\dfrac{R_B}{R_S+R_B} \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}$ simplifies algebraically to $\dfrac{R_B \parallel R_S}{R_B \parallel R_S + r_\pi}$:

$$\boxed{A_{v(mid)} = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) \cdot \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}}$$

**Step 8 — Idealized form (Rs → 0)**

If $R_S = 0$ (ideal voltage source), then $R_S \parallel R_B = 0$, and the ratio $\dfrac{0}{0+r_\pi} \to 0$, but a more careful analysis shows:

$$A_{v(mid),~ideal} = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

This is the **maximum possible gain** of the CE stage and is used in many textbook problems.

**Step 9 — Include output resistance $r_o$ (more accurate)**

If $r_o$ is not negligible:

$$\boxed{A_{v(mid)} = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o) \cdot \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of $f_{LS}$ — Lower Cutoff Due to Input Coupling Capacitor $C_S$

**Step 1 — Identify the resistance seen by $C_S$**

The capacitor $C_S$ is in series with the source $v_s$ and the input node of the amplifier. The resistance seen by $C_S$ is the **Thevenin resistance** between its two terminals, found by deactivating $v_s$ (short circuit) and looking into the amplifier input:

$$R_{C_S} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$$

**Step 2 — Form the high-pass RC network**

The input section behaves as a high-pass filter with time constant:

$$\tau_S = R_{C_S} \cdot C_S = (R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi) \cdot C_S$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the cutoff frequency**

$$f_{LS} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_S} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi) C_S}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of $f_{LE}$ — Lower Cutoff Due to Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$

**Step 1 — Find the resistance seen by $C_E$**

When $C_E$ is removed, looking into the emitter terminal (with $C_S$ and $C_C$ shorted at mid-frequency), the resistance is:

$$R_{E1} = R_E$$

Looking from the emitter *back into the transistor* (with base driven by $R_S \parallel R_B$):

$$R_{E2} = r_e + \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{\beta+1}$$

where $r_e = 1/g_m$.

**Step 2 — Combine in parallel**

The total resistance seen by $C_E$ is the parallel combination of $R_E$ and $R_{E2}$:

$$R_{C_E} = R_E \parallel \left( r_e + \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{\beta+1} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the cutoff frequency**

$$f_{LE} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{C_E} C_E}$$

**Note**: In most practical designs, $R_{C_E}$ is much smaller than the resistances seen by $C_S$ and $C_C$, so $f_{LE}$ tends to be the **dominant (highest)** lower cutoff frequency. This means the bypass capacitor is the **critical design parameter** for setting the lower cutoff.

## 3.4 Derivation of $f_{LC}$ — Lower Cutoff Due to Output Coupling Capacitor $C_C$

**Step 1 — Identify the resistance seen by $C_C$**

With $v_s$ deactivated, the resistance seen by $C_C$ between its terminals is:

$$R_{C_C} = R_C + R_L$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the cutoff frequency**

$$f_{LC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_C}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Overall Lower Cutoff Frequency $f_L$

**Approach 1 — Dominant Pole Approximation**:

If one cutoff frequency is much higher than the others (by a factor of 4 or more), it dominates the response:

$$f_L \approx \max(f_{LS}, f_{LE}, f_{LC})$$

**Approach 2 — Sum of Squared Cutoffs (for non-interacting, widely spaced poles)**:

$$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{LS}^2 + f_{LE}^2 + f_{LC}^2}$$

**Approach 3 — Direct Sum (Simpler but less accurate)**:

$$f_L \approx f_{LS} + f_{LE} + f_{LC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Method 3 overestimates** $f_L$, while Method 1 underestimates it (when poles are not well separated). For KTU exams, **Method 1 is most commonly accepted** unless specifically asked otherwise.

## 3.6 Derivation of the Overall Low-Frequency Transfer Function

The complete low-frequency transfer function (assuming non-interacting RC sections) is:

$$\frac{A_v(j\omega)}{A_{v(mid)}} = \dfrac{j\omega/\omega_{LS}}{1 + j\omega/\omega_{LS}} \cdot \dfrac{j\omega/\omega_{LE}}{1 + j\omega/\omega_{LE}} \cdot \dfrac{j\omega/\omega_{LC}}{1 + j\omega/\omega_{LC}}$$

Taking the magnitude:

$$\left| \dfrac{A_v(j\omega)}{A_{v(mid)}} \right| = \dfrac{(\omega/\omega_{LS})(\omega/\omega_{LE})(\omega/\omega_{LC})}{\sqrt{1+(\omega/\omega_{LS})^2} \sqrt{1+(\omega/\omega_{LE})^2} \sqrt{1+(\omega/\omega_{LC})^2}}$$

In decibels:

$$20 \log_{10} \left| \dfrac{A_v}{A_{v(mid)}} \right| = 20 \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_{LS}) + 20 \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_{LE}) + 20 \log_{10}(\omega/\omega_{LC}) - 10\log_{10}[1+(\omega/\omega_{LS})^2] - \ldots$$

Each high-pass section contributes **+20 dB/decade** slope at low frequencies and a flat 0 dB response above its cutoff.

## 3.7 Numerical Example — Worked Problem

**Given**:
- $R_S = 1~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 100~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 50~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 5~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 5~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 2~\text{k}\Omega$
- $C_S = 1~\mu\text{F}$, $C_C = 1~\mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 100~\mu\text{F}$
- $\beta = 100$, $I_C = 1~\text{mA}$, $V_A \to \infty$ (ignore $r_o$)
- $V_T = 26~\text{mV}$

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m$ and $r_\pi$**

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{1~\text{mA}}{26~\text{mV}} = 38.46~\text{mA/V} = 0.03846~\text{S}$$

$$r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{100}{0.03846} = 2.6~\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_B$**

$$R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \dfrac{100 \times 50}{100+50} = 33.33~\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute Mid-Frequency Gain**

$$R_S \parallel R_B = 1~\text{k}\Omega \parallel 33.33~\text{k}\Omega = 0.971~\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_C \parallel R_L = 5~\text{k}\Omega \parallel 5~\text{k}\Omega = 2.5~\text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) \cdot \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}$$

$$A_{v(mid)} = -0.03846 \times 2500 \times \dfrac{971}{971+2600} = -96.15 \times 0.272 = -26.15$$

$$\boxed{A_{v(mid)} \approx -26.15 \text{ V/V (or 28.35 dB)}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $f_{LS}$**

$$R_{C_S} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi = 1~\text{k}\Omega \parallel 33.33~\text{k}\Omega \parallel 2.6~\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_{C_S} = (0.971 \parallel 2.6)~\text{k}\Omega = \dfrac{0.971 \times 2.6}{0.971+2.6} = 0.7065~\text{k}\Omega$$

$$f_{LS} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 706.5 \times 1\times 10^{-6}} = 225.2~\text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $f_{LE}$**

$$r_e = 1/g_m = 26~\Omega$$

$$R_{E2} = r_e + \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{\beta+1} = 26 + \dfrac{971}{101} = 26 + 9.61 = 35.61~\Omega$$

$$R_{C_E} = R_E \parallel R_{E2} = 2000 \parallel 35.61 = \dfrac{2000 \times 35.61}{2000+35.61} = 35~\Omega$$

$$f_{LE} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 35 \times 100\times 10^{-6}} = 45.47~\text{Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $f_{LC}$**

$$R_{C_C} = R_C + R_L = 5~\text{k}\Omega + 5~\text{k}\Omega = 10~\text{k}\Omega$$

$$f_{LC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10000 \times 1\times 10^{-6}} = 15.92~\text{Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Determine Overall $f_L$**

$$f_L \approx \max(225.2, 45.47, 15.92) = 225.2~\text{Hz} \quad \text{(dominated by } f_{LS} \text{)}$$

If we use the sum-of-squares method:

$$f_L = \sqrt{225.2^2 + 45.47^2 + 15.92^2} = \sqrt{50715 + 2068 + 253} = \sqrt{53036} = 230.3~\text{Hz}$$

The results are very close because $f_{LS}$ dominates. **The amplifier bandwidth begins at approximately 230 Hz.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the CE Amplifier

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete signal-flow architecture** of a CE amplifier with all three reactive elements, identifying the role of each section in determining the frequency response.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[V_s Source] --> rsw[R_S Source Resistor]
    rsw --> csnode{C_S Coupling}
    csnode --> rdiv[R_1 parallel R_2 Bias Divider]
    csnode --> rpi[r_pi Base-Emitter R]
    rdiv --> gnd1((Ground))
    rpi --> gnd1
    csnode --> gmsrc[Controlled Current Source g_m v'_pi]
    gmsrc --> rcnode{R_C Collector}
    rcnode --> gnd1
    rcnode --> ccnode{C_C Coupling}
    ccnode --> rload[R_L Load]
    rload --> gnd1
    gmsrc --> rebranch[R_E Emitter R]
    rebranch --> gnd1
    rebranch -.-> cebranch{C_E Bypass}
    cebranch -.-> gnd1
```

> **Note on the diagram**: The dashed lines represent the bypass capacitor $C_E$ in parallel with $R_E$, which becomes a short at mid-frequency. The three capacitor blocks ($C_S$, $C_C$, $C_E$) are the low-frequency determining elements.

## 4.2 Frequency Response Architecture (Bode Plot Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram describes the **frequency response of the CE amplifier** as a cascade of three high-pass filters (low-frequency side) and the flat mid-band region, culminating in the high-frequency roll-off (covered later).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Input Signal V_in] --> hp1[High-Pass Filter 1: f_LS due to C_S]
    hp1 --> hp2[High-Pass Filter 2: f_LE due to C_E]
    hp2 --> hp3[High-Pass Filter 3: f_LC due to C_C]
    hp3 --> midband[Mid-Band Region: Flat Gain A_v_mid]
    midband --> output[Output Signal V_out]
    
    hp1 -.->|f_LS dominates?| compare[Overall f_L approx max of all]
    hp2 -.-> compare
    hp3 -.-> compare
    compare --> midband
```

## 4.3 Hybrid-π Model — Intrinsic Transistor Equivalent

The following block diagram maps the **internal structure** of the BJT as represented by the Hybrid-π model.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    bterm[B Base Terminal] --> rpi_el[r_pi]
    rpi_el --> eterm[E Emitter Terminal]
    bterm --> cm_cap[C_pi Capacitance]
    cm_cap --> eterm
    bterm --> cmu_cap[C_mu Feedback Capacitance]
    cmu_cap --> cterm[C Collector Terminal]
    cterm --> gmsrc[Dependent Source g_m times v_prime_pi]
    gmsrc --> eterm
    cterm --> ro_res[r_o Output Resistance]
    ro_res --> eterm
```

> **Key insight from the model**: The control voltage $v'_\pi$ is the voltage across $r_\pi$ (not from base to external emitter, which would be $v_{be}$). The current $g_m v'_\pi$ flows from collector to emitter.

## 4.4 Low-Frequency Response Curve (Conceptual Bode Plot)

The frequency response of a CE amplifier in the low-to-mid frequency region can be visualized as:

```
Gain |A_v| (dB)
  ↑
  |          ___________________________  (Mid-band flat region)
  |         /                            \
  |        /                              \
  |       /                                \  <-- 0 dB reference = A_v(mid)
  |      /                                  \
  |     /                                    \
  |    /                                      \
  |   /                                        \
  |  /                                          \
  | /                                            \
  |/                                              \
  +-----------+----------------+------------------→  Frequency (Hz)
  |           |                |
 f_LE       f_LC             f_LS
            (or vice versa)
            Overall f_L
           (highest pole)
  ↑
  -3 dB point: gain = 0.707 × A_v(mid)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Bode Plot**:
> - The **slope below f_L** is **+20 dB/decade** for a single dominant pole. With three poles, the initial slope can be up to **+60 dB/decade** if all three cutoffs coincide.
> - The **mid-band region** is flat (0 dB slope).
> - The **fL point** is the lowest frequency at which the gain is within 3 dB of the mid-band value.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the significance of the small-signal hybrid-π model in analyzing a CE amplifier at mid-frequency.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer** (3 marks):
>
> The hybrid-π model is a small-signal equivalent circuit of the BJT valid for mid-frequency analysis. Its significance lies in:
> 1. **It is a physically based model**: The parameters $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$ are directly derived from the device physics and bias conditions, unlike h-parameters which are empirical.
> 2. **It is independent of the configuration**: The same model is used for CE, CB, or CC configurations; only the external circuit changes.
> 3. **It can be extended to high frequencies** by simply adding the capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$, making it a unified model for all frequency ranges.
> 4. **It provides direct insight** into the role of each physical parameter (e.g., $g_m$ relates input voltage to output current).
>
> At mid-frequency, the coupling and bypass capacitors behave as short circuits, and the junction capacitances behave as open circuits, reducing the hybrid-π model to its purely resistive form, which is used to compute the mid-band voltage gain $A_{v(mid)}$.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the hybrid-π parameters and state the relationship between $g_m$ and $r_\pi$.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer** (3 marks):
>
> The hybrid-π parameters of a BJT in the active region are:
> 1. **$g_m$ (Transconductance)**: $g_m = I_C / V_T$, where $V_T = 26$ mV at room temperature.
> 2. **$r_\pi$ (Base-emitter input resistance)**: $r_\pi = \beta_{ac}/g_m = V_T/I_B$.
> 3. **$r_o$ (Output resistance)**: $r_o = V_A / I_C$, where $V_A$ is the Early voltage.
> 4. **$C_\pi$ (Base-emitter capacitance)** and **$C_\mu$ (Base-collector feedback capacitance)**.
>
> **Relationship**: $g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta_{ac}$, i.e., $g_m$ and $r_\pi$ are inversely related at a given collector current. As $I_C$ increases, $g_m$ increases linearly while $r_\pi$ decreases linearly.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Draw the small-signal hybrid-π equivalent circuit of a CE amplifier and derive the expression for the **mid-frequency voltage gain** $A_{v(mid)}$ from source to output. Include the effect of source resistance $R_S$ and output resistance $r_o$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the CE amplifier shown, compute:
- $I_C = 1.5$ mA, $\beta = 120$, $V_A = 100$ V, $V_T = 26$ mV
- $R_S = 600~\Omega$, $R_1 = 60~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 20~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 2.2~\text{k}\Omega$ (fully bypassed)

Calculate: **(i)** $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$ **(ii)** $A_{v(mid)}$ in dB. **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution**:
>
> **(a) Derivation** — *See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete 9-step derivation.*
>
> The final expression is:
>
> $$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o) \cdot \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}$$
>
> where $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2$. The derivation proceeds by:
> 1. Replacing the BJT with its hybrid-π model ($g_m v'_\pi$ current source, $r_\pi$, $r_o$).
> 2. Shorting all capacitors at mid-frequency.
> 3. Computing the Thevenin resistance $R_S \parallel R_B$ seen at the base.
> 4. Writing the KVL for the input loop to find $i_b = v_b/(R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi)$.
> 5. Computing $v'_\pi = i_b \cdot r_\pi$.
> 6. Computing $i_c = g_m v'_\pi$.
> 7. Computing $v_{out} = -i_c (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o)$.
> 8. Forming the gain ratio.
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution**:
>
> **(i) Parameters**:
>
> $$g_m = I_C / V_T = 1.5~\text{mA} / 26~\text{mV} = 57.69~\text{mA/V}$$
>
> $$r_\pi = \beta / g_m = 120 / 0.05769 = 2080~\Omega = 2.08~\text{k}\Omega$$
>
> $$r_o = V_A / I_C = 100 / 1.5~\text{mA} = 66.67~\text{k}\Omega$$
>
> **(ii) Mid-Frequency Gain**:
>
> $$R_B = 60 \parallel 20 = 15~\text{k}\Omega$$
>
> $$R_S \parallel R_B = 0.6 \parallel 15 = 0.577~\text{k}\Omega = 577~\Omega$$
>
> $$R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o = 4.7 \parallel 4.7 \parallel 66.67$$
>
> First, $4.7 \parallel 4.7 = 2.35~\text{k}\Omega$. Then $2.35 \parallel 66.67 \approx 2.27~\text{k}\Omega$.
>
> $$A_v = -0.05769 \times 2270 \times \dfrac{577}{577+2080} = -130.96 \times 0.2172 = -28.45$$
>
> $$\boxed{A_{v(mid)} = -28.45~\text{V/V} \approx 29.08~\text{dB}}$$

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how the three coupling and bypass capacitors in a CE amplifier affect the **low-frequency response**. Derive the expressions for the three lower cutoff frequencies $f_{LS}$, $f_{LE}$, and $f_{LC}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same numerical example in Question A part (b), with $C_S = 10~\mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 470~\mu\text{F}$, $C_C = 1~\mu\text{F}$, determine the **overall lower cutoff frequency $f_L$** using (i) the dominant-pole approximation and (ii) the sum-of-squares method. Comment on which is more accurate. **(7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution**:
>
> **(a) Explanation and Derivation** — *See Sections 2.3, 3.2, 3.3, 3.4 of these notes.*
>
> **Summary of the three cutoff frequencies**:
> - **$f_{LS}$** (input coupling): due to $C_S$ with resistance $(R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi)$:
>
>   $$f_{LS} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi) C_S}$$
>
> - **$f_{LE}$** (emitter bypass): due to $C_E$ with resistance $R_E \parallel (r_e + (R_S \parallel R_B)/(\beta+1))$:
>
>   $$f_{LE} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{C_E} C_E}$$
>
> - **$f_{LC}$** (output coupling): due to $C_C$ with resistance $(R_C + R_L)$:
>
>   $$f_{LC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_C}$$
>
> **(b) Numerical Solution**:
>
> Using values from Question A: $R_S = 600~\Omega$, $R_B = 15~\text{k}\Omega$, $r_\pi = 2.08~\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 120$, $g_m = 57.69$ mA/V, $R_E = 2.2~\text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = R_L = 4.7~\text{k}\Omega$.
>
> **Compute $r_e$**:
>
> $$r_e = 1/g_m = 1/0.05769 = 17.33~\Omega$$
>
> **Compute $R_{C_S}$** (resistance seen by $C_S$):
>
> $$R_S \parallel R_B = 577~\Omega$$
>
> $$R_{C_S} = 577 \parallel 2080 = 452~\Omega$$
>
> **Compute $R_{C_E}$** (resistance seen by $C_E$):
>
> $$R_{E2} = r_e + (R_S \parallel R_B)/(\beta+1) = 17.33 + 577/121 = 17.33 + 4.77 = 22.1~\Omega$$
>
> $$R_{C_E} = R_E \parallel R_{E2} = 2200 \parallel 22.1 = 21.88~\Omega$$
>
> **Compute $R_{C_C}$**:
>
> $$R_{C_C} = R_C + R_L = 4.7 + 4.7 = 9.4~\text{k}\Omega$$
>
> **Compute the three cutoff frequencies**:
>
> $$f_{LS} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 452 \times 10\times 10^{-6}} = 35.21~\text{Hz}$$
>
> $$f_{LE} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 21.88 \times 470\times 10^{-6}} = 15.47~\text{Hz}$$
>
> $$f_{LC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 9400 \times 1\times 10^{-6}} = 16.93~\text{Hz}$$
>
> **(i) Dominant-Pole Approximation**:
>
> $$f_L \approx \max(35.21, 15.47, 16.93) = 35.21~\text{Hz} \quad \text{(dominated by } f_{LS} \text{)}$$
>
> **(ii) Sum-of-Squares Method**:
>
> $$f_L = \sqrt{f_{LS}^2 + f_{LE}^2 + f_{LC}^2} = \sqrt{35.21^2 + 15.47^2 + 16.93^2}$$
>
> $$= \sqrt{1239.7 + 239.3 + 286.6} = \sqrt{1765.6} = 42.02~\text{Hz}$$
>
> **Comment on Accuracy**: The dominant-pole approximation **underestimates** $f_L$ because it ignores the contribution of the lower-frequency poles. The sum-of-squares method is **more accurate** when the poles are not well separated (within a factor of 4 of each other), as is the case here where $f_{LE}$ and $f_{LC}$ are roughly half of $f_{LS}$. The **direct sum method** would give $f_L \approx 35.21 + 15.47 + 16.93 = 67.6$ Hz, which **overestimates**.
>
> $$\boxed{f_L \approx 42~\text{Hz (sum-of-squares, most accurate)}}$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**:
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_T = 26$ mV with $25$ mV**: The thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K. Using 25 mV is acceptable, but 26 mV is the **standard KTU answer**. Some students mistakenly use $V_T = 0.026$ V without unit conversion.
>
> 2. **Sign error in the gain formula**: $A_v$ for CE is **negative** (phase inversion of 180°). Forgetting the negative sign loses 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Using $\beta_{dc}$ instead of $\beta_{ac}$**: The hybrid-π model uses the **small-signal** $\beta_{ac} = h_{fe}$, not the DC $\beta_{dc} = h_{FE}$. In modern BJTs these are usually similar, but you should use the value given in the problem.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the parallel combination of $R_C$ and $R_L$**: The effective AC load is $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$, **not** just $R_C$. Many students omit $R_L$ if it is not explicitly in the bias circuit.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2$ with $R_1 + R_2$**: This is a frequent slip; the biasing resistors appear in **parallel** in the small-signal AC equivalent.
>
> 6. **Wrong resistance for the bypass capacitor**: The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_E$ is $R_E \parallel \left( r_e + (R_S \parallel R_B)/(\beta+1) \right)$, **not** just $R_E$. Using $R_E$ alone gives an answer that is off by orders of magnitude.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to convert units**: $C$ in $\mu$F must be converted to F before calculation. $f$ must be in Hz, not rad/s (unless you keep $\omega$ throughout).
>
> 8. **Not stating units in the final answer**: KTU examiners **do deduct marks** if the final numerical answer is given without units (e.g., write "$A_v = -26.15$ V/V" or "28.35 dB", not just "$-26.15$").

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for CE Amplifier Mid-Frequency and Low-Frequency Analysis**:
>
> ✅ **Mid-Frequency Conditions**: All external capacitors (Cs, CC, CE) are **shorts**; all internal capacitances (Cπ, Cμ) are **opens**.
>
> ✅ **Hybrid-π Parameters to Remember**:
> - $g_m = I_C / V_T$ (most-used formula)
> - $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ (or $V_T / I_B$)
> - $r_o = V_A / I_C$ (often ignored if $r_o \gg R_C$)
> - $g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta$ (key identity)
>
> ✅ **Mid-Band Gain (with Rs and ro)**:
>
> $$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o) \cdot \dfrac{R_S \parallel R_B}{R_S \parallel R_B + r_\pi}$$
>
> ✅ **Ideal Mid-Band Gain** (no source resistance, infinite ro):
>
> $$A_{v(mid)} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$
>
> ✅ **Input Resistance (at amplifier base)**: $R_{in(base)} = r_\pi$
>
> ✅ **Input Resistance (at amplifier terminals)**: $R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi$
>
> ✅ **Output Resistance (looking into amplifier)**: $R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o \approx R_C$
>
> ✅ **Three Lower Cutoff Frequencies**:
> - $f_{LS}$: due to Cs with $R_{CS} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$
> - $f_{LE}$: due to CE with $R_{CE} = R_E \parallel [r_e + (R_S \parallel R_B)/(\beta+1)]$
> - $f_{LC}$: due to CC with $R_{CC} = R_C + R_L$
>
> ✅ **Overall $f_L$ Calculation**:
> - **Dominant pole**: $f_L = \max(f_{LS}, f_{LE}, f_{LC})$ (used most often)
> - **Sum of squares**: $f_L = \sqrt{f_{LS}^2 + f_{LE}^2 + f_{LC}^2}$ (more accurate)
> - **Direct sum**: $f_L = f_{LS} + f_{LE} + f_{LC}$ (overestimate, rarely used)
>
> ✅ **Standard Numerical Values to Memorize**:
> - $V_T = 26$ mV at room temperature (27 °C)
> - $\beta = 100$ (typical small-signal value)
> - $V_A = 100$ V (typical Early voltage)
>
> ✅ **Key Observation**: The bypass capacitor $C_E$ usually has the **smallest** Thevenin resistance ($R_{CE} \approx$ tens of ohms), so it needs the **largest** capacitance to achieve the same cutoff as the other two. This is why $C_E$ is often 10×–100× larger than $C_S$ or $C_C$ in real designs.
>
> ✅ **Frequency Regions to Distinguish Clearly**:
> - **Low** frequency: governed by external capacitors, gain rises at **+20 dB/decade per pole** as frequency increases.
> - **Mid** frequency: flat gain, purely resistive model.
> - **High** frequency: governed by $C_\pi$ and **Miller-multiplied** $C_\mu$, gain falls at **−20 dB/decade per pole**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input and Output Impedance)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Input and Output Impedance)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$)** is defined as the impedance presented by a circuit or two-port network to the source at its input terminals. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the input voltage to the input current with the load connected:
> $$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}}\Big\vert_{R_L \text{ connected}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$)** is defined as the equivalent impedance looking back into the output terminals of a circuit, with all independent sources deactivated (voltage sources shorted, current sources opened) and the load removed. It is the Thevenin equivalent resistance at the output:
> $$Z_{out} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}}\Big\vert_{\text{sources off, } R_L \text{ removed}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fire hose connected to a fire hydrant**:
- The **Input Impedance** is like the **inlet of the hose** — if the inlet is narrow (low $Z_{in}$), the hydrant struggles to push water efficiently, and most of the pressure drops at the connection itself. If the inlet is wide open (high $Z_{in}$), the hydrant's pressure transfers cleanly to the hose.
- The **Output Impedance** is like the **nozzle of the hose** — if the nozzle is tight (high $Z_{out}$), the water flow (current) is restricted, so pressure drops when someone tries to draw a lot. If the nozzle is wide (low $Z_{out}$), it can deliver the rated flow regardless of the consumer attached.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Standard Metrics to remember:**
> - **Ideal Voltage Amplifier:** $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$ (perfect pressure source behavior)
> - **Ideal Current Amplifier:** $Z_{in} = 0$, $Z_{out} = \infty$ (perfect flow source behavior)
> - **Ideal Transconductance Amplifier (VCCS):** $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = \infty$
> - **Ideal Transresistance Amplifier (CCVS):** $Z_{in} = 0$, $Z_{out} = 0$

### The Two-Port Perspective

Every analog amplifier can be modeled as a **black-box two-port network** with an input port (where the source connects) and an output port (where the load connects). The amplifier sits between the source — which has its own internal resistance $R_S$ — and the load $R_L$. The interplay of these four impedances completely determines signal transfer, gain, and power delivery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Examiners Test This Topic:** Input and output impedance form the foundation for understanding **loading effects**, **cascading of amplifier stages**, **impedance matching**, and the **Miller effect** — all of which are high-yield topics across Module 1 and Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Input and Output Impedance Matter

The role of $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ can be understood through three engineering concerns:

**1. Voltage Transfer Efficiency (Signal Integrity):**
When a source with internal impedance $R_S$ drives an amplifier with input impedance $Z_{in}$, a **voltage divider** is formed at the input. The voltage actually reaching the amplifier is:
$$V_{in} = V_S \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + R_S}$$

If $Z_{in} \gg R_S$, then $V_{in} \approx V_S$ (no signal loss). This is why voltmeters and voltage amplifiers must have **very high input impedance**.

**2. Loading Effect at the Output:**
The output voltage delivered to the load is:
$$V_L = V_{th} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + Z_{out}}$$

If $Z_{out} \ll R_L$, then $V_L \approx V_{th}$ (no signal loss at output). This is why voltage amplifiers must have **very low output impedance**.

**3. Maximum Power Transfer (Matching):**
From network theory, maximum power is delivered to the load when $Z_L = Z_{out}^*$ (complex conjugate). For purely resistive cases, $R_L = Z_{out}$. This is critical in **RF and communication circuits**, though less emphasized in low-frequency audio where voltage transfer is the priority.

### 2.2 Methods to Calculate $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$

**To find $Z_{in}$:**
- Apply a test source ($V_{test}$ or $I_{test}$) at the input terminals.
- Keep the load $R_L$ connected.
- Find the resulting current (or voltage).
- Compute the ratio $Z_{in} = V_{test}/I_{test}$.

**To find $Z_{out}$:**
- Deactivate all independent sources (set $V_S = 0$, $I_S = 0$).
- Remove the load $R_L$.
- Apply a test source at the output terminals.
- Find the ratio $Z_{out} = V_{test}/I_{test}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Mistake (Examiner Alert):** Dependent sources (like the $g_m v_{be}$ current source in a small-signal BJT model) are **NOT turned off** when computing $Z_{out}$. Only independent sources are deactivated.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize Before Every Exam**

| Amplifier Configuration | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **BJT Common Emitter (CE)** | $R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_{\pi}$ | $R_C$ |
| **BJT Common Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower** | $R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel (\beta+1)(R_E \parallel R_L)$ | $R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_{\pi} + R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2}{\beta+1}$ |
| **BJT Common Base (CB)** | $R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_{\pi}}{\beta+1}$ | $R_C$ |
| **MOSFET Common Source (CS)** | $R_G$ (very high) | $R_D$ |
| **MOSFET Common Drain (CD) / Source Follower** | $R_G$ | $\dfrac{1}{g_m} \parallel R_S \parallel R_L$ |
| **MOSFET Common Gate (CG)** | $\dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $R_D$ |

| Generic Two-Port Relationship | Formula |
|---|---|
| Voltage gain (loaded) | $A_v = A_{vo} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L + Z_{out}}$ |
| Input voltage divider | $V_{in} = V_S \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + R_S}$ |
| Overall voltage gain | $A_{vs} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_S} = \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in}+R_S} \cdot A_{vo} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L+Z_{out}}$ |
| Current gain (loaded) | $A_i = A_{io} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in}+R_S} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{out}}{Z_{out}+R_L}$ |
| h-parameter: $h_{ie} = Z_{in}\vert_{v_{ce}=0}$ | $h_{oe} = 1/Z_{out}\vert_{i_b=0}$ |

> Real-World Utility: Input and output impedance specifications govern the **cascading of amplifier stages**, **op-amp data sheet design** (e.g., TL072 has $Z_{in} = 10^{12} \, \Omega$), **cable driving** in audio systems, **antenna matching** in RF, and **probe selection** in oscilloscopes ($1 \text{ M}\Omega$ vs $50 \, \Omega$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Input Impedance of BJT Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier

**Given:** Standard CE amplifier with bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, emitter resistor $R_E$ (un-bypassed), collector resistor $R_C$, and BJT small-signal parameters $r_{\pi}$, $\beta$, $g_m = \beta/r_{\pi}$. Load $R_L$ is connected at the output.

**Step 1: Draw the small-signal equivalent (hybrid-$\pi$ model of BJT).**

The input port sees the bias network in parallel with the BJT base. Since $R_E$ is un-bypassed, it appears in the small-signal emitter leg.

**Step 2: Apply KVL at the input loop.**

With $v_s$ applied (after $R_S$ drop), the voltage at the base drives $r_{\pi}$:

$$v_{be} = i_b \cdot r_{\pi} + (\beta+1)i_b \cdot R_E$$

The factor $(\beta+1)$ appears because the emitter current is $(\beta+1)$ times the base current.

**Step 3: Solve for $v_{be}/i_b$ (which is the impedance looking into the base).**

$$Z_{b} = \frac{v_{be}}{i_b} = r_{\pi} + (\beta+1)R_E$$

**Step 4: Combine with bias resistors to get total $Z_{in}$.**

The input impedance seen by the source is the parallel combination of the bias divider and the impedance looking into the base:

$$Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel \big[ r_{\pi} + (\beta+1)R_E \big]$$

**Step 5: Simplification when $R_E$ is fully bypassed by $C_E$.**

If a bypass capacitor shorts $R_E$ at signal frequency, then $Z_b = r_{\pi}$, and:

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_{\pi}}$$

---

### Derivation 2: Output Impedance of BJT Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier

**Step 1: Deactivate the source.**
Set $v_s = 0$. With $v_s$ off, $i_b = 0$ (since $i_b = v_s / (R_S + r_{\pi})$). Therefore the dependent current source $g_m v_{be} = g_m \cdot i_b \cdot r_{\pi} = 0$, behaving as an open circuit.

**Step 2: Apply a test source $v_{test}$ at the output (with $R_L$ removed).**
Looking back from the output node, we see only the collector resistor $R_C$, because the dependent source branch is open and $i_b = 0$ means no current flows in the $r_{\pi}$ branch either.

**Step 3: Compute the ratio.**

$$\boxed{Z_{out} = R_C}$$

**Insight:** This is why CE amplifiers are good voltage amplifiers but poor drivers of low-impedance loads — every $R_L$ connected forms a divider with $R_C$, reducing gain.

---

### Derivation 3: Input Impedance of MOSFET Common-Source (CS) Amplifier

**Step 1: For a MOSFET, the gate is insulated — it draws no DC current.**
At low/medium frequencies, the input impedance looking into the gate is **infinite** (capacitance neglected).

**Step 2: The bias network sets $Z_{in}$.**

In a standard CS configuration, gate bias is provided by $R_G$ (a large resistor, typically $1 \text{ M}\Omega$ to $10 \text{ M}\Omega$), with the source grounded.

$$Z_{in} = R_G$$

**Step 3: Output Impedance.**

With $v_{gs} = 0$ (input shorted) and dependent current source $g_m v_{gs} = 0$ open:

$$Z_{out} = R_D$$

---

### Derivation 4: Output Impedance of Emitter Follower (CC) — The Bootstrap Effect

This is a more involved derivation frequently tested by KTU. Recall the emitter follower has the load in the emitter, with $R_E$ in parallel with $R_L$.

**Step 1: Deactivate the source ($v_s = 0$), remove $R_L$, apply $v_{test}$ at the emitter.**

**Step 2: KCL at the emitter node.**
The test current $i_{test}$ splits into two paths: through $R_E$ to ground, and into the emitter of the BJT (which then flows up through $r_{\pi}$ and back into the source via $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2$).

The current into the emitter is $i_e = -i_{test}$ (entering the emitter). This creates a base current $i_b = i_e/(\beta+1)$.

**Step 3: KVL around the base-emitter loop.**

$$v_{test} = i_b \cdot r_{\pi} + i_e \cdot R_S'$$

**Step 4: Substitute $i_b = i_e/(\beta+1)$.**

$$v_{test} = \frac{i_e \cdot r_{\pi}}{\beta+1} + i_e \cdot R_S' = i_e \left( \frac{r_{\pi}}{\beta+1} + R_S' \right)$$

**Step 5: Combine with $R_E$ in parallel.**

$$\boxed{Z_{out} = R_E \parallel \left( \frac{r_{\pi} + R_S'}{\beta+1} \right)}$$

where $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The emitter follower presents a very **low output impedance** — on the order of a few ohms to tens of ohms — which is why it is used as a **buffer stage** between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load (e.g., speaker driver).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Amplifier with Source and Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source V_S] -->|internal R_S| Bnode[Input Port]
    Bnode -->|Z_in| C[Amplifier Core]
    C -->|Z_out| Dnode[Output Port]
    Dnode -->|Load R_L| E[Output V_L]
    F[Input Voltage V_in] -.-> Bnode
    G[Output Voltage V_out] -.-> Dnode
    H[Thevenin V_th] -.-> C
```

**Node Legend:**
- `Source V_S` — Ideal signal source with internal resistance `R_S`
- `Amplifier Core` — Active device with voltage gain `A_vo`
- `Output V_L` — Voltage delivered to the actual load
- `Z_in` and `Z_out` — The two impedances under study

---

### 4.2 Two-Port Network Parameter Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Port 1 Input] -->|I1| B1[Two-Port Network]
    A1 -->|V1| B1
    B1 -->|I2| A2[Port 2 Output]
    B1 -->|V2| A2
    C1[h11 = Z_in] -.-> B1
    C2[h22 = 1 over Z_out] -.-> B1
    C3[h21 = Current Gain] -.-> B1
    C4[h12 = Reverse Feedback] -.-> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the h-parameter (hybrid) model, **$h_{11} = h_i$** is the **input impedance with output shorted**, and **$1/h_{22} = 1/h_o$** is the **output impedance with input open**. These are the KTU-approved parameters for BJT small-signal analysis.

---

### 4.3 Effect of Mismatched Impedances — Loading Effect Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V_S with R_S] -->|Divider 1| B[V_in at Z_in]
    B -->|Amplifier A_vo| C[V_out at Z_out]
    C -->|Divider 2| D[V_L at R_L]
    E[Step 1: V_in = V_S x Z_in over Z_in+R_S] -.-> B
    F[Step 2: V_out = A_vo x V_in] -.-> C
    G[Step 3: V_L = V_out x R_L over R_L+Z_out] -.-> D
```

**Reading the diagram:** Signal loss occurs at **two voltage dividers** — one at the input (source $R_S$ vs amplifier $Z_{in}$) and one at the output (amplifier $Z_{out}$ vs load $R_L$). Overall gain is the product of both dividers and the intrinsic gain.

---

### 4.4 Impedance Comparison Across BJT Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Common Emitter CE] -->|Z_in = R1||R2||r_pi| A1[Medium Z_in]
    A -->|Z_out = R_C| A2[High Z_out]
    B[Common Collector CC] -->|Z_in = R1||R2||beta+1 x R_E| B1[High Z_in]
    B -->|Z_out = R_E||small term| B2[Low Z_out]
    C[Common Base CB] -->|Z_in = small| C1[Very Low Z_in]
    C -->|Z_out = R_C| C2[High Z_out]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define input impedance and output impedance of an amplifier. Why is a high input impedance and low output impedance desired for an ideal voltage amplifier?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Input Impedance** is the impedance presented by the amplifier to the source at its input terminals: $Z_{in} = V_{in}/I_{in}$ with the load connected. **(1 mark)**
- **Output Impedance** is the Thevenin equivalent impedance looking back into the output terminals with sources deactivated and load removed. **(1 mark)**
- For an ideal voltage amplifier: high $Z_{in}$ ensures the source voltage is not dropped across the source resistance (no loading at input), and low $Z_{out}$ ensures the output voltage is delivered to the load without being divided down by the internal impedance (no loading at output). **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Differentiate between the input impedances of common-emitter, common-collector, and common-base BJT amplifier configurations.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Configuration | Input Impedance | Order of Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Common Emitter | $R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_{\pi}$ | **Medium** ($\sim$ k$\Omega$) |
| Common Collector | $R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel (\beta+1)R_E$ | **High** (tens of k$\Omega$ to M$\Omega$) |
| Common Base | $R_E \parallel r_{\pi}/(\beta+1)$ | **Very Low** (few $\Omega$ to tens of $\Omega$) |

The common-collector has the highest input impedance due to the $(\beta+1)$ multiplier on $R_E$, while the common-base has the lowest because the input is at the emitter. **(1 mark for trend explanation)**

---

### Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the input impedance $Z_{in}$ of a BJT common-emitter amplifier with an unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$, bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$, and load $R_L$ connected. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same amplifier, derive the output impedance $Z_{out}$ and explain why a CE amplifier is unsuitable for driving low-impedance loads. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution:**

The small-signal model of the CE amplifier shows the base connected through $r_{\pi}$ to the emitter, which has $R_E$ to ground (un-bypassed). Applying KVL at the input loop:

$$v_{in} = i_b \cdot r_{\pi} + i_e \cdot R_E$$

Since $i_e = (\beta+1)i_b$:

$$v_{in} = i_b \cdot r_{\pi} + (\beta+1)i_b \cdot R_E = i_b \big[ r_{\pi} + (\beta+1)R_E \big]$$

The impedance looking into the base is:

$$Z_b = \frac{v_{in}}{i_b} = r_{\pi} + (\beta+1)R_E$$

Combining with the bias network in parallel:

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel \big[ r_{\pi} + (\beta+1)R_E \big]}$$

**Marks Distribution (a):**
- Correct KVL setup: **2 marks**
- Substitution of $i_e$ relation: **2 marks**
- Final $Z_b$ expression: **1 mark**
- Final $Z_{in}$ with bias network: **2 marks**

**(b) Solution:**

To find $Z_{out}$, set $v_s = 0$. With the source shorted, the base is connected to ground through $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2$, making $i_b = 0$ (no driving voltage across $r_{\pi}$). The dependent current source $g_m v_{be}$ then becomes an **open circuit** since $v_{be} = i_b \cdot r_{\pi} = 0$.

Applying a test voltage $v_{test}$ at the output (with $R_L$ removed):

$$Z_{out} = \frac{v_{test}}{i_{test}} = R_C$$

$$\boxed{Z_{out} = R_C}$$

**Why CE is unsuitable for low-impedance loads:**

The loaded voltage gain is:
$$A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel R_L) \cdot \frac{r_{\pi}}{r_{\pi} + (\beta+1)R_E}$$

If $R_L \ll R_C$, then $R_C \parallel R_L \approx R_L$, so $A_v \approx -g_m R_L$ which becomes very small. The high output impedance ($Z_{out} = R_C$) means the amplifier's output voltage divides across $R_C$ and $R_L$, with most of the signal being dropped across $R_C$ when $R_L$ is small. **A buffer (emitter follower) is needed to drive low-impedance loads.** **(3 marks explanation)**

**Marks Distribution (b):**
- Deactivation of source: **2 marks**
- Justification of $i_b = 0$ and dependent source open: **1 mark**
- Final $Z_{out} = R_C$: **1 mark**
- Loading effect explanation: **3 marks**

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the input and output impedance of a MOSFET common-source amplifier with bias resistor $R_G$, drain resistor $R_D$, and load $R_L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the input and output impedances of CS, CD (source follower), and CG configurations. State one application of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solution:**

For a MOSFET common-source amplifier:
- The gate is insulated, drawing no current at signal frequencies (ignoring capacitances).
- The signal is applied at the gate through $R_G$.
- The drain sees $R_D$ in parallel with $R_L$ and the dependent current source $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source.

**Input Impedance:**
Since the gate draws no current, the input sees only $R_G$:
$$\boxed{Z_{in} = R_G}$$

Typically $R_G = 1 \text{ M}\Omega$ to $10 \text{ M}\Omega$, making MOSFET CS ideal for high-impedance sources.

**Output Impedance:**
With $v_{gs} = 0$ (input grounded), $g_m v_{gs} = 0$, so the dependent source is open. With $R_L$ removed:
$$\boxed{Z_{out} = R_D}$$

**Marks Distribution (a):**
- Identification that gate draws no current: **2 marks**
- $Z_{in} = R_G$ derivation: **1 mark**
- $Z_{out}$ setup with source off: **2 marks**
- Final $Z_{out} = R_D$: **2 marks**

**(b) Comparison and Applications:**

| Configuration | $Z_{in}$ | $Z_{out}$ | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Common Source (CS)** | $R_G$ (very high, M$\Omega$) | $R_D$ (moderate) | **Voltage amplifier** — general purpose gain stage |
| **Common Drain (CD) / Source Follower** | $R_G$ (very high) | $1/g_m \parallel R_S$ (very low) | **Buffer** — impedance matching between high-Z source and low-Z load |
| **Common Gate (CG)** | $1/g_m$ (very low) | $R_D$ (moderate) | **High-frequency amplifier** — eliminates Miller effect, used in RF |

**Marks Distribution (b):**
- CS values: **1 mark**
- CD values: **2 marks** (CD requires showing $Z_{out} = 1/g_m \parallel R_S$)
- CG values: **1 mark**
- Applications: **3 marks** (1 mark per correct application)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to turn off independent sources** when finding $Z_{out}$ — but **never turn off dependent sources**. This is the #1 mistake costing 2-3 marks per question.
> 2. **Mixing up the formula for CE with bypassed vs unbypassed $R_E$.** If $R_E$ is bypassed by $C_E$, use $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_{\pi}$. If unbypassed, use the $(\beta+1)R_E$ version.
> 3. **For MOSFET circuits, students often write $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2$** as in BJT. But MOSFET gates draw no current, so $Z_{in} = R_G$ (the single large gate resistor, not the divider).
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU examiners often deduct 0.5 mark for missing units like $\Omega$, k$\Omega$, M$\Omega$.
> 5. **Confusing Zout with R_L.** Zout is the impedance of the amplifier alone, **not including the load**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 LAST-MINUTE REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 SCHEME**
> - [ ] **$Z_{in}$** = $V_{in}/I_{in}$ with **load connected**.
> - [ ] **$Z_{out}$** = Thevenin impedance at output with **sources off, load removed**.
> - [ ] **Independent sources are turned OFF**; **dependent sources are NOT turned off**.
> - [ ] **Ideal voltage amplifier:** $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$.
> - [ ] **Ideal current amplifier:** $Z_{in} = 0$, $Z_{out} = \infty$.
> - [ ] **CE BJT:** $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_{\pi}$ (bypassed), $Z_{out} = R_C$.
> - [ ] **CC BJT (Emitter Follower):** $Z_{in}$ is **HIGH** (has $\beta+1$ multiplier); $Z_{out}$ is **LOW** (has $\beta+1$ divisor).
> - [ ] **CB BJT:** $Z_{in}$ is **LOW** ($r_{\pi}/(\beta+1)$); $Z_{out} = R_C$.
> - [ ] **CS MOSFET:** $Z_{in} = R_G$ (very high), $Z_{out} = R_D$.
> - [ ] **CD MOSFET (Source Follower):** $Z_{in} = R_G$, $Z_{out} = 1/g_m \parallel R_S$ (very low).
> - [ ] **CG MOSFET:** $Z_{in} = 1/g_m$ (very low), $Z_{out} = R_D$.
> - [ ] **Overall voltage gain** involves **two voltage dividers** — one at input, one at output.
> - [ ] **h-parameter link:** $h_{ie} = Z_{in}\vert_{v_{ce}=0}$ and $h_{oe} = 1/Z_{out}\vert_{i_b=0}$.
> - [ ] **Loading effect** = signal loss caused by $R_S$ interacting with $Z_{in}$, and $Z_{out}$ interacting with $R_L$.
> - [ ] **Use buffer (CC/CD) stages** when cascading high-Z source to low-Z load.
> - [ ] **Always include units** in the final answer ($\Omega$, k$\Omega$, M$\Omega$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Frequency Equivalent Circuits of BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** High Frequency Equivalent Circuits of BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **High Frequency Equivalent Circuit of a BJT** is a small-signal model that accurately represents the transistor's behaviour when the operating signal frequencies become comparable to, or exceed, the internal transit-time and junction-charging effects of the device. At low frequencies, the coupling and bypass capacitors dominate the response; at high frequencies, the **internal parasitic capacitances** of the transistor (specifically the base–emitter depletion capacitance and the base–collector depletion capacitance) dominate and dictate the upper cutoff frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The hybrid-π model is the most widely used high-frequency small-signal equivalent circuit of a BJT. It includes the **base-spreading resistance $r_{bb'}$,** the **input resistance $r_{\pi}$,** the **voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{b'e}$,** the **output resistance $r_o$,** the **base–emitter diffusion capacitance $C_{\pi}$,** and the **base–collector junction capacitance $C_{\mu}$.**

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Tap & Water Hammer"

Imagine a **BJT as a high-precision water tap** mounted on a wall:
- The **base terminal** is the *control handle* — the harder you turn, the more water (collector current) flows.
- The **water flowing out** is the *collector current* $i_C$.
- At **low frequencies** (slowly turning the handle), the tap responds smoothly and predictably.
- At **high frequencies** (rapidly jiggling the handle), the *inertia of water* in the long pipe attached to the tap causes a **"water hammer" effect** — a pressure wave that lags and distorts the output. This lag is exactly analogous to the **junction capacitances $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$** inside the BJT, which store charge and slow down the response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics used in this module (Kerala, KTU):**
> - Thermal Voltage at $T = 300\,\text{K}$: $V_T = \mathbf{26\,\text{mV}}$ (commonly rounded as $\mathbf{25\,\text{mV}}$)
> - Boltzmann constant: $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}}$
> - Electronic charge: $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}}$
> - Reference Temperature: $T = \mathbf{300\,\text{K}}$

### 1.3 Why High-Frequency Analysis is Needed

At low frequencies, the coupling/bypass capacitors and the BJT's internal capacitances act as **open circuits**, leading to the familiar $h$-parameter or simplified hybrid-π model. As frequency rises:

1. The **base–emitter diffusion capacitance $C_{\pi}$** becomes a low-impedance path, *bypassing* the input signal away from the base–emitter junction.
2. The **base–collector capacitance $C_{\mu}$** (Miller capacitance) provides an internal *feedforward path* from output back to input, causing gain roll-off.
3. The **current gain $\beta$** is no longer constant — it falls off at **$-20\,\text{dB/decade}$** beyond a certain pole frequency $f_{\beta}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The two highest-weightage derivations are (i) the **CE short-circuit current gain** expression and (ii) the **Gain–Bandwidth Product ($f_T$)**. Master these — they appear in nearly every End Semester Examination (ESE) paper.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hybrid-π Model — Component-by-Component Breakdown

The **hybrid-π equivalent circuit of a BJT** is drawn between three intrinsic nodes: the *internal base node* $B'$, the *emitter node* $E$, and the *collector node* $C$. The external base $B$ is connected to $B'$ through the **base-spreading resistance $r_{bb'}$**.

**Step 1 — Base-Spreading Resistance ($r_{bb'}$):**
- Represents the **ohmic resistance** of the inactive base region between the external base contact and the active base region under the emitter.
- Typical value: **$10\,\Omega$ to $300\,\Omega$**.
- Physically, it is the resistance of the bulk semiconductor between the base terminal and the effective base junction.

**Step 2 — Input Resistance ($r_{\pi}$):**
- Models the **forward-biased base–emitter junction**.
- $r_{\pi}$ is the small-signal resistance seen looking into the base–emitter junction with collector shorted to AC ground.
- $r_{\pi} = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta \cdot V_T}{I_C}$

**Step 3 — Transconductance ($g_m$):**
- Quantifies how effectively the input voltage $v_{b'e}$ controls the output collector current.
- $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T} = \dfrac{\beta}{r_{\pi}}$
- Units: **Siemens (A/V)** or **mho**.

**Step 4 — Output Resistance ($r_o$):**
- Models the **Early effect** (base-width modulation).
- $r_o = \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$, where $V_A$ is the Early voltage.
- For many high-frequency problems, $r_o$ is taken as $\infty$ unless explicitly given.

**Step 5 — Base–Emitter Diffusion Capacitance ($C_{\pi}$):**
- Models the **charge storage** in the forward-biased base–emitter junction due to minority-carrier injection.
- Dominant at high frequencies.
- $C_{\pi} = C_{be} + C_{je}$, where $C_{be}$ is the diffusion capacitance and $C_{je}$ is the depletion-region capacitance.

**Step 6 — Base–Collector Capacitance ($C_{\mu}$):**
- Models the **reverse-biased base–collector junction depletion capacitance**.
- Often called the **Miller capacitance** because of its effect via Miller's theorem.
- $C_{\mu} = C_{bc}$ (small-signal capacitance of BC junction).

### 2.2 Frequency Response of CE Short-Circuit Current Gain

When the output is **AC-shorted** ($R_L = 0$), the only capacitances that matter are $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$ because $C_{\mu}$ has no bootstrapping effect when output is grounded.

The current gain expression is:

$$A_{is} = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + j\omega \cdot r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

This is a **single-pole low-pass response** with:
- **DC (low-frequency) gain:** $\beta_0$
- **3-dB cutoff (Beta cutoff) frequency:** $\omega_{\beta} = \dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$

### 2.3 Miller's Theorem and the High-Frequency CE Voltage Amplifier

When the BJT is used in a **CE amplifier with a load $R_L$**, $C_{\mu}$ connects the output node to the input node. Using **Miller's Theorem**, $C_{\mu}$ is replaced by:
- **Miller input capacitance:** $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)$
- **Miller output capacitance:** $C_{M,\text{out}} = C_{\mu}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{g_m R_L}\right) \approx C_{\mu}$

The effective **input pole** becomes:

$$\omega_H = \frac{1}{(r_{bb'} \Vert r_{\pi}) \cdot [C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)]}$$

### 2.4 The Unity-Gain Frequency ($f_T$) — Gain-Bandwidth Product

The **transition frequency $f_T$** is defined as the frequency at which the **common-emitter short-circuit current gain drops to unity** ($\vert A_{is} \vert = 1$).

Beyond $f_{\beta}$, the gain magnitude falls at $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$. Therefore, the **gain-bandwidth product** is constant:

$$f_T = \beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — The "Universal Trade-off":** Every BJT has a fixed $f_T$ (typically 100 MHz to 10 GHz for modern devices). If you want **high gain**, you must accept a **low bandwidth**, and vice versa. This is the central design constraint in every RF and high-speed analog circuit — from your Wi-Fi receiver to the op-amp inside a medical ECG machine.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Parameter / Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $g_m$ | $\dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Transconductance | $\text{A/V}$ |
| $r_{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Input base–emitter resistance | $\Omega$ |
| $r_o$ | $\dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$ | Output resistance (Early) | $\Omega$ |
| $\omega_{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$ | Beta cutoff angular frequency | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| $f_{\beta}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$ | Beta cutoff frequency | $\text{Hz}$ |
| $f_T$ | $\dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$ | Unity-gain / transition frequency | $\text{Hz}$ |
| $C_M$ (Miller input) | $C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)$ | Effective input capacitance | $\text{F}$ |
| $C_{M,\text{out}}$ (Miller output) | $C_{\mu}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{g_m R_L}\right)$ | Effective output capacitance | $\text{F}$ |
| $A_{is}(\omega)$ | $\dfrac{\beta_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_{\beta}}$ | CE short-circuit current gain | dimensionless |
| $\vert A_{is} \vert$ | $\dfrac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega/\omega_{\beta})^2}}$ | Magnitude of current gain | dimensionless |
| $f_{\text{3dB (voltage)}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi (r_{bb'}\Vert r_{\pi}) [C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)]}$ | CE amplifier 3-dB freq | $\text{Hz}$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **RF Amplifiers (Radio Receivers):** The 3-dB bandwidth of tuned RF stages is engineered using $f_T$ considerations.
- **High-Speed Op-Amps (e.g., LM7171, THS4631):** Designers select BJTs with $f_T > 10\,\text{GHz}$ to ensure gain flatness in the MHz-to-GHz range.
- **Oscilloscope Front-Ends & Probes:** Use BJTs with $f_T$ well above the oscilloscope's rated bandwidth.
- **Optical Communication Transimpedance Amplifiers (TIAs):** Bandwidth is limited by the Miller capacitance $C_M$; designers use the **cascode configuration** to mitigate this.
- **Bluetooth/Wi-Fi Transceivers:** $f_T$ determines the upper limit of the RF front-end's gain at 2.4 GHz / 5 GHz.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of CE Short-Circuit Current Gain $A_{is}(s)$

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 1 of PEICT413 (Analog Circuits). The KTU 2024 scheme typically allocates 7 marks for this exact derivation.

**Step 1 — Circuit Setup**
Consider a BJT in CE configuration with output **AC-shorted** (i.e., $R_L = 0$, $v_{ce} = 0$). Apply a small-signal test current $i_b$ at the base.

**Step 2 — Identify the Current-Voltage Relationship**
The voltage developed at the internal base–emitter node is:

$$v_{b'e} = i_b \cdot Z_{\text{in}}$$

where $Z_{\text{in}}$ is the impedance seen from $B'$ to $E$ — this is $r_{\pi}$ in parallel with $C_{\pi}$, **plus** $C_{\mu}$ (which is grounded at the other end because output is shorted):

$$Z_{\text{in}} = r_{\pi} \Vert \frac{1}{sC_{\pi}} \Vert \frac{1}{sC_{\mu}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the $B'$ Node**
The total admittance from $B'$ to $E$ is:

$$Y_{\text{in}} = \frac{1}{r_{\pi}} + sC_{\pi} + sC_{\mu}$$

Therefore:

$$v_{b'e} = \frac{i_b}{\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Transconductance Source**
The collector current produced by the controlled source is:

$$i_c = g_m \cdot v_{b'e} = \frac{g_m \cdot i_b}{\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 5 — Express Current Gain**
The CE short-circuit current gain is:

$$A_{is}(s) = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = \frac{g_m}{\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $g_m r_{\pi} = \beta_0$ and Factor**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{is}(s) &= \frac{g_m \cdot r_{\pi}}{1 + s \cdot r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} \\
&= \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s \cdot r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify the Pole Frequency**
Substituting $s = j\omega$ and defining:

$$\omega_{\beta} = \frac{1}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

we obtain the **canonical form**:

$$\boxed{A_{is}(j\omega) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + j\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{\beta}}}}$$

**Step 8 — Magnitude Form**

$$\vert A_{is} \vert = \frac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{\beta}}\right)^2}}$$

At $\omega = 0$ (DC): $\vert A_{is} \vert = \beta_0$ (the low-frequency current gain).
At $\omega = \omega_{\beta}$: $\vert A_{is} \vert = \dfrac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,\beta_0$ (the $-3\,\text{dB}$ point).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Unity-Gain Frequency $f_T$

**Step 1 — Use the High-Frequency Approximation**
At $\omega \gg \omega_{\beta}$:

$$\vert A_{is} \vert \approx \frac{\beta_0 \cdot \omega_{\beta}}{\omega}$$

**Step 2 — Set $\vert A_{is} \vert = 1$ to find $f_T$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= \frac{\beta_0 \cdot \omega_{\beta}}{\omega_T} \\
\omega_T &= \beta_0 \cdot \omega_{\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Expressions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_T &= \beta_0 \cdot \frac{1}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} \\
&= \frac{g_m \cdot r_{\pi}}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} \\
&= \frac{g_m}{C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hz**

$$\boxed{f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Result:** The **gain-bandwidth product** $f_T$ is a **constant for a given BJT at a given bias point**. Increasing bias current $I_C$ increases $g_m$ proportionally — so $f_T$ also rises (until transit-time effects take over). This is why RF designers often run BJTs at higher collector currents.

### 3.3 Derivation of Miller's Theorem (Applied to $C_{\mu}$)

**Step 1 — Statement of the Theorem**
Miller's Theorem states that an impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes with a voltage gain $K = v_o / v_i$ between them can be replaced by:
- An impedance $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - K}$ between the input node and ground.
- An impedance $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z \cdot K}{K - 1}$ between the output node and ground.

**Step 2 — Apply to a Capacitor $C_{\mu}$ with Gain $K$**
For a CE amplifier with midband voltage gain $A_v = -g_m R_L$:

$$K = -g_m R_L$$

The Miller input capacitance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= C_{\mu} (1 - K) \\
&= C_{\mu} (1 + g_m R_L) \quad \text{(since $K$ is negative)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The Miller output capacitance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,\text{out}} &= C_{\mu} \cdot \frac{K - 1}{K} \\
&= C_{\mu} \left(1 + \frac{1}{g_m R_L}\right) \approx C_{\mu} \quad \text{(for $g_m R_L \gg 1$)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Dominant Pole Approximation**
The input pole determines the upper $-3\,\text{dB}$ frequency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_H &= \frac{1}{R_{\text{eq}} \cdot C_{\text{eq}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{(r_{bb'} \Vert r_{\pi}) \cdot \left[C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $R_{\text{eq}} = r_{bb'} \Vert r_{\pi}$ is the equivalent resistance at the input node, and $C_{\text{eq}} = C_{\pi} + C_M$.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A BJT has the following parameters at $I_C = 1\,\text{mA}$ and $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$: $\beta_0 = 100$, $C_{\pi} = 20\,\text{pF}$, $C_{\mu} = 2\,\text{pF}$, $r_{bb'} = 50\,\Omega$. The BJT is used in a CE amplifier with $R_L = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate $g_m$, $r_{\pi}$, $f_{\beta}$, $f_T$, and $f_H$.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Transconductance $g_m$**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03846\,\text{S} = 38.46\,\text{mA/V}$$

**Step 2 — Input Resistance $r_{\pi}$**

$$r_{\pi} = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.03846} = 2600\,\Omega = 2.6\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Beta Cutoff Frequency $f_{\beta}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\beta} &= \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot r_{\pi} \cdot (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2600 \times (20 + 2) \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2600 \times 22 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= \frac{1}{3.593 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 2.78\,\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Unity-Gain Frequency $f_T$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_T &= \beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta} = 100 \times 2.78\,\text{MHz} \\
&= 278\,\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Voltage-Gain Midband**

$$A_v = -g_m R_L = -0.03846 \times 5000 = -192.3$$

**Step 6 — Miller Input Capacitance**

$$C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L) = 2\,\text{pF} \times (1 + 192.3) = 386.6\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 7 — Equivalent Input Resistance**

$$R_{\text{eq}} = r_{bb'} \Vert r_{\pi} = 50 \Vert 2600 = \frac{50 \times 2600}{2650} = 49.06\,\Omega$$

**Step 8 — Equivalent Input Capacitance**

$$C_{\text{eq}} = C_{\pi} + C_M = 20 + 386.6 = 406.6\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 9 — Upper 3-dB Frequency $f_H$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{eq}} C_{\text{eq}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 49.06 \times 406.6 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1.253 \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= 7.98\,\text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observations for the Board Examiner:** Notice that the **Miller effect multiplied $C_{\mu} = 2\,\text{pF}$ by 193×** to give $C_M \approx 387\,\text{pF}$. This is the **dominant** capacitance at the input — it is the primary bottleneck for high-frequency performance of CE amplifiers.
