## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the hybrid-π model of a BJT. List its components and state the significance of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The hybrid-π model is a small-signal equivalent circuit of a BJT valid for high-frequency analysis. Its components are:
1. **$r_{bb'}$** — Base-spreading resistance (ohmic resistance of the inactive base region).
2. **$r_{\pi}$** — Input resistance of the forward-biased base–emitter junction, $r_{\pi} = \beta / g_m$.
3. **$g_m v_{b'e}$** — Voltage-controlled current source representing the transistor's amplifying action, $g_m = I_C / V_T$.
4. **$r_o$** — Output resistance accounting for the Early effect, $r_o = V_A / I_C$.
5. **$C_{\pi}$** — Base–emitter diffusion + depletion capacitance, dominant at high frequencies.
6. **$C_{\mu}$** — Base–collector depletion capacitance, responsible for the Miller effect.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**What is the unity-gain frequency $f_T$ of a BJT? Derive its expression in terms of $g_m$, $C_{\pi}$, and $C_{\mu}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **unity-gain frequency $f_T$** (also called the transition frequency or current-gain-bandwidth product) is the frequency at which the magnitude of the common-emitter short-circuit current gain falls to unity.

**Expression:**

$$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta}$$

**Significance:** $f_T$ is a **figure of merit** of the BJT. It represents the upper frequency limit beyond which the device can no longer provide current gain. For a given device, the product of low-frequency current gain $\beta_0$ and bandwidth $f_{\beta}$ remains constant and equal to $f_T$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the CE short-circuit current gain $A_{is}$ of a BJT using the hybrid-π model. Show that the gain rolls off at $-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ above $f_{\beta}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Setup:** With output AC shorted, $v_{ce} = 0$, the capacitances $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$ appear in parallel between $B'$ and $E$.

**Step 2 — Input Impedance:**

$$Z_{\text{in}}(s) = r_{\pi} \Vert \frac{1}{sC_{\pi}} \Vert \frac{1}{sC_{\mu}} = \frac{r_{\pi}}{1 + s\,r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 3 — Internal Base–Emitter Voltage:**

$$v_{b'e}(s) = i_b(s) \cdot Z_{\text{in}}(s) = \frac{i_b(s) \cdot r_{\pi}}{1 + s\,r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 4 — Collector Current:**

$$i_c(s) = g_m \cdot v_{b'e}(s) = \frac{g_m \cdot r_{\pi} \cdot i_b(s)}{1 + s\,r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 5 — Current Gain:**

$$A_{is}(s) = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s\,r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ and define $\omega_{\beta}$:**

$$\omega_{\beta} = \frac{1}{r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} \Rightarrow A_{is}(j\omega) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + j\dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{\beta}}}$$

**Step 7 — High-Frequency Magnitude:**

For $\omega \gg \omega_{\beta}$:

$$\vert A_{is} \vert \approx \frac{\beta_0 \cdot \omega_{\beta}}{\omega}$$

In decibels:

$$\vert A_{is} \vert_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(\beta_0 \cdot \omega_{\beta}) - 20\log_{10}(\omega)$$

Since the second term varies as $-20\log_{10}(\omega)$, the gain rolls off at **$-20\,\text{dB/decade}$** (or equivalently, $-6\,\text{dB/octave}$). This proves the required rolloff. **(7 marks — all 7 steps awarded full credit)**

---

**(b) A BJT has $\beta_0 = 150$, $C_{\pi} = 15\,\text{pF}$, $C_{\mu} = 1.5\,\text{pF}$, $r_{bb'} = 100\,\Omega$, and is biased at $I_C = 2\,\text{mA}$ with $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$. It is used in a CE amplifier with $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate $g_m$, $r_{\pi}$, $f_{\beta}$, $f_T$, and the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Transconductance (1 mark):**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0769\,\text{S} = 76.9\,\text{mA/V}$$

**Step 2 — Input Resistance (1 mark):**

$$r_{\pi} = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{150}{0.0769} = 1950\,\Omega \approx 1.95\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Beta Cutoff Frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$f_{\beta} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot r_{\pi} \cdot (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1950 \times 16.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 4.95\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Unity-Gain Frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$f_T = \beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta} = 150 \times 4.95\,\text{MHz} = 742.5\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 5 — Miller Capacitance (1 mark):**

$$C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L) = 1.5\,\text{pF} \times (1 + 0.0769 \times 4700) = 1.5 \times 362.4 = 543.6\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent Input Resistance (0.5 mark):**

$$R_{\text{eq}} = r_{bb'} \Vert r_{\pi} = \frac{100 \times 1950}{2050} = 95.12\,\Omega$$

**Step 7 — Upper 3-dB Frequency (1 mark):**

$$C_{\text{eq}} = C_{\pi} + C_M = 15 + 543.6 = 558.6\,\text{pF}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{eq}} C_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 95.12 \times 558.6 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.99\,\text{MHz}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2, Module 1]**

**(a) Draw the high-frequency hybrid-π equivalent circuit of a BJT. Explain the physical origin of each parasitic capacitance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**

The hybrid-π model is drawn with:
- External base $B$ → $r_{bb'}$ → internal base $B'$
- $r_{\pi}$ between $B'$ and $E$
- $C_{\pi}$ in parallel with $r_{\pi}$
- $C_{\mu}$ between $B'$ and $C$
- $g_m v_{b'e}$ current source from $C$ to $E$
- $r_o$ in parallel with the current source

**Step 2 — $C_{\pi}$: Base–Emitter Diffusion Capacitance (1.5 marks):**

The forward-biased base–emitter junction has stored minority-carrier charge in the base region. When $v_{b'e}$ changes, this stored charge must change, giving rise to a **diffusion capacitance**. Additionally, the depletion region has a small junction capacitance. Thus:

$$C_{\pi} = C_{d} + C_{je} \quad \text{where } C_{d} = \tau_F g_m$$

**Step 3 — $C_{\mu}$: Base–Collector Junction Capacitance (1.5 marks):**

The base–collector junction is reverse-biased in the active region. A reverse-biased junction behaves as a **depletion capacitance** because varying the reverse voltage changes the depletion width, which is equivalent to varying the stored charge. Typical value: 0.5 to 5 pF.

**Step 4 — $r_{bb'}$: Base-Spreading Resistance (1 mark):**

The physical resistance of the bulk semiconductor path from the external base contact to the effective base region under the emitter. Increases with decreasing base doping.

**Step 5 — Significance (1 mark):** $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$ are the **primary high-frequency limiters**; at low frequencies they appear as open circuits and can be neglected.

---

**(b) Using Miller's theorem, derive the input and output Miller capacitances for a CE amplifier with midband voltage gain $A_v = -g_m R_L$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Statement of Miller's Theorem (1 mark):**

An impedance $Z$ connected between input node (voltage $v_i$) and output node (voltage $v_o$) with $v_o / v_i = K$ can be replaced by:
- $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - K}$ at the input.
- $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z \cdot K}{K - 1}$ at the output.

**Step 2 — Apply to $C_{\mu}$ (1 mark):**

For a capacitor $C_{\mu}$ with $K = A_v = -g_m R_L$:

$$Z = \frac{1}{sC_{\mu}}$$

**Step 3 — Miller Input Capacitance (2 marks):**

$$Z_1 = \frac{1}{sC_{\mu}(1 - K)} = \frac{1}{sC_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)}$$

This is equivalent to a capacitor:

$$C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)$$

**Step 4 — Miller Output Capacitance (1.5 marks):**

$$Z_2 = \frac{K}{sC_{\mu}(K - 1)} = \frac{1}{sC_{\mu}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{K}\right)} \approx \frac{1}{sC_{\mu}}$$

$$\Rightarrow C_{M,\text{out}} = C_{\mu}\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{g_m R_L}\right) \approx C_{\mu}$$

**Step 5 — Dominant Pole Expression (1.5 marks):**

The total input capacitance:

$$C_{\text{in,total}} = C_{\pi} + C_M = C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)$$

The upper 3-dB frequency:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (r_{bb'} \Vert r_{\pi}) [C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_L)]}$$

This shows that high-gain CE amplifiers (large $g_m R_L$) suffer from **reduced bandwidth** — the **gain-bandwidth trade-off**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 1):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})$ in $f_{\beta}$:** Many students write $f_{\beta} = 1/(2\pi r_{\pi} C_{\pi})$ only. The correct expression **must include $C_{\mu}$** because with output shorted, $C_{\mu}$ appears in parallel with $C_{\pi}$. (KTU Board marks: 1 mark deduction.)
> 2. **Confusing $f_{\beta}$ with $f_H$:** $f_{\beta}$ is the current-gain bandwidth (output shorted). $f_H$ is the **voltage-gain** bandwidth (with $R_L$). They are **different** by a factor related to $R_L$ and the Miller effect.
> 3. **Sign error in Miller's Theorem:** Always remember $K = -g_m R_L$ (negative for inverting CE amplifier). Using $K = +g_m R_L$ gives $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 - g_m R_L)$, which is **wrong** and can even give a negative capacitance.
> 4. **Not stating units:** $g_m$ in A/V or S, $f$ in Hz, $\omega$ in rad/s. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units in numerical answers.
> 5. **Ignoring $r_{bb'}$ in $f_H$ calculation:** $R_{\text{eq}} = r_{bb'} \Vert r_{\pi}$, NOT $r_{\pi}$ alone. This is a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Using $V_T = 25\,\text{mV}$ vs $26\,\text{mV}$:** Either is acceptable in KTU 2024, but **state the value used** explicitly to avoid ambiguity.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High Frequency Equivalent Circuits of BJT (Module 1)**

- ✅ The **hybrid-π model** is the standard high-frequency small-signal model of a BJT.
- ✅ Key elements: $r_{bb'}$, $r_{\pi}$, $g_m v_{b'e}$, $r_o$, $C_{\pi}$, $C_{\mu}$.
- ✅ **$g_m = I_C / V_T$** — transconductance increases linearly with bias current.
- ✅ **$r_{\pi} = \beta_0 / g_m$** — input resistance decreases with increasing bias.
- ✅ **$C_{\pi}$** = base–emitter diffusion capacitance; **$C_{\mu}$** = base–collector depletion capacitance.
- ✅ CE short-circuit current gain: $A_{is} = \dfrac{\beta_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_{\beta}}$, where $\omega_{\beta} = 1/[r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})]$.
- ✅ At $\omega \gg \omega_{\beta}$, current gain magnitude rolls off at **$-20\,\text{dB/decade}$**.
- ✅ **Miller's Theorem:** $C_{\mu}$ is multiplied by $(1 + g_m R_L)$ at the input — this is the **dominant limitation** on CE amplifier bandwidth.
- ✅ **Unity-gain frequency:** $f_T = g_m / [2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})] = \beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta}$ — the **gain-bandwidth product** is constant.
- ✅ $f_T$ is the **figure of merit** of a high-frequency BJT; typical values 100 MHz – 10 GHz.
- ✅ To extend bandwidth, designers use **cascode configuration** (which reduces Miller effect) or **negative feedback**.
- ✅ In numerical problems, $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at $T = 300\,\text{K}$ is the standard KTU assumption.
- ✅ Always remember: **higher $R_L$ → higher midband gain → lower $f_H$** (gain-bandwidth trade-off).
- ✅ $r_{bb'}$ is often the **dominant resistance** at the input node for high $f_T$ devices and must be included in $f_H$ calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Miller's Theorem (Definition):** If an impedance $Z_F$ is connected between two nodes where one is an input node and the other is an output node, and the voltage gain from the input node to the output node is $A_v = \dfrac{V_o}{V_i}$, then the impedance $Z_F$ can be replaced by two separate equivalent impedances: $Z_1$ connected from the input node to ground, and $Z_2$ connected from the output node to ground, where:
> $$Z_1 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{and} \quad Z_2 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - \frac{1}{A_v}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Miller Effect (Specific Case):** The Miller Effect is the application of Miller's Theorem to a **floating capacitance** (or impedance) that bridges the input and output terminals of an inverting amplifier. The effective capacitance seen at the input terminals appears **multiplied** by a factor of $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, which significantly reduces the bandwidth of the amplifier by creating a dominant low-frequency pole at the input.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Analogy:** Imagine you are trying to fill a small bucket (the input) with water from a tap, but the bucket has a small hole (the original feedback capacitor $C_{\mu}$) that leaks water into a much larger draining basin (the output). Now, suppose a clever mechanism amplifies the leak: every drop leaving the small bucket causes the basin to drop, which through a seesaw action **sucks an even larger amount of additional water** out of the small bucket to compensate. From the tap's perspective, it now looks like the bucket has a **much bigger hole** — even though physically the hole size hasn't changed. This "amplified leak" is the **Miller Effect**, and the apparent larger hole size is the **Miller Capacitance**, $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$.

## 1.3 Key Physical Insights

- The Miller Effect is fundamentally a consequence of **KCL (Kirchhoff's Current Law)** at the input node combined with the **voltage amplification** between two nodes.
- The gain $A_v$ is generally **negative** for common-emitter / common-source amplifiers, hence the use of $(1 - A_v)$ in the denominator, which becomes $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ for magnitudes.
- The constant of proportionality **$\mathbf{C_{\mu}}$** (the feedback capacitance) and the **midband voltage gain** $\mathbf{A_v}$ together determine the dominant high-frequency pole of the amplifier.
- The Miller Effect is most pronounced in **Common-Emitter (CE)** and **Common-Source (CS)** configurations, and it is the **fundamental reason** for the high-frequency limitation (3 dB cutoff) in BJT and FET amplifiers.
- Standard metric: The dominant pole frequency is $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,Miller}}$ where $R_{eq}$ is the Thévenin equivalent resistance at the input node.

## 1.4 Why is it Important for KTU 2024?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Miller Effect is a **high-yield topic** in KTU Analog Circuits. Questions frequently appear in **Part A (3 marks)** as direct formula-based questions, and in **Part B (14 marks)** as part of frequency response analysis of single-stage and multi-stage amplifiers. Mastering the Miller multiplier $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ is non-negotiable for board exam success.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Miller's Theorem — The Underlying Logic

The derivation of Miller's Theorem rests on three simple but powerful circuit observations:

- **Node Observation 1:** A floating impedance $Z_F$ between input node (voltage $V_i$) and output node (voltage $V_o$) carries a current $I = \dfrac{V_i - V_o}{Z_F}$.

- **Node Observation 2:** The voltage at the input node can be expressed as $V_i = V_i - 0$ (i.e., referenced to ground), and similarly $V_o = A_v \cdot V_i$ (i.e., referenced to the input).

- **Node Observation 3 (The Splitting Idea):** Instead of one floating impedance, we can split it into two grounded impedances provided each one draws the **same current** as the original at the respective node.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Current through original $Z_F$ from input to output:**
$$I = \frac{V_i - V_o}{Z_F} = \frac{V_i - A_v V_i}{Z_F} = \frac{V_i (1 - A_v)}{Z_F}$$

2. **Equivalent input current (using $Z_1$ from input to ground):**
$$I_1 = \frac{V_i - 0}{Z_1} = \frac{V_i}{Z_1}$$

3. **Equating $I_1 = I$ for the input node to remain electrically identical:**
$$\frac{V_i}{Z_1} = \frac{V_i(1 - A_v)}{Z_F} \implies Z_1 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - A_v}$$

4. **Repeating the process for the output side** (current entering output from the floating $Z_F$):
$$I' = \frac{V_o - V_i}{Z_F} = \frac{V_o(1 - \frac{1}{A_v})}{Z_F}$$

5. **Equivalent output impedance:**
$$Z_2 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - \frac{1}{A_v}} = \frac{Z_F \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern IC design (op-amps, RF amplifiers, video amplifiers), the Miller Effect is deliberately exploited or mitigated. **Cascode amplifiers** are specifically designed to **nullify the Miller Effect** by reducing the gain across the parasitic $C_{\mu}$, dramatically improving high-frequency response. Conversely, in **Miller Compensated op-amps**, a large capacitor is intentionally placed in the feedback path and the Miller multiplication is used to create a dominant low-frequency pole for stability.

## 2.2 The Miller Capacitance — Specialization for Capacitors

When the floating impedance is a **pure capacitor** $C_{\mu}$, its impedance is $Z_F = \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_{\mu}}$. Substituting into the Miller Theorem:

$$Z_1 = \frac{1}{j \omega C_{\mu}(1 - A_v)} = \frac{1}{j \omega C_M}$$

where $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v)$ is the **Miller Capacitance**.

For an inverting amplifier where $A_v$ is **negative** (e.g., $A_v = -100$ for a CE stage), we get:
$$C_M = C_{\mu}(1 - (-100)) = C_{\mu}(1 + 100) = 101 \, C_{\mu}$$

This is the famous **Miller Multiplication** factor.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Miller's Theorem (Input) | $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z_F}{1 - A_v}$ | $Z_F$ = floating impedance, $A_v$ = voltage gain | Valid for any linear 2-port |
| 2 | Miller's Theorem (Output) | $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z_F}{1 - \frac{1}{A_v}}$ | Same as above | Equivalent output impedance |
| 3 | Miller Capacitance (Input) | $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v)$ | $C_{\mu}$ = feedback capacitance | When $A_v$ is negative, $C_M \approx C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ |
| 4 | Miller Capacitance (Output) | $C_{M,out} = C_{\mu}\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right)$ | Output side Miller cap | Often neglected when $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$ |
| 5 | Total Input Capacitance (CE) | $C_{in} = C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | $C_{\pi}$ = base-emitter diffusion cap | $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$ from hybrid-$\pi$ model |
| 6 | Dominant Pole Frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig} \vert \vert R_B \cdot C_{in}}$ | $R_{sig}$ = source resistance, $R_B$ = bias resistor | Upper 3-dB frequency |
| 7 | Effective Load Resistance | $R_{eq} = R_C \vert \vert R_L$ | For CE amplifier output | Thevenin at collector |
| 8 | CE Midband Voltage Gain | $A_v = -g_m (R_C \vert \vert R_L)$ | $g_m$ = transconductance | Negative sign = phase inversion |
| 9 | Gain-Bandwidth Product Invariance | $(GBW)_{original} = (GBW)_{with \, Miller} = constant$ | Holds in ideal cascaded stages | Used in cascode design verification |
| 10 | Miller Multiplier Factor | $M = (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Multiplier of $C_{\mu}$ at input | **Most important KTU formula** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In all KTU 2024 board answers, the absolute value $\vert A_v \vert$ is **mandatory** when writing the Miller capacitance expression for an inverting amplifier. Writing $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v)$ without absolute value will result in **partial mark deduction** by strict examiners, since $C_M$ is a physical capacitance and must be positive.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **BJT and FET High-Frequency Amplifiers:** The Miller Effect sets the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ in single-stage CE/CS amplifiers.
- **Op-Amp Stability Compensation:** The dominant-pole compensation technique (used in the classic $\mu$A741 op-amp) uses a 30 pF capacitor and Miller multiplication to achieve an effective capacitance of several hundred pF on-chip.
- **Cascode Topology:** Engineers cascade a CG (common-gate) or CB (common-base) stage to reduce the voltage gain across $C_{\mu}$ to nearly unity, effectively eliminating the Miller Effect.
- **RF and Microwave Amplifier Design:** At GHz frequencies, the Miller capacitance becomes a critical parasitic that must be modeled and minimized.
- **Integrated Circuit Layout:** Modern VLSI designers place the base/collector junction (or gate/drain junction) to minimize $C_{\mu}$ since it gets multiplied by the stage gain.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Miller's Theorem (General Impedance Case)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Derivation Setup:** Consider a linear two-port network with a floating impedance $Z_F$ connected between the input node (voltage $V_1$) and the output node (voltage $V_2$). We assume the network is linear so that superposition applies, and we define the voltage gain from the input port to the output port as $A_v = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$.

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Current flowing through the floating impedance $Z_F$ from input to output**

By Ohm's law applied to $Z_F$:

$$I_F = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_F}$$

**Step 2: Express the output voltage in terms of the input voltage using the gain $A_v$**

$$V_2 = A_v \cdot V_1$$

**Step 3: Substitute $V_2$ into the current expression**

$$I_F = \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z_F} = \frac{V_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z_F}$$

**Step 4: Define an equivalent input-side grounded impedance $Z_1$ that draws the same current $I_F$ from the input source**

The current drawn by $Z_1$ connected between the input node and ground is:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_1 - 0}{Z_1} = \frac{V_1}{Z_1}$$

**Step 5: Equate $I_1 = I_F$ (the input node must not "see" any difference)**

$$\frac{V_1}{Z_1} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z_F}$$

**Step 6: Cancel $V_1$ from both sides (assuming $V_1 \neq 0$) and solve for $Z_1$**

$$Z_1 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - A_v}$$

**Step 7: Derive the output-side equivalent impedance $Z_2$**

The current leaving the output node into $Z_F$ (i.e., entering $Z_F$ from the output side):

$$I_F' = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{Z_F} = \frac{V_2 - \frac{V_2}{A_v}}{Z_F} = \frac{V_2\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right)}{Z_F}$$

**Step 8: Equate to the current drawn by a grounded $Z_2$ at the output**

$$\frac{V_2}{Z_2} = \frac{V_2\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right)}{Z_F}$$

**Step 9: Solve for $Z_2$**

$$Z_2 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - \frac{1}{A_v}} = \frac{A_v \cdot Z_F}{A_v - 1}$$

**Step 10: Verification of Power Conservation**

The total power absorbed by $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ in the equivalent circuit must equal the power absorbed by $Z_F$ in the original circuit.

$$P_{Z_F} = \vert I_F \vert^2 \cdot \text{Re}(Z_F) = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert^2 \vert 1 - A_v \vert^2}{\vert Z_F \vert^2} \cdot \text{Re}(Z_F)$$

$$P_{Z_1} + P_{Z_2} = \frac{\vert V_1 \vert^2}{\vert Z_1 \vert} \cdot \cos(\theta_1) + \frac{\vert V_2 \vert^2}{\vert Z_2 \vert} \cdot \cos(\theta_2)$$

Since the impedances are all proportional, the power balance holds. ✓

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Miller Capacitance for CE Amplifier

> [!NOTE]
> **Application to BJT Common-Emitter Amplifier using the hybrid-$\pi$ model:** The small-signal high-frequency model of a BJT has two parasitic capacitances: $C_{\pi}$ (base-emitter diffusion + junction capacitance) connected from base to emitter, and $C_{\mu}$ (collector-base junction capacitance) connected from base to collector. The $C_{\mu}$ is the "floating" impedance that gives rise to the Miller Effect.

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Identify the floating impedance**

$$Z_F = \frac{1}{j \omega C_{\mu}}$$

**Step 2: Identify the voltage gain of the CE stage (midband value)**

For a CE amplifier with collector resistor $R_C$ and load $R_L$:

$$A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_{be}} = -g_m (R_C \mid\mid R_L)$$

The negative sign indicates phase inversion. Let $A_v = -K$ where $K = g_m (R_C \mid\mid R_L) > 0$.

**Step 3: Apply Miller's Theorem to find the input equivalent capacitance**

$$C_{in,Miller} = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v) = C_{\mu}(1 - (-K)) = C_{\mu}(1 + K)$$

**Step 4: Recognize that the base-emitter capacitance $C_{\pi}$ is already grounded at the emitter, so it adds in parallel**

$$C_{in,total} = C_{\pi} + C_{in,Miller} = C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + K)$$

**Step 5: Apply the equivalent input resistance from the source side**

The Thévenin resistance at the base node is $R_{eq} = R_{sig} \mid\mid R_B \mid\mid r_{\pi}$, where $R_{sig}$ is the source resistance and $R_B$ is the bias network.

**Step 6: Calculate the upper 3-dB frequency (dominant pole)**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} \, C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} [C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)]}$$

**Step 7: Verify with the time-constant method (Open-Circuit Time Constants)**

The open-circuit time constant at the input node is:

$$\tau_{in} = R_{eq} \cdot [C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)]$$

The Miller contribution dominates when $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$ and $C_{\mu} \vert A_v \vert \gg C_{\pi}$, which is the typical scenario for moderate-to-high gain amplifiers.

## 3.3 Derivation: Output-Side Miller Capacitance

**Step 1: Apply Miller's Theorem at the output**

$$C_{out,Miller} = C_{\mu}\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right)$$

**Step 2: For a CE amplifier with $A_v = -K$**

$$C_{out,Miller} = C_{\mu}\left(1 - \frac{1}{-K}\right) = C_{\mu}\left(1 + \frac{1}{K}\right)$$

**Step 3: When $K \gg 1$ (typical case), simplify**

$$C_{out,Miller} \approx C_{\mu}$$

This is **much smaller** than the input-side Miller capacitance, which is why the input pole dominates the high-frequency response.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A common-emitter amplifier has the following parameters: $g_m = 40$ mA/V, $r_{\pi} = 1$ k$\Omega$, $C_{\pi} = 10$ pF, $C_{\mu} = 2$ pF, $R_C = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_{sig} = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_B = 10$ k$\Omega$. Calculate the midband voltage gain, the total input Miller capacitance, and the upper 3-dB frequency.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate the midband voltage gain**

$$A_v = -g_m (R_C \mid\mid R_L) = -(40 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot \left(\frac{5 \times 5}{5 + 5}\right) \times 10^3$$

$$R_C \mid\mid R_L = \frac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L} = \frac{5 \times 5}{5 + 5} = 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = -40 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.5 \times 10^{3} = -100$$

**Step 2: Calculate the input-side Miller capacitance**

$$C_{M,in} = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v) = 2 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 - (-100)) = 2 \times 101 = 202 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the total input capacitance**

$$C_{in,total} = C_{\pi} + C_{M,in} = 10 \text{ pF} + 202 \text{ pF} = 212 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the equivalent input resistance**

$$R_{eq} = R_{sig} \mid\mid R_B \mid\mid r_{\pi} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega \mid\mid 10 \text{ k}\Omega \mid\mid 1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{1} = 0.1 + 1 + 1 = 2.1 \text{ mS}$$

$$R_{eq} = \frac{1}{2.1 \text{ mS}} \approx 476.2 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 5: Calculate the upper 3-dB cutoff frequency**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 476.2 \times 212 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1.0096 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{6.343 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.576 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 6: Verify by checking without Miller Effect (sanity check)**

$$f_{H,no \, Miller} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 476.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 33.4 \text{ MHz}$$

The Miller Effect reduced the bandwidth by a factor of $\approx 21$, demonstrating its critical impact.

## 3.5 Key Analytical Insights from the Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight 1:** The Miller capacitance is proportional to $\vert A_v \vert$. Doubling the midband gain (e.g., by increasing $R_C$) will also **double** the Miller capacitance, which **halves** the bandwidth — keeping the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** approximately constant. This is a fundamental trade-off in amplifier design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight 2:** Since the Miller capacitance at the input side is $C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ and at the output side is approximately $C_{\mu}$, the input pole is **dominant** in a CE amplifier. This is why single-pole approximations work well.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight 3:** In a **Common-Base (CB)** or **Common-Collector (CC)** amplifier, the voltage gain magnitude is much smaller (CB has $\vert A_v \vert \approx 1$, CC has $\vert A_v \vert \approx 1$ but no phase inversion), so the Miller Effect is **negligible**. This is why CB and CC stages have excellent high-frequency response.
