# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Miller's Theorem. Derive the expression for Miller capacitance at the input of an inverting amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Miller's Theorem:** If an impedance $Z_F$ is connected between the input node (voltage $V_1$) and the output node (voltage $V_2$) of a linear amplifier with voltage gain $A_v = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$, then $Z_F$ can be replaced by two equivalent grounded impedances:
> $$Z_1 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{(at the input side)} \quad \text{and} \quad Z_2 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - \frac{1}{A_v}} \quad \text{(at the output side)}$$

For a pure feedback capacitor $C_{\mu}$, $Z_F = \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_{\mu}}$. Substituting:

$$C_{M,in} = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v)$$

For an inverting amplifier with $A_v = -\vert A_v \vert$:

$$C_{M,in} = C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

This is the **Miller capacitance**. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2: Application-Based Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A common-emitter amplifier has a feedback capacitance $C_{\mu} = 5$ pF and a midband voltage gain of $\vert A_v \vert = 80$. Calculate the Miller capacitance and comment on its impact on the amplifier's high-frequency response.**

**Model Answer:**

Miller capacitance at the input side:

$$C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 80) = 5 \times 81 = 405 \text{ pF}$$

**Comment:** The effective input capacitance becomes 81 times larger than the physical feedback capacitance. This drastically reduces the upper 3-dB frequency of the CE amplifier, as the dominant pole is determined by $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,total}}$, where $C_{in,total} = C_{\pi} + C_M$. This is the **Miller Effect**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** KTU 2024 Part B questions feature **internal choice** between two questions. Both options below are complete, independent 14-mark questions, each with sub-parts (a) at 7 marks and (b) at 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q(a) State and prove Miller's Theorem. Show that the Miller capacitance at the input of an inverting amplifier with gain $A_v$ and feedback capacitor $C_f$ is $C_{M,in} = C_f(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Statement of Miller's Theorem**

Refer to the formal definition in Section 1.1. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Proof for the Input Side Impedance**

Consider a floating impedance $Z_F$ between input node (voltage $V_1$) and output node (voltage $V_2$).

Current through $Z_F$:

$$I_F = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_F}$$

Since $V_2 = A_v V_1$:

$$I_F = \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z_F} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z_F}$$

For an equivalent input impedance $Z_1$ between the input and ground:

$$\frac{V_1}{Z_1} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z_F} \implies Z_1 = \frac{Z_F}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{...(i)} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Specialization for Capacitor**

For $Z_F = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_f}$:

$$Z_1 = \frac{1}{j\omega C_f (1 - A_v)} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_{M,in}}$$

Therefore:

$$C_{M,in} = C_f(1 - A_v) \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4: For Inverting Amplifier**

Since $A_v < 0$ for an inverting amplifier, let $A_v = -\vert A_v \vert$:

$$C_{M,in} = C_f(1 - (-\vert A_v \vert)) = C_f(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

**Q(b) For a CE amplifier, $g_m = 50$ mA/V, $r_{\pi} = 800$ $\Omega$, $C_{\pi} = 15$ pF, $C_{\mu} = 3$ pF, $R_C = 4$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 4$ k$\Omega$, $R_{sig} = 500$ $\Omega$, and $R_B = 20$ k$\Omega$. Determine the midband gain, total input capacitance using Miller's theorem, and the upper 3-dB frequency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Midband Voltage Gain**

$$A_v = -g_m (R_C \mid\mid R_L) = -50 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{4 \times 4}{4 + 4} \times 10^3$$

$$A_v = -50 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^3 = -100 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2: Miller Capacitance at the Input**

$$C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 3 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 100) = 303 \text{ pF} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Total Input Capacitance**

$$C_{in,total} = C_{\pi} + C_M = 15 \text{ pF} + 303 \text{ pF} = 318 \text{ pF} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4: Equivalent Input Resistance**

$$R_{eq} = R_{sig} \mid\mid R_B \mid\mid r_{\pi} = 500 \mid\mid 20000 \mid\mid 800$$

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{500} + \frac{1}{20000} + \frac{1}{800} = 0.002 + 0.00005 + 0.00125 = 0.0033 \text{ S}$$

$$R_{eq} = \frac{1}{0.0033} \approx 303 \text{ }\Omega \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 5: Upper 3-dB Frequency**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 303 \times 318 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{6.054 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.651 \text{ MHz} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q(a) Explain the Miller Effect with reference to a CE amplifier. Discuss how it affects the high-frequency response of the amplifier. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Concept of Miller Effect**

The Miller Effect refers to the apparent multiplication of the feedback capacitance $C_{\mu}$ (between collector and base) when viewed from the input terminals of an inverting amplifier such as the CE stage. The voltage gain $A_v$ between the two terminals of $C_{\mu}$ causes the current through $C_{\mu}$ to be $(1 - A_v)$ times the current that would flow with the same input voltage and a grounded capacitor. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2: Quantitative Expression**

By Miller's Theorem:

$$C_{M,in} = C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

This effective capacitance adds in parallel with $C_{\pi}$ at the base node, leading to:

$$C_{in,total} = C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Impact on High-Frequency Response**

The dominant pole (upper 3-dB cutoff) is:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,total}}$$

Since $C_{M,in}$ is proportional to $\vert A_v \vert$, a higher midband gain causes a **proportionally lower** $f_H$. This is the **gain-bandwidth trade-off** inherent in CE amplifiers. The bandwidth may reduce by a factor of 50–100 in practical circuits. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Mitigating the Effect**

Techniques to mitigate the Miller Effect include:
- Using a **cascode** configuration (CE + CB cascade), which reduces the voltage gain across $C_{\mu}$ to nearly unity.
- Reducing $C_{\mu}$ at the device level (e.g., using high-frequency transistors with low collector-base junction capacitance).
- Using common-base or common-collector stages, where $\vert A_v \vert \approx 1$. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q(b) A multistage amplifier has a CE input stage with $\vert A_{v1} \vert = 60$, $C_{\pi 1} = 20$ pF, $C_{\mu 1} = 4$ pF. The source resistance $R_{sig} = 2$ k$\Omega$ and the base bias resistance $R_B = 50$ k$\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the Miller capacitance, (ii) total input capacitance, (iii) upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ assuming $r_{\pi} \gg R_{sig} \mid\mid R_B$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Miller Capacitance at the Input**

Given $\vert A_{v1} \vert = 60$ and $C_{\mu 1} = 4$ pF:

$$C_M = C_{\mu 1}(1 + \vert A_{v1} \vert) = 4 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 60) = 4 \times 61 = 244 \text{ pF} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2: Total Input Capacitance**

$$C_{in,total} = C_{\pi 1} + C_M = 20 \text{ pF} + 244 \text{ pF} = 264 \text{ pF} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Equivalent Input Resistance**

Since $r_{\pi} \gg R_{sig} \mid\mid R_B$:

$$R_{eq} \approx R_{sig} \mid\mid R_B = \frac{2 \times 50}{2 + 50} = \frac{100}{52} \approx 1.923 \text{ k}\Omega \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Upper 3-dB Cutoff Frequency**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1.923 \times 10^3 \times 264 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 5.077 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{3.19 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 313.4 \text{ kHz} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Miller Effect Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Miller Capacitance:** Writing $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 - A_v)$ without substituting $A_v < 0$ correctly. Remember: for an inverting CE amplifier, $A_v$ is **negative**, so $(1 - A_v) = (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. **Always state both forms** in the exam to be safe.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $C_{\pi}$ in Parallel:** Students often calculate only $C_M$ and forget that $C_{\pi}$ adds in parallel at the base. The total input capacitance is $C_{in} = C_{\pi} + C_{M,in}$, **not just** $C_{M,in}$.
>
> 3. **Wrong Equivalent Resistance:** The Thévenin resistance at the base is $R_{eq} = R_{sig} \mid\mid R_B \mid\mid r_{\pi}$, not just $R_{sig}$ or just $R_B$. Omitting $r_{\pi}$ or $R_B$ will lead to incorrect $f_H$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Units:** Capacitance in pF, resistance in k$\Omega$ gives $f_H$ in **MHz** when using the formula $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. Always show unit conversion explicitly.
>
> 5. **Output-Side Miller Capacitance Ignored:** Examiners sometimes ask about the output side too. Always derive $C_{M,out} \approx C_{\mu}$ and mention why the input pole dominates.
>
> 6. **No Diagram/Approximation Statement:** KTU expects a **labeled hybrid-$\pi$ circuit** with the Miller equivalent shown. Always draw the diagram in your answer to fetch full marks.
>
> 7. **Mixing up CB and CE behavior:** In CB and CC stages, the Miller Effect is **negligible** because $\vert A_v \vert \approx 1$. Stating this contrast often earns a **bonus mark** from KTU examiners.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Miller Effect (KTU 2024)**
>
> - **Definition:** Miller's Theorem splits a floating impedance $Z_F$ into two grounded impedances $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z_F}{1 - A_v}$ and $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z_F}{1 - 1/A_v}$.
> - **Miller Capacitance Formula:** $C_{M,in} = C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ for inverting amplifiers. This is the **single most important formula** for the exam.
> - **Origin:** The effect arises because the voltage across $C_{\mu}$ is $(V_{in} - V_{out}) = V_{in}(1 - A_v)$, amplifying the current that the source must supply.
> - **Dominant Pole:** $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C_{in,total}}$ where $C_{in,total} = C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$.
> - **Where it matters most:** Common-Emitter (CE) and Common-Source (CS) amplifiers, where $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$.
> - **Where it doesn't matter:** Common-Base (CB), Common-Gate (CG), Common-Collector (CC), Common-Drain (CD) — all have $\vert A_v \vert \approx 1$.
> - **Mitigation Techniques:** Cascode configuration, low-$C_{\mu}$ transistors, and emitter/source degeneration (which reduces $\vert A_v \vert$).
> - **Engineering Use:** Intentionally exploited in op-amp compensation (e.g., $\mu$A741 uses Miller multiplication to create a large on-chip compensation capacitance).
> - **Sign Convention:** Always express $A_v$ with a sign; magnitude alone is insufficient when applying Miller's Theorem in its general form.
> - **Output Side:** $C_{M,out} \approx C_{\mu}$ when $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$, hence the input pole dominates and a single-pole approximation is valid.
> - **Bandwidth Trade-off:** Doubling gain halves bandwidth in a CE stage — the Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) remains approximately constant.
> - **KTU Pattern:** 3-mark questions test the formula directly; 14-mark questions combine Miller's Theorem derivation with numerical $f_H$ computation.
> - **Always draw:** The hybrid-$\pi$ model with $C_{\mu}$ highlighted, and the Miller-equivalent circuit with the multiplied $C_{M,in}$ at the base.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of High Frequency Response of CE Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analysis of High Frequency Response of CE Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**High-Frequency Response Analysis of a Common Emitter (CE) Amplifier** is the systematic study of the amplifier's small-signal gain behaviour in the frequency range where the internal **parasitic (junction) capacitances** of the BJT — namely, the base–emitter diffusion capacitance $C_{\pi}$ and the base–collector depletion (Miller) capacitance $C_{\mu}$ — can no longer be treated as open circuits. The analysis uses the **hybrid-π model** augmented with these capacitances, applies **Miller's Theorem** to fold $C_{\mu}$ into the input node, and yields the upper 3-dB cutoff frequency $f_H$ together with the **transition frequency $f_T$** (the unity-current-gain frequency, a key figure of merit of the transistor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PEICT413, Module 1):**
> Students are expected to (a) draw the high-frequency hybrid-π equivalent, (b) apply Miller's theorem, (c) derive the upper cutoff $f_H$, and (d) derive the expression for the short-circuit current gain $A_{is}$ and the transition frequency $f_T$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the CE amplifier as a **multi-lane highway** for an electrical signal:
* At **low frequencies (LF)** the road is wide open — coupling and bypass capacitors act like overpasses that let the DC bias flow freely, and the signal travels without any "toll booths."
* As the signal frequency **rises**, microscopic **"toll booths"** appear on the highway. These toll booths are the tiny internal capacitances $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$ inside every BJT.
* At very **high frequencies (HF)**, the toll booths close lanes rapidly; the signal cannot get through fast enough, so the **gain starts to roll off** at a rate of **−20 dB/decade** per pole.
* The frequency at which the gain has fallen by **3 dB (≈ 0.707 of the mid-band value)** is called the **upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Key Metric:**
> * **3 dB rule:** Gain rolls off by **3 dB** (to 70.7 %) at $f_H$.
> * **Roll-off slope:** **−20 dB/decade** or **−6 dB/octave** per pole.
> * **Transition frequency $f_T$:** The frequency at which the **common-emitter short-circuit current gain $h_{fe}$ falls to unity (1)**. It is the figure of merit that limits the usable bandwidth of the transistor.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Curriculum

Every modern analog system — RF amplifiers, op-amp input stages, video amplifiers, high-speed data converters — operates in a regime where these tiny internal capacitances dominate. **Designing for high $f_T$** is the reason modern high-frequency BJTs (e.g., RF transistors, microwave devices) are physically smaller. Analysing $f_H$ correctly lets the designer know **up to what frequency the amplifier is useful**, which is central to the KTU 2024 Course Outcome *CO2: "Analyse and design amplifier circuits at low and high frequencies."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The High-Frequency Hybrid-π Model

In the **high-frequency regime**, the coupling/bypass capacitors (external) act as short circuits, while the **internal junction capacitances** $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$ must be retained. The model is shown logically below:

* **rπ** = base–emitter dynamic resistance
* **gm · vπ** = voltage-controlled current source (output side)
* **Cπ** = base–emitter diffusion (junction) capacitance
* **Cμ** = base–collector depletion (Miller) capacitance
* **ro** = output resistance (often neglected at HF for simplicity)

### 2.2 Why Cμ Causes Trouble — Miller's Theorem

$C_{\mu}$ connects the **input node (B)** to the **output node (C)**. Because the output voltage $v_{ce}$ is much larger than the input voltage $v_{\pi}$ (in magnitude, but inverted), a small current through $C_{\mu}$ at the input is multiplied by a large factor when referred back to the input side.

**Miller's Theorem (1940, formal statement):** If an impedance $Z$ is connected between input node and output node of an amplifier with voltage gain $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$, then $Z$ can be replaced by two grounded impedances:

$$Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{(at the input)}$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{Z}{1 - \dfrac{1}{A_v}} \quad \text{(at the output)}$$

For a CE amplifier $A_v \approx -g_m R_C$ (large and negative), so $1 - A_v \approx 1 + g_m R_C$. The **Miller-multiplied capacitance** seen at the input is enormous:

$$C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> $C_M$ is called the **Miller Capacitance**. It is the dominant pole-forming element at the input. This is why CE amplifiers are inherently **bandwidth-limited** by the very capacitance that also provides voltage gain — a fundamental trade-off.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Reasoning Behind the High-Frequency Analysis

1. **Start with the mid-band model** (no internal capacitances) — gain is flat, equal to $-g_m R_C$.
2. **Add $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$** between base-emitter and base-collector nodes.
3. **Apply Miller's Theorem** to replace $C_{\mu}$ by $C_M$ at the input and $C_{\mu}$ at the output.
4. **The output pole** (at the collector) is typically at a much higher frequency than the input pole because $R_C \ll g_m R_C^2$ — it is **non-dominant** and may be neglected.
5. **The dominant input pole** determines $f_H$.
6. **Short-circuit the output** ($R_C = 0$) and analyse current gain $A_{is} = I_o/I_i$ to find the **transition frequency $f_T$** — a property of the transistor alone (independent of external resistors).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Hybrid-π input resistance | $r_{\pi} = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m}$ | $\Omega$ | From $g_m v_{\pi} = \beta i_b$ |
| 2 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | A/V | $V_T \approx 26 \,\text{mV}$ at 300 K |
| 3 | Miller Capacitance | $C_M = C_{\mu}\,(1 + g_m R_C)$ | F | $R_C$ is the **total** collector load |
| 4 | Equivalent input capacitance | $C_{in} = C_{\pi} + C_M$ | F | After applying Miller's theorem |
| 5 | Thévenin resistance at base | $R_{th} = R_B \parallel r_{\pi}$ | $\Omega$ | $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2$ |
| 6 | Upper 3-dB frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\, R_{th}\, C_{in}}$ | Hz | **Dominant-pole approximation** |
| 7 | Short-circuit CE current gain (exact) | $A_{is}(s) = \dfrac{\beta_0}{1 + s\,\dfrac{C_{\pi}+C_{\mu}}{g_m}}$ | – | $\beta_0$ = low-frequency $\beta$ |
| 8 | Transition frequency | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$ | Hz | Unity-gain frequency of $A_{is}$ |
| 9 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = \beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta} = f_T$ | Hz | $f_{\beta}$ = $\beta$-cutoff frequency |
| 10 | Approximate $C_{\pi}$ | $C_{\pi} \approx \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi f_T}$ | F | Used when $f_T$ is given |
| 11 | CE voltage gain (mid-band) | $A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_C$ | – | Sign indicates phase inversion |
| 12 | Miller input factor | $k = 1 + g_m R_C$ | – | Dimensionless gain magnitude factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> The relation $f_T = g_m/[2\pi(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})]$ is the **master equation** for transistor high-frequency design. By minimising $(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})$ and maximising $g_m$, RF designers push modern SiGe, GaAs, GaN HBTs into the **hundreds of GHz** range. The relation $A_{v,mid} \cdot f_H \approx f_T / (2\pi R_C C_{\mu})$ shows the **gain-bandwidth trade-off**: increasing $R_C$ boosts gain but lowers $f_H$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Miller's Theorem

Consider an amplifier with input node 1 and output node 2, and an impedance $Z$ connected between them. Let the voltage gain be $A_v = v_2 / v_1$.

* Current through $Z$ from node 1 to node 2:

$$I_Z = \frac{v_1 - v_2}{Z} = \frac{v_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z}$$

* The same current must be drawn from node 1 by an equivalent shunt impedance $Z_1$ across $v_1$:

$$I_Z = \frac{v_1}{Z_1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}$$

* Similarly, the current injected into node 2 is the same, so the equivalent shunt at node 2 is $Z_2 = Z/(1 - 1/A_v)$. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> For a **CE amplifier** $A_v$ is negative and large in magnitude, hence $1 - A_v \approx 1 + \vert A_v\vert$, giving a large multiplying factor for the input Miller impedance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Upper 3-dB Frequency $f_H$

**Step 1** — Replace $C_{\mu}$ by its Miller counterpart at the input. Let $R_C$ be the total collector resistance.

$$C_{M} = C_{\mu}\,(1 + g_m R_C) = C_{\mu}\,k, \quad \text{where } k = 1 + g_m R_C$$

**Step 2** — The total capacitance shunting the input node is:

$$C_{in} = C_{\pi} + C_{M} = C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C)$$

**Step 3** — Compute the Thévenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$. The signal source has source resistance $R_S$ and is connected through bias network $R_1 \parallel R_2 = R_B$:

$$R_{th} = R_S \,\big\Vert\, R_B \,\big\Vert\, r_{\pi}$$

**Step 4** — The dominant input pole is a simple RC low-pass, so the upper 3-dB cutoff is:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi\, R_{th}\, C_{in}}$$

$$\boxed{\,f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi\, R_{th}\bigl[\,C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C)\,\bigr]}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Short-Circuit Current Gain $A_{is}(s)$

To find $f_T$, we **short the output** ($R_C = 0$) so that the Miller multiplier becomes unity and $C_{\mu}$ no longer "sees" the gain.

**Step 1** — Apply a test current $I_i$ into the base node. With output shorted, $C_{\mu}$ appears in **parallel** with $C_{\pi}$ because both ends of $C_{\mu}$ are now at $v_{\pi}$.

**Step 2** — The total capacitance seen at the input is $C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}$. The voltage $v_{\pi}$ across this capacitance is:

$$v_{\pi} = \frac{I_i}{\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 3** — The short-circuit output current is delivered by the controlled source $g_m v_{\pi}$:

$$I_o = g_m\, v_{\pi} = \frac{g_m\, I_i}{\dfrac{1}{r_{\pi}} + s(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 4** — Divide by $I_i$ and multiply numerator and denominator by $r_{\pi}$. Note that $g_m r_{\pi} = \beta_0$:

$$A_{is}(s) = \frac{I_o}{I_i} = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s\,r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$

**Step 5** — Since $g_m = \beta_0 / r_{\pi}$, we can rewrite the time constant:

$$\tau = r_{\pi}(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}) = \frac{\beta_0(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}{g_m}$$

$$A_{is}(s) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s\,\dfrac{\beta_0(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}{g_m}}$$

**Step 6** — Magnitude falls to unity when $\vert 1 + s\tau \vert \approx \omega \tau$ (high-frequency limit) and $g_m / [\omega (C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})] = 1$:

$$\boxed{\,f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Relation Between Voltage Gain and $f_H$ — Gain–Bandwidth Product

From Sections 3.2 and 3.3, eliminating $g_m$ yields the **Gain–Bandwidth Product** of a single-stage CE amplifier:

$$\vert A_{v,mid} \vert \cdot f_H = \frac{g_m R_C}{2\pi R_{th} [C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C)]}$$

If $R_{th} \approx r_{\pi}$ and $C_M \gg C_{\pi}$ (typical at high $R_C$):

$$\vert A_{v,mid}\vert \cdot f_H \;\approx\; \frac{g_m}{2\pi\, C_{\mu}} \;<\; f_T$$

This is the **fundamental trade-off**: increasing $|A_v|$ by raising $R_C$ *reduces* $f_H$, so their product is approximately constant.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A CE amplifier uses a BJT with $g_m = 50\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_{\pi} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{\pi} = 10\,\text{pF}$, $C_{\mu} = 2\,\text{pF}$, collector load $R_C = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, $R_B \gg R_S$. Find $f_H$ and $f_T$.

**Step 1** — Miller capacitance:

$$C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C) = 2\,\text{pF} \times (1 + 0.05 \times 2000) = 2 \times 101 = 202\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 2** — Total input capacitance:

$$C_{in} = 10 + 202 = 212\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 3** — Thévenin resistance: $R_{th} = R_S \parallel r_{\pi} = 600 \parallel 1000 = 375\,\Omega$.

**Step 4** — Upper cutoff:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 375 \cdot 212 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.00\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 5** — Transition frequency:

$$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \frac{0.05}{2\pi \times 12 \times 10^{-12}} = 663\,\text{MHz}$$

**Step 6** — Verification: $\beta_0 = g_m r_{\pi} = 50$, so $\beta$-cutoff $f_{\beta} = f_T/\beta_0 = 663/50 = 13.3\,\text{MHz}$, which is much higher than $f_H = 2\,\text{MHz}$. Confirms the dominant pole is at the input due to the Miller effect. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Frequency Hybrid-π Equivalent (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcNode["Vsignal with Rs"] --> b1["B base node"]
    b1 --> rpiNode["r_pi to E"]
    b1 --> cpiNode["C_pi to E"]
    b1 --> cmicro["C_mu to C collector node"]
    cmicro --> cNode["C collector node"]
    cNode --> rcNode["R_C to VCC"]
    cNode --> gmc["g_m v_pi current source to E"]
    gmc --> eNode["E emitter node grounded"]
    rpiNode --> eNode
    cpiNode --> eNode
```

### 4.2 Miller Equivalent (After Applying Miller's Theorem)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inNode["B base input node"] --> rth["R_th to source"]
    inNode --> cin["C_in = C_pi + C_mu(1 + g_m R_C)"]
    cin --> gndA["GND"]
    inNode --> gout["controlled source g_m v_pi"]
    gout --> cNode2["C output node"]
    cNode2 --> rc2["R_C to VCC"]
    cNode2 --> cmu2["C_mu (residual, to GND)"]
    cmu2 --> gndB["GND"]
```

### 4.3 Frequency Response Block (Bode-Magnitude Sketch Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DC and low frequencies flat at A_mid dB"] --> B["3 dB drop at f = f_H"]
    B --> C["Slope minus 20 dB per decade after f_H"]
    C --> D["Continue to f_T where current gain = 1"]
    D --> E["Then slope minus 40 dB per decade if second pole"]
```

### 4.4 Dominant-Pole Approximation Flowchart (How the KTU Solution is Built)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Start HF hybrid-pi model"] --> s2["Apply Miller to C_mu across C_pi"]
    s2 --> s3["Compute C_in at base"]
    s2 --> s4["Compute C_out at collector"]
    s3 --> s5["Compute R_th at base"]
    s4 --> s6["Compute R_out at collector"]
    s5 --> s7["Input pole: f_H1 = 1 over 2 pi R_th C_in"]
    s6 --> s8["Output pole: f_H2 = 1 over 2 pi R_out C_out"]
    s7 --> s9{"Is f_H1 less than f_H2?"}
    s9 -- "Yes dominant" --> s10["f_H approx f_H1"]
    s9 -- "No" --> s11["Recompute, swap dominant"]
    s8 --> s9
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** State and prove **Miller's Theorem**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Miller's Theorem states that an impedance $Z$ connected between input node 1 and output node 2 of an amplifier of voltage gain $A_v$ can be replaced by two impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ connected from the respective nodes to ground, where:
$$Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}, \qquad Z_2 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - 1/A_v}$$
**Proof:** The current through $Z$ is $(v_1 - v_2)/Z = v_1(1 - A_v)/Z$. This must equal the current $v_1/Z_1$ drawn from node 1 by $Z_1$ to ground, giving $Z_1 = Z/(1 - A_v)$. By symmetry at node 2: $Z_2 = Z/(1 - 1/A_v)$. (1 mark for statement, 1 mark for figure, 1 mark for proof.) ∎

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Define **transition frequency $f_T$** of a BJT. Why is it called the **gain-bandwidth product**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The transition frequency $f_T$ of a BJT is the frequency at which the **common-emitter short-circuit current gain** $h_{fe}(\omega)$ falls to **unity (1, or 0 dB)**. It is a property of the transistor alone and is given by:
$$f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})}$$
It is called the gain–bandwidth product because for the CE short-circuit current gain the magnitude is:
$$\vert A_{is}\vert = \dfrac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{\beta})^2}}$$
At any frequency $f \gg f_{\beta}$, $\vert A_{is}\vert \cdot f \approx \beta_0 f_{\beta} = f_T =$ constant. Hence $f_T$ equals the product of low-frequency current gain and the $\beta$-cutoff frequency — i.e., the **gain–bandwidth product**. ∎

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
> **QUESTION A (14 marks)**
> **(a)** Draw the **high-frequency hybrid-π equivalent circuit** of a CE amplifier and derive the expression for the **upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$** using **Miller's Theorem**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** For a BJT with $g_m = 40\,\text{mA/V}$, $C_{\pi} = 8\,\text{pF}$, $C_{\mu} = 1.5\,\text{pF}$, $R_C = 3\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $r_{\pi} = 1.25\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_B = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, compute $f_H$, $\beta_0$ and $f_T$. Comment on the **dominant-pole** nature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. Draw the hybrid-π model with $r_{\pi}$ between B and E, $C_{\pi}$ between B and E, $C_{\mu}$ between B and C, dependent source $g_m v_{\pi}$ between C and E, and $R_C$ at the collector. *(2 marks)*
2. Apply Miller's Theorem to $C_{\mu}$: $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C)$. The total input capacitance is $C_{in} = C_{\pi} + C_M$. *(2 marks)*
3. Thévenin resistance at base: $R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{\pi}$. *(1 mark)*
4. Dominant-pole approximation gives $f_H = 1/[2\pi R_{th} C_{in}]$. *(2 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Miller multiplier: $k = 1 + g_m R_C = 1 + 0.04 \times 3000 = 121$.

**Step 2:** $C_M = 1.5 \times 121 = 181.5\,\text{pF}$.

**Step 3:** $C_{in} = 8 + 181.5 = 189.5\,\text{pF}$.

**Step 4:** $R_{th} = 1000 \parallel 10000 \parallel 1250 = (1000 \parallel 10000) \parallel 1250$.
$1000 \parallel 10000 = \dfrac{1000 \times 10000}{11000} = 909.09\,\Omega$.
$R_{th} = 909.09 \parallel 1250 = 525.4\,\Omega$.

**Step 5:** $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 525.4 \times 189.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.598\,\text{MHz}$.

**Step 6:** $\beta_0 = g_m r_{\pi} = 0.04 \times 1250 = 50$.

**Step 7:** $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})} = \dfrac{0.04}{2\pi \times 9.5 \times 10^{-12}} = 670\,\text{MHz}$.

**Step 8:** $f_{\beta} = f_T / \beta_0 = 670/50 = 13.4\,\text{MHz}$. Since $f_H = 1.6\,\text{MHz} \ll f_{\beta} = 13.4\,\text{MHz}$, the input RC pole is **dominant** and our single-pole approximation is valid. Also $f_H \ll f_T$ as expected, confirming $C_M \gg C_{\pi}$ dominates the bandwidth limitation. *(1 mark for comment)*

---

> **QUESTION B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **short-circuit current gain** $A_{is}(s)$ of a CE transistor and obtain the **transition frequency $f_T$**. Show that $f_T = g_m/[2\pi(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})]$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A transistor has $f_T = 500\,\text{MHz}$ and $\beta_0 = 100$. If the base is driven by a source with $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $r_{\pi} = 2.5\,\text{k}\Omega$, find (i) the $\beta$-cutoff frequency $f_{\beta}$, (ii) the approximate value of $C_{\pi} + C_{\mu}$ assuming $g_m = 0.04\,\text{A/V}$, and (iii) the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$ of a CE stage with $R_C = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_{\mu} = 2\,\text{pF}$ (use dominant-pole approximation). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

Refer to derivation in **Section 3.3** above. Award 2 marks for setting up the short-circuit condition, 2 marks for KCL at the base node, 2 marks for the final expression, and 1 mark for the $f_T$ identification. Key final result:
$$A_{is}(s) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s\,\dfrac{\beta_0(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})}{g_m}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**(i)** $f_{\beta} = f_T / \beta_0 = 500/100 = 5\,\text{MHz}$. *(2 marks)*

**(ii)** $C_{\pi} + C_{\mu} = g_m / (2\pi f_T) = 0.04 / (2\pi \times 500 \times 10^{6}) = 12.73\,\text{pF}$. *(2 marks)*

**(iii)** Miller multiplier: $k = 1 + g_m R_C = 1 + 0.04 \times 1000 = 41$.
$C_M = 2\,\text{pF} \times 41 = 82\,\text{pF}$.
$C_{in} = 12.73 + 82 - C_{\mu} = C_{\pi} + C_M = 10.73 + 82 = 92.73\,\text{pF}$ (using $C_{\pi} = 10.73\,\text{pF}$).
$R_{th} = R_S \parallel r_{\pi} = 1 \parallel 2.5 = 0.714\,\text{k}\Omega$.
$f_H = 1 / (2\pi \times 714 \times 92.73 \times 10^{-12}) = 2.40\,\text{MHz}$. *(3 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deducting Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign in Miller's Theorem:** Always use $1 - A_v$, NOT $1 + A_v$ directly, although for an inverting CE stage this works out the same numerically. State the theorem symbolically.
> 2. **Mixing up $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$:** $C_{\pi}$ is the *diffusion* (base–emitter) capacitance; $C_{\mu}$ is the *depletion* (base–collector) capacitance. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Including the output pole without checking:** The output pole $f_{H2} = 1/(2\pi R_C C_{\mu})$ is non-dominant only if $R_C \ll g_m R_C^2$ — usually true. If $R_C$ is very small, check both poles.
> 4. **Wrong units in $f_T$ formula:** $g_m$ in **A/V**, $C$ in **F**, the result is in **Hz**. Converting pF to F requires $\times 10^{-12}$.
> 5. **Conflating $f_{\beta}$ with $f_H$:** $f_{\beta}$ is a property of the **transistor** alone (current gain cutoff); $f_H$ is a property of the **complete amplifier** (with source resistance and load).
> 6. **Skipping the comment on dominant-pole assumption:** KTU examiners award a bonus 1 mark for explicitly stating why the dominant-pole approximation is valid (e.g., $f_H \ll f_{H2}$ and $f_H \ll f_T$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **High-frequency model** keeps $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$; couples/ bypass capacitors are short.
- **Miller's Theorem:** $Z_1 = Z/(1 - A_v)$ and $Z_2 = Z/(1 - 1/A_v)$.
- **Miller Capacitance** $C_M = C_{\mu}(1 + g_m R_C)$ is the **dominant** pole-forming element.
- **Input pole:** $f_H = 1/[2\pi R_{th}(C_{\pi} + C_M)]$.
- **$R_{th}$** = $R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{\pi}$ (source plus bias network plus $r_{\pi}$).
- **Short-circuit current gain** $A_{is}(s) = \beta_0/[1 + s\beta_0(C_{\pi}+C_{\mu})/g_m]$.
- **Transition frequency** $f_T = g_m/[2\pi(C_{\pi} + C_{\mu})]$ — the **figure of merit** of the BJT.
- **Gain–Bandwidth Product:** $\beta_0 \cdot f_{\beta} = f_T$.
- **Trade-off:** $\vert A_{v,mid} \vert \cdot f_H \approx$ constant (≈ $f_T / [2\pi R_C C_{\mu}]$ for $C_M \gg C_{\pi}$).
- **Roll-off slope** beyond $f_H$ is **−20 dB/decade** per pole; $f_T$ is on this roll-off curve where $A_{is} = 1$ (0 dB).
- **Design levers:** Reduce $R_C$ to raise $f_H$ at the cost of gain; use a transistor with higher $f_T$ to push $f_H$ upward; use **cascode** to mitigate Miller effect (a key KTU 2024 follow-up topic).
- **Mid-band voltage gain:** $A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_C$ (negative sign — phase inversion).
- **Approximation** $C_{\pi} \approx g_m/(2\pi f_T)$ when $C_{\mu} \ll C_{\pi}$ — useful in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clippers and Clampers, Multivibrators: Types of Multivibrators - Monostable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clippers and Clampers, Multivibrators: Types of Multivibrators - Monostable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wave Shaping Circuits — The Foundation

In analog electronics, **wave-shaping circuits** are linear/non-linear networks used to alter the shape of a periodic waveform (typically sinusoidal or rectangular) without changing its fundamental frequency. The two principal families of wave-shaping circuits are **Clippers** and **Clampers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEICT413 – Module 2):**
> *Clippers and Clampers, Multivibrators: Types of Multivibrators – Monostable, Astable, Bistable.* This module belongs to CO2 of the course and is heavily tested in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Clippers — The "Electronic Lawn Mower"

### Formal Definition
A **clipper** is a wave-shaping circuit that **limits the amplitude of an input signal to a predefined level** by removing (clipping) the portion of the waveform that exceeds a reference voltage. Clippers use non-linear elements (diodes) in conjunction with linear elements (resistors) and DC reference voltages.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Lawn Mower:** Imagine a freshly grown lawn (your input signal) that has grass blades of varying heights. A lawn mower is set to a fixed height — everything above this cut-off level is shaved off. The result is a clean, level surface (the clipped output). The mower's height setting is analogous to the **reference (clipping) voltage** of the circuit, and the grass that gets cut is analogous to the **excess signal amplitude** that the diode shunts to ground.

### Classification of Clippers

| Category | Sub-Type | Behaviour |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Based on Configuration** | Series Clipper | Diode in series with the load |
| | Shunt (Parallel) Clipper | Diode in parallel with the load |
| **Based on Polarity Clipped** | Positive Clipper | Removes positive half-cycle |
| | Negative Clipper | Removes negative half-cycle |
| **Based on Reference** | Unbiased Clipper | Reference is 0 V (ground) |
| | Biased Clipper | Reference is ±V_B (DC source) |

---

## 1.3 Clampers — The "DC Restorer"

### Formal Definition
A **clamper** is a wave-shaping circuit that **shifts an entire waveform either above or below a reference DC level** without changing its peak-to-peak amplitude or shape. It is essentially a **DC level shifter** that adds or subtracts a DC offset to the input signal.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Floating Boat:** Consider a boat (the AC waveform) floating on a tide that ebbs and flows. If we add buoys underneath the boat, the entire boat floats higher (positive clamping) without changing its size. If we fill the boat with sandbags, it sits lower in the water (negative clamping). The water level (the average DC value) shifts, but the boat's vertical oscillation amplitude remains unchanged. In electronics, the capacitor in a clamper acts like the buoys/sandbags — it stores charge and holds a steady DC offset.

### The Clamper Equation
A clamper must satisfy two conditions:
1. The diode-capacitor time constant during the charging half-cycle must be **very small** (≈ 5τ ≈ negligible) compared to the signal period.
2. The discharge time constant during the non-conducting half-cycle must be **very large** (ideally infinite) so the capacitor holds its charge.

---

## 1.4 Multivibrators — The "Heart of Digital Timing"

### Formal Definition
A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative amplifier circuit (using two transistors with positive feedback via cross-coupled RC networks) that generates non-sinusoidal waveforms such as square waves, rectangular pulses, and trigger pulses. It is the fundamental building block of timing, sequencing, and pulse-generation circuits.

### The Three Tribes of Multivibrators

| Type | Stable States | Output Waveform | Application |
|------|---------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Astable** (Free-running) | **0** stable states | Continuous square wave | Clock generators, oscillators |
| **Monostable** (One-shot) | **1** stable state | Single pulse on trigger | Pulse stretching, delay circuits |
| **Bistable** (Flip-flop) | **2** stable states | Toggle between two levels | Memory, latches, counters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the monostable called a "one-shot"?**
> Because it produces exactly **one** output pulse for every **one** external trigger pulse applied, regardless of the trigger's duration. The "mono" prefix indicates its single stable state.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Automatic Door
> [!NOTE]
> **The Automatic Sliding Door Analogy:** Imagine a door that stays open all the time (this is its stable state). When a person walks in front of the sensor (the trigger pulse), the door closes for a fixed duration T (the quasi-stable state), and then springs back open automatically. The door *cannot* be re-triggered while it is in the process of closing (this is the **recovery time**). A monostable multivibrator behaves identically — it has one resting (stable) state, briefly switches to an alternate state for a precise time T upon being triggered, then automatically returns.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following values are standard references used throughout this module:

- **Thermal voltage V_T = kT/q ≈ 26 mV** (at room temperature 300 K)
- **Intrinsic Si diode forward drop V_D ≈ 0.7 V**
- **Logic HIGH level in TTL/CMOS ≈ 5 V** (or 3.3 V)
- **Standard clock frequencies in KTU lab: 1 kHz, 10 kHz, 100 kHz**
- **Standard supply voltages: ±5 V, ±12 V, ±15 V**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Clippers — Operation & Variants

### 2.1.1 Positive Series Clipper (Unbiased)

**Circuit Topology:** A diode D in series with the load R_L, oriented such that its cathode faces the source (anode at input).

**Operating Principle:**
- When V_in > 0 (positive half-cycle): Diode D is **forward biased (ON)**. Output follows input minus diode drop → V_out = V_in − 0.7 V.
- When V_in < 0 (negative half-cycle): Diode D is **reverse biased (OFF)**. No current flows → V_out = 0 V (the negative portion is "clipped off").

**Output Waveform:** Only the positive half-cycles appear across the load; the negative half-cycles are flattened to zero.

### 2.1.2 Negative Series Clipper (Unbiased)

This is the mirror image of the positive clipper — the diode is reversed. Only negative half-cycles appear at the output.

### 2.1.3 Biased Positive Clipper

A DC bias voltage V_B is introduced in series with the diode. The clipping level is now V_B + V_D (for silicon).

- V_in > V_B + 0.7: D is ON, V_out = V_in − (V_B + 0.7)
- V_in < V_B + 0.7: D is OFF, V_out = V_B (the bias level)

### 2.1.4 Shunt Clipper

The diode is placed **in parallel (shunt)** with the load.

- When the diode conducts, it shunts the load → V_out ≈ 0.7 V (or V_B + 0.7 V for biased)
- When the diode is OFF, the load sees the full V_in

---

## 2.2 Clampers — Operation & Analysis

### 2.2.1 Negative Clamper

**Goal:** Shift the entire waveform *downward* so that the **positive peak sits at 0 V** (clamped to ground from above).

**Circuit:** A capacitor C in series with the input, followed by a diode D to ground (cathode to ground, anode at the C-D junction), and the load in parallel with the diode.

**Operation:**
- **During first positive half-cycle:** Diode conducts heavily (charging time τ = R_on × C is very small). Capacitor charges to V_m (peak input) with polarity: + on the diode-side plate, − on the source-side plate.
- **During negative half-cycle:** Diode is OFF. Capacitor cannot discharge (load R_L is very large). The output equals V_in + V_C = V_in + V_m. Since V_in swings between −V_m and +V_m, V_out swings between 0 and +2V_m.

### 2.2.2 Positive Clamper

The diode is reversed. The negative peak is clamped to 0 V, and the waveform shifts *upward*. Output swings between 0 and +2V_m (still 2V_m peak-to-peak, just shifted).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Limiting Condition | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Diode Thermal Voltage** | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | T = 300 K, V_T ≈ 26 mV | V |
| 2 | **Ideal Diode Forward Drop** | $V_D \approx 0$ | For piecewise analysis | V |
| 3 | **Practical Si Diode Drop** | $V_D \approx 0.7$ | At I_F ≈ 10 mA | V |
| 4 | **Clamper Peak-to-Peak** | $V_{out(p-p)} = V_{in(p-p)}$ | Capacitor does not discharge significantly | V |
| 5 | **Clamper DC Shift** | $V_{shift} = V_m$ | m is peak of input | V |
| 6 | **Mono MV Pulse Width** | $T = 0.693 \, R C$ | For collector-to-base coupled mono MV | s |
| 7 | **Mono MV Recovery Time** | $T_r \approx (3 \text{ to } 5) R_C C$ | Capacitor must recharge to V_CC | s |
| 8 | **Mono MV Minimum Trigger Period** | $T_{min} = T + T_r$ | To avoid re-trigger during recovery | s |
| 9 | **Astable Frequency (Collector-coupled)** | $f = \dfrac{1}{0.693 \, (R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)}$ | For symmetric: f = 1.44/(R C) | Hz |
| 10 | **Bistable Trigger Pulse Width** | $t_p \ll RC$ | Trigger must be narrow | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The constant **0.693** is **ln(2)** — it appears in *every* multivibrator timing equation because the capacitor charges/discharges exponentially from one threshold to another threshold that is 50% of the supply. This is a **favourite KTU question**: "Derive the 0.693 factor in monostable pulse width."

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clippers** are used in **TV receivers** for synchronizing pulses, in **AM/FM demodulators** for noise clipping, in **overvoltage protection** circuits, and in **test equipment** (function generators).
- **Clampers** are essential in **TV receivers** for DC restoration of the composite video signal, in **radar systems** for baseline stabilization, and in **AC voltmeters** to ensure unidirectional current through the meter movement.
- **Monostable Multivibrators** are used in **digital logic** (e.g., 74121, 74123 ICs), **missing pulse detectors**, **debouncing circuits**, **time-delay relays**, and **frequency-to-voltage converters**.

---

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a capacitor in a clamper?** Because a capacitor stores charge and **resists sudden changes in voltage** — this is exactly the property needed to add/subtract a fixed DC offset.
- **Why cross-coupling in multivibrators?** Because positive feedback (regeneration) is the only way to make the circuit **switch abruptly** between saturation and cut-off, producing sharp square edges.
- **Why RC time constant in monostable?** Because the pulse width is determined by **how long it takes the capacitor to charge to the threshold voltage** of the ON transistor — an exponential process governed by RC.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the 0.693 Factor in Monostable Pulse Width

Consider a **collector-coupled monostable multivibrator** using two NPN transistors Q1 and Q2 with:
- V_CC = supply voltage
- R_C1, R_C2 = collector resistors
- R = timing resistor (base of Q1)
- C = timing capacitor (collector of Q2 to base of Q1)
- R1, R2 = base bias resistors of Q2

**Initial Stable State:**
Q1 is **OFF** (no base drive — base resistor R connects to −V_BB or simply to ground through a large R). Q2 is **ON** (saturated, base driven through R2 from V_CC).

**Trigger Arrival:**
A negative trigger pulse at the base of Q1 turns Q1 ON. The collector of Q1 falls rapidly from V_CC toward 0 V. This negative-going step is coupled through C to the base of Q2, driving Q2 into **cut-off**. The collector of Q2 rises toward V_CC.

**Quasi-Stable State Begins:**
With Q2 OFF, the capacitor C (which was previously charged to nearly V_CC with the + plate at Q2's collector) now begins to **discharge through R** toward −V_BB (or to 0 V through the emitter follower path).

Wait — let us be more careful. The standard analysis uses a **PNP-equivalent positive clamp** and assumes R connects to V_CC (positive supply). The capacitor C has its + plate at the collector of Q2 (which is at V_CC) and its − plate at the base of Q1 (which is now at ~0 V because Q1 is ON).

**Voltage Across Capacitor Just After Triggering:**
The voltage across C cannot change instantaneously.

$$V_C(0^+) = V_{C2}(0^+) - V_{B1}(0^+) = V_{CC} - 0.7 \approx V_{CC} - 0$$

So $V_C \approx V_{CC}$ with the polarity: + plate on the Q2-collector side, − plate on the Q1-base side.

**Capacitor Discharge Equation:**
After triggering, Q1 is ON (base held at 0.7 V), Q2 is OFF. The capacitor C begins to **discharge through R** because the + plate (Q2 collector, now isolated since Q2 is OFF) is being pulled toward... actually, R connects the base of Q1 to V_CC, so current flows from V_CC through R into the base of Q1.

Let us write the **KVL loop equation** for the capacitor voltage $V_C(t)$ measured with + reference on the Q2-collector plate:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/RC} - V_{BB} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/RC})$$

For a symmetric supply (V_BB = V_CC):

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/RC} - V_{CC} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/RC}) = V_{CC} \cdot (2e^{-t/RC} - 1)$$

**Switching Condition:**
The quasi-stable state ends when the base of Q2 rises to V_BE(on) = 0.7 V. At this instant, the voltage at the Q2-base side of the capacitor equals 0.7 V, and the voltage at the Q2-collector side equals V_CC. Therefore:

$$V_C(T) = V_{CC} - 0.7 \approx V_{CC}$$

**Solving for T:**

Set $V_C(T) = V_{CC}$:

$$V_{CC} = V_{CC} \cdot (2e^{-T/RC} - 1)$$

Dividing both sides by $V_{CC}$:

$$1 = 2e^{-T/RC} - 1$$

$$2 = 2e^{-T/RC}$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = 1 \Rightarrow T/RC = 0 \text{ — this is incorrect!}$$

Let us redo this with the correct sign convention.

**Corrected Derivation (Standard KTU Approach):**

Define $V_B(t)$ = voltage at the base of Q2 (relative to ground) during the quasi-stable state.

At $t = 0^+$ (just after triggering): $V_B(0^+) = -V_{CC}$ (because the capacitor was charged to V_CC with the polarity that drives the Q2-base side strongly negative).

**Wait** — the polarity depends on whether Q1 was previously OFF. Let us restart with a clear initial state.

### Clean Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1: Identify the initial steady state.**
- Q1 is OFF. Its collector is at $V_{C1} = V_{CC}$.
- Q2 is ON (saturated). Its base is at $V_{B2} = 0.7$ V. Its collector is at $V_{C2} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V.
- The capacitor C connects $V_{C1}$ to $V_{B2}$. The voltage across C is:
$$V_C = V_{C1} - V_{B2} = V_{CC} - 0.7 \approx V_{CC}$$
- The **+ plate** is on the Q1-collector side (left plate), the **− plate** is on the Q2-base side (right plate).

**Step 2: Apply the trigger.**
A negative pulse at the base of Q1 turns Q1 ON. $V_{C1}$ falls from $V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V. Since the voltage across C cannot change instantaneously:
$$V_{B2}(0^+) = V_{C1}(0^+) - V_C = 0.2 - V_{CC} = -V_{CC} + 0.2 \approx -V_{CC}$$

Q2 is therefore driven deep into **cut-off**. $V_{C2}$ rises toward $V_{CC}$.

**Step 3: Quasi-stable state — capacitor charging.**
With Q2 OFF, no current is drawn from the base of Q2 by the transistor. The capacitor C now **charges** through the resistor R (which connects $V_{B2}$ to $V_{CC}$) and through $R_{C1}$ (from Q1's collector side).

Thevenin equivalent seen by the capacitor:
- $V_{Th} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R}{R + R_{C1}}$ (assuming R >> R_C1, this ≈ V_CC)
- $R_{Th} = R \parallel R_{C1} \approx R_{C1}$ (if R >> R_{C1})

**The voltage at the base of Q2 as a function of time:**

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{Th} - (V_{Th} - V_{B2}(0^+)) \cdot e^{-t/R_{Th} C}$$

Substituting $V_{B2}(0^+) \approx -V_{CC}$ and $V_{Th} \approx V_{CC}$ (since R >> R_{C1}):

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - (-V_{CC})) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} \cdot (1 - 2e^{-t/RC})$$

**Step 4: Switching condition.**
Q2 turns ON when $V_{B2}(T) = 0.7$ V $\approx 0$. Setting:

$$0 = V_{CC} \cdot (1 - 2e^{-T/RC})$$

$$2e^{-T/RC} = 1$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-\dfrac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2) = -0.693$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{T = 0.693 \, R C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the famous "0.693 RC" formula** for the pulse width of a collector-coupled monostable multivibrator. The factor 0.693 = ln(2) emerges because the base voltage must recover from $-V_{CC}$ to 0 V — i.e., it must rise by an amount equal to its starting offset (a factor of 2 in voltage).

**Step 5: Recovery Time.**
After Q2 turns ON, $V_{C2}$ falls from $V_{CC}$ to 0.2 V. The capacitor C must recharge to its original voltage $V_C \approx V_{CC}$ through $R_{C1}$. The recovery time constant is:

$$\tau_r = R_{C1} \cdot C$$

The capacitor is considered fully recharged after $3\tau_r$ to $5\tau_r$. KTU standard: $T_r \approx 5 R_{C1} C$.

**Step 6: Maximum Triggering Frequency.**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T + T_r} = \dfrac{1}{0.693 RC + 5 R_{C1} C}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A monostable multivibrator uses R = 10 kΩ, C = 0.1 μF, and R_C = 1 kΩ with V_CC = 12 V. Calculate: (a) the pulse width T, (b) the recovery time T_r, (c) the maximum triggering frequency f_max, and (d) the duty cycle if the circuit is triggered periodically at f_max.

**Solution:**

**(a) Pulse Width:**
$$T = 0.693 \, R C = 0.693 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$T = 0.693 \times 10^{-3} = 0.693 \text{ ms}$$

**(b) Recovery Time:**
$$T_r = 5 R_{C} C = 5 \times 1 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$T_r = 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

**(c) Maximum Triggering Frequency:**
$$T_{total} = T + T_r = 0.693 + 0.5 = 1.193 \text{ ms}$$
$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{1.193 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 838 \text{ Hz}$$

**(d) Duty Cycle at f_max:**
$$\text{Duty Cycle} = \dfrac{T}{T + T_r} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.693}{1.193} \times 100\% \approx 58.1\%$$

---

## 3.3 Clipper Transfer Characteristic — Analytical Matrix

For a **biased positive shunt clipper** with bias V_B:

| Input Range | Diode State | Output V_out |
|-------------|-------------|--------------|
| $V_{in} < V_B + 0.7$ | OFF | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ |
| $V_{in} \geq V_B + 0.7$ | ON | $V_{out} = V_B + 0.7$ |

For a **negative clamper** with input peak $V_m$:

| Time | Capacitor State | Output Peak |
|------|-----------------|-------------|
| First positive peak | Charging (≈instantaneous) | $V_{out} = 0.7$ V |
| Steady state negative peak | Holding charge | $V_{out} = -2V_m + 0.7$ |
| Steady state positive peak | Holding charge | $V_{out} = 0.7$ V |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Monostable Multivibrator — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    sourceA[DC Supply VCC] --> resA[Resistor RC1]
    resA --> nodeA[Collector of Q1]
    nodeA --> capA[Timing Capacitor C]
    capA --> nodeB[Base of Q2]
    sourceA --> resB[Base Resistor R]
    resB --> nodeB
    nodeA --> outA[Output Vout1 taken here]
    sourceA --> resC[Resistor RC2]
    resC --> nodeC[Collector of Q2]
    nodeC --> resD[Base Resistor R2]
    resD --> nodeD[Base of Q1]
    nodeD --> outB[Stable State Output]
    nodeD --> trigger[External Trigger Pulse Input]
    nodeB --> crossFB[Cross Coupled Feedback Path]
    crossFB --> nodeA
    nodeC --> outC[Quasi Stable Output Vout2]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- `nodeA` = Collector of transistor Q1
- `nodeB` = Base of transistor Q2 (timing node)
- `nodeC` = Collector of transistor Q2
- `nodeD` = Base of transistor Q1 (trigger input point)
- `capA` = Timing capacitor C that couples Q1's collector to Q2's base

---

## 4.2 Clipper Circuit Topology — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    input[Input Signal Vin] --> resA[Series Resistor R1]
    resA --> diodeA[Diode D in Shunt]
    diodeA --> groundA[Ground Reference]
    resA --> outA[Output Vout across RL]
    groundA --> outA
    biasA[DC Bias VB] --> diodeA
    input --> refA[Reference Voltage Comparator]
    refA --> stateA{Diode State}
    stateA -->|ON| shunt[Shunt to Ground]
    stateA -->|OFF| pass[Pass Signal to Output]
    shunt --> outA
    pass --> outA
```

---

## 4.3 Clamper Circuit — Charge-Discharge Functional Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcA[AC Input Source] --> capA[Capacitor C Series Element]
    capA --> nodeJ[Diode Capacitor Junction Node]
    nodeJ --> diodeA[Diode D to Ground]
    nodeJ --> loadA[Load Resistor RL to Ground]
    diodeA --> gndA[Ground]
    loadA --> gndA
    srcA --> posHalf[Positive Half Cycle Phase]
    posHalf --> chgAct[Diode ON Capacitor Charges to Vm]
    chgAct --> store[Capacitor Holds Charge Vm]
    posHalf --> negHalf[Negative Half Cycle Phase]
    negHalf --> disChk[Diode OFF Capacitor Holds Charge]
    disChk --> shifted[Output is Vin plus Vm Shifted Upward]
    shifted --> waveOut[Clamped Waveform with DC Offset]
```

---

## 4.4 Monostable State Machine — Logical Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StableState
    StableState --> StableState: Q1 OFF Q2 ON No Trigger
    StableState --> QuasiStable: External Trigger Pulse Applied
    QuasiStable --> QuasiStable: Q1 ON Q2 OFF Capacitor Charging Through R
    QuasiStable --> RecoveryPhase: T equals 0.693 RC Time Elapsed
    RecoveryPhase --> StableState: Q2 ON Q1 OFF Capacitor Recharges Through RC1
    RecoveryPhase --> RecoveryPhase: Recovery Time 5 RC1 C
    note right of QuasiStable: Duration T equals 0.693 RC
    note right of RecoveryPhase: Duration Tr equals 5 RC1 C
```

---

## 4.5 Waveform Timing Diagram (Textual Block Representation)

```
TIME AXIS ---->
   |
   |     STABLE              QUASI-STABLE              RECOVERY
   |   ___________          _____________           ____________
V_B2      +V_CC|--                \                       /
   |             |                 \                     /
   |             |                  \                   /
   |          ___|                   \_________________/
   |   0V ____|                          0.7V threshold
   |____________________________________________________
                  ^                       ^
              TRIGGER                T = 0.693 RC
              APPLIED
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Left region: Q1 OFF, Q2 ON (stable state). $V_{B2}$ held at +0.7 V.
- Middle region (quasi-stable): $V_{B2}$ dips negative to $-V_{CC}$ at trigger, then exponentially rises toward $+V_{CC}$ through R.
- Right region: $V_{B2}$ crosses +0.7 V at time $T = 0.693\,RC$, switching the state. Recovery follows as the capacitor recharges.
