## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **"Intrinsic Standoff Ratio" (η)** of a UJT. What is its typical range?

**Model Answer:**

The intrinsic standoff ratio η is defined as the ratio of the resistance of the emitter-to-B1 region (R_B1) to the total inter-base resistance (R_BB = R_B1 + R_B2) of the UJT bar, measured with the emitter open-circuited:

$$\eta = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{B1} + R_{B2}} = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{BB}}$$

It is a structural/geometric property of the device, **dimensionless**, and its **typical range is 0.5 to 0.8** (usually around 0.6 to 0.7 for standard 2N2646 UJTs). It determines the firing voltage of the UJT and is largely **insensitive to temperature**.

**[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for range]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between the **peak point** and the **valley point** of a UJT V-I characteristics. What is the significance of the negative resistance region between them?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Peak Point | Valley Point |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Voltage | V_P (Maximum firing voltage) | V_V (Minimum ON voltage) |
| Current | I_P (Minimum current to fire) | I_V (Minimum current to stay ON) |
| Region Marker | Start of negative resistance region | End of negative resistance region |
| Typical values | I_P ≈ μA, V_P ≈ several volts | I_V ≈ mA, V_V ≈ 1–3 V |

**Significance of Negative Resistance Region:**
Between V_P and V_V, the UJT exhibits **negative differential resistance** (dV/dI < 0). This is because once holes are injected from the emitter, R_B1 collapses rapidly, allowing more current to flow even as Vₑ drops. This region is **exploited in relaxation oscillators** to generate fast-trigger pulses and in SCR firing circuits for phase control.

**[3 marks: 1 each for peak/valley, 1 for significance]**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and equivalent circuit of a UJT**. Derive the expression for the **peak point voltage (V_P)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UJT has η = 0.7, V_BB = 18 V, R_BB = 10 kΩ, and V_D = 0.7 V. It is used in a relaxation oscillator with R_C = 47 kΩ and C = 0.05 μF. Calculate (i) the peak point voltage V_P, (ii) the period of oscillation T, and (iii) the frequency f. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Construction and Equivalent Circuit of UJT:**

**Construction:** (3 marks)
A UJT is constructed from a bar of **lightly doped N-type silicon**. A small **heavily doped P-type pellet** is alloyed into the bar (closer to the B2 terminal), forming the **single PN junction**. Ohmic contacts are made at both ends of the bar — these are **Base 1 (B1)** and **Base 2 (B2)**. A lead is attached to the P-region, forming the **Emitter (E)** terminal.

**Equivalent Circuit:** (2 marks)
The equivalent circuit consists of:
- A voltage divider of R_B1 (between E and B1) and R_B2 (between E and B2)
- A diode D (anode at E, cathode at the divider tap)
- V_BB applied between B2 and B1; Vₑ applied at the emitter

**Derivation of V_P:** (2 marks)
With the emitter open, the open-circuit voltage at the emitter point (relative to B1) is:
$$V_{E}^{open} = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{B1} + R_{B2}} \cdot V_{BB} = \eta \cdot V_{BB}$$

The junction fires when the emitter voltage exceeds this by the diode drop V_D:
$$V_P = \eta \cdot V_{BB} + V_D$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** η = 0.7, V_BB = 18 V, R_BB = 10 kΩ, V_D = 0.7 V, R_C = 47 kΩ, C = 0.05 μF.

**(i) Peak Point Voltage:** (2 marks)
$$V_P = \eta \cdot V_{BB} + V_D = 0.7 \times 18 + 0.7 = 12.6 + 0.7 = 13.3 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Period of Oscillation:** (3 marks)
$$T = R_C \cdot C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\eta}\right) = (47 \times 10^3)(0.05 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-0.7}\right)$$

$$T = 2.35 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \ln(1/0.3) = 2.35 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \ln(3.333)$$

$$T = 2.35 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.2040 = 2.829 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) Frequency:** (2 marks)
$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2.829 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 353.5 \text{ Hz}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **V-I characteristics of a UJT** with a clearly labeled diagram. Mark the **cutoff, negative resistance, and saturation** regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **circuit diagram**, explain the operation of a **UJT relaxation oscillator**. Derive an expression for its **frequency of oscillation**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) V-I Characteristics of UJT:** (7 marks)

The V-I characteristics plot emitter voltage Vₑ (Y-axis) versus emitter current Iₑ (X-axis), and has three distinct regions:

1. **Cutoff Region (0 to Peak Point P):** (2 marks)
   - Vₑ increases from 0 with very small Iₑ (microampere range).
   - Diode is reverse-biased or weakly forward-biased.
   - At peak point P: Vₑ = V_P = η·V_BB + V_D, Iₑ = I_P (very small).
   - Slope is nearly vertical (high resistance).

2. **Negative Resistance Region (Peak to Valley):** (2 marks)
   - Once V_P is exceeded, holes are injected into the N-bar.
   - R_B1 collapses, so Iₑ rises **rapidly** while Vₑ **falls**.
   - This is the **negative differential resistance** region: dVₑ/dIₑ < 0.
   - It terminates at valley point V (V_V, I_V).

3. **Saturation Region (Beyond Valley Point):** (2 marks)
   - Vₑ rises with Iₑ in a near-linear fashion (positive resistance).
   - The device behaves like a conventional forward-biased diode from E to B1.
   - Vₑ asymptotically approaches a saturation value.

A **labeling** on the diagram must include: V_P, I_P, V_V, I_V, the three regions, and the asymptotic saturation curve.

---

**(b) UJT Relaxation Oscillator:** (7 marks)

**Circuit:** (2 marks)
A UJT relaxation oscillator consists of:
- A DC supply V_BB feeding a resistor R_C and capacitor C in series.
- The junction of R_C and C is connected to the **emitter E**.
- B1 is grounded, and B2 is connected to V_BB.
- An output pulse is taken across a small resistor R₁ (between B1 and ground).

**Operation:** (2 marks)
- Capacitor C charges through R_C exponentially toward V_BB.
- When V_C = V_P, the UJT fires — its emitter-to-B1 resistance drops sharply.
- C discharges rapidly through E to B1, producing a sharp output pulse across R₁.
- When V_C falls below V_V, the UJT turns OFF, and the cycle repeats.
- Result: a **sawtooth waveform at C** and a **pulse train at the output**.

**Frequency Derivation:** (3 marks)
The capacitor voltage follows:
$$V_C(t) = V_{BB} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/(R_C C)}\right)$$

Setting V_C(T) = V_P ≈ η·V_BB (neglecting V_D):
$$1 - e^{-T/(R_C C)} = \eta$$

$$T = R_C C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\eta}\right)$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{R_C C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-\eta}\right)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting V_D in V_P:** Many students write V_P = η·V_BB only, losing 1 mark. Always include V_D ≈ 0.7 V.
> 2. **Confusing I_P and I_V:** I_P is the **minimum** current to *fire* (very small, μA); I_V is the **minimum** current to *stay ON* (larger, mA). Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Mixing up peak and valley voltages:** V_P > V_V always. The voltage **drops** from V_P to V_V as the UJT fires.
> 4. **Sign of negative resistance:** The slope dV/dI is **negative**, NOT the resistance itself being negative in magnitude. Use precise language.
> 5. **Drawing the UJT symbol with the emitter arrow the wrong way:** The arrow points *from P (emitter) toward N (bar)* — i.e., **towards the bar**, NOT away from it. (Conventions differ; verify with the KTU textbook diagram in your module.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ UJT has **three terminals** (E, B1, B2) but only **one PN junction** (between P-emitter and N-bar).
- ✅ It is a **voltage-controlled triggering device**, not an amplifying device.
- ✅ **Intrinsic Standoff Ratio:** $\eta = R_{B1}/R_{BB}$, **dimensionless**, typically **0.5–0.8**.
- ✅ **Peak Voltage:** $V_P = \eta \cdot V_{BB} + V_D$; **Valley Voltage:** V_V (from data sheet).
- ✅ **Peak Current I_P** is in **μA**; **Valley Current I_V** is in **mA** (I_V >> I_P).
- ✅ V-I curve has **three regions**: Cutoff, Negative Resistance, Saturation.
- ✅ The **negative resistance** arises from **hole injection** collapsing R_B1.
- ✅ **Relaxation Oscillator Period:** $T = R_C C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-\eta}\right)$.
- ✅ UJT is widely used to **trigger SCRs** in phase-controlled rectifiers, dimmers, and motor drives.
- ✅ UJT is **unipolar** in the sense that conduction occurs through one type of bar material modulated by emitter injection — do not confuse with BJT, which is a bipolar current amplifier.
- ✅ The device is **robust, cheap, and thermally stable** in triggering applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triac
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Triac

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **TRIAC** (an acronym for **TRIode for Alternating Current**) is a five-layer, three-terminal bidirectional semiconductor power device that belongs to the thyristor family. It functions essentially as **two Silicon Controlled Rectifiers (SCRs) connected in anti-parallel (inverse parallel)** within a single monolithic silicon chip, sharing a common gate terminal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *A TRIAC is a bidirectional three-terminal power switch that can conduct current in either direction between its main terminals (MT1 and MT2) when triggered by a gate signal. It is used for controlling AC loads where full-wave conduction is required.*

The three terminals are:
- **MT1** (Main Terminal 1) — Reference terminal, usually connected to the neutral/ground side of the AC supply.
- **MT2** (Main Terminal 2) — The other main terminal where the AC line is applied.
- **Gate (G)** — The control terminal that triggers conduction in both polarities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Two-Way Light Dimmer Switch":**
> 
> Imagine a **light dimmer switch** in your living room. An ordinary dimmer can only reduce brightness — it works in **one direction** (one half of the AC cycle). A **TRIAC-based dimmer**, however, is like a "smart" dimmer that can smoothly control the lamp's brightness in *both halves* of the AC sine wave (the positive and the negative half). 
> 
> Think of a TRIAC as **two ordinary one-way valves (SCRs) placed back-to-back**, but operated by a *single common knob (gate)*. Whichever direction the water (current) is trying to flow, a small tap on the gate "opens" that side's valve and lets the current pass. The amount of time you wait before tapping the gate each half-cycle controls how much power the lamp receives — exactly the principle behind every domestic lamp dimmer, fan regulator, and heater controller.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Breakover Voltage (V_BO)**: Typically **400 V to 800 V** for standard 50/60 Hz domestic TRIACs.
- **On-State Voltage Drop (V_T)**: Around **1.5 V to 2.5 V** at rated RMS current.
- **Gate Trigger Current (I_GT)**: Ranges from **5 mA to 50 mA** depending on quadrant of operation.
- **Holding Current (I_H)**: Typically **10 mA to 75 mA**.
- **Latching Current (I_L)**: Slightly higher than I_H.
- **Standard AC mains**: **230 V RMS, 50 Hz (India/Europe)** or **110 V RMS, 60 Hz (USA)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters:** KTU problems often plug in 230 V, 50 Hz Indian domestic supply values. Always use the peak value $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times V_{rms}$ for derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction & Internal Structure

A TRIAC is fabricated as a **five-layer (P-N-P-N-P)**, three-terminal device. It can be viewed structurally as two SCRs integrated into a single wafer:

- **Upper half (P-N-P-N)** → Equivalent to **SCR₁** (conducts during positive half-cycle).
- **Lower half (N-P-N-P)** → Equivalent to **SCR₂** (conducts during negative half-cycle).
- Both halves share a **common gate** terminal and a **common MT1** terminal.

> [!NOTE]
> Although the TRIAC is functionally two anti-parallel SCRs, the internal junctions (J1, J2, J3, J4) interact such that the gate sensitivity varies with the **quadrant of operation** (see Section 2.4).

## 2.2 Symbol & Pinout

The schematic symbol of a TRIAC resembles two SCR symbols drawn back-to-back with a single common gate lead entering from the side.

## 2.3 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **OFF State (Blocking Mode):** When the gate is open (no trigger), the TRIAC blocks both polarities of applied voltage up to its breakover limits. Only a small leakage current flows.
2. **Triggering:** When a gate pulse of sufficient magnitude and proper polarity is applied, the device "fires" (latches ON).
3. **Conduction:** The TRIAC conducts from MT2 to MT1 (or vice versa, depending on the polarity of MT2 with respect to MT1) with a low forward voltage drop (~1.5–2 V).
4. **Natural Commutation:** Because the device is in an AC circuit, the current naturally falls to zero at every half-cycle. When the current drops below the **holding current (I_H)**, the TRIAC turns OFF automatically — no external commutation circuitry is required.

## 2.4 Four Modes / Quadrants of Operation

The TRIAC can be triggered in **four distinct quadrants** depending on the polarities of $V_{MT2-MT1}$ and $V_{G-MT1}$:

| Quadrant | MT2 Polarity w.r.t. MT1 | Gate Polarity w.r.t. MT1 | Sensitivity |
|----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|-------------|
| **I**    | Positive (+)             | Positive (+)              | **High** (most common, easiest triggering) |
| **II**   | Positive (+)             | Negative (−)              | Low         |
| **III**  | Negative (−)             | Negative (−)              | High        |
| **IV**   | Negative (−)             | Positive (+)              | Very Low (avoided in practice) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Why is Quadrant IV operation avoided in TRIAC triggering circuits?"*
> **Answer:** Because the gate triggering current required in Quadrant IV is very high, and the device may *mis-fire* or get damaged. Designers use Quadrants I and III (preferably I) for reliable operation.

## 2.5 V-I Characteristics (Qualitative)

The V-I characteristics of a TRIAC are **symmetric about the origin** in both the first and third quadrants — it behaves like two identical SCR characteristics reflected through the origin.

- **Quadrant I:** MT2 positive, current flows MT2 → MT1.
- **Quadrant III:** MT2 negative, current flows MT1 → MT2.
- The breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ and forward voltage drop $V_T$ are similar in both quadrants.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Peak Supply Voltage | $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$ | $V_{rms}$ = RMS line voltage (V) |
| 2 | RMS Output Voltage (Phase Control) | $V_{o(rms)} = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}$ | $\alpha$ = firing angle (rad) |
| 3 | Average Output Voltage | $V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\,(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | For full-wave phase control |
| 4 | RMS Supply Voltage | $V_s = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Constant for given AC line |
| 5 | Power Delivered to Load | $P = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}{R_L}$ | Watts (W) |
| 6 | Conduction Angle | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ | Radians (one half-cycle) |
| 7 | Conduction Period (per half-cycle) | $T_{on} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\omega}$ | Seconds |
| 8 | Quadrant I/III: $I_{GT}$ | $I_{GT} \approx 5\text{–}25$ mA | Sensitive gate region |
| 9 | Latching Current Relation | $I_L \approx (1.2\text{–}2)\cdot I_H$ | Empirical |
| 10 | RMS Current (sinusoidal conduction) | $I_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}}{R_L}$ | Amperes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation Formatting Note:** In the table above, the absolute-value notation has been replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption. For instance, $\vert V_m \vert$ is written as $\vert V_m \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX command.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic lamp dimmers & fan regulators** (the classical use case).
- **Heat control** in electric ovens, soldering stations, and incubators.
- **Single-phase AC motor speed controllers** (small universal / shaded-pole motors).
- **AC static switches** (solid-state relays for industrial control).
- **Light chasers** and **flickering decorative lighting** in signboards.
- **Three-phase TRIAC-based soft starters** (using one TRIAC per phase).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage of a TRIAC Phase-Controlled Circuit

Consider a **TRIAC-based full-wave AC phase control circuit** feeding a resistive load $R_L$ from an AC source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. The TRIAC is fired at an angle $\alpha$ in **each half-cycle** and conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$, and from $\omega t = (\pi + \alpha)$ to $\omega t = 2\pi$.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Write the instantaneous output voltage

During conduction (from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ in the positive half-cycle):

$$
v_o(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for} \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi
$$

The output is **zero** from $0$ to $\alpha$ (TRIAC is OFF).

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Definition of RMS value

The general RMS definition over a full period $T = 2\pi/\omega$ is:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^{\,2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Substitute and split the integral

Because the waveform is **half-wave symmetric** (conduction pattern repeats in each half-cycle with same shape), the integral over $[0, 2\pi]$ equals **twice** the integral over $[\alpha, \pi]$:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot 2 \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \left(V_m \sin(\omega t)\right)^{2} d(\omega t)}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Pull constants out

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{\,2}}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Apply the half-angle identity

Use the identity $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{\,2}}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, d(\omega t)}
$$

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{\,2}}{2\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \left[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\right] d(\omega t)}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Perform the integration

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} 1 \, d(\omega t) = \pi - \alpha
$$

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \left[\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{\sin(2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} = -\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Combine the results

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{\,2}}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) - \left(-\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right)\right]}
$$

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^{\,2}}{2\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Factor out $V_m^{\,2}/\pi$ and take the square root

$$
V_{o(rms)} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\,\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}
$$

Since $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = V_{rms}$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o(rms)} = V_{rms}\,\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special case verification:** When $\alpha = 0$ (full conduction, TRIAC ON for the entire half-cycle), the term in the brackets becomes $\pi - 0 + 0 = \pi$, so $V_{o(rms)} = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{\pi/\pi} = V_{rms}$. ✔ Correct — full supply appears across the load.
> 
> When $\alpha = \pi$ (no conduction), brackets give $0 + 0 = 0$, so $V_{o(rms)} = 0$. ✔ Correct — no power delivered.

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Output Voltage

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Average value definition

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Substitute $v_o$

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot 2\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Evaluate the limits

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha)\right] = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[1 + \cos(\alpha)\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\,(1 + \cos\alpha)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow V_{o(avg)} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ (which is the average of a full sine wave — the textbook $2V_m/\pi$ result). $\alpha = \pi \Rightarrow V_{o(avg)} = 0$. Both checks pass.

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Delivered to Resistive Load

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Instantaneous power

$$
p(\omega t) = \frac{v_o^{\,2}(\omega t)}{R_L} = \frac{V_m^{\,2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)}{R_L}
$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Average over the full cycle

$$
P = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi} p(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^{\,2}}{2\pi R_L}\cdot 2\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Using the result of Section 3.1.6

$$
P = \frac{V_m^{\,2}}{2\pi R_L}\cdot 2 \cdot \left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P = \frac{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}{R_L} = \frac{V_{rms}^{\,2}}{R_L}\cdot \frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]\,}
$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A TRIAC is used to control a $100\ \Omega$ resistive heater from a **230 V, 50 Hz** AC supply. The firing angle is $\alpha = 60^{\circ} = \pi/3\ \text{rad}$. Find:
1. RMS output voltage
2. Average output voltage
3. Power delivered to the heater

### 3.4.1 Step 1 — Identify inputs

$$
V_{rms} = 230\ \text{V},\quad V_m = \sqrt{2}\cdot 230 \approx 325.27\ \text{V},\quad \alpha = 60^{\circ} = \pi/3\ \text{rad}
$$

$$
\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(120^{\circ}) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660
$$

### 3.4.2 Step 2 — RMS output voltage

$$
V_{o(rms)} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \frac{\pi}{3} + \frac{0.8660}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
V_{o(rms)} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[2.0944 + 0.4330\right]} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2.5274}{3.1416}}
$$

$$
V_{o(rms)} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{0.8044} = 230 \cdot 0.8969 \approx \mathbf{206.3\ V}
$$

### 3.4.3 Step 3 — Average output voltage

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{325.27}{\pi}\left(1 + \cos 60^{\circ}\right) = 103.53 \cdot (1 + 0.5) = 103.53 \cdot 1.5 \approx \mathbf{155.3\ V}
$$

### 3.4.4 Step 4 — Power delivered

$$
P = \frac{(206.3)^{2}}{100} = \frac{42559.7}{100} \approx \mathbf{425.6\ W}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Operating Quadrants

| Parameter | Quadrant I | Quadrant II | Quadrant III | Quadrant IV |
|-----------|------------|-------------|--------------|-------------|
| $V_{MT2}$ polarity | $+$ | $+$ | $-$ | $-$ |
| $V_{G}$ polarity | $+$ | $-$ | $-$ | $+$ |
| $I_{GT}$ (typical) | 5–25 mA | 30–75 mA | 5–25 mA | 75–150 mA |
| Mode of firing | Remote gate | Avalanche | Remote gate | Avalanche |
| dv/dt capability | High | Low | High | Low |
| Reliability | Best | Avoid | Good | Worst — avoid |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define TRIAC. List its three terminals and state one important application.**

**Model Answer:**

A **TRIAC (TRIode for Alternating Current)** is a **bidirectional three-terminal semiconductor device** belonging to the thyristor family. It can conduct current in **either direction** between its two main terminals when triggered by a gate signal, making it suitable for **AC power control**.

**Three terminals:**
1. **MT1** — Main Terminal 1
2. **MT2** — Main Terminal 2
3. **Gate (G)** — Control terminal

**Application:** Domestic **lamp dimmer** / **fan regulator** / **AC static switch** / **heat control in electric ovens**. *(Any one — 3 marks total.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Why is Quadrant IV mode of triggering generally avoided in TRIAC operation?**

**Model Answer:**

Quadrant IV operation requires the **MT2 terminal to be negative** and the **gate to be positive** with respect to MT1. In this mode:

1. The required **gate trigger current $I_{GT}$ is very high** (often 75–150 mA or more).
2. The device has **poor dv/dt capability**, making it susceptible to **false triggering** due to transient voltages.
3. The triggering is unreliable and can lead to **uneven conduction** between the two half-cycles, producing a **DC component** in the load.
4. Prolonged operation in Quadrant IV can cause **thermal runaway** and **device failure**.

Hence, designers prefer **Quadrants I and III** for reliable TRIAC operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions with Sub-Parts

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction, symbol, and equivalent circuit of a TRIAC**. Mention its **three terminals** and describe **how it can be viewed as two anti-parallel SCRs**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A TRIAC is used to control a **$50\ \Omega$ resistive load** from a **$230\ \text{V}, 50\ \text{Hz}$ AC supply** at a firing angle of $\alpha = 90^{\circ}$. Calculate:
- (i) RMS output voltage
- (ii) Average output voltage
- (iii) Power delivered to the load

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Construction:** A TRIAC is a **five-layer (P-N-P-N-P)** three-terminal device fabricated on a single silicon wafer. The layers are arranged such that the device behaves like **two SCRs connected in anti-parallel**, sharing a common MT1 terminal and a common gate.

**Symbol:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 block diagram)

**Three Terminals:**
- **MT1** — Main Terminal 1 (reference)
- **MT2** — Main Terminal 2
- **Gate (G)** — Trigger input

**Equivalent Circuit:** Two SCRs, SCR₁ (P-N-P-N) and SCR₂ (N-P-N-P), connected in **anti-parallel** between MT1 and MT2, with their gates tied together to form a common gate G.

**Operation:** When MT2 is positive w.r.t. MT1, a positive gate pulse fires SCR₁. When MT2 is negative w.r.t. MT1, a negative gate pulse fires SCR₂. The AC line voltage naturally commutates (turns OFF) the device at every zero-crossing.

*(7 marks — distributed as: 2 marks construction, 2 marks symbol/diagram, 3 marks equivalent circuit & operation.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_{rms} = 230\ \text{V}$, so $V_m = \sqrt{2}\cdot 230 = 325.27\ \text{V}$
- $R_L = 50\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 90^{\circ} = \pi/2\ \text{rad}$, $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$

**(i) RMS output voltage:**

Using the formula:

$$
V_{o(rms)} = V_{rms}\,\sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]}
$$

$$
\sin(2\alpha) = \sin(180^{\circ}) = 0
$$

$$
V_{o(rms)} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \frac{\pi}{2} + 0\right]} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\pi/2}{\pi}} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{0.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{o(rms)} = 230 \cdot 0.7071 \approx 162.6\ \text{V}}
$$

**(ii) Average output voltage:**

$$
V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{325.27}{\pi}(1 + \cos 90^{\circ})
$$

$$
\cos 90^{\circ} = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{o(avg)} = \frac{325.27}{\pi}\cdot 1 \approx 103.5\ \text{V}}
$$

**(iii) Power delivered:**

$$
P = \frac{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}{R_L} = \frac{(162.6)^{2}}{50} = \frac{26439.6}{50}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 528.8\ \text{W}}
$$

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the **V-I characteristics of a TRIAC** and explain its **four modes (quadrants) of operation**. Compare the **gate sensitivity** in each quadrant. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain with a **neat circuit diagram** how a **TRIAC is used as a lamp dimmer** using a **DIAC-based triggering circuit**. Describe the role of the DIAC and the phase-shift network. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**V-I Characteristics:** The TRIAC V-I curve is **symmetric about the origin**, with operation possible in **Quadrant I (MT2 positive)** and **Quadrant III (MT2 negative)**. The characteristics resemble two SCR curves reflected through the origin.

**Four Quadrants of Operation:**

| Quadrant | $V_{MT2}$ | $V_G$ | Sensitivity | Remarks |
|----------|-----------|-------|-------------|---------|
| I | $+$ | $+$ | **High** | Most preferred |
| II | $+$ | $-$ | Low | Used occasionally |
| III | $-$ | $-$ | High | Equivalent to Q-I in opposite polarity |
| IV | $-$ | $+$ | **Very Low** | Avoided in practice |

**Explanation:**
- In **Quadrant I**, MT2 is positive and gate is positive — this is the most sensitive and reliable mode. A small positive gate current (~5–25 mA) latches the device.
- In **Quadrant II**, MT2 is positive but gate is negative — the device is triggered through the **avalanche mode** of the gate junction, requiring higher $I_{GT}$.
- In **Quadrant III**, MT2 is negative and gate is negative — symmetric to Quadrant I, with similar sensitivity.
- In **Quadrant IV**, MT2 is negative and gate is positive — least sensitive; high $I_{GT}$ and poor dv/dt tolerance.

**Comparison:** Quadrants I and III are mirror images and offer the **best gate sensitivity**, hence designers fire the TRIAC in these quadrants to minimize the gate driver power requirement.

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 4 for quadrant explanation & comparison.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a) — Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1["QUADRANT I\nMT2 POSITIVE\nGATE POSITIVE\nHIGH SENSITIVITY\nVBR POSITIVE\nI POSITIVE"]:::q1
    Q3["QUADRANT III\nMT2 NEGATIVE\nGATE NEGATIVE\nHIGH SENSITIVITY\nVBR NEGATIVE\nI NEGATIVE"]:::q3
    ORIGIN["ORIGIN\nZERO VOLTAGE\nZERO CURRENT\nTRIAC OFF STATE"]:::center
    Q1 --- ORIGIN
    Q3 --- ORIGIN
    Q1 --- Q3

    classDef q1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef q3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef center fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Circuit Description:** Refer to SECTION 4.4 — the lamp dimmer consists of a **TRIAC (BT136 or similar)** in series with the **lamp (load)**, and a **DIAC (DB3)** in the gate circuit, along with a **phase-shift RC network** ($R_{variable}$ and $C$).

**Working:**

1. During each half-cycle of the AC mains, the capacitor $C$ charges through the variable resistor $R$.
2. The voltage across $C$ rises until it reaches the **breakover voltage of the DIAC** (~30–35 V for DB3).
3. The DIAC then **suddenly conducts** and dumps the capacitor charge into the TRIAC gate, firing it.
4. The TRIAC then **latches ON** and conducts for the remainder of that half-cycle.
5. At the next zero-crossing, the TRIAC turns OFF (natural commutation), and the process repeats.

**Role of the DIAC:**
- Provides a **sharp, symmetric trigger pulse** in both half-cycles.
- Prevents **hysteresis and half-wave asymmetry** that would occur if the gate were triggered directly by the RC voltage.
- Ensures the **firing angle is identical** in the positive and negative half-cycles, eliminating any DC component in the load.

**Role of the Phase-Shift Network (R and C):**
- The RC time constant determines **how quickly the DIAC breakover voltage is reached**, which sets the **firing angle $\alpha$**.
- Varying the **variable resistor $R$** shifts $\alpha$ between nearly $0^{\circ}$ (lamp full brightness) and nearly $180^{\circ}$ (lamp off).
- This gives **smooth, continuous dimming control**.

*(7 marks — 3 for circuit diagram & working, 2 for DIAC role, 2 for phase-shift network.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in TRIAC Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $V_m$ with $V_{rms}$:** Many students plug $V_{rms}$ directly into the average formula $V_{o(avg)} = V_m(1+\cos\alpha)/\pi$. Always convert $V_{rms} \to V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$ **first**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the half-wave factor of 2:** When the conduction extends over both half-cycles (full-wave phase control), the integral spans $2\pi$ in the denominator. Students often write $\pi$ instead of $2\pi$, giving **wrong RMS values**.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up Latching and Holding Currents:** $I_L$ is the minimum anode current required to **establish** conduction at the moment of triggering. $I_H$ is the minimum current required to **maintain** conduction thereafter. $I_L > I_H$ always.
> 
> 4. **Saying "TRIAC = two SCRs in parallel":** This is a **common exam error**. The correct phrase is **"two SCRs in anti-parallel"** (or "inverse parallel"). Parallel means both conduct the *same direction*; anti-parallel means they conduct *opposite directions* — which is what a TRIAC does.
> 
> 5. **Quadrant labelling confusion:** Quadrant I is **MT2 +, Gate +**, not "the first firing" or "positive half-cycle only." Memorize the table in Section 2.4.
> 
> 6. **Unit mismatches:** Firing angle $\alpha$ must be in **radians** inside trigonometric functions. If the problem gives $\alpha$ in degrees, **convert first**: $\alpha_{rad} = \alpha_{deg} \cdot \pi / 180$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- TRIAC = **TRIode for Alternating Current**; a **bidirectional** thyristor.
- It is **functionally equivalent to two SCRs in anti-parallel**, sharing a common gate.
- **Three terminals:** **MT1, MT2, Gate**.
- **Five-layer structure:** P-N-P-N-P.
- **Four operating quadrants** classified by polarities of $V_{MT2}$ and $V_G$ w.r.t. MT1.
- **Quadrant I** is the **most sensitive**; **Quadrant IV** is the **least sensitive** (avoid).
- TRIAC is **naturally commutated** at every AC zero-crossing — **no commutation circuit** needed.
- Used in **AC power control** — lamp dimmers, fan regulators, heat controllers, static switches.
- **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$
  - $V_{o(rms)} = V_{rms} \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$
  - $V_{o(avg)} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$
  - $P = \frac{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}{R_L}$
  - Conduction angle per half-cycle: $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$
- **DIAC** is the most common TRIAC trigger device in domestic dimmers.
- **Quadrant IV avoided** because of high $I_{GT}$ and unreliable triggering.
- **Latching current $I_L$ > Holding current $I_H$** — remember the order.
- The **gate signal need not be sustained** — a **single short pulse** is sufficient to latch the TRIAC.
- The TRIAC symbol **looks like two SCR symbols back-to-back** with a common gate lead.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diac - Operation and VI Characteristics, Transferred Electron Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The DIAC (Diode for Alternating Current)

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **DIAC** stands for **"Diode for Alternating Current"**. It is a two-terminal, three-layer (or five-layer), bidirectional semiconductor switching device that remains in the non-conducting (OFF) state until the voltage across its terminals reaches a critical **breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$)**. Once this threshold is crossed, the device abruptly switches into a low-impedance conducting (ON) state, behaving symmetrically for both polarities of applied voltage.

The DIAC is the semiconductor equivalent of two Zener diodes connected back-to-back in anti-series. It is a member of the **thyristor family** but lacks a gate terminal, making it a purely voltage-triggered device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The DIAC is primarily used as a **triggering device for TRIACs** in phase-control AC circuits such as lamp dimmers, fan regulators, and heater controllers. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know its V-I curve, breakover voltage, and triggering behavior.

### Constructional Overview
- The DIAC is fabricated as a **p-n-p-n-p (five-layer)** structure, or equivalently as a **p-n-p structure with a resistive layer**.
- It has **no gate terminal** — conduction is initiated purely by the magnitude of the applied voltage.
- The two outer layers are heavily doped ($p^+, n^+$), while the inner region controls the breakdown behavior.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressure Relief Valve"
Imagine a **pressure relief valve** fitted to a water pipeline:

- The valve remains **firmly closed (blocking mode)** as long as the water pressure is below a critical limit.
- The instant the pressure **crosses the safety threshold (breakover voltage)**, the valve snaps open, allowing water (current) to gush through with very little resistance.
- Once the pressure drops below a lower **holding threshold**, the valve closes again.
- This valve is **direction-agnostic** — it works whether the water tries to push from the left or the right.

This is exactly how a DIAC behaves with electrical voltage and current. The breakover voltage is the "pressure limit," and the device's bidirectional nature means it triggers for either polarity of an AC signal.

---

## 1.2 Transferred Electron Devices (TEDs)

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Transferred Electron Devices (TEDs)** are bulk (i.e., not junction-based) semiconductor devices whose operation relies on the **Gunn Effect** — a phenomenon in which electrons transfer from a low-energy, high-mobility valley to a high-energy, low-mobility valley of the conduction band when the applied electric field exceeds a critical threshold. The most prominent TED is the **Gunn Diode**, which exhibits **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** in a specific range of applied voltages.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Lane Highway with a Speed Bump"
Picture a two-lane highway:

- **Lane 1 (Valley 1):** A wide, smooth expressway where cars (electrons) travel at high speed (high mobility, low effective mass).
- **Lane 2 (Valley 2):** A narrow, bumpy service road where cars are forced to crawl (low mobility, high effective mass).

Normally, all traffic flows on the expressway. But when a "force field" (electric field) becomes very strong, the cars are pushed up a ramp and transferred onto the slower service lane. The overall average speed of traffic **decreases even though the force pushing the cars has increased** — this is the essence of **negative differential resistance**.

A **Gunn Diode** exploits exactly this effect to convert DC into microwave AC oscillations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be able to explain the **two-valley model** (Ridley-Watkins-Hilsum mechanism), sketch the **V-I characteristics** of a Gunn diode showing the NDR region, and identify the **threshold voltage ($V_{th}$)** and **threshold field ($E_{th}$)**. They should also know typical applications in **microwave oscillators** and **parametric amplifiers**.

---

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Silicon breakover field | — | $\approx 3 \times 10^5$ | V/cm |
| DIAC breakover voltage | $V_{BO}$ | **30 – 50** | V |
| DIAC holding current | $I_H$ | **5 – 25** | mA |
| Gunn diode threshold field (GaAs) | $E_{th}$ | **3.2** | kV/cm |
| Gunn diode threshold voltage | $V_{th}$ | **3.3** | V |
| Operating frequency (Gunn) | $f$ | **1 – 100** | GHz |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DIAC — Detailed Operating Principle

The DIAC's operation is best understood by tracing its three regions of operation. The device exhibits a **symmetric, non-linear V-I curve** about the origin.

### Operating States of the DIAC

**State 1 — Forward Blocking Mode (Region I):**
- The applied voltage $V < V_{BO}$ (positive direction).
- The DIAC exhibits a **high dynamic resistance** (typically $\geq 100\ \text{k}\Omega$).
- Only a tiny leakage current (microamperes) flows.
- The device is essentially OFF.

**State 2 — Breakover / Avalanche Mode (Region II — Negative Resistance Region):**
- Once $V = V_{BO}$ is reached, the central junction undergoes **avalanche breakdown**.
- The carrier multiplication causes a rapid rise in current.
- The dynamic resistance becomes **negative** — voltage drops as current increases.
- This region is inherently unstable and is traversed almost instantaneously.

**State 3 — On-State (Region III — Forward Conduction):**
- The DIAC now behaves like a forward-biased diode.
- The voltage across it stabilizes at a low **on-state voltage** ($V_T \approx 1$ to $2$ V).
- The current is limited only by the external load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mirror Symmetry:** The same three states occur for the negative half-cycle with $-V_{BO}$. This is why the V-I characteristic of a DIAC is **odd-symmetric** about the origin.

### Why is the V-I Curve Symmetric?
The DIAC is constructed so that its two halves are **electrostatically and geometrically identical**. The p-n-p and n-p-n halves are mirror twins, ensuring identical behavior for positive and negative voltages. This makes the DIAC ideal for **AC triggering** of TRIACs.

---

## 2.2 Transferred Electron Devices (TEDs) — Two-Valley Model

The Gunn Effect, discovered by **J. B. Gunn in 1963**, cannot be explained by conventional p-n junction theory. It is a **bulk effect** arising from the band structure of certain compound semiconductors.

### Material Selection Criteria
For a transferred electron effect to occur, the semiconductor must possess:
1. A **direct bandgap**.
2. A **central valley** in the conduction band separated by **$\Delta E < 100\ \text{meV}$** from a satellite valley.
3. A **higher effective mass** in the satellite valley (lower mobility).
4. A **negative differential mobility** between the two valleys.

The canonical example is **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)**. Other materials include InP, CdTe, and InSb.

### The Two-Valley Conduction Mechanism (Ridley-Watkins-Hilsum Model)

The conduction band of GaAs has two principal minima:

| Valley | Effective Mass ($m^*$) | Mobility ($\mu$) | Separation from Valley 1 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Central Valley (1)** | Low ($m_1^* = 0.067\ m_0$) | High ($\mu_1 \approx 8500\ \text{cm}^2/\text{Vs}$) | 0 eV (reference) |
| **Satellite Valley (2)** | High ($m_2^* = 1.2\ m_0$) | Low ($\mu_2 \approx 150\ \text{cm}^2/\text{Vs}$) | $\Delta E \approx 0.36\ \text{eV}$ |

where $m_0$ is the free electron mass.

**Step-by-step Electron Behavior:**

- **Low Field ($E < E_{th}$):** Nearly all conduction electrons occupy the **central valley**, with high mobility. Current increases linearly with voltage (Ohmic region).

- **At Threshold Field ($E = E_{th} \approx 3.2\ \text{kV/cm}$ for GaAs):** Electrons gain enough kinetic energy from the field to transfer into the **satellite valley**, where their mobility drops sharply.

- **Beyond Threshold ($E > E_{th}$):** An increasing fraction of electrons populate the heavy-mass satellite valley. The drift velocity begins to **decrease** even as the field increases — this is the **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** region.

- **High Field ($E \gg E_{th}$):** All electrons are in the satellite valley. The velocity saturates at a low value. The device behaves like a high-resistance element.

### Negative Differential Resistance (NDR) — The Heart of TEDs

Mathematically, the differential conductivity is:
$$\sigma_d = \frac{dJ}{dE} = q \cdot n \cdot \mu_d$$
where $\mu_d$ is the **differential mobility**. In the NDR region:
$$\mu_d < 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{dJ}{dE} < 0$$

This NDR property is what allows a Gunn diode to **self-oscillate** and generate microwaves.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### DIAC

| Formula | Meaning | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{BO} \approx 30\text{–}50\ \text{V}$ | Breakover voltage (symmetric $\pm$) | Threshold trigger |
| $I_{BO} \approx 10\ \text{μA}$ | Breakover current | At triggering |
| $I_H \approx 5\text{–}25\ \text{mA}$ | Holding current | Minimum to stay ON |
| $V_T \approx 1.5\ \text{V}$ | On-state voltage drop | During conduction |
| $P_D = V_T \cdot I_{RMS}$ | Power dissipation | Steady state |

### Gunn Diode / TED

| Formula | Meaning | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu_d = \frac{dv_d}{dE}$ | Differential mobility | Negative in NDR region |
| $J = q \cdot n \cdot v_d$ | Current density | $v_d$ is drift velocity |
| $v_d(E) = \frac{\mu_1 \cdot E}{1 + \left(\frac{E}{E_{th}}\right)^{n}}$ | Empirical velocity-field curve | $n \approx 2$ for GaAs |
| $f_{osc} = \frac{v_d}{L}$ | Transit-time frequency | $L$ = active length |
| $\tau = \frac{L}{v_{sat}}$ | Electron transit time | $v_{sat} \approx 10^7\ \text{cm/s}$ |
| $P_{out} \propto \frac{n_0 \cdot L \cdot A}{V_{th}}$ | Output power scaling | $n_0$ = doping |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DIAC:** Acts as a **bi-directional trigger pulse generator** for TRIAC-based phase-controlled AC switches. It solves the "hysteresis / latching" problem of TRIACs by providing a clean, sharp, symmetric trigger pulse at the beginning of every half-cycle.
- **Gunn Diode:** Used as a **solid-state microwave source** in radar transceivers, police speed guns, microwave relay links, and as a **local oscillator** in superheterodyne receivers. Its compactness, reliability, and low-voltage operation make it preferable to vacuum tubes at GHz frequencies.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DIAC Triggering Voltage for TRIAC Phase Control

A TRIAC is typically triggered by a **DIAC + RC network** (the classic lamp dimmer / fan regulator topology). The time at which the DIAC fires determines the **firing angle $\alpha$** of the TRIAC, which in turn sets the RMS voltage across the load.

### Circuit Configuration

A sinusoidal source $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ charges a capacitor $C$ through resistor $R$. The DIAC is connected across the capacitor, and once $V_C$ reaches $V_{BO}$, the DIAC fires, dumping the capacitor charge into the TRIAC's gate.

### Step-by-Step Derivation of the Firing Angle

**Step 1 — Write the KVL equation:**
The source charges the RC combination. The capacitor voltage follows the standard first-order charging law.

**Step 2 — General solution for capacitor voltage across an AC source:**
The capacitor voltage, in the absence of the DIAC, would lag the source by an angle $\phi$ determined by the RC time constant:
$$\tan\phi = \omega RC$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor voltage expression:**
$$V_C(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 4 — Firing condition:**
The DIAC fires when $V_C(t) = V_{BO}$. Setting this:
$$V_m \sin(\omega t - \phi) = V_{BO}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the firing angle $\alpha = \omega t$ at the moment of firing:**
$$\sin(\alpha - \phi) = \frac{V_{BO}}{V_m}$$

**Step 6 — Final expression for firing angle:**
$$\boxed{\alpha = \phi + \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{V_{BO}}{V_m}\right)}$$

**Step 7 — Conduction angle and RMS output:**
The TRIAC conducts from $\alpha$ to $\pi$ (positive half-cycle) and from $(\pi + \alpha)$ to $2\pi$ (negative half-cycle). The conduction angle is:
$$\gamma = \pi - \alpha$$

The RMS output voltage across the load is:
$$V_{RMS} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$$

**Step 8 — Limiting cases:**
- If $V_m \gg V_{BO}$ and $\omega RC \ll 1$, then $\alpha \to 0$ (full conduction, full power).
- If $V_m$ is just slightly above $V_{BO}$, $\alpha \to \pi/2$ (partial conduction, half power).
- If $V_m < V_{BO}$, the DIAC **never fires** and the TRIAC remains OFF.

This elegant relationship shows how varying $R$ (or $C$) directly controls the firing angle and hence the load power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Transit-Time Frequency of a Gunn Diode

The Gunn diode oscillates because the domain (high-field region) drifts from cathode to anode in a finite **transit time**. This sets the fundamental oscillation frequency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define active length:**
Let $L$ be the length of the active (n-type GaAs) region of the Gunn diode.

**Step 2 — Electron drift velocity after domain formation:**
Once the field exceeds $E_{th}$, electrons in the domain travel with a saturated drift velocity:
$$v_{sat} \approx 10^7\ \text{cm/s}\ \text{(for GaAs)}$$

**Step 3 — Transit time:**
The time taken for a domain to traverse the active length is:
$$\tau = \frac{L}{v_{sat}}$$

**Step 4 — Frequency of oscillation:**
The oscillation frequency is the inverse of the transit time:
$$f_{osc} = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{v_{sat}}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute typical values for a GaAs Gunn diode:**
Let $L = 10\ \text{μm} = 10 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}$.

$$f_{osc} = \frac{10^7\ \text{cm/s}}{10 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}} = 10^{10}\ \text{Hz} = 10\ \text{GHz}$$

**Step 6 — General expression with threshold field:**
The threshold voltage across the active region is:
$$V_{th} = E_{th} \cdot L$$

For GaAs, $E_{th} = 3.2\ \text{kV/cm}$, so for $L = 10\ \text{μm}$:
$$V_{th} = 3.2 \times 10^3\ \text{V/cm} \times 10 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm} = 3.2\ \text{V}$$

This matches the empirical threshold voltage of GaAs Gunn diodes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table for KTU Board Exams

| Feature | DIAC | SCR | TRIAC | Gunn Diode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Terminals** | 2 | 3 | 3 | 2 |
| **Direction of conduction** | Bidirectional | Unidirectional | Bidirectional | Unidirectional (bulk) |
| **Triggering** | Voltage only | Gate current | Gate current / DIAC | DC bias field |
| **Negative resistance region?** | Yes (brief) | No | No | **Yes (NDR)** |
| **V-I symmetry** | Yes ($\pm V_{BO}$) | No | No (gate asymmetric) | No |
| **Primary use** | TRIAC trigger | AC/DC switch | AC power control | Microwave oscillator |
| **Operating frequency** | Line frequency (50/60 Hz) | Up to kHz | Up to kHz | **GHz range** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice that the **DIAC's negative-resistance region** is a transient, unstable breakover spike, whereas the **Gunn diode's NDR** is a stable, sustained operating region. Both, however, exploit negative differential resistance for fundamentally different purposes — the DIAC for triggering, the Gunn diode for oscillation.

---

## 3.4 Detailed V-I Characteristic Curve — Description for Examination Drawing

### DIAC V-I Characteristics (for Q-Q plane sketch)

- **Quadrant I (Forward, $V > 0$):** Starts with a small positive leakage current at origin. Rises slowly and almost linearly until $V = +V_{BO}$. At this point, the curve **bends sharply back** (negative slope — the negative resistance region), then drops to a low on-state voltage $V_T$ and rises almost vertically.
- **Quadrant III (Reverse, $V < 0$):** **Mirror image** of Quadrant I, breaking back at $V = -V_{BO}$.
- **Quadrants II and IV:** No current (DIAC blocks reverse voltage once triggered in the other direction).

### Gunn Diode V-I Characteristics

- **Origin to $V_{th}$:** Linear (Ohmic), slope = $1/R_0$ (positive resistance).
- **At $V = V_{th}$:** Current peaks at $I_{th} = I_{peak}$.
- **$V_{th} < V < V_{valley}$:** Current **decreases** with increasing voltage — the NDR region.
- **At $V = V_{valley}$:** Current bottoms out at $I_{valley}$.
- **$V > V_{valley}$:** Current rises again, saturating at a lower asymptotic value as all electrons occupy the satellite valley.

The peak-to-valley current ratio $I_{peak}/I_{valley}$ is typically **2:1 to 3:1** for GaAs, which is a key performance metric in KTU problems.

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **construction, schematic symbol, and three operational regions** of a DIAC in a unified block-level architecture.

```mermaid

    end

        C1["Region I: Blocking (R very high, I microamps)"]
        C2["Region II: NDR (Avalanche breakover at V_BO)"]

```

### Description of Regions
- **A** shows the symmetric five-layer sandwich, with the **central p-layer** acting as the "voltage-controlled switch."
- **B** is the circuit symbol (bidirectional breakover diode).
- **C** enumerates the three operational states, traversed in sequence during each half-cycle.
- **D** maps the **DIAC + TRIAC lamp-dimmer circuit** — the canonical real-world application.

---

## 4.2 Gunn Diode — Two-Valley Band Structure and Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph E["Conduction Band of GaAs - Two Valleys"]
        E1["Valley 1: Central Minimum E1, low m*, high mu"]
        E2["Valley 2: Satellite Minimum E2 = E1 + 0.36 eV, high m*, low mu"]
        E3["Energy gap Delta E = 0.36 eV"]
        E1 --- E3 --- E2
    end

    subgraph F["Electric Field Response"]
        F1["E less than E_th: All electrons in Valley 1, ohmic behavior"]
        F2["E equals E_th: Electrons start transferring to Valley 2"]
        F3["E greater than E_th: NDR region, drift velocity falls"]
        F4["E much greater than E_th: All electrons in Valley 2, saturated low velocity"]
        F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4
    end

    subgraph G["Device Functional Flow"]
        G1["DC bias applied across n-GaAs slab"]
        G2["Domain nucleation near cathode"]
        G3["High-field domain drifts to anode"]
        G4["Domain disappears at anode, current pulse generated"]
        G5["New domain forms - cycle repeats - microwave oscillation"]
        G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 --> G5
    end

    E --- F
    F --- G
```

### Reading the Diagram
- **E** illustrates the **two-valley band structure** with the central and satellite valleys separated by **$\Delta E = 0.36\ \text{eV}$** for GaAs.
- **F** traces the **electron population and mobility** as a function of applied field, identifying the four critical regimes.
- **G** shows the **Gunn domain cycle** — nucleation, drift, annihilation, and re-nucleation — which produces the microwave output.

---

## 4.3 TRIAC + DIAC Phase-Triggering Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC["AC Mains Source (V_m sin wt)"] --> RSER["Variable Resistor R"]
    RSER --> CAP["Capacitor C"]
    CAP --> DIA["DIAC (fires at V_BO)"]
    DIA --> GATE["TRIAC Gate (G terminal)"]
    GATE --> TRC["TRIAC Main Terminals (MT1, MT2)"]
    TRC --> LOAD["Load (Lamp or Fan)"]
    LOAD --> SRC
    FB["Feedback: TRIAC shorts, resets C voltage"] --> CAP
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. AC source charges $C$ through $R$.
2. When $V_C \geq V_{BO}$, DIAC fires.
3. Capacitor dumps its charge into the TRIAC's gate.
4. TRIAC turns ON, delivering power to the load.
5. At the next zero-crossing, TRIAC turns OFF, and the cycle restarts.
