# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define breakover voltage. How is it related to the operation of a DIAC?**

**Model Answer:**
Breakover voltage ($V_{BO}$) is the **critical value of the applied voltage** at which a DIAC transitions from its high-impedance blocking state to its low-impedance conducting state. It is the same in magnitude for both polarities (typically 30–50 V for commercial DIACs), which is why the DIAC's V-I characteristic is symmetric about the origin. Once $V_{BO}$ is reached, the inner junction undergoes avalanche breakdown, the device exhibits a brief negative resistance, and it snaps into conduction. This voltage-triggered, polarity-independent behavior is what makes the DIAC ideal for AC phase-control triggering of TRIACs.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the Gunn Effect? Name the device that uses it and state its typical application.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Gunn Effect** is the phenomenon of **negative differential resistance (NDR)** in bulk semiconductors (notably GaAs) caused by the transfer of conduction electrons from a high-mobility, low-energy valley to a low-mobility, high-energy satellite valley when the applied electric field exceeds a critical threshold ($E_{th} \approx 3.2\ \text{kV/cm}$ for GaAs). The device exploiting this effect is the **Gunn Diode**, a two-terminal, bulk-effect, negative-resistance device. Its primary application is as a **solid-state microwave oscillator** in radar systems, microwave communication links, and as a local oscillator in microwave receivers (typical operating frequencies: 1–100 GHz).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and V-I characteristics of a DIAC. How does the symmetry of its V-I curve arise from its construction?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain how a DIAC is used to trigger a TRIAC for phase-angle control of an AC load. Derive the expression for the firing angle in terms of the source voltage, the DIAC breakover voltage, and the RC time constant.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Construction:**
The DIAC is a **two-terminal, five-layer (p-n-p-n-p)** semiconductor device. The two outer layers ($p^+$ and $n^+$) are heavily doped and serve as the main terminals (MT1 and MT2). The central region is a **lightly doped p-layer** sandwiched between two n-layers, forming two complementary junctions J1 (p-n) and J2 (n-p). There is **no gate terminal**, so conduction is purely voltage-controlled.

**Why the symmetry arises:**
The p-n-p and n-p-n halves are **geometrically and electrostatically identical mirror images** of each other. The device is fabricated so that the electric field distribution and breakdown characteristics of J1 and J2 are equal in magnitude for opposite polarities. Consequently, the device exhibits **identical breakover voltages** $+V_{BO}$ and $-V_{BO}$, producing a V-I characteristic that is **odd-symmetric** about the origin. This symmetry is essential for triggering the TRIAC on both half-cycles of an AC waveform.

**V-I Characteristic Description:**
1. For $|V| < V_{BO}$: A small leakage current flows (microampere range); the device is in the blocking state.
2. At $|V| = V_{BO}$: Avalanche breakdown of the central junction; the current rises sharply while voltage falls — the negative-resistance region.
3. Beyond breakover: The device is in the ON state, with a small on-state voltage $V_T \approx 1.5\ \text{V}$ and the current limited by external load.
4. The same sequence is mirrored in the negative half-cycle.

*(For full marks, students should draw a properly labeled V-I curve showing the three regions, label $V_{BO}$, $-V_{BO}$, $V_T$, $-V_T$, and the leakage current.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Circuit Diagram:** A standard DIAC-TRIAC lamp dimmer circuit is drawn (AC source → variable R → C → DIAC in parallel with TRIAC's gate–MT1 → TRIAC MT1-MT2 → load → source).

**Working Principle:**
- The AC source $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ charges the capacitor $C$ through the variable resistor $R$.
- The capacitor voltage $V_C(t)$ rises approximately as $V_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$, where $\tan\phi = \omega RC$.
- The instant $V_C(t)$ reaches the DIAC's breakover voltage $V_{BO}$, the DIAC switches ON.
- The capacitor's stored charge is rapidly dumped into the **TRIAC's gate**, firing the TRIAC.
- The TRIAC now conducts for the remainder of that half-cycle, delivering power to the load.
- At the AC zero-crossing, the TRIAC turns OFF, and the cycle repeats for the next half-cycle.

**Derivation of Firing Angle:**

The capacitor voltage (with respect to the source zero) is:
$$V_C(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t - \phi), \quad \text{where}\ \tan\phi = \omega RC$$

The DIAC fires when $V_C(t) = V_{BO}$:
$$V_m \sin(\omega t - \phi) = V_{BO}$$

Letting $\alpha = \omega t$ be the firing angle:
$$\sin(\alpha - \phi) = \frac{V_{BO}}{V_m}$$

Solving for $\alpha$:
$$\boxed{\alpha = \phi + \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{V_{BO}}{V_m}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:** Varying $R$ changes $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$, which shifts the firing angle $\alpha$. A larger $R$ increases $\phi$, increasing $\alpha$ (later firing), reducing the conduction angle $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$, and thus reducing the power delivered to the load. This is the basis of the **lamp dimmer / fan regulator**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of the two-valley model, explain the operation of a Gunn diode. Define negative differential resistance and show how it arises from inter-valley electron transfer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Sketch and explain the V-I characteristics of a Gunn diode. Define threshold voltage, threshold field, and valley voltage. Mention two applications of Gunn diodes.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Two-Valley Model Explanation:**
The conduction band of GaAs has two principal minima (valleys) in the E-k diagram:
- **Valley 1 (central):** Located at the Brillouin zone center ($\Gamma$ point). Effective mass $m_1^* = 0.067\ m_0$, mobility $\mu_1 \approx 8500\ \text{cm}^2/\text{Vs}$.
- **Valley 2 (satellite):** Located along the $\langle 100 \rangle$ direction. Effective mass $m_2^* = 1.2\ m_0$, mobility $\mu_2 \approx 150\ \text{cm}^2/\texttext{Vs}$, situated at an energy $\Delta E = 0.36\ \text{eV}$ above Valley 1.

At **low electric fields** ($E < E_{th}$), conduction electrons predominantly occupy Valley 1, contributing a high drift velocity. As the field increases to $E_{th} \approx 3.2\ \text{kV/cm}$, electrons gain enough kinetic energy ($\geq \Delta E$) to **transfer into Valley 2**, where their mobility is roughly **50 times lower**. As the field rises further, an increasing population transfers to Valley 2, and the **average drift velocity of the electron ensemble decreases** — this is the origin of the **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)**.

**Definition of NDR:**
Negative differential resistance is the condition in which an increase in the applied voltage results in a **decrease in the current**, i.e., the differential conductivity is negative:
$$\frac{dJ}{dE} = q \cdot n \cdot \frac{dv_d}{dE} = q \cdot n \cdot \mu_d < 0$$

In the NDR region, $\mu_d < 0$. This is a fundamentally non-Ohmic, bulk-semiconductor phenomenon that allows the Gunn diode to sustain **microwave oscillations** when biased into the NDR region with a suitable resonant cavity.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**V-I Characteristic Sketch and Explanation:**

The V-I curve has **four distinct regions**:
1. **Ohmic region** ($0 \le V \le V_{th}$): Linear relationship $I = V/R_0$, with $R_0$ the low-field resistance.
2. **Negative differential resistance region** ($V_{th} < V < V_{valley}$): Current decreases as voltage increases.
3. **Saturation region** ($V > V_{valley}$): All electrons are in Valley 2; current rises slowly.
4. **Breakdown region** (at very high fields): Impact ionization, device damage threshold.

**Key Definitions:**
- **Threshold field $E_{th}$:** The minimum electric field at which significant electron transfer to Valley 2 begins. For GaAs, $E_{th} \approx 3.2\ \text{kV/cm}$.
- **Threshold voltage $V_{th}$:** The voltage across the device at threshold. $V_{th} = E_{th} \cdot L$, typically **3.3 V** for GaAs.
- **Valley voltage $V_{valley}$:** The voltage at which current reaches its minimum (valley point) before rising again. Typically $V_{valley} \approx 2 \cdot V_{th}$.

**Applications of Gunn Diodes:**
1. **Microwave oscillators** — used as a CW source in police radar guns, microwave relay links, and radar local oscillators.
2. **Parametric amplifiers / negative-resistance amplifiers** — exploiting the NDR to provide gain at microwave frequencies.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse the **DIAC's negative-resistance region** (a transient breakdown) with the **Gunn diode's stable NDR region**. Remember: DIAC's NDR is a **brief breakover spike**, while the Gunn diode's NDR is a **sustained DC operating regime**.
> 2. Many students write "$V_{BO} = 30\ \text{V}$" without specifying the **symmetry** ($+V_{BO}$ and $-V_{BO}$). Always mention both polarities.
> 3. In firing-angle derivations, students often forget the phase-lag $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ introduced by the RC network. The firing angle is **not** simply $\sin^{-1}(V_{BO}/V_m)$ — that expression is only the inner part; the full expression includes $\phi$.
> 4. In the two-valley model, students often reverse the mobility values. **Memorize:** Valley 1 (central) = **high mobility, low mass**; Valley 2 (satellite) = **low mobility, high mass**. Swapping these will cost you the CO-mark for the NDR explanation.
> 5. Do **not** confuse "Gunn diode" (two-terminal, bulk effect) with "Gunn transistor" or "Gunn oscillator" (which is the diode plus a resonant cavity). The diode is the active element; the oscillator is the system.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for the DIAC and TEDs Module**

### DIAC — Rapid Recap
- ✅ DIAC = **Diode for AC**; **two terminals, no gate, bidirectional**.
- ✅ Construction: **5-layer (p-n-p-n-p)** or **3-layer equivalent**.
- ✅ Equivalent to **two Zener diodes in anti-series**.
- ✅ Stays OFF until $|V| = V_{BO} \approx 30\text{–}50\ \text{V}$.
- ✅ V-I curve: **three regions — blocking, NDR (breakover), ON state**.
- ✅ V-I curve is **odd-symmetric** about origin ($+V_{BO} = -V_{BO}$ in magnitude).
- ✅ Holding current $I_H \approx 5\text{–}25\ \text{mA}$; on-state voltage $V_T \approx 1.5\ \text{V}$.
- ✅ Primary use: **TRIAC trigger pulse generator** in lamp dimmers, fan regulators, heater controls.
- ✅ Firing angle formula: $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(\omega RC) + \sin^{-1}(V_{BO}/V_m)$.
- ✅ Power delivery to load is controlled by **varying $R$**, which shifts the firing angle.

### Transferred Electron Devices (TEDs) — Rapid Recap
- ✅ TEDs are **bulk (non-junction) devices** exploiting **inter-valley electron transfer**.
- ✅ **Gunn Diode** is the canonical TED; **GaAs** is the canonical material.
- ✅ Band structure: **two valleys** separated by $\Delta E = 0.36\ \text{eV}$ (GaAs).
- ✅ Valley 1 = central, **high mobility** ($\mu_1 \approx 8500\ \text{cm}^2/\text{Vs}$), low effective mass.
- ✅ Valley 2 = satellite, **low mobility** ($\mu_2 \approx 150\ \text{cm}^2/\text{Vs}$), high effective mass.
- ✅ At $E_{th} \approx 3.2\ \text{kV/cm}$ (GaAs), electrons transfer to Valley 2 → **NDR region**.
- ✅ Differential mobility $\mu_d < 0$ in NDR → differential conductivity $\sigma_d < 0$.
- ✅ Threshold voltage $V_{th} = E_{th} \cdot L \approx 3.3\ \text{V}$ for typical GaAs diodes.
- ✅ V-I curve: **Ohmic → NDR → Saturation** regions, with peak-to-valley current ratio **2:1 to 3:1**.
- ✅ Oscillation frequency $f = v_{sat}/L$; for $L = 10\ \text{μm}$, $f \approx 10\ \text{GHz}$.
- ✅ Applications: **microwave oscillators**, **radar**, **local oscillators**, **parametric amplifiers**.
- ✅ Also called **TEDs**, **Gunn oscillators**, **bulk-effect devices** — all refer to the same family.
- ✅ Mechanism named after **J. B. Gunn (1963)**; theoretical framework by **Ridley, Watkins, and Hilsum**.

### Quick Mnemonics for the Board Exam
- **"DIAC = DIscrete AC trigger"** — remembers the device's two-terminal bidirectional nature.
- **"Valley 1 is FAST, Valley 2 is FAT (high mass, low speed)"** — remembers the mobility order.
- **"Gunn = Generation of Undesirable Negative-resistance Noise"** — remembers the NDR essence (and the namesake J. B. Gunn).
- **"$V_{BO} \to \text{Break}$, $\phi \to \text{Phase lag}$, $\alpha = \phi + \text{arcsin}$"** — recalls the firing-angle formula structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the V-I curve of a DIAC, **always label both $+V_{BO}$ and $-V_{BO}$**, the leakage current, $V_T$, and shade the negative-resistance region. When asked about the Gunn diode, **always** mention the **two-valley model first**, then define $E_{th}$, and only then sketch the curve. This order is the standard KTU valuation sequence and guarantees full CO-marks for the topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Electron Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hot Electron Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Hot Electrons – Formal Definition

In a semiconductor at thermal equilibrium, charge carriers possess an average thermal energy of approximately **kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K** (where **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** is Boltzmann's constant and **q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** is the electronic charge). When an electric field **E** is applied across a semiconductor, the carriers (electrons/holes) gain kinetic energy from the field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hot Electrons** are charge carriers whose **kinetic energy (or effective temperature) is significantly greater than the lattice thermal energy (kT)**. They are "hot" because their energy is well above the surrounding crystal lattice temperature. This typically happens when the electric field exceeds **10⁵ V/cm** in materials like GaAs or Si.

The "hotness" is quantified by the **electron temperature Tₑ**:

$$T_e = T_L + \frac{2}{3}\frac{q \tau_e \mu_n E^2}{k}$$

where **T_L** is the lattice temperature, **τₑ** is the energy relaxation time, and **μₙ** is the electron mobility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold field for hot electron regime (Si):** E ≈ 10⁵ V/cm
> **Threshold field for hot electron regime (GaAs):** E ≈ 3 × 10³ V/cm (lower due to smaller effective mass and higher mobility)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Roller Coaster" Picture

Imagine electrons in a semiconductor as marbles rolling on a flat, smooth floor at room temperature — they just shuffle around with mild thermal jitter (≈ 25 mV of energy). This is "cold" electron behavior.

Now, tilt the floor very steeply (apply a large electric field). The marbles start rolling downhill, gaining enormous speed. They become "hot" — much faster than the floor (lattice) can absorb their energy. When these "hot" marbles slam into the lattice or cross into a different energy valley in the crystal, fascinating quantum effects occur:

1. **Velocity Saturation / Overshoot** – Marbles can't go faster than a terminal speed.
2. **Intervalley Transfer** – Marbles jump into a different track (energy valley) where they actually move *slower*.
3. **Impact Ionization** – A fast marble smashes into a bound electron, knocking it free → creates an electron-hole pair.
4. **Quantum Tunneling** – Hot marbles punch through energy barriers that cold marbles could never cross.

These phenomena form the **operational basis of Hot Electron Devices (HEDs)**.

## 1.3 Classification of Hot Electron Devices

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Map – Hot Electron Devices (Module 3):**

| Device | Operating Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Schottky Diode** | Metal-Semiconductor hot-electron injection | RF mixer, detector |
| **Tunnel (Esaki) Diode** | Quantum mechanical tunneling | Microwave oscillator, high-speed switch |
| **Gunn Diode / TED** | Transferred-electron effect (intervalley transfer) | Microwave oscillator (4–100 GHz) |
| **IMPATT Diode** | Impact ionization + Transit-time delay | Microwave power source |
| **TRAPATT Diode** | Trapped plasma + avalanche | Pulsed microwave generation |
| **BARITT Diode** | Barrier injection + transit time | Low-noise microwave source |
| **MESFET** | Schottky gate + field effect | RF amplifier, microwave IC |
| **HEMT** | Modulation-doped 2DEG (heterojunction) | Ultra-high-frequency low-noise amp |

## 1.4 Why Hot Electrons Matter in Analog Circuits

In modern **sub-micron CMOS**, the channel field easily exceeds 10⁵ V/cm within a few tens of nanometers. This causes:

- **Velocity Saturation** → degrades transconductance
- **Hot Carrier Injection (HCI)** → device degradation, threshold voltage shift
- **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)**
- **Subthreshold slope degradation**

Hence, hot-electron physics isn't just a curiosity — it dictates the **reliability and performance limits** of every analog transistor you will design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Velocity–Electric Field Relationship (The Foundation)

In a low-field regime, carrier drift velocity is linear:

$$v_d = \mu E$$

When **E** increases beyond a critical value **E_c** (the **critical/velocity-saturation field**), the drift velocity no longer follows Ohm's law. The empirical Caughey-Thomas model captures this:

$$v_d = \frac{\mu_0 E}{\left(1 + \left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)^{\beta}\right)^{1/\beta}}$$

where **μ₀** is low-field mobility, **E_c = v_sat / μ₀**, and **β ≈ 1 to 2** (a fitting parameter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Hot carriers lose energy by emitting optical phonons. The rate of energy loss balances the rate of energy gain from the field, leading to a steady-state **saturation velocity v_sat**.
> - For **Si**: v_sat ≈ 1 × 10⁷ cm/s
> - For **GaAs**: v_sat ≈ 1 × 10⁷ cm/s
> - For **InGaAs (HEMT channel)**: v_sat ≈ 2.5 × 10⁷ cm/s

## 2.2 Transferred Electron Effect (Gunn Effect)

In direct-bandgap materials like **GaAs**, the conduction band has multiple valleys:

- **Γ valley (Central valley):** low effective mass m* = 0.067 m₀, high mobility μ ≈ 8500 cm²/V·s, low-energy minimum.
- **L valley (Satellite valley):** high effective mass m* = 1.2 m₀, low mobility μ ≈ 180 cm²/V·s, energy offset **ΔE ≈ 0.31 eV** above Γ valley.

When the electric field exceeds the **threshold field E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm**:

1. Electrons in the Γ valley gain enough energy to scatter into the L valley.
2. In the L valley, electrons have a much larger effective mass → lower mobility.
3. Result: **drift velocity DECREASES** as E increases → **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR):** A region in the I–V or v–E curve where the differential quantity is **negative** (dv/dE < 0 or dI/dV < 0). NDR is the operational basis of **Gunn diodes** and **tunnel diodes**.

## 2.3 Quantum Mechanical Tunneling (Tunnel Diode)

In a heavily doped p-n junction (degenerate semiconductor, doping > 10¹⁹ cm⁻³), the depletion region is extremely thin (≈ 10 nm). According to quantum mechanics, electrons have a finite probability of **penetrating** a thin potential barrier even when their energy is less than the barrier height. This is the **tunneling effect**.

For an electron with energy **E** encountering a barrier of height **V₀ > E** and width **d**, the transmission probability is:

$$T \approx \exp\left(-2 \int_0^d \kappa(x) \, dx\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \kappa(x) = \frac{\sqrt{2m^*(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Tunneling is **not** a classical process. It depends exponentially on the **barrier width** and the **square root of barrier height**. Even a 1 nm increase in depletion width can suppress tunneling by orders of magnitude.

## 2.4 Impact Ionization and Avalanche Multiplication

When a hot carrier (electron or hole) gains kinetic energy greater than the **bandgap energy E_g** from the field, it can collide with a bound electron in the valence band and create a new electron-hole pair. This is **impact ionization**.

The ionization coefficient **αₙ** (or **αₚ**) is field-dependent:

$$\alpha_n(E) = A_n \exp\left(-\frac{B_n}{E}\right)$$

Typical values (Si, E ≈ 3×10⁵ V/cm): **αₙ ≈ 10⁴ cm⁻¹**.

In a reverse-biased p-n junction, this process cascades → **avalanche breakdown**. In the IMPATT diode, the impact-ionization avalanche is deliberately coupled to a **transit-time delay** to create NDR at microwave frequencies.

## 2.5 The Metal-Semiconductor Junction (Schottky Contact)

When a metal with work function **Φ_m** contacts an n-type semiconductor with electron affinity **χ_s**, the barrier height is:

$$\Phi_B = \Phi_m - \chi_s$$

The depletion width in a Schottky contact under bias **V** is:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} - V)}{q N_D}}$$

where **V_bi = Φ_B − Vₙ** is the built-in potential, **Vₙ = (kT/q) ln(N_C/N_D)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **MESFET Insight:** The MESFET is essentially a Schottky diode (the gate) integrated with two ohmic contacts (source and drain). The depletion width **W** under the gate **modulates the channel thickness**, controlling the drain current. This is the **field-effect** action of the MESFET.

## 2.6 Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG) and HEMT

A **heterojunction** between two semiconductors with different bandgaps (e.g., AlGaAs / GaAs) creates a band discontinuity. Donors in the wide-bandgap AlGaAs drop their electrons into the narrow-bandgap GaAs, where they get trapped in a **quantum well** at the interface.

These confined electrons form a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** with:

- Extremely high sheet carrier density **n_s ≈ 10¹² cm⁻²**
- Ultra-high mobility **μ ≈ 10⁶ cm²/V·s at 77 K** (vs. ≈ 8500 cm²/V·s in bulk GaAs)
- Zero impurity scattering (donors are physically separated in AlGaAs)

The sheet carrier density is controlled by the gate voltage:

$$n_s(V_G) = \frac{\epsilon}{q(d + \Delta d)}\left(V_G - V_{th}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HEMTs are Superior:** Reduced impurity scattering → higher mobility → higher transconductance → higher cut-off frequency f_T → **f_T > 500 GHz** in modern InP-based HEMTs.

---

## KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers ~80% of numerical questions in KTU Module 3.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hot electron temperature | $T_e = T_L + \dfrac{2 q \tau_e \mu_n E^2}{3 k}$ | $\tau_e$: energy relaxation time | Define hot electron |
| 2 | Velocity-field (Caughey-Thomas) | $v_d = \dfrac{\mu_0 E}{\left[1 + (E/E_c)^{\beta}\right]^{1/\beta}}$ | $E_c = v_{sat}/\mu_0$ | MESFET/HEMT I-V |
| 3 | Tunneling transmission | $T \approx \exp\left(-2\int_0^d \kappa \, dx\right)$ | $\kappa = \sqrt{2m^*(V_0-E)}/\hbar$ | Tunnel diode |
| 4 | Impact ionization coefficient | $\alpha = A \exp(-B/E)$ | $A, B$ material constants | IMPATT diode |
| 5 | Schottky barrier height | $\Phi_B = \Phi_m - \chi_s$ | $\chi_s$: electron affinity | MESFET gate |
| 6 | MESFET threshold voltage | $V_{th} = V_{bi} - \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \epsilon_s}$ | $a$: channel half-thickness | MESFET pinch-off |
| 7 | MESFET saturation drain current | $I_{D,sat} = I_{P}\left[1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{bi}-V_{GS}}{V_P}}\right]$ | $I_P, V_P$: pinchoff parameters | MESFET biasing |
| 8 | MESFET transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{V_{DS}}$ | – | Amplifier design |
| 9 | HEMT 2DEG sheet density | $n_s = \dfrac{\epsilon}{q(d + \Delta d)}(V_G - V_{th})$ | $d$: spacer thickness | HEMT analysis |
| 10 | Transit-time frequency | $f_T = \dfrac{v_{sat}}{2\pi L_g}$ | $L_g$: gate length | Cutoff frequency |
| 11 | Critical field (velocity sat.) | $E_c = \dfrac{v_{sat}}{\mu_0}$ | – | Hot electron onset |
| 12 | Gunn threshold field (GaAs) | $E_{th} \approx 3.2$ kV/cm | – | Gunn diode |
| 13 | Avalanche breakdown voltage | $V_{BR} = \dfrac{\epsilon_s E_{crit}^2}{2 q N_D}$ | $E_{crit}$ ≈ 3×10⁵ V/cm (Si) | IMPATT design |
| 14 | Tunnel diode peak-valley ratio | $PVR = I_P / I_V$ | $I_P$: peak, $I_V$: valley current | Tunnel diode spec |
| 15 | Mean free path of hot electron | $\lambda = v_{th} \tau_e$ | $v_{th}$: thermal velocity | Energy relaxation |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Device | Engineering Field | Specific Use |
|---|---|---|
| Tunnel diode | Microwave + Nuclear physics | Spin precession magnetometer, oscillator up to 100 GHz |
| Gunn diode | Radar, Police speed gun | Doppler radar at 10–94 GHz |
| IMPATT diode | Telecom backbone | mm-wave CW power up to 300 GHz |
| MESFET | RF ICs (legacy) | LNA, mixer in satellite receivers |
| HEMT (pHEMT, mHEMT) | Modern RF/mm-wave | 5G base stations, satellite LNA, radar front-ends |
| Hot-carrier injection | CMOS reliability | Determines MOSFET operating voltage limits |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Negative Differential Mobility in the Transferred-Electron Effect

This is the **central physical derivation** of the Gunn diode. We will show rigorously *why* the differential mobility becomes negative.

### Step 1 — Two-Valley Conduction Band Picture

In GaAs, the conduction band minimum (the **Γ valley**) lies at **k = 0**. The first satellite valley is the **L valley** at the Brillouin-zone edge, at energy **ΔE ≈ 0.31 eV** above Γ. Each valley has its own:

- Effective density of states $N_C^{\Gamma}$ and $N_C^{L}$
- Effective mass $m_\Gamma^*$ and $m_L^*$
- Mobility $\mu_\Gamma$ and $\mu_L$

### Step 2 — Electron Population Distribution (Boltzmann Statistics)

Let **n₁** = number of electrons in the Γ valley and **n₂** = number in the L valley. The ratio at any field is governed by Boltzmann statistics:

$$\frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{N_C^L}{N_C^\Gamma} \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{kT_e}\right)$$

where **Tₑ** is the **electron temperature** (which depends on the field E).

### Step 3 — Effective Drift Velocity

The total drift velocity is the population-weighted average:

$$v_d = \frac{n_1 \mu_\Gamma + n_2 \mu_L}{n_1 + n_2} E$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by **n₁**:

$$v_d = \frac{\mu_\Gamma + R \, \mu_L}{1 + R} E \quad \text{where} \quad R = \frac{N_C^L}{N_C^\Gamma} \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{kT_e}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Threshold Condition

At low fields (**E → 0**), **Tₑ → T_L = 300 K**, and since **kT = 0.026 eV ≪ ΔE = 0.31 eV**, the exponential term is tiny → **R ≈ 0** → **v_d ≈ μ_Γ E** (linear, ohmic).

As **E** increases, **Tₑ** rises, and eventually **R → 1** when **Tₑ ≈ ΔE/k ≈ 3600 K**. At this point roughly half the electrons are in the heavy-mass L valley, and the **average mobility drops sharply**.

### Step 5 — Computing the Differential Mobility

Differential mobility is defined as:

$$\mu_d = \frac{dv_d}{dE}$$

Since **v_d** contains **E** both explicitly and implicitly (via **Tₑ**), we apply the chain rule:

$$\mu_d = \frac{\partial v_d}{\partial E} + \frac{\partial v_d}{\partial T_e} \cdot \frac{dT_e}{dE}$$

Substituting the expression for **v_d** and simplifying (assume **Tₑ ∝ E²** for energy balance):

$$\mu_d = \frac{\mu_\Gamma + R \mu_L}{1 + R} + \frac{(\mu_\Gamma - \mu_L) R}{(1 + R)^2} \cdot \frac{d(\ln R)}{d(\ln T_e)} \cdot \frac{d(\ln T_e)}{d(\ln E)}$$

The second term is **negative** when **μ_Γ > μ_L** (true for GaAs). The crossover where **μ_d = 0** defines the **threshold field E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm** in GaAs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Above E_th, **dv_d/dE < 0** → **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)**. This is the operational basis of the Gunn diode / Transferred Electron Device (TED).

## 3.2 Derivation: MESFET Saturation Current (Shockley Model)

### Step 1 — Geometry Setup

Consider an n-channel MESFET with channel thickness **a**, gate length **L**, channel width **Z**. Let the depletion region under the gate extend a distance **h(x)** from the top of the channel at position **x** along the channel.

### Step 2 — Channel Charge Density

The charge per unit area in the conducting part of the channel:

$$Q_n(x) = q N_D [a - h(x)]$$

### Step 3 — Gradual Channel Approximation

The drain current is the integral of charge times velocity:

$$I_D = q N_D Z [a - h(x)] \mu_n \frac{dV(x)}{dx}$$

### Step 4 — Depletion Width Relation

From the depletion approximation in a uniformly doped semiconductor:

$$h(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s [V_{bi} - V_{GS} + V(x)]}{q N_D}}$$

### Step 5 — Substituting h(x) and Integrating

After substitution and integration from **x = 0** (source, V=0) to **x = L** (drain, V = V_DS):

$$I_D = \frac{Z \mu_n \epsilon_s}{L a} \left[(V_{bi} - V_{GS}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} - \frac{2}{3a}\sqrt{\frac{2\epsilon_s}{qN_D}}\left[(V_{bi}-V_{GS}+V_{DS})^{3/2} - (V_{bi}-V_{GS})^{3/2}\right]\right]$$

### Step 6 — Pinchoff and Saturation

Pinchoff occurs when **a − h(L) = 0**, i.e., **h(L) = a**. This gives the **pinchoff voltage**:

$$V_P = \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \epsilon_s}$$

For **V_DS > V_GS − V_bi + V_P**, the channel pinches off near the drain. The **saturation drain current** is (after further algebra):

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{Z \mu_n \epsilon_s}{2a L} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} \right]^2 \left[ 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_P}} \right]$$

where the **threshold voltage** is:

$$V_{th} = V_{bi} - V_P = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \epsilon_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classical Shockley MESFET model** — a guaranteed KTU problem. Memorize the form of **I_{D,sat}** and the meaning of **V_P**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A GaAs MESFET has the following parameters:
- Channel doping **N_D = 1 × 10¹⁷ cm⁻³**
- Channel half-thickness **a = 0.15 μm**
- Built-in potential **V_bi = 0.8 V**
- Dielectric constant **ε_s = 13.1 × ε₀**, ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹⁴ F/cm
- μₙ = 6000 cm²/V·s
- Z = 100 μm, L = 1 μm

**Find:** (i) Pinchoff voltage, (ii) Threshold voltage, (iii) Saturation current for V_GS = 0 V.

### Solution

**(i) Pinchoff voltage:**

$$V_P = \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \epsilon_s}$$

$$\epsilon_s = 13.1 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} = 1.16 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}$$

$$V_P = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1 \times 10^{17})(0.15 \times 10^{-4})^2}{2 \times 1.16 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$V_P = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1 \times 10^{17})(2.25 \times 10^{-10})}{2.32 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$V_P = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-12}}{2.32 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.55 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Threshold voltage:**

$$V_{th} = V_{bi} - V_P = 0.8 - 1.55 = -0.75 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Saturation current at V_GS = 0:**

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{Z \mu_n \epsilon_s}{2 a L} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} \right]^2 \left[ 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_P}} \right]$$

$$= \frac{(100 \times 10^{-4})(6000)(1.16 \times 10^{-12})}{2 \times (0.15 \times 10^{-4})(1 \times 10^{-4})} \left[0 - (-0.75)\right]^2 \left[1 - \sqrt{\frac{0.8 - 0}{1.55}}\right]$$

$$= \frac{6.96 \times 10^{-10}}{3 \times 10^{-9}} (0.5625)\left[1 - \sqrt{0.516}\right]$$

$$= 0.232 \times 0.5625 \times [1 - 0.7185]$$

$$= 0.1305 \times 0.2815 = 0.0367 \text{ A} = 36.7 \text{ mA}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When V_GS = 0 and V_{th} < 0 (depletion-mode MESFET, which is normal for GaAs), the device is **normally-on**. This is the **standard GaAs MESFET** — KTU loves asking "Is your MESFET normally-on or normally-off?"

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Hot Electron Device Classification – Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HOT ELECTRON DEVICES]:::root
    A --> B[Two-Terminal Devices]:::branch
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Module 3, KTU Pattern)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEICT413]**
**Define hot electrons. How does the electron temperature differ from the lattice temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hot electrons are charge carriers in a semiconductor whose kinetic energy (or effective temperature Tₑ) is significantly greater than the lattice thermal energy kT. In thermal equilibrium, the lattice and carriers share the same temperature. Under a strong electric field, carriers gain energy from the field faster than they can dissipate it to the lattice via phonon emission. This imbalance creates two distinct "temperatures": the lattice remains at **T_L ≈ 300 K** while the electron gas reaches **Tₑ > T_L**. The relation is:

$$T_e = T_L + \frac{2 q \tau_e \mu_n E^2}{3 k}$$

where **τₑ** is the energy relaxation time (typically 1 ps in GaAs). **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for T_e concept, 1 mark for equation + physical explanation)**

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEICT413]**
**What is the transferred electron effect? Mention the material in which it is observed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **transferred electron effect** (Gunn effect) is the phenomenon where electrons in a direct-bandgap semiconductor transfer from a low-energy, low-effective-mass conduction band valley (Γ valley) to a higher-energy, high-effective-mass valley (L valley) when the applied electric field exceeds a threshold. In the L valley, electrons have lower mobility, so the average drift velocity **decreases** as the field increases — producing **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)**.

It is observed in **direct-bandgap III–V compounds** like **GaAs, InP, CdTe, ZnSe** — materials with multiple conduction band valleys separated by small energy differences (ΔE ≈ 0.31 eV in GaAs). **(1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark material with example)**

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (KTU ESE Style, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. You attempt **ONE full question** (either Q A OR Q B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**Q A(a) [7 marks]:** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the construction, working principle, and V–I characteristics of the **Gunn diode**. Discuss the role of the threshold field.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
The Gunn diode is a simple two-terminal device made of a single piece of n-type GaAs (or InP) with ohmic contacts on both ends. It has no p-n junction. The active region length is typically **L = 5–100 μm** with doping **N_D = 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ cm⁻³**.

**Energy Band Diagram and Working Principle (3 marks):**
GaAs has two conduction band valleys:
- **Γ valley** at energy 0, with effective mass m* = 0.067 m₀, high mobility μ = 8500 cm²/V·s.
- **L valley** at energy ΔE = 0.31 eV above Γ, with effective mass m* = 1.2 m₀, low mobility μ = 180 cm²/V·s.

At low applied fields (E < 3.2 kV/cm), all conduction electrons remain in the Γ valley → drift velocity v_d = μ_Γ·E (linear, ohmic region).

When E exceeds the **threshold field E_th ≈ 3.2 kV/cm**, electrons gain enough energy from the field to scatter into the L valley. In the L valley, their mobility drops by a factor of ~50, so the **average drift velocity decreases** with increasing field. This produces **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)**: dv_d/dE < 0.

**V–I Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Region 1 (0 to V_th): v ∝ E (linear, ohmic, positive resistance).
- Region 2 (V_th to V_valley): v decreases as E increases (NDR region).
- Region 3 (V > V_valley): velocity saturates at v_sat ≈ 10⁷ cm/s.
- The corresponding I–V curve shows a peak current **I_P** at V_th and a valley current **I_V** at V_valley. Between V_th and V_valley, **dI/dV < 0** (NDR). This NDR enables the device to function as a microwave oscillator when placed in a resonant cavity.

**Q A(b) [7 marks]:** Explain the **Gunn domain formation** and the various modes of operation of a Gunn diode. Mention two applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Gunn Domain Formation (4 marks):**
When a dc bias above threshold is applied, any local fluctuation in carrier density (e.g., due to doping non-uniformity or random noise) creates a **dipole domain**:
1. A small accumulation of electrons forms a negatively charged region.
2. A corresponding depletion region (positive charge from ionized donors) forms adjacent to it.
3. The electric field inside the domain **E_in** becomes much higher than outside **E_out**.
4. Inside the domain, electrons have already transferred to the L valley → low velocity.
5. Outside the domain, electrons are still in the Γ valley → high velocity.
6. The domain travels from cathode to anode at the (slower) L-valley drift velocity ≈ 10⁷ cm/s.
7. When the domain reaches the anode, it collapses, a new domain nucleates at the cathode, and the cycle repeats.
8. This periodic current oscillation has a period **T = L/v_d**, giving oscillation frequency **f = v_d / L** (typically 4–100 GHz for L = 100–5 μm).

**Modes of Operation (2 marks):**
- **Gunn Oscillation Mode (Transit-Time Mode):** f = v_d / L (most common)
- **LSA Mode (Limited Space-Charge Accumulation):** Bias pulsed, domain not fully formed; high efficiency
- **Quenched & Delayed Modes:** Domain formation suppressed for frequency multiplication

**Applications (1 mark):**
1. Continuous-wave microwave oscillator in **police Doppler radar** (X-band, 10.5 GHz)
2. Oscillator in **collision-avoidance radar** for automotive safety
3. **Local oscillator** in mm-wave receivers
4. Pump source in **parametric amplifiers**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**Q B(a) [7 marks]:** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the construction and working of a **MESFET**. Derive an expression for the **pinchoff voltage** and the **saturation drain current**.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A MESFET (Metal-Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor) consists of:
- An **n-type GaAs channel** of thickness **a** (typically 0.1–0.3 μm) with doping **N_D ≈ 10¹⁷ cm⁻³**.
- A **Schottky metal gate** (e.g., Ti/Pt/Au) on top forming a rectifying contact with built-in potential **V_bi ≈ 0.8 V**.
- **Ohmic source and drain** contacts at the two ends of the channel.
- A **semi-insulating (SI) GaAs substrate** below for isolation.

**Working (2 marks):**
- With **V_GS = 0** and **V_bi > 0**, a depletion region of width **W** forms under the gate.
- Applying a **reverse bias** to the gate increases **W**, narrowing the conducting channel.
- When **W = a** (channel fully depleted), the device is **pinched off**.
- Drain current flows when **V_DS > 0**: electrons travel from source to drain; their flow is **modulated by the gate voltage** through field effect.

**Derivation of Pinchoff Voltage (2 marks):**
The depletion width under the gate:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS})}{q N_D}}$$

Pinchoff occurs when **W = a**:

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s (V_{bi} - V_{GS,P})}{q N_D}}$$

Solving:

$$V_{GS,P} = V_{bi} - \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \epsilon_s} \equiv V_{th}$$

The pinchoff voltage (the voltage required to pinch off from V_GS = 0):

$$V_P = \frac{q N_D a^2}{2 \epsilon_s}$$

**Saturation Drain Current (1 mark):**
From the Shockley model, the saturation drain current is:

$$I_{D,sat} = I_P \left[1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{bi} - V_{GS}}{V_P}}\right]$$

where $I_P = \dfrac{Z \mu_n \epsilon_s}{2 a L} V_P^2$ is the maximum current at **V_GS = 0** (full channel).

**Q B(b) [7 marks]:** Explain the construction and working of a **HEMT (High Electron Mobility Transistor)**. Why is its 2DEG mobility so high? Compare MESFET and HEMT.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A HEMT uses a **heterojunction** between two semiconductors with different bandgaps:
- **AlGaAs** (wide bandgap, E_g ≈ 1.8 eV) — doped with donors (n⁺)
- **GaAs** (narrow bandgap, E_g = 1.42 eV) — undoped channel layer
- A thin **undoped AlGaAs spacer** (≈ 3 nm) sits between them to prevent dopant diffusion into the channel.
- On top: a **Schottky metal gate** (Ti/Al).
- Below: a **superlattice buffer** (e.g., AlGaAs/GaAs alternating layers) to trap dislocations.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
1. Donors in n⁺-AlGaAs ionize and drop their free electrons into the lower-energy GaAs layer.
2. At the heterointerface, a **quantum well** forms due to the conduction band discontinuity (ΔE_c ≈ 0.3 eV).
3. Electrons are confined to a thin (~10 nm) sheet at the interface → **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)**.
4. Gate voltage modulates the depth of the quantum well and hence the 2DEG density n_s.
5. Drain current flows as electrons drift through the 2DEG channel.

**Why is 2DEG mobility so high? (2 marks):**
1. **Spatial separation of dopants and carriers:** The donors are physically in the AlGaAs layer, while the electrons are in the undoped GaAs. This **eliminates ionized-impurity scattering** — the dominant mobility-limiting mechanism at low temperatures.
2. **No dopant ions in the channel:** The undoped GaAs has no charged defects to scatter carriers.
3. **Quantum confinement:** Carriers are squeezed into a 2D sheet, which further reduces phonon scattering.
4. **Reduced surface scattering:** The 2DEG is buried below the gate, away from surface traps.

Result: Mobility **μ ≈ 10⁶ cm²/V·s at 77 K** (vs. 8500 cm²/V·s in doped GaAs at 300 K). The corresponding **transit frequency f_T > 500 GHz** in modern InP HEMTs.

**Comparison: MESFET vs HEMT (1 mark):**

| Parameter | MESFET | HEMT |
|---|---|---|
| Gate structure | Schottky (metal-semiconductor) | Schottky on heterojunction |
| Channel | Doped GaAs | Undoped GaAs (2DEG) |
| Mobility | 6000 cm²/V·s (300 K) | 10⁶ cm²/V·s (77 K), 8000 cm²/V·s (300 K) |
| Noise figure | Higher | Lower |
| f_T | 50–100 GHz | 500+ GHz |
| Application | RF amplifiers, mixers | 5G base stations, satellite LNA |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify "GaAs"** when discussing the Gunn effect. It is observed ONLY in direct-bandgap materials with multiple valleys. Si and Ge **do not** show the transferred electron effect.
> 2. **Confusing "MESFET is normally-off."** A standard GaAs MESFET with V_bi > V_P has a **negative threshold voltage** → it is **normally-ON** (depletion-mode). Only a specific design makes it normally-OFF.
> 3. **Writing "HEMT uses a p-n junction."** HEMT uses a **heterojunction** (e.g., AlGaAs/GaAs) — not a p-n junction. This is the most common wrong answer in KTU valuations.
> 4. **Mixing up Tunnel diode and IMPATT diode principles.** Tunnel diode = quantum tunneling; IMPATT = impact ionization + transit time. They are different.
> 5. **In derivations, skipping the assumption of "gradual channel approximation"** before writing the MESFET current equation. State the assumption explicitly.
> 6. **Drawing the v-E curve of Gunn diode with the wrong threshold field.** For GaAs, E_th ≈ **3.2 kV/cm**, not 32 kV/cm or 0.32 kV/cm.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision: Memorize this section before walking into the exam hall.**

- ☐ **Hot electron:** Carrier energy >> kT; occurs when **E > 10⁵ V/cm** (Si) or **> 3 kV/cm** (GaAs).
- ☐ **Electron temperature Tₑ > lattice temperature T_L** under high fields.
- ☐ **Velocity-field curve:** linear at low E, saturates at v_sat ≈ 10⁷ cm/s for both Si and GaAs.
- ☐ **Two-valley model of GaAs:** Γ valley (low m*, high μ) and L valley (high m*, low μ) separated by **ΔE = 0.31 eV**.
- ☐ **Transferred electron effect:** Transfer from Γ → L valley when E > **3.2 kV/cm** → **NDR**.
- ☐ **Gunn diode:** Bulk GaAs, no junction, ohmic contacts, oscillates at **f = v_d / L**.
- ☐ **Modes:** Transit-time, LSA, quenched, delayed.
- ☐ **Tunnel (Esaki) diode:** Heavily doped p-n, quantum tunneling, exhibits **NDR**, peak-valley ratio **PVR = I_P / I_V**.
- ☐ **Schottky barrier:** $\Phi_B = \Phi_m - \chi_s$. Rectifying contact.
- ☐ **MESFET:** GaAs channel, Schottky gate, normally-ON, depletion-mode operation.
- ☐ **MESFET pinchoff voltage:** $V_P = \dfrac{q N_D a^2}{2 \epsilon_s}$
- ☐ **MESFET I_{D,sat}:** $I_{D,sat} = I_P \left[1 - \sqrt{(V_{bi}-V_{GS})/V_P}\right]$
- ☐ **HEMT:** AlGaAs/GaAs heterojunction, **2DEG** at interface, ultra-high mobility (10⁶ cm²/V·s at 77 K).
- ☐ **2DEG sheet density:** $n_s = \dfrac{\epsilon}{q(d+\Delta d)}(V_G - V_{th})$
- ☐ **Why HEMT is superior:** spatial separation of dopants and carriers eliminates impurity scattering.
- ☐ **IMPATT diode:** Impact ionization + transit-time delay → NDR at mm-wave frequencies.
- ☐ **TRAPATT:** Trapped plasma avalanche triggered transit (pulsed high-power microwave).
- ☐ **BARITT:** Barrier injection transit time (low-noise microwave source).
- ☐ **Schottky diode:** Metal-semiconductor junction with $\Phi_B = \Phi_m - \chi_s$.
- ☐ **MESFET I-V derivation requires Gradual Channel Approximation (GCA).**
- ☐ **Modern HEMT (mHEMT, pHEMT, InP-based) achieves f_T > 500 GHz.**
- ☐ **Hot electron effects in short-channel MOSFETs:** velocity saturation, HCI, DIBL.

topics.title: Varactor Diode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Varactor Diode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Varactor Diode** (also called a **Varicap Diode**, **Tuning Diode**, or **Epicap Diode**) is a specially fabricated, heavily doped p-n junction diode whose junction capacitance varies in a controlled and predictable manner as a function of the reverse-bias voltage applied across it. The varactor is **always operated in the reverse-biased region** and is designed so that the **depletion-layer (space-charge) capacitance** is the dominant electrical effect, while the forward conduction is suppressed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** A varactor diode is a voltage-controlled variable-capacitance diode in which the capacitance of the depletion region changes with the applied reverse-bias voltage, exploiting the non-linear junction capacitance of a reverse-biased p-n junction.

Mathematically, the device behaves as a voltage-dependent capacitor:

$$C_j = \frac{dQ}{dV_R}$$

where $Q$ is the stored charge in the depletion region and $V_R$ is the reverse-bias voltage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **stretchable rubber membrane** (the depletion region) stretched across a circular ring (the junction area). The two conducting plates on either side of the membrane act like a parallel-plate capacitor. Now, when you pull the membrane *wider* (i.e., increase the reverse bias), the distance between the two conducting surfaces increases — and a parallel-plate capacitor with a **larger plate separation stores less charge**, i.e., its **capacitance decreases**.

So a varactor is essentially an **"electrically stretchable capacitor"**:
- **Higher reverse voltage** → depletion region widens → **capacitance decreases**
- **Lower reverse voltage** (or zero) → depletion region narrows → **capacitance increases**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy (water tap analogy):** Think of the varactor as a *valve* whose "flow" (capacitance) is controlled by how much you *tighten* (reverse-bias) it. The tighter you turn the knob, the narrower the channel and the lesser the capacitance. No current flows through the valve (just like no current flows in reverse bias), but the *capacity* changes.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Reverse Breakdown Voltage ($V_{BR}$):** Typically **15 V to 90 V** for commercial varactors.
- **Capacitance Range ($C_j$):** Typically **5 pF to 500 pF** depending on voltage.
- **Capacitance Ratio ($C_{max}/C_{min}$):** Usually between **2:1 and 15:1** for a given voltage range.
- **Cut-off Frequency ($f_c$ or $f_{co}$):** Typically in the **GHz range** (modern varactors exceed 100 GHz).
- **Series Resistance ($R_S$):** A few ohms (this is what limits the cut-off frequency).
- **Built-in Potential ($V_{bi}$):** Around **0.5 V to 0.9 V** for silicon, depending on doping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike a regular signal diode, a varactor is *never* forward-biased in normal operation. Reverse bias is the *intended* mode of operation — the varactor's terminals must NEVER see forward voltage in a functional circuit.

## 1.4 Why is the Varactor Important?

The varactor replaces mechanical variable capacitors (trimmer capacitors) in many high-frequency circuits, providing:
- **Electronic tunability** (no moving parts).
- **Smaller size** and **higher reliability**.
- **Faster tuning speed** (microseconds vs. mechanical).
- **Remote / automatic tuning** capability (used in PLL and AFC loops).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Junction Capacitance

When a p-n junction is reverse-biased, mobile charge carriers (holes in p-side and electrons in n-side) are pushed *away* from the junction, leaving behind a region of **immobile ionized dopants** — the **depletion region (W)**. This region is depleted of free carriers and acts as a **dielectric** between two conducting regions (the neutral p and n materials on either side).

The structure is geometrically identical to a **parallel-plate capacitor**:
- **Plate area** = junction cross-sectional area ($A$).
- **Plate separation** = depletion width ($W$).
- **Dielectric** = semiconductor material (permittivity $\varepsilon_s$).

Hence, the junction capacitance is:

$$C_j = \frac{\varepsilon_s A}{W}$$

where $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ is the semiconductor permittivity.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Voltage Dependence

The depletion width $W$ of an abrupt p-n junction is given by:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q} \left( \frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D} \right)}$$

For an **abrupt junction** with one side heavily doped (e.g., $N_A \gg N_D$), this simplifies to:

$$W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q N_D}}$$

Substituting this $W$ into $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W$:

$$C_j = \frac{\varepsilon_s A}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q N_D}}} = A \sqrt{\frac{q N_D \varepsilon_s}{2 (V_{bi} + V_R)}}$$

Defining the **zero-bias capacitance** $C_0 = A \sqrt{\dfrac{q N_D \varepsilon_s}{2 V_{bi}}}$ we get:

$$\boxed{C_j = \frac{C_0}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}}}}$$

This is the **fundamental varactor equation** for an abrupt junction. The exponent is $n = 1/2$ for an abrupt junction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note on Grading Coefficient:** A graded junction (linearly graded profile) has a different exponent:
> - **Abrupt junction:** $C_j \propto (V_{bi} + V_R)^{-1/2}$ → $n = 0.5$
> - **Linearly graded junction:** $C_j \propto (V_{bi} + V_R)^{-1/3}$ → $n = 0.33$
> - **Hyper-abrupt junction:** $C_j \propto (V_{bi} + V_R)^{-1}$ to $(V_{bi} + V_R)^{-2}$ → $n = 1$ to $2$ (used for wide-range VCOs).

The **general varactor equation** is:

$$C_j = \frac{C_0}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}\right)^n}$$

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Operation

1. **Doping profile selection:** A varactor is fabricated with a **non-uniform doping** (often a hyper-abrupt profile) to enhance the capacitance variation with voltage.
2. **Reverse-bias application:** The cathode (n-side) is held at a higher potential than the anode (p-side), with $|V_R| < V_{BR}$.
3. **Depletion region widens:** As $|V_R|$ increases, more ionized acceptors/donors are uncovered, increasing $W$.
4. **Capacitance reduces:** Since $C_j \propto 1/W$, $C_j$ decreases monotonically.
5. **Tuning effect:** The varactor is typically placed in a resonant LC tank. Its variable capacitance changes the resonant frequency:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \, C_j(V_R)}}$$

This is the **electronic tuning principle** used in TV tuners, VCOs, and PLLs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $C_j = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s A}{W}$ | Junction capacitance (parallel-plate model) | Farads (F) |
| 2 | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q N_D}}$ | Depletion width (abrupt, one-sided) | metres (m) |
| 3 | $C_j = \dfrac{C_0}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}\right)^n}$ | **General varactor equation** | $C_0$: zero-bias capacitance; $n$: grading coefficient |
| 4 | $C_0 = A \sqrt{\dfrac{q N_D \varepsilon_s}{2 V_{bi}}}$ | Zero-bias capacitance | Farads (F) |
| 5 | $n = 0.5$ | Abrupt junction exponent | dimensionless |
| 6 | $n = 0.33$ | Linearly graded junction exponent | dimensionless |
| 7 | $n = 1$ to $2$ | Hyper-abrupt junction exponent | dimensionless (VCO use) |
| 8 | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_j}}$ | Resonant frequency of LC tank | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_S C_j}$ | Cut-off frequency of the varactor | Hertz (Hz) |
| 10 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f R_S C_j}$ | Quality factor of the varactor | dimensionless |
| 11 | Tuning range: $\dfrac{f_{max}}{f_{min}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C_{j,max}}{C_{j,min}}}$ | Frequency tuning ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | Capacitance ratio: $K = \dfrac{C_{j,max}}{C_{j,min}} = \left(1 + \dfrac{V_{BR}}{V_{bi}}\right)^n$ | Maximum capacitance swing | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside the table cells — it would break the markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation in math expressions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VCO (Voltage-Controlled Oscillator):** Converts a control voltage into a frequency — used in phase-locked loops, function generators, and frequency synthesizers.
- **TV / Radio Tuners:** Replaces mechanical ganged capacitors with electronic tuning.
- **Automatic Frequency Control (AFC):** The varactor capacitance is dynamically adjusted to keep a receiver locked onto a station.
- **Parametric Amplifiers:** Use the varactor's time-varying capacitance to amplify weak microwave signals with very low noise (maser-like behaviour).
- **Frequency Multipliers:** Driven into non-linear capacitance operation to generate harmonics.
- **Low-Noise Microwave Mixers:** Used in radar and satellite receivers.
- **Voltage-Tuned Filters:** Bandpass/band-reject filters with electronically adjustable cut-off frequencies.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Junction Capacitance from Poisson's Equation

We start with **Poisson's equation in one dimension** for the depletion region on the lightly-doped n-side (assuming $N_A \gg N_D$, so most depletion is on the n-side):

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

where $\rho(x) = +q N_D$ is the charge density (ionized donors) on the n-side.

**Step 1: First integration**

Integrate once with respect to $x$, using the boundary condition that the electric field is zero at the edge of the depletion region ($x = W$):

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = E(x) = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(W - x)$$

**Step 2: Second integration**

Integrate $E(x)$ across the depletion region. The total voltage drop is $V_{bi} + V_R$ (built-in + applied reverse bias):

$$V_{bi} + V_R = \int_0^W E(x)\, dx = \int_0^W \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(W - x)\, dx$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bi} + V_R &= \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} \left[ Wx - \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_0^W \\
&= \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} \left( W^2 - \frac{W^2}{2} \right) \\
&= \frac{q N_D W^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $W$**

$$\boxed{W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q N_D}}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $W$ into $C_j = \varepsilon_s A / W$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_j &= \frac{\varepsilon_s A}{W} = \frac{\varepsilon_s A}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q N_D}}} \\
&= A \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_s q N_D}{2 (V_{bi} + V_R)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Identify zero-bias capacitance**

At $V_R = 0$:

$$C_0 = A \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_s q N_D}{2 V_{bi}}}$$

**Step 7: Final form**

$$\boxed{C_j(V_R) = \frac{C_0}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** As $V_R \to \infty$, $C_j \to 0$ ✓ (wider depletion = less capacitance). At $V_R = 0$, $C_j = C_0$ ✓ (maximum capacitance). The result passes the physical sanity test.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cut-off Frequency of a Varactor

The varactor has a parasitic series resistance $R_S$ (from the bulk neutral semiconductor regions and ohmic contacts). This resistance, in series with the ideal junction capacitance $C_j$, forms an **RC low-pass filter**. The cut-off frequency is:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_j}$$

**Step-by-step justification:**

1. At low frequencies, the varactor looks like a pure capacitor.
2. As frequency rises, the series resistance $R_S$ becomes comparable to the capacitive reactance $X_C = 1 / (2\pi f C_j)$.
3. The cut-off is defined where $\vert X_C \vert = R_S$, i.e.:

$$\frac{1}{2\pi f_c C_j} = R_S$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_j}$$

4. The **Quality factor** is then:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2\pi f R_S C_j} = \frac{f_c}{f}$$

The varactor is *useful* only when $Q \gg 1$, i.e., when operating well below $f_c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Note:** When asked to compute the cut-off frequency, students often forget that $C_j$ itself is voltage-dependent. The examiner expects you to use $C_j$ at the *operating point*, not at zero bias.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tuning Range of a Varactor-Tuned Resonator

A varactor is placed in parallel with an inductor $L$ in an LC tank. The resonant frequency is:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_j}}$$

As $V_R$ varies from $0$ to $V_{BR}$, $C_j$ varies from $C_0$ to $C_{min} = C_0/(1 + V_{BR}/V_{bi})^n$.

- **Maximum resonant frequency** (minimum $C_j$):

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_{min}}}$$

- **Minimum resonant frequency** (maximum $C_j$):

$$f_{min} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_0}}$$

- **Tuning range ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{max}}{f_{min}} &= \sqrt{\frac{C_0}{C_{min}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\left(1 + \frac{V_{BR}}{V_{bi}}\right)^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, with $V_{bi} = 0.7$ V, $V_{BR} = 30$ V, and an abrupt junction ($n = 0.5$):

$$\frac{f_{max}}{f_{min}} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{30}{0.7}}^{0.5} = (43.86)^{0.5} \approx 6.62$$

A **frequency tuning range** of about **6.6 : 1** is achieved.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A silicon varactor has $C_0 = 80$ pF at zero bias, built-in voltage $V_{bi} = 0.7$ V, and grading coefficient $n = 0.5$ (abrupt junction). Find the junction capacitance at a reverse bias of $V_R = 10$ V. Also calculate the corresponding resonant frequency when placed in parallel with $L = 10$ nH. (Assume $R_S$ is negligible.)

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Junction capacitance at $V_R = 10$ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_j &= \frac{C_0}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}\right)^n} \\
&= \frac{80 \text{ pF}}{\left(1 + \dfrac{10}{0.7}\right)^{0.5}} \\
&= \frac{80}{\sqrt{15.286}} \\
&= \frac{80}{3.910} \\
&\approx 20.46 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Resonant frequency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_j}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(10 \times 10^{-9})(20.46 \times 10^{-12})}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{2.046 \times 10^{-19}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi (4.523 \times 10^{-10})} \\
&\approx 3.52 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} \\
&\approx 352 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $C_j \approx 20.5$ pF and $f_r \approx 352$ MHz.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Students frequently forget to take the square root in the denominator when $n = 0.5$ and write $(1 + V_R/V_{bi})^{0.5}$ as $(1 + V_R/V_{bi})^{1/2}$ directly, which is correct, but then mistakenly square the entire expression. Always re-derive the exponent carefully.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Abrupt Junction | Linearly Graded | Hyper-Abrupt |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|--------------|
| Grading coefficient $n$ | 0.5 | 0.33 | 1 to 2 |
| $C_j$ vs $V_R$ slope | Moderate | Mild | Very steep |
| Tuning range | Low–medium | Low | **Very high** (best for VCOs) |
| Linearity | Poor | Fair | **Poor** (highly non-linear) |
| Typical application | General tuning | Low-frequency | **Wide-range VCOs** |
| Fabrication complexity | Simple | Moderate | Complex (epitaxial) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition Question [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q: Define a varactor diode. Why is it always operated in reverse bias?**

**Model Answer:**

A varactor diode is a specially fabricated p-n junction diode whose junction capacitance varies as a function of the reverse-bias voltage applied across it. It is also called a varicap or tuning diode.

It is always operated in reverse bias because:
1. The depletion region width (and hence the capacitance) is **controllable** by the reverse-bias voltage.
2. In forward bias, the diode conducts heavily and the **diffusion capacitance** dominates, which is not voltage-controlled in a useful, predictable way.
3. In reverse bias, the diode draws **negligible DC current**, making the device behave as a **pure voltage-dependent capacitor** with low loss.

> [!NOTE]
> The hallmark of a varactor is that its junction capacitance is monotonically and predictably dependent on the reverse-bias voltage, with negligible conduction current.

### Question A2: Short-Answer Application Question [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: List any three applications of a varactor diode and mention the property of the varactor that makes it suitable for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Application | Key Property Used |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) | Voltage-variable junction capacitance tunes the LC tank frequency. |
| TV/Radio Electronic Tuner | Replaces bulky mechanical variable capacitors with an electronically tunable, small, reliable capacitor. |
| Parametric Amplifier | The non-linear, time-varying reactance of the varactor can transfer energy from a pump source to the signal, achieving low-noise amplification. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option A)

### Question A: Varactor Diode Comprehensive Problem [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the junction capacitance of an abrupt p-n junction diode as a function of applied reverse-bias voltage. Clearly state the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon varactor diode has zero-bias capacitance $C_0 = 100$ pF and built-in potential $V_{bi} = 0.7$ V. If it is operated in an LC tank circuit with $L = 5$ nH, calculate:
   (i) The junction capacitance when $V_R = 5$ V. (Assume abrupt junction, $n = 0.5$.)
   (ii) The corresponding resonant frequency.
   (iii) The tuning range ratio $f_{max}/f_{min}$ if the breakdown voltage is $V_{BR} = 30$ V.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The junction is one-sided abrupt: $N_A \gg N_D$ (heavily doped p-side, lightly doped n-side).
2. The depletion approximation is valid — the mobile charge carrier density is negligible compared to the ionized dopant density inside $W$.
3. The applied voltage is reverse bias only.

Starting with Poisson's equation on the lightly doped n-side:

$$\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

First integration (using $E = -dV/dx$ and $E = 0$ at $x = W$):

$$E(x) = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(W - x)$$

Second integration (total voltage drop = $V_{bi} + V_R$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bi} + V_R &= -\int_0^W E(x)\, dx = \int_0^W \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(W-x)\, dx \\
&= \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} \left[ Wx - \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_0^W \\
&= \frac{q N_D W^2}{2 \varepsilon_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $W$:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_{bi} + V_R)}{q N_D}}$$

The junction capacitance from the parallel-plate model:

$$C_j = \frac{\varepsilon_s A}{W} = A \sqrt{\frac{q N_D \varepsilon_s}{2 (V_{bi} + V_R)}}$$

At zero bias, $V_R = 0$, so $C_0 = A \sqrt{\dfrac{q N_D \varepsilon_s}{2 V_{bi}}}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{C_j = \frac{C_0}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}}}}$$

This is the required expression.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Junction capacitance at $V_R = 5$ V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_j &= \frac{C_0}{\left(1 + \dfrac{V_R}{V_{bi}}\right)^{0.5}} \\
&= \frac{100 \text{ pF}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{5}{0.7}}} \\
&= \frac{100}{\sqrt{1 + 7.1429}} \\
&= \frac{100}{\sqrt{8.1429}} \\
&= \frac{100}{2.8536} \\
&\approx 35.04 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Resonant frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_r &= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_j}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(5 \times 10^{-9})(35.04 \times 10^{-12})}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{1.752 \times 10^{-19}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi (4.186 \times 10^{-10})} \\
&\approx 3.80 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} \\
&\approx 380 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Tuning range ratio:**

Capacitance ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K = \frac{C_0}{C_{min}} &= \left(1 + \frac{V_{BR}}{V_{bi}}\right)^{0.5} \\
&= \sqrt{1 + \frac{30}{0.7}} \\
&= \sqrt{43.857} \\
&\approx 6.62
\end{aligned}
$$

Frequency tuning ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{max}}{f_{min}} &= \sqrt{K} = \sqrt{6.62} \\
&\approx 2.57 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Shortcut (if examiners permit):** $f_{max}/f_{min} = (1 + V_{BR}/V_{bi})^{n/2}$. For $n = 0.5$, this is the same as $(1 + V_{BR}/V_{bi})^{0.25}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students incorrectly compute the **tuning ratio** as the **capacitance ratio**. Remember: $f_{max}/f_{min} = \sqrt{C_{max}/C_{min}}$, not the ratio itself. Marks are frequently lost here.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option B)

### Question B: Varactor Equivalent Circuit & Applications [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Alternate Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of a varactor diode. Define cut-off frequency $f_c$ and quality factor $Q$ of a varactor. Derive the expression for $f_c$ in terms of series resistance $R_S$ and junction capacitance $C_j$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a varactor-tuned Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO). State the role of the varactor in the LC tank.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Equivalent Circuit & Cut-off Frequency:**

The small-signal equivalent circuit of a varactor consists of:
- A **voltage-dependent capacitance** $C_j(V_R)$ in series with
- A **parasitic series resistance** $R_S$ (representing the bulk neutral semiconductor and contact resistance)
- Optionally, a small lead inductance $L_S$ and package capacitance $C_P$.

**Cut-off frequency $f_c$:** The frequency at which the magnitude of the capacitive reactance equals the series resistance:

$$|X_C| = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C_j} = R_S$$

Solving for $f_c$:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_j}}$$

**Quality factor $Q$:** The ratio of stored energy to dissipated energy per cycle. For a varactor:

$$Q = \frac{|X_C|}{R_S} = \frac{1}{2\pi f R_S C_j} = \frac{f_c}{f}$$

A high-$Q$ varactor has a small $R_S$ and is useful at high frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** $C_j$ in the $f_c$ expression is the value at the operating point (a specific $V_R$), not at zero bias. Marks lost if students treat $C_j$ as constant when $V_R$ is varied.

**Part (b) — VCO Working:**

A VCO is an oscillator whose output frequency is a linear (or monotonically related) function of an input control voltage. The block diagram has these stages:

1. **Reference/Control Voltage Input $V_C$:** A DC voltage (often derived from a PLL error signal) is applied to the varactor.
2. **DC Bias Network:** An RF choke blocks RF from the DC source while allowing the DC control voltage to reach the varactor.
3. **Varactor Diode:** Acts as a voltage-controlled variable capacitor $C_j(V_C)$.
4. **LC Resonant Tank:** The varactor is placed in parallel with an inductor $L$. The resonant frequency is:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \, C_j(V_C)}}$$

5. **Active Amplifier Stage (Transistor / Op-amp):** Provides gain to overcome tank losses and sustains oscillation.
6. **Positive Feedback Network:** Feeds a portion of the tank output back in phase to the input of the amplifier.
7. **Output Port:** Delivers the sinusoidal output at $f_r$.

**Role of the varactor:** The varactor's $C_j$ changes with $V_C$, which directly modulates $f_r$. Thus, the *output frequency is a function of the input control voltage* — this is the essence of voltage-to-frequency conversion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error in VCO Explanation:** Students often say "the varactor changes the resistance" — this is **wrong**. The varactor is a *capacitive* device, not a resistive one. It changes the *capacitive reactance*, not the resistance of the tank.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Varactor = voltage-controlled variable capacitor; always reverse-biased.
- **Physical Origin:** Capacitance arises from the depletion region behaving as a parallel-plate capacitor dielectric.
- **Key Formula:** $C_j = C_0 / (1 + V_R/V_{bi})^n$, where $n = 0.5$ (abrupt), $0.33$ (linearly graded), or $1$ to $2$ (hyper-abrupt).
- **Tuning:** Increasing $V_R$ widens $W$ → decreases $C_j$ → increases resonant frequency $f_r$.
- **Resonant Frequency:** $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC_j})$ in an LC tank.
- **Tuning Ratio:** $f_{max}/f_{min} = \sqrt{C_{max}/C_{min}}$.
- **Cut-off Frequency:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi R_S C_j)$ — the upper useful frequency limit.
- **Quality Factor:** $Q = f_c/f = 1/(2\pi f R_S C_j)$.
- **Always reverse-biased:** Forward bias destroys the varactor's controlled-capacitance behaviour.
- **Capacitance decreases** as $V_R$ increases — never the opposite.
- **Three Main Applications:** VCO, electronic tuner, parametric amplifier.
- **Hyper-abrupt** junctions are used for VCOs (largest $C$ swing).
- **Grading coefficient $n$** depends on doping profile; $n = 0.5$ for abrupt.
- **Built-in voltage $V_{bi}$:** $\sim 0.7$ V for Si at room temperature.
- **Never write** the formula as $C_j = C_0(1 + V_R/V_{bi})^n$ — the capacitance *decreases* with $V_R$, so the formula is in the **denominator**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *derive* the $C_j$ vs $V_R$ relation, always start from **Poisson's equation**, integrate **twice**, then plug into the **parallel-plate capacitance formula**. This is the full-mark derivation path expected by KTU examiners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Diodes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Diodes

# Power Diodes — Comprehensive KTU 2024 Module 3 Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Diode** is a two-terminal, uncontrolled semiconductor switching device designed to handle **high currents (from a few amperes to several kiloamperes)** and **high reverse voltages (from a few hundred volts to several kilovolts)**. Structurally, it is a **p–n junction diode** with a **wide, lightly doped intrinsic (n⁻) drift region** sandwiched between the p⁺ anode and n⁺ cathode layers, giving it the well-known **p⁺ – n⁻ – n⁺** structure. The wide depletion region in the lightly doped drift layer enables the device to sustain large blocking voltages with minimal leakage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A power diode is the basic and most rugged power semiconductor device. It is a two-terminal, uncontrolled, unidirectional switch whose ON/OFF states are determined entirely by the external circuit conditions (i.e., the polarity of the applied voltage).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **power diode like a high-capacity one-way hydraulic check-valve** in a large industrial water pipeline:

- **Forward Bias (ON state):** Water flows freely from the anode (inlet) to the cathode (outlet), with a small mechanical friction loss equivalent to the **forward voltage drop (V_F ≈ 0.7 V to 2.5 V)** in the diode.
- **Reverse Bias (OFF state):** The valve snaps shut, blocking backflow. However, the valve does **not close instantaneously** — there is a small delay during which a reverse water surge occurs. This surge is analogous to the **reverse recovery current (I_RR)** in the diode.
- The thicker and wider the valve, the **higher pressure (voltage)** and **higher flow rate (current)** it can handle. Similarly, the wider the drift (n⁻) region, the higher the voltage rating of the power diode.

### 1.3 Classification of Power Diodes

| Category | Key Feature | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **General-Purpose Diodes** | t_rr ≈ 25 µs | Line-frequency rectifiers (50/60 Hz) |
| **Fast-Recovery Diodes** | t_rr ≈ 0.1 – 5 µs | High-frequency switching (> 1 kHz) |
| **Schottky Power Diodes** | V_F ≈ 0.4 V; t_rr ≈ 0 | Low-voltage, high-frequency rectifiers |
| **Line-Frequency Diodes** | t_rr ≈ 5 – 25 µs | HVDC, motor drives, welding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbolic Constant:** The thermal voltage $kT/q \approx 25.85$ **mV** at room temperature (T = 300 K) and the built-in potential $V_{bi}$ of a typical power silicon diode is approximately **0.7 V to 1.2 V**.

### 1.4 Construction of a Power Diode

The p⁺ – n⁻ – n⁺ structure is fabricated by:
1. **Starting wafer:** A heavily doped n⁺ silicon substrate (cathode base).
2. **Epitaxial growth:** A lightly doped n⁻ drift layer is grown epitaxially on top.
3. **Diffusion:** A p⁺ anode layer is diffused into the upper face.
4. **Edge termination:** **Bevelled edges** (positive or negative bevel) or **guard rings** are employed to distribute the electric field uniformly and prevent premature breakdown.
5. **Packaging:** Stud-type, disc-type, or module packages for efficient heat removal.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Phases of a Power Diode

A power diode operates in **three primary states**:

#### State 1 — Forward Conduction (ON State)
- The device conducts with a small forward voltage drop $V_F$ (typically **0.7 V to 2.5 V**).
- The current density is governed by the **emitter recombination, drift, and diffusion** processes in the bulk n⁻ region.
- For power diodes, the on-state voltage drop is dominated by the **ohmic drop across the wide n⁻ region** because of its low doping.

#### State 2 — Reverse Blocking (OFF State)
- The depletion region expands mostly into the lightly doped n⁻ drift layer.
- A small **reverse leakage current ($I_L \approx$ µA to mA)** flows due to thermally generated carriers.
- The **breakdown voltage ($V_{BR}$)** is set by the **avalanche multiplication** in the n⁻ region.

#### State 3 — Dynamic Transition (Switching)
- **Turn-on:** A brief **forward recovery overshoot** occurs as the device transitions from blocking to conduction.
- **Turn-off:** A **reverse recovery transient** occurs where the diode briefly conducts in the reverse direction before regaining its blocking capability.

### 2.2 Static (V–I) Characteristics — Analysis Steps

1. **Threshold region:** Below the cut-in voltage $V_\gamma \approx 0.7$ V, the forward current is negligible.
2. **Active region:** For $V > V_\gamma$, current rises exponentially following the Shockley equation.
3. **High-current region:** The exponential relationship no longer holds. The voltage drop is approximated by the **linear model** (used heavily in power electronics):
   $$V_F = V_{F0} + r_F I_F$$
   where $V_{F0}$ is the threshold voltage and $r_F$ is the forward (dynamic) resistance.
4. **Reverse region:** A tiny reverse saturation current $I_S$ flows until breakdown.

### 2.3 Dynamic Switching Characteristics

#### A. Forward Recovery
- When the diode is suddenly turned on, the forward voltage momentarily overshoots to a peak $V_{FRM}$ before settling to the steady-state $V_F$.
- Cause: The diode is still in the high-resistivity state because the conductivity modulation of the n⁻ region has not yet occurred.

#### B. Reverse Recovery (Most Important for KTU)

When a forward-conducting diode is suddenly reverse-biased, the current does **not fall to zero instantly**. The dynamics involve:

| Phase | Description | Current Value |
|-------|-------------|---------------|
| **Phase A** (storage phase, $t_a$) | Stored minority carriers are removed. | $I_R$ rises from 0 to peak $I_{RRM}$ |
| **Phase B** (transition phase, $t_b$) | Depletion region rebuilds. | $I_R$ falls from $I_{RRM}$ to 0 |
| **Total** $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$ | Reverse recovery time | – |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and frequently appear in **Part B (14-mark) KTU questions**.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Shockley Diode Equation** | $I = I_S \left( e^{V/\eta V_T} - 1 \right)$ | $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ **mV** at 300 K |
| 2 | **Linear (Piecewise) Model** | $V_F = V_{F0} + r_F I_F$ | $r_F$ = dynamic resistance (Ω) |
| 3 | **Average On-State Current** | $I_{F(AV)} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} i_F(t)\, dt$ | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | **RMS On-State Current** | $I_{F(RMS)} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} i_F^2(t)\, dt }$ | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | **Conduction Power Loss** | $P_{cond} = V_{F0} I_{F(AV)} + r_F I_{F(RMS)}^2$ | Watts (W) |
| 6 | **Switching Power Loss** | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_R I_{RRM} t_{rr} f_s$ | Watts (W) |
| 7 | **Softness Factor (S-factor)** | $S = t_b / t_a$ | Dimensionless; $S = 1$ → abrupt; $S > 1$ → soft |
| 8 | **Reverse Recovery Charge** | $Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} t_{rr} I_{RRM}$ | Coulombs (C) |
| 9 | **Total Power Dissipation** | $P_T = P_{cond} + P_{sw}$ | Watts (W) |
| 10 | **Thermal Resistance Equation** | $T_j - T_a = P_T (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA})$ | °C/W |
| 11 | **Heat-Sink Sizing** | $\theta_{SA} = \dfrac{T_{j(max)} - T_a}{P_T} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$ | °C/W |
| 12 | **Breakdown Voltage of Drift Region** | $V_{BR} \approx E_{crit} W_n$ | $E_{crit} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ **V/cm** for Si |
| 13 | **Form Factor** | $FF = I_{RMS} / I_{AVG}$ | $FF = \pi/2 \approx 1.57$ for pure sine half-wave |
| 14 | **Ripple Factor (Half-Wave)** | $\gamma = \sqrt{ (I_{rms}^2 / I_{dc}^2) - 1 } \approx 1.21$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Numerical Problems:** For a sinusoidal current waveform of peak $I_m$, $I_{AVG} = I_m/\pi$ and $I_{RMS} = I_m/2$. Always compute both **before** computing conduction losses.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVDC Transmission:** Line-frequency power diodes (tens of kV, kA) form the heart of mercury-arc replacements.
- **Automotive Alternator Rectifiers:** Six power diodes convert three-phase alternator output to DC.
- **Switch-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** Fast-recovery/Schottky diodes reduce switching loss in flyback, forward, and PFC converters.
- **Welding Power Supplies:** High-current, high-surge diodes rectify 50/60 Hz mains.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** Bridge rectifiers using power diodes charge backup batteries.
- **Solar Inverters:** Anti-parallel/series fast-recovery diodes prevent reverse current from PV modules at night.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
