<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three advantages of IGBT over Power BJT and Power MOSFET.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher input impedance** than BJT (voltage-controlled like MOSFET) → simpler drive circuit, lower control power.
2. **Lower ON-state voltage drop** than MOSFET at high voltages → lower conduction losses.
3. **Higher current and voltage handling** than MOSFET, and higher switching speed than BJT.
4. Has no **second breakdown** problem like BJT.
5. Voltage-controlled device → requires very low gate drive current.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare SCR and GTO in terms of turn-off capability.*

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | SCR | GTO |
|---|---|---|
| Turn-off method | By reducing anode current below holding current $I_H$ (natural or forced commutation) | By applying a **negative gate pulse** |
| Gate control | Gate can only turn ON | Gate can turn **ON and OFF** |
| Turn-off circuit | Complex (commutation circuits needed) | Simple negative gate pulse |
| Switching frequency | Low (a few hundred Hz) | Higher (a few kHz) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — IGBT Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **construction and working principle** of an Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor (IGBT) with a neat **equivalent circuit**. Compare IGBT with Power MOSFET and Power BJT.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Draw and explain the **static I-V characteristics** of IGBT. Mention the role of **conductivity modulation** in determining its ON-state voltage drop.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Construction:**
The IGBT is a four-layer device with terminals **Gate (G)**, **Collector (C)**, and **Emitter (E)**. Its vertical structure consists of:
- **p+ emitter** (substrate) — at the bottom; acts as the injector of minority carriers.
- **n- drift region** — lightly doped; sustains the high blocking voltage.
- **p body / well** — diffused into the n- layer; forms the MOSFET channel.
- **n+ source** — heavily doped region inside the p-body.
- **Gate oxide (SiO₂)** — thin insulating layer covering the p-body between n+ source regions.
- **Gate metallization (polysilicon)** — on top of the oxide.

**Working Principle:**

1. When $V_{GE} = 0$ or $V_{GE} < V_{GE(th)}$: No channel forms in the p-body. The device is in the **OFF state**. The p+ emitter / n- drift junction is forward biased but no electrons can reach it; hence no current flows (only small leakage).

2. When $V_{GE} > V_{GE(th)}$ (typically > 5 V): An **inversion layer** (n-channel) is induced in the p-body. Electrons flow from the n+ source through this channel into the n- drift region. This is the MOSFET action.

3. The electron current into the n- drift region acts as the **base current** for the internal p-n-p BJT. This drives the BJT into conduction.

4. The p+ emitter now injects **holes** into the n- drift region. The combination of electrons and holes in the n- drift causes **conductivity modulation** — the effective resistance drops by 1–2 orders of magnitude.

5. Current flows from the collector to the emitter with a small saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5$–$3$ V.

6. To turn OFF, $V_{GE}$ is brought below $V_{GE(th)}$. The channel disappears, electron flow stops, and the stored minority carriers recombine / are swept out — producing a brief **tail current**.

**Equivalent Circuit:**
The IGBT is equivalent to a **pnp BJT** driven by an **n-channel MOSFET**, with the MOSFET's drain connected to the BJT's base, and a JFET/pinch resistance representing the n- drift region. The p+ emitter acts as the BJT's emitter.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Power BJT | Power MOSFET | **IGBT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control | Current-controlled | Voltage-controlled | **Voltage-controlled** |
| Input impedance | Low | Very high | **Very high** |
| Switching speed | Medium | Very fast | **Fast** (slower than MOSFET) |
| ON-state voltage | Low | High at high $V$ | **Low** |
| Current capacity | High | Medium | **High** |
| Second breakdown | Yes | No | **No** |
| Drive power | High | Very low | **Very low** |

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

The IGBT's static I-V characteristics are plotted as $I_C$ (vertical) versus $V_{CE}$ (horizontal) for various values of $V_{GE}$ as the parameter.

**Three regions of operation:**

1. **Cut-off / Forward Blocking Region:** When $V_{GE} < V_{GE(th)}$, no channel exists. $I_C$ is essentially zero (only a small leakage current). The device can sustain a high positive $V_{CE}$ up to the rated breakdown.

2. **Active / Linear Region:** When $V_{GE} > V_{GE(th)}$ and $V_{CE}$ is small, the IGBT operates like a transconductance device. The collector current is given by $I_C = g_m \cdot (V_{GE} - V_{GE(th)})$. This region is used for analog amplification, but in power electronics this region is generally avoided to keep losses low.

3. **Saturation Region:** When $V_{CE}$ exceeds a small knee voltage (~1–2 V), the IGBT enters saturation. The $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ curve flattens. The device behaves as a closed switch with $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5$–$3$ V. **This is the desired ON state** for power switching applications.

**Role of Conductivity Modulation:**
The p+ emitter injects a large concentration of holes into the n- drift region. These holes, together with the electrons from the MOSFET channel, dramatically increase the carrier density in the n- layer — a phenomenon called **conductivity modulation**. The n- layer's effective resistance drops by orders of magnitude, leading to a much **lower $V_{CE(sat)}$** than a MOSFET of equivalent voltage rating. This is the key reason IGBTs outperform high-voltage MOSFETs in conduction loss.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — GTO Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **construction and two-transistor equivalent model** of a GTO. Explain the **turn-off mechanism** in detail.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Draw and explain the **static I-V characteristics** of a GTO. Define the terms **latching current** and **holding current**, and explain why a GTO requires a large negative gate current for turn-off.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Construction:**
A GTO is a four-layer, three-terminal device (Anode A, Cathode K, Gate G) with the same p-n-p-n structure as an SCR. The crucial design difference is that the **gate and cathode are heavily interdigitated** (comb-like pattern), which maximises the gate-cathode junction perimeter and enables a uniform turn-off of the entire cathode area. The n- drift region is optimised for carrier extraction.

**Two-Transistor Equivalent Model:**
The GTO is modelled as two coupled bipolar transistors:
- $T_1$: p-n-p transistor with common-base current gain $\alpha_1$ (Anode side)
- $T_2$: n-p-n transistor with common-base current gain $\alpha_2$ (Cathode side)

The collector of $T_1$ is tied to the base of $T_2$, and the collector of $T_2$ to the base of $T_1$. The total anode current is $I_A = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$.

**Turn-on:** A positive gate pulse $I_{GF}$ causes $I_{C2} \uparrow$ → $I_{B1} \uparrow$ → $I_{C1} \uparrow$ → $I_{B2} \uparrow$ → ... regenerative loop. Latching occurs when $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$.

**Turn-off Mechanism (Step-by-Step):**
1. A **strong negative gate pulse** $I_{GRQ}$ is applied (typically one-fifth to one-third of the anode current).
2. This **reverse-biases the gate-cathode junction** $J_3$.
3. Stored carriers in the p-base and n+ cathode are **rapidly extracted** by the negative gate current.
4. The n-p-n transistor $T_2$ is starved of base drive → $\alpha_2 \to 0$.
5. With $T_2$ cut off, the p-n-p transistor $T_1$ has no base drive ($I_{B1} = I_{C2} \to 0$).
6. The condition $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$ is re-established.
7. The anode current $I_A$ collapses, and the device returns to the **forward blocking state**.

**Turn-off time** consists of:
- **Storage time $t_s$**: time for stored charge to be removed.
- **Fall time $t_f$**: time for $I_A$ to fall from 90% to 10% of its initial value.
- **Tail time $t_t$**: small residual current due to remaining carriers.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Static I-V Characteristics:**
The GTO's $I_A$–$V_{AK}$ curve is plotted with gate current $I_G$ as the parameter. It has three regions:

1. **Forward Blocking Region:** With $I_G = 0$ and $V_{AK} < V_{BO}$, only a small leakage current flows. The device is OFF.
2. **Forward Conduction Region:** Once $I_A > I_L$ (latching current) or $V_{AK} > V_{BO}$, the device latches ON. $V_{AK}$ drops to about 1.5–3 V. The curve resembles a low-resistance straight line with a small slope.
3. **Reverse Blocking Region:** For $V_{AK} < 0$, the GTO blocks reverse voltage up to its reverse breakdown rating, with only a small reverse leakage.

**Latching Current $I_L$:**
The minimum anode current required to keep the GTO in the ON state **after the gate pulse is removed**. If the anode current falls below $I_L$ momentarily, the device will revert to the OFF state.

**Holding Current $I_H$:**
The minimum anode current below which the GTO will **spontaneously turn OFF** even without any gate action. Always $I_H < I_L$.

**Why a large negative gate current is required:**
The two-transistor model gives the relationship:

$$
I_{GRQ} = I_A \cdot \frac{(1 - \alpha_1)(1 - \alpha_2)}{\alpha_2}
$$

The turn-off current gain $\beta_{off} = \dfrac{I_A}{I_{GRQ}}$ is typically only 3–5. This is because:
- $\alpha_2$ must be reduced to nearly zero, which requires sweeping out a huge concentration of stored carriers in the n+ cathode and p-base regions.
- Only a fraction of the negative gate current actually contributes to carrier extraction; the rest is shunted.
- The interdigitated structure means a large junction area must be de-energised simultaneously.

Hence, to turn off a 1000 A GTO, one needs a negative gate pulse of about 200–300 A — a major disadvantage that motivated the development of the IGCT.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the p+ injector layer** when drawing the IGBT structure — it is the *defining* feature that distinguishes IGBT from MOSFET. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 2. **Stating that IGBT is current-controlled** — it is *voltage-controlled* like a MOSFET. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 3. **Claiming SCR and GTO have identical turn-off** — explicitly state that SCR needs commutation, GTO uses negative gate pulse. (Lose 2 marks.)
> 4. **Drawing the IGBT V-I curve as straight lines like a resistor** — it has a *knee* (active to saturation transition), like a BJT. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_H$** — remember: $I_L$ is for the gate pulse to be removable; $I_H$ is for the device to stay on. Always $I_L > I_H$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IGBT = MOSFET (input) + pnp BJT (output)**, with an additional **p+ injector layer** that causes conductivity modulation.
- **IGBT terminals:** Gate, Collector, Emitter. **Voltage-controlled.**
- **IGBT ON condition:** $V_{GE} > V_{GE(th)} \approx 4$–$6$ V.
- **IGBT V-I curve** has three regions: cut-off, active, saturation — saturation is the switching ON state.
- **Conductivity modulation** is the key reason IGBTs have low $V_{CE(sat)}$.
- **GTO = SCR with bidirectional gate control** — can be turned OFF by a negative gate pulse.
- **GTO latching condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$.
- **GTO turn-off condition:** $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$, achieved by collapsing $\alpha_2 \to 0$ via carrier extraction.
- **Turn-off current gain $\beta_{off}$** for a GTO is small (3–5), requiring large negative gate currents.
- **Interdigitated gate-cathode** geometry is the structural key to GTO turn-off capability.
- **IGBT vs GTO:** IGBT is voltage-driven, easier to control, faster; GTO handles much higher voltages and currents but needs complex gate drives.
- **Latching $I_L$ > Holding $I_H$** — always.
- **Tail current** is the residual current at IGBT/GTO turn-off — caused by stored minority carriers in the drift region.
- **Second breakdown:** BJT suffers, MOSFET and IGBT do not — major IGBT advantage.
- **KTU-favourite one-liner:** *"IGBT is the workhorse of medium-power, medium-frequency converters; GTO was the workhorse of high-power traction, now largely replaced by IGCTs."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Voltage Regulators: Load and Line Regulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear Voltage Regulators: Load and Line Regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Voltage Regulator?

A **voltage regulator** is an electronic circuit or device that maintains a constant output voltage regardless of variations in the input voltage, load current, or ambient temperature. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, it is classified as a **DC voltage regulator** when used to stabilize DC supply rails in electronic systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standard):** A voltage regulator is a subsystem that holds the output DC voltage within specified limits (tolerance band) in spite of (a) variations in the input supply voltage (line variations) and (b) variations in the load current drawn by the load (load variations).

Mathematically, the *ideal* regulator satisfies:

$$V_{out} = \text{constant} \quad \forall \, V_{in} \in [V_{in,min}, V_{in,max}], \; I_L \in [0, I_{L,max}]$$

However, in real (non-ideal) regulators, the output voltage drifts slightly. The two key metrics that quantify this drift are **Line Regulation** and **Load Regulation**.

---

## 1.2 Line Regulation (LR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Line Regulation is the measure of the ability of the regulator to maintain a constant output voltage despite variations in the input (line) supply voltage, with the load current and temperature held constant.

It quantifies *how much the output changes when the input changes*.

$$\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \quad \text{(units: V/V or dimensionless ratio)}$$

It is also commonly expressed in **% per Volt** or **mV/V**:

$$\text{Line Regulation (\%/V)} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}/V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$$

A **lower value of Line Regulation indicates a better regulator** (output is more immune to input fluctuations).

---

## 1.3 Load Regulation (LDR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Load Regulation is the measure of the ability of the regulator to maintain a constant output voltage when the load current is varied, with the input voltage and temperature held constant.

It quantifies *how much the output droops when the load draws more current*.

$$\text{Load Regulation} = V_{NL} - V_{FL} \quad \text{(units: Volts)}$$

Or in percentage form:

$$\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $V_{NL}$ = Output voltage at **No Load** ($I_L = 0$)
- $V_{FL}$ = Output voltage at **Full Load** ($I_L = I_{L,max}$)

Again, **a lower value of Load Regulation is desirable** (output is more stable against load swings).

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy: The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine the regulator as a **smart water-pressure regulator** attached to a municipal supply:

| Electronic Concept | Water Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Input voltage $V_{in}$** | Pressure of water entering your house from the main pipe |
| **Output voltage $V_{out}$** | Pressure of water at your tap |
| **Load current $I_L$** | Number of taps opened in the house |
| **Regulator** | Pressure-reducing valve with feedback |

- **Line Regulation** → The municipal pressure fluctuates between low (morning, everyone uses) and high (night, few use). Does your tap pressure stay constant? If yes, your "regulator" has good **line regulation**.
- **Load Regulation** → You open 0 taps vs. 10 taps simultaneously. Does the pressure at the remaining taps drop? If it stays constant, your regulator has good **load regulation**.

The **shunt path** in a regulator is like a "controlled leak" that absorbs excess pressure/charge to keep the output stable — analogous to opening a relief valve to dump excess water.

---

## 1.5 Why These Two Parameters Matter (KTU Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, students frequently confuse these two terms. Remember:
> - **Line** = source side (input) variation → tests response to $V_{in}$ changes.
> - **Load** = consumer side variation → tests response to $I_L$ changes.
>
> Both are essential specifications on every commercial regulator datasheet (e.g., LM7805, LM317, 7812).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Linear Voltage Regulators

Linear voltage regulators are broadly classified into three architectures:

### (a) Series (Pass-Element) Regulator
A variable resistance element (transistor) is placed **in series** between the input and the output. The control circuit adjusts the resistance to absorb the excess voltage ($V_{in} - V_{out}$). This is the most common and efficient linear topology.

### (b) Shunt Regulator
A variable resistance element is placed **in parallel** with the load. Excess current is "shunted" away from the load to ground. Used in low-current, precision reference applications (e.g., Zener-based references).

### (c) Integrated-Series Regulator (e.g., 78xx, LM317)
Combines a series pass element, a reference (bandgap/Zener), an error amplifier, and protection circuitry (current limit, thermal shutdown) in a single IC package.

---

## 2.2 Series Regulator: Block-Level Working

The classic series regulator has the following control loop:

1. A **reference voltage** $V_{REF}$ is generated internally (Zener or bandgap).
2. A **sampling network** (resistor divider $R_1, R_2$) senses the output voltage $V_{out}$ and produces a feedback voltage $V_{FB} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
3. An **error amplifier** compares $V_{FB}$ with $V_{REF}$ and produces an error signal.
4. The error signal drives the **pass transistor** (series element), modulating its $V_{CE}$ to keep $V_{out}$ constant.

Using the closed-loop feedback principle:

$$V_{out} = V_{REF} \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) \quad \text{(for an ideal op-amp based regulator)}$$

---

## 2.3 Shunt Regulator: Block-Level Working

The shunt regulator uses the same feedback principle, but the control element is a transistor in **parallel** with the load. When $V_{out}$ rises (due to $V_{in}$ increase or $I_L$ decrease), the error amplifier sinks more current through the shunt transistor, dumping the excess to ground and clamping $V_{out}$.

The total current from the source is:

$$I_S = I_L + I_{shunt}$$

The shunt transistor absorbs any change: $\Delta I_S = \Delta I_L + \Delta I_{shunt}$ such that $V_{out}$ remains constant.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all key formulas** you need for solving any KTU 2024 question on this topic. Memorize these and the corresponding units.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Line Regulation (ratio form)** | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}}$ | V/V or mV/V | Smaller is better |
| 2 | **Line Regulation (% per volt)** | $LR_{\%} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{out} \cdot \Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | %/V | Standard datasheet form |
| 3 | **Load Regulation (volt form)** | $LDR = V_{NL} - V_{FL}$ | V (or mV) | Difference form |
| 4 | **Load Regulation (% form)** | $LDR_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ | % | Most common form |
| 5 | **Output Impedance** | $Z_{out} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L}\Big\vert_{V_{in}=\text{const}}$ | $\Omega$ | Equivalent Thevenin resistance |
| 6 | **Input Regulation Factor** | $S_V = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}}$ | V/V | Synonym of Line Regulation |
| 7 | **Temperature Coefficient** | $TC = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta T}$ | V/°C (or ppm/°C) | Drift with temperature |
| 8 | **Power Dissipation (series)** | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ | W | Determines heat-sink sizing |
| 9 | **Efficiency (linear)** | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | Always $< 100\%$ |
| 10 | **Closed-loop output (series)** | $V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | V | For op-amp feedback regulator |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear voltage regulators (despite their lower efficiency compared to switch-mode regulators) are the **industry workhorse** for the following reasons:

- **Ultra-low noise output** — critical for RF, audio, and analog signal-chain circuits.
- **No EMI/RFI** — unlike SMPS, they do not generate high-frequency switching noise.
- **Simple, cheap, robust** — 78xx/79xx series cost less than ₹20 in India.
- **Fast transient response** — op-amp feedback loop bandwidth is wide.

Common KTU-referenced ICs: **LM7805 (5 V)**, **LM7812 (12 V)**, **LM317 (adjustable)**, **LM7905 (–5 V)**, **TL431 (precision shunt reference)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Line Regulation of a Series Pass Regulator

Consider a simple series regulator with a pass transistor $Q_1$ whose small-signal emitter (or source) resistance is $r_e$, and an error amplifier with open-loop gain $A$. The feedback fraction is $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.

The unregulated input $V_{in}$ is applied to the collector of $Q_1$; the output $V_{out}$ is at the emitter.

### Step 1: Write the unregulated (open-loop) relationship
If there were no feedback, the output would simply follow the input minus the $V_{BE}$ drop:

$$V_{out,\text{open}} \approx V_{in} - V_{BE} - I_L \cdot r_e$$

For small AC variations around the operating point:

$$\Delta V_{out,\text{open}} = \Delta V_{in} - \Delta I_L \cdot r_e$$

### Step 2: Include the feedback loop
With feedback active, the error amplifier forces:

$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{out,\text{open}}}{1 + A\beta}$$

where $A\beta$ is the **loop gain** of the regulator.

### Step 3: Express Line Regulation
Line Regulation is defined at constant load ($\Delta I_L = 0$):

$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{in}}{1 + A\beta}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}}$$

### Step 4: Interpretation
- A high loop gain $A\beta$ produces a **small** line regulation value → **excellent regulation**.
- For $A\beta \to \infty$, Line Regulation $\to 0$ (ideal regulator).
- The output impedance of a closed-loop regulator is reduced by the same factor:

$$Z_{out,\text{closed}} = \frac{r_e}{1 + A\beta} \quad \text{(directly linked to Load Regulation)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The feedback loop effectively divides both the input-referred disturbance (line variation) and the output impedance by the loop gain. This is a classic negative-feedback result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Load Regulation of a Series Pass Regulator

Now hold $V_{in}$ constant ($\Delta V_{in} = 0$) and vary the load current $I_L$.

### Step 1: Open-loop load sensitivity
Without feedback, increasing $I_L$ causes a voltage drop across $r_e$:

$$\Delta V_{out,\text{open}} = - \Delta I_L \cdot r_e$$

The negative sign indicates the output voltage *decreases* when the load current *increases*.

### Step 2: Apply closed-loop feedback
The feedback senses the drop and adjusts the pass transistor to compensate:

$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{out,\text{open}}}{1 + A\beta} = - \frac{\Delta I_L \cdot r_e}{1 + A\beta}$$

### Step 3: Derive Output Impedance
By definition, $Z_{out} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L}\Big\vert_{\Delta V_{in}=0}$:

$$Z_{out} = \frac{r_e}{1 + A\beta}$$

### Step 4: Compute Load Regulation in Volts
Going from no-load ($I_L = 0$) to full-load ($I_L = I_{FL}$):

$$\boxed{\text{Load Regulation} = V_{NL} - V_{FL} = I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out} = \frac{I_{FL} \cdot r_e}{1 + A\beta}}$$

### Step 5: Percentage form
$$\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\% = \frac{I_{FL} \cdot r_e}{(1 + A\beta) \cdot V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A series linear regulator uses a pass transistor with $r_e = 5 \,\Omega$ and an error amplifier with open-loop gain $A = 2000$. The feedback network has $R_1 = 1 \,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 1 \,\text{k}\Omega$. The reference voltage is $V_{REF} = 2.5 \,\text{V}$. The full-load current is $I_{FL} = 100 \,\text{mA}$.

**(a) Calculate the closed-loop output voltage.**

Using the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1\,\text{k}}{1\,\text{k} + 1\,\text{k}} = 0.5$$

$$V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = 2.5 \times \left(1 + \frac{1}{1}\right) = 2.5 \times 2 = 5.0 \,\text{V}$$

**(b) Calculate the Loop Gain $A\beta$ and Load Regulation in mV.**

$$A\beta = 2000 \times 0.5 = 1000$$

$$\text{Load Regulation} = \frac{I_{FL} \cdot r_e}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{0.1 \times 5}{1 + 1000} = \frac{0.5}{1001} \approx 4.995 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{V} \approx 0.50 \,\text{mV}$$

**(c) Calculate the percentage Load Regulation.**

$$\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{0.5 \,\text{mV}}{5.0 \,\text{V}} \times 100\% = 0.01\%$$

**(d) If $V_{in}$ varies from 8 V to 12 V, find the change in $V_{out}$ (Line Regulation in mV/V).**

$$\Delta V_{in} = 12 - 8 = 4 \,\text{V}$$

$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{in}}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{4}{1001} \approx 3.996 \,\text{mV}$$

$$\text{Line Regulation (mV/V)} = \frac{3.996 \,\text{mV}}{4 \,\text{V}} \approx 0.999 \,\text{mV/V} \approx 1 \,\text{mV/V}$$

---

## 3.4 Combined Regulation: Why Both Matter Simultaneously

In a real power-supply scenario, *both* $V_{in}$ and $I_L$ vary together. The total worst-case output deviation is approximately:

$$\Delta V_{out,\text{total}} \approx \frac{\Delta V_{in}}{1 + A\beta} + I_{FL} \cdot \frac{r_e}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{\Delta V_{in} + I_{FL} \cdot r_e}{1 + A\beta}$$

This is why **high loop gain** is the universal design lever to improve *both* metrics simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Series Linear Voltage Regulator

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **closed-loop control structure** of a series linear regulator. Note that all node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels are clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[DC INPUT SOURCE Vin] --> B1[PASS TRANSISTOR Q1 SERIES ELEMENT]
    B1 --> C1[OUTPUT NODE Vout]
    C1 --> D1[SAMPLING NETWORK R1 R2 DIVIDER]
    D1 --> E1[ERROR AMPLIFIER COMPARATOR]
    F1[VOLTAGE REFERENCE Vref BANDGAP ZENER] --> E1
    E1 -->|Error Signal Vc| B1
    C1 --> G1[LOAD RL]

    style A1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C1 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D1 fill:#7b1fa2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E1 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F1 fill:#5d4037,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style G1 fill:#37474f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

**Working Summary (mapped to the diagram):**
1. **Vin** (unregulated DC) enters the series pass transistor Q1.
2. **Q1** drops the excess voltage as heat; its conduction is modulated.
3. **Vout** appears at the load RL.
4. **R1–R2 divider** samples a fraction of Vout as feedback.
5. **Error amplifier** compares feedback against **Vref** (bandgap/Zener reference).
6. The error signal drives the base/gate of Q1 to maintain $V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Shunt Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
graph LR
    A2[DC INPUT Vin] --> R2[SERIES RESISTOR Rs]
    R2 --> B2[OUTPUT NODE Vout]
    B2 --> C2[SHUNT TRANSISTOR Q2 PARALLEL ELEMENT]
    C2 --> GND2[GND]
    B2 --> D2[SAMPLING NETWORK R3 R4]
    D2 --> E2[ERROR AMPLIFIER]
    F2[Vref REFERENCE] --> E2
    E2 -->|Drive Signal| C2
    B2 --> L2[LOAD RL]

    style A2 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B2 fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C2 fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style GND2 fill:#000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D2 fill:#7b1fa2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E2 fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F2 fill:#5d4037,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#37474f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

**Working Summary:**
1. Excess current is dumped to **GND** through the shunt transistor Q2.
2. The series resistor Rs absorbs the surplus voltage $(V_{in} - V_{out})$.
3. Feedback control adjusts Q2's conduction to keep $V_{out}$ constant.

---

## 4.3 Regulation Characteristics — Behavioural Plot (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block captures the **input-output and load-output characteristics** of a real (non-ideal) regulator.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CHART_A[Input Output Characteristic Line Regulation]
        P1[Vin increases] --> Q1[Vout rises slightly slope equals 1 by 1 plus A beta]
    end
    subgraph CHART_B[Load Output Characteristic Load Regulation]
        P2[IL increases] --> Q2[Vout droops slightly slope equals negative r_e by 1 plus A beta]
    end

    style CHART_A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style CHART_B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style P1 fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1
    style Q1 fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1
    style P2 fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100
    style Q2 fill:#ffb74d,stroke:#e65100
```

| Curve Type | X-Axis | Y-Axis | Ideal Slope | Real Slope |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Line Regulation | $V_{in}$ | $V_{out}$ | $0$ (flat) | $\dfrac{1}{1 + A\beta}$ (very small) |
| Load Regulation | $I_L$ | $V_{out}$ | $0$ (flat) | $-\dfrac{r_e}{1 + A\beta}$ (very small negative) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Define Line Regulation and Load Regulation of a voltage regulator. Mention the ideal values of both for a perfect regulator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Line Regulation** is defined as the change in regulated output voltage for a corresponding change in the unregulated input (line) voltage, with load current and temperature held constant.
$$\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{out} \cdot \Delta V_{in}} \times 100\% \,\%/\text{V}$$
- **Load Regulation** is defined as the change in regulated output voltage when the load current is varied from no-load to full-load, with input voltage and temperature held constant.
$$\text{Load Regulation} = V_{NL} - V_{FL} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$
- For a **perfect (ideal) regulator**, both Line Regulation and Load Regulation are equal to **zero** (output is completely independent of input and load).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Distinguish between a series regulator and a shunt regulator. State one merit and one demerit of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator |
|---|---|---|
| **Position of pass element** | In **series** with the load | In **parallel** (shunt) with the load |
| **Current handling** | Pass element carries full load current | Shunt element carries only excess current |
| **Efficiency** | Higher (only drop = $V_{in} - V_{out}$) | Lower (always bleeds current to ground) |
| **Short-circuit protection** | Naturally limited by pass transistor | Poor (Rs limits current, but can overheat) |
| **Typical application** | 78xx, LM317, general-purpose supplies | Zener references, TL431, low-current precision |
| **Merit** | High efficiency, good regulation | Simple, inherently short-circuit safe |
| **Demerit** | Needs current-limit/thermal protection | Low efficiency, high quiescent current |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by not drawing the regulator block diagram before writing equations.
> - Students **lose 1–2 marks** for not stating units with final answers (mV, mV/V, %, etc.).
> - **Never** confuse **Line Regulation** with **Load Regulation** in definitions — examiners deduct heavily (typically 1 mark each occurrence).
> - Always write the **closed-loop output equation** $V_{out} = V_{REF}(1 + R_1/R_2)$ when working with feedback regulators.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Series Regulator Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) Draw the block diagram of a series linear voltage regulator and explain the function of each block. Derive the expression for Line Regulation in terms of loop gain $A\beta$.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A 7805 regulator supplies a load that varies from 0 mA to 500 mA. The no-load output is 5.05 V and the full-load output is 4.95 V. Calculate (i) Load Regulation in mV and in %, (ii) the output impedance of the regulator. If the input varies from 8 V to 12 V and the output varies by 20 mV, find (iii) Line Regulation in mV/V.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the series regulator block diagram in SECTION 4.1. The four functional blocks are:

1. **Pass Transistor (Q1):** Acts as a variable resistor; drops the excess voltage $(V_{in} - V_{out})$ and delivers load current to the output.
2. **Sampling Network ($R_1, R_2$):** A resistor divider that samples the output voltage and produces a scaled feedback voltage $V_{FB} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
3. **Error Amplifier:** A high-gain differential amplifier (op-amp) that compares $V_{FB}$ with the internal reference $V_{REF}$ and produces an error signal $V_c = A(V_{REF} - V_{FB})$.
4. **Voltage Reference ($V_{REF}$):** A temperature-compensated Zener or bandgap reference (typically 1.25 V for LM317 or 2.5 V for many modern ICs).

**Derivation of Line Regulation (4 marks):**

The open-loop transfer from input to output (small-signal) is approximately unity, minus the drop across the pass transistor:

$$\Delta V_{out,\text{open}} \approx \Delta V_{in} \quad \text{(at constant load)}$$

When the feedback loop is closed with loop gain $A\beta$, the disturbance is divided by $(1 + A\beta)$:

$$\Delta V_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{in}}{1 + A\beta}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}}$$

This shows that **a high loop gain sharply reduces line regulation** (improves performance).

---

**Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:** $I_L$ varies from 0 to 500 mA, $V_{NL} = 5.05$ V, $V_{FL} = 4.95$ V, $V_{in}$ varies from 8 V to 12 V, $\Delta V_{out} = 20$ mV.

**(i) Load Regulation in mV and % (3 marks):**

$$\text{Load Regulation} = V_{NL} - V_{FL} = 5.05 - 4.95 = 0.10 \,\text{V} = 100 \,\text{mV}$$

$$\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.10}{4.95} \times 100\% = 2.02\%$$

**(ii) Output Impedance (2 marks):**

$$Z_{out} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL} - 0} = \frac{0.10 \,\text{V}}{0.500 \,\text{A}} = 0.20 \,\Omega$$

**(iii) Line Regulation in mV/V (2 marks):**

$$\Delta V_{in} = 12 - 8 = 4 \,\text{V}, \quad \Delta V_{out} = 20 \,\text{mV} = 0.020 \,\text{V}$$

$$\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{0.020}{4} = 0.005 \,\text{V/V} = 5 \,\text{mV/V}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Shunt Regulator & Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) Explain the working of a shunt voltage regulator with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the percentage load regulation.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Design a Zener-based shunt regulator to deliver a regulated 6 V at a load current varying from 5 mA to 50 mA. The unregulated input varies from 10 V to 14 V. The Zener diode requires a minimum current of 10 mA to stay in breakdown. Find (i) the value of the series resistor $R_S$, and (ii) the maximum power dissipation in the Zener.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the shunt regulator block diagram in SECTION 4.2. Functional blocks:

1. **Series Resistor $R_S$:** Drops the difference $(V_{in} - V_{out})$ and limits the current.
2. **Shunt Element (Q2 / Zener):** Provides a controlled current path to ground.
3. **Sampling Network + Error Amplifier + Reference:** Same as series regulator, but the error signal drives the shunt element.

**Working (2 marks):**
- If $V_{out}$ rises (e.g., $V_{in}$ increases or $I_L$ decreases), the feedback loop increases the shunt current through Q2, dumping the excess to ground and pulling $V_{out}$ back to the regulated value.
- The total current from the source: $I_S = I_L + I_{shunt}$. When $I_L$ falls, $I_{shunt}$ rises; when $V_{in}$ rises, $I_{shunt}$ rises.

**Derivation of % Load Regulation (3 marks):**

Output impedance of the closed-loop shunt regulator:

$$Z_{out,\text{closed}} = \frac{Z_{out,\text{open}}}{1 + A\beta}$$

For a shunt regulator, the open-loop output impedance is approximately $R_S$ (in parallel with the Zener dynamic resistance $r_z$, but dominated by the regulated action). Hence:

$$\text{Load Regulation} = V_{NL} - V_{FL} = I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out,\text{closed}} = \frac{I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out,\text{open}}}{1 + A\beta}$$

$$\text{Load Regulation (\%)} = \frac{I_{FL} \cdot Z_{out,\text{open}}}{(1 + A\beta) \cdot V_{FL}} \times 100\%$$

---

**Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_Z = 6$ V, $I_{L,min} = 5$ mA, $I_{L,max} = 50$ mA, $V_{in,min} = 10$ V, $V_{in,max} = 14$ V, $I_{Z,min} = 10$ mA.

**Step 1: Worst-case resistor design (2 marks):**

The series resistor $R_S$ is given by:

$$R_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{I_S} = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_L}$$

The worst case occurs when $(V_{in} - V_Z)$ is **minimum** and the total required current $(I_{Z,min} + I_{L,max})$ is **maximum** (otherwise the Zener will drop out of regulation):

$$R_S = \frac{V_{in,min} - V_Z}{I_{Z,min} + I_{L,max}} = \frac{10 - 6}{0.010 + 0.050} = \frac{4}{0.060} = 66.67 \,\Omega$$

**Step 2: Verify regulation at the other extreme (1 mark):**

At $V_{in,max} = 14$ V and $I_{L,min} = 5$ mA:

$$I_S = \frac{V_{in,max} - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{14 - 6}{66.67} = \frac{8}{66.67} = 0.120 \,\text{A} = 120 \,\text{mA}$$

$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 120 - 5 = 115 \,\text{mA}$$

Since $I_Z$ (115 mA) $\gg$ $I_{Z,min}$ (10 mA), the Zener stays in regulation. **Design is valid.**

**Step 3: Maximum Zener power dissipation (2 marks):**

Maximum Zener current occurs at $V_{in,max}$ and $I_{L,min}$:

$$I_{Z,max} = 115 \,\text{mA}$$

$$P_{Z,max} = V_Z \times I_{Z,max} = 6 \times 0.115 = 0.690 \,\text{W} = 690 \,\text{mW}$$

**Step 4: Power rating selection (2 marks):**

For reliability, a safety factor of $1.5\times$ to $2\times$ is applied. A **1 W or 1.5 W Zener** (with appropriate heat-sinking) is recommended.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Voltage Regulator** = Circuit that holds $V_{out}$ constant against $V_{in}$ and $I_L$ variations.
- **Line Regulation** = Response to **input voltage** changes (load held constant). Formula: $\Delta V_{out}/\Delta V_{in}$ (V/V) or per cent per volt.
- **Load Regulation** = Response to **load current** changes (input held constant). Formula: $V_{NL} - V_{FL}$ (in V or mV), or in %.
- **Ideal values**: Both line and load regulation $\to 0$.
- **Series Regulator** → pass element in **series**; higher efficiency; carries full load current.
- **Shunt Regulator** → pass element in **parallel**; lower efficiency; dumps excess to ground.
- **Closed-loop output**: $V_{out} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ — applicable for op-amp based series regulators.
- **Loop gain** $A\beta$ improves *both* regulation parameters simultaneously:
  - Line Regulation $= \dfrac{1}{1 + A\beta}$
  - Output Impedance $Z_{out} = \dfrac{r_e}{1 + A\beta}$
  - Load Regulation (V) $= \dfrac{I_{FL} \cdot r_e}{1 + A\beta}$
- **Output Impedance** = $\Delta V_{out}/\Delta I_L$ at constant $V_{in}$ — directly tied to load regulation.
- **Efficiency of linear regulator** = $(V_{out}/V_{in}) \times 100\%$ — always less than 100% (lost as heat in the pass transistor).
- **Power dissipation in series regulator** = $(V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_L$ — determines heat-sink requirement.
- **Units to remember**: mV, mV/V, %, Ω, °C, W, %/V.
- **Commercial examples**: 78xx (positive fixed), 79xx (negative fixed), LM317 (adjustable), TL431 (precision shunt reference).
- **Pitfall to avoid**: Do **not** write $\Delta V_{out}/\Delta V_{in}$ when the question asks for "per cent per volt" — multiply by $100/V_{out}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Voltage Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Voltage Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Voltage Regulator** is an electronic circuit or device that maintains a constant output voltage irrespective of variations in the input voltage, load current, or temperature. The **Types of Voltage Regulators** refer to the systematic classification of regulator topologies based on their regulating element placement, conduction mode, feedback mechanism, and switching characteristics.

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies voltage regulators into the following primary categories:

1. **Series (Linear) Voltage Regulators** – regulating element in series with the load.
2. **Shunt (Linear) Voltage Regulators** – regulating element in parallel with the load.
3. **Integrated Circuit (IC) Voltage Regulators** – monolithic three-terminal regulators.
4. **Switching (SMPS) Voltage Regulators** – regulators using high-frequency switching.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PEICT413 (Analog Circuits) mandates comprehensive study of **Series, Shunt, Monolithic, and Switching regulators** including their transfer characteristics, efficiency analyses, and practical design using op-amps and dedicated regulator ICs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a voltage regulator as the **pressure-reducing valve (PRV) at the inlet of a building's water pipeline**:
- The municipal water supply has fluctuating pressure (variable input voltage).
- The PRV constantly adjusts its internal mechanism to deliver a steady stream (constant output voltage).
- A **shunt regulator** is like a **drain valve** that dumps excess water back to the supply – wasteful but simple.
- A **series regulator** is like a **narrowing aperture** in the pipe – throttling the flow to keep downstream pressure constant – very efficient for small adjustments.
- A **switching regulator** is like a **fast on-off valve** that pulses water to maintain average pressure – highly efficient across wide load ranges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept:** The choice between regulator types is fundamentally a trade-off between **efficiency (η)**, **regulation accuracy (line/load regulation)**, **ripple rejection (PSRR)**, **complexity**, and **cost**.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

Every KTU exam expects familiarity with these **standard regulator metrics**:

- **Line Regulation** $S_V = \dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} \bigg\vert_{R_L = const}$
- **Load Regulation** $R_L\% = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$
- **Ripple Rejection (PSRR)** typically in **dB**
- **Dropout Voltage** $V_{DO}$ — minimum $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT})$ for regulation
- **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{OUT}}{P_{IN}} \times 100\%$
- **Temperature Coefficient** $TC = \dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{V_{OUT} \cdot \Delta T}$ in **ppm/°C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Flowchart (Conceptual)

Voltage Regulators
├── Based on Regulating Element
│   ├── **Series Regulators** (Pass transistor in series)
│   └── **Shunt Regulators** (Pass element in parallel)
├── Based on Control Method
│   ├── **Linear (Continuous conduction)**
│   └── **Switched-Mode (Discrete ON/OFF states)**
├── Based on Output
│   ├── **Fixed Output** (78xx, 79xx)
│   ├── **Adjustable Output** (LM317, LM337)
│   └── **Tracking Regulators**
└── Based on Topology (SMPS)
    ├── **Buck (Step-Down)**
    ├── **Boost (Step-Up)**
    └── **Buck-Boost / Cuk / SEPIC**

## 2.2 Series Voltage Regulator (Emitter Follower Type)

**Operating Principle:** A pass transistor $Q_1$ is placed in **series** between the unregulated DC input $V_{IN}$ and the load $R_L$. A feedback network samples $V_{OUT}$ and adjusts the base current of $Q_1$ to maintain constant $V_{OUT}$.

**Why Series?**
- The pass transistor operates in its **active (linear) region**, behaving as a variable resistor.
- Excess voltage $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT})$ is dropped across the transistor.
- The regulator is **NOT a short circuit**; it is a controlled variable resistor.

**Design Steps:**
1. Zener diode $D_Z$ sets the reference voltage $V_{REF} = V_Z$.
2. Sampling resistors $R_1, R_2$ form a voltage divider giving feedback $V_{FB} = V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$.
3. Op-amp compares $V_{FB}$ with $V_{REF}$; output drives $Q_1$ base.
4. If $V_{OUT} \uparrow \Rightarrow V_{FB} \uparrow \Rightarrow V_{BE} \downarrow \Rightarrow I_C \downarrow \Rightarrow V_{OUT}$ restored.

## 2.3 Shunt Voltage Regulator (Zener Type)

**Operating Principle:** A Zener diode is connected in **parallel** with the load. The series resistor $R_S$ absorbs the difference between input and output.

**Current Relations:**
- $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ (KCL at the output node)
- $V_{OUT} = V_Z$ (constant)
- $I_L = V_Z / R_L$ (depends on load)

**Why Shunt?** Used when load is constant and input variation is small. Provides inherent short-circuit protection (the Zener simply draws more current).

## 2.4 IC Voltage Regulators (Three-Terminal)

**LM317 (Adjustable Positive Regulator):**
The most frequently tested IC in KTU exams. Uses bandgap reference and Darlington output.

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$$

where $V_{REF} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ and $I_{ADJ} \leq 100\,\mu\text{A}$ (often neglected).

**78xx (Fixed Positive):** $V_{OUT}$ is fixed (e.g., 7805 → +5V, 7812 → +12V).
**79xx (Fixed Negative):** Negative fixed outputs (e.g., 7905 → −5V).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** For the LM317 design problem, KTU typically gives $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$ (standard). You must show the calculation of $R_2$ for desired $V_{OUT}$. Always check power dissipation: $P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$.

## 2.5 Switching Voltage Regulators (SMPS)

The pass transistor operates as a **switch** (either fully ON or fully OFF), achieving efficiencies of **75–95%** (versus 30–60% for linear regulators).

**Three Canonical Topologies:**

| Topology | Function | Transfer Ratio |
|----------|----------|----------------|
| **Buck** | Step-Down | $V_{OUT} = D \cdot V_{IN}$ |
| **Boost** | Step-Up | $V_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{IN}}{1-D}$ |
| **Buck-Boost** | Inverting | $V_{OUT} = -V_{IN} \cdot \dfrac{D}{1-D}$ |

where $D$ is the **duty cycle** $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON}+T_{OFF}}$.

**Why Switching?** Linear regulators dissipate $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ as heat. Switching regulators minimize this loss by operating the transistor in saturation/cutoff.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 1: Master Formula Sheet for Voltage Regulators**

| Regulator Type | Output Voltage | Line Regulation | Efficiency |
|----------------|----------------|-----------------|------------|
| Zener Shunt | $V_{OUT} = V_Z$ | $S_V = \dfrac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z}$ | $\eta = \dfrac{V_Z \cdot I_L}{V_{IN} \cdot I_S}$ |
| Series Pass (BJT) | $V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Depends on loop gain $A$ | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{IN}}$ |
| 78xx / 79xx IC | Fixed (5V, 12V, etc.) | typ. **0.01%/V** | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{IN}}$ |
| LM317 IC | $1.25\left(1+\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | typ. **0.01%/V** | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{IN}}$ |
| Buck Converter | $D \cdot V_{IN}$ | — | Up to **95%** |
| Boost Converter | $\dfrac{V_{IN}}{1-D}$ | — | Up to **92%** |
| Buck-Boost | $-V_{IN} \cdot \dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | — | Up to **90%** |

| Quantity | Formula | Unit |
|----------|---------|------|
| Loop Gain (with feedback) | $A_{CL} = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | dimensionless |
| Output Impedance (closed loop) | $Z_{OUT,CL} = \dfrac{Z_{OUT,OL}}{1 + A\beta}$ | ohms |
| Line Regulation | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} = \dfrac{1}{A\beta}$ (linear) | V/V |
| Power Dissipation (Pass) | $P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ | watts |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile phones and laptops** → use **LDO regulators** (Low Dropout variants of LM317) for RF/analog rails, and **Buck converters** for high-current CPU cores.
- **Automotive ECUs** → use **78xx/79xx** for sensor supplies.
- **Solar PV systems** → use **MPPT buck-boost** converters.
- **Bench power supplies** → use **adjustable LM317/LM337** for ±15V analog rails in lab equipment.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of LM317 Adjustable Regulator

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A standard KTU 2024 numerical — design an LM317-based regulator for a desired output.

**Given:** $V_{REF} = 1.25\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$, desired $V_{OUT} = 9\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1: Start with the basic LM317 transfer function (from data sheet internal node analysis):**

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2 + V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}$$

Since the ADJ pin current is very small, we approximate $I_{ADJ} \approx 0$, giving:

$$V_{OUT} \approx V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute $V_{OUT} = 9\,\text{V}$, $V_{REF} = 1.25\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$:**

$$9 = 1.25 \left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{240}\right)$$

**Step 3: Divide both sides by 1.25:**

$$\dfrac{9}{1.25} = 1 + \dfrac{R_2}{240}$$

$$7.2 = 1 + \dfrac{R_2}{240}$$

**Step 4: Subtract 1 from both sides:**

$$6.2 = \dfrac{R_2}{240}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $R_2$:**

$$R_2 = 6.2 \times 240 = 1488\,\Omega$$

**Step 6: Select nearest standard E12 value → $R_2 = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$ (giving $V_{OUT} \approx 10.06\,\text{V}$).**

## 3.2 Derivation: Zener Shunt Regulator Performance

**Given:** $V_{IN} = 15\,\text{V}$, $V_Z = 8.2\,\text{V}$, $R_S = 220\,\Omega$, $R_L = 470\,\Omega$, Zener impedance $r_Z = 8\,\Omega$.

**Step 1: Compute load current $I_L$:**

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_Z}{R_L} = \dfrac{8.2}{470} = 17.45\,\text{mA}$$

**Step 2: Compute series current $I_S$ (current through $R_S$):**

$$I_S = \dfrac{V_{IN} - V_Z}{R_S} = \dfrac{15 - 8.2}{220} = 30.91\,\text{mA}$$

**Step 3: Compute Zener current using KCL:**

$$I_Z = I_S - I_L = 30.91 - 17.45 = 13.46\,\text{mA}$$

**Step 4: Compute power dissipation in Zener:**

$$P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_Z = 8.2 \times 13.46 = 110.4\,\text{mW}$$

**Step 5: Compute line regulation using small-signal model:**

$$S_V = \dfrac{\Delta V_{OUT}}{\Delta V_{IN}} = \dfrac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z} = \dfrac{8}{220 + 8} = 0.0351\,\text{V/V}$$

**Step 6: Efficiency calculation:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{OUT}}{P_{IN}} = \dfrac{V_Z \cdot I_L}{V_{IN} \cdot I_S} = \dfrac{8.2 \times 17.45\,\text{mW}}{15 \times 30.91\,\text{mW}} = \dfrac{143.1}{463.65} = 0.3086 \approx 30.86\%$$

**Step 7: Critical analysis (KTU-style comment):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The efficiency is **low (~31%)** because nearly 70% of power is dissipated in $R_S$ and the Zener. This is the fundamental drawback of shunt regulators — they are practical only for low-power, fixed-load applications.

## 3.3 Derivation: Buck Converter Steady-State Output

**Given:** $V_{IN} = 24\,\text{V}$, $D = 0.4$, $L = 100\,\mu\text{H}$, $C = 47\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_L = 10\,\Omega$, $f_{sw} = 50\,\text{kHz}$.

**Step 1: Apply volt-second balance on inductor (steady-state condition):**

$$V_L \cdot D \cdot T = -V_L \cdot (1-D) \cdot T$$

This gives the fundamental relation:

$$V_{OUT} = D \cdot V_{IN}$$

**Step 2: Compute $V_{OUT}$:**

$$V_{OUT} = 0.4 \times 24 = 9.6\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3: Compute load current:**

$$I_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{R_L} = \dfrac{9.6}{10} = 0.96\,\text{A}$$

**Step 4: Compute inductor current ripple $\Delta I_L$:**

For a buck converter in CCM:

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{(V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot D}{L \cdot f_{sw}}$$

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{(24 - 9.6) \times 0.4}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{3}} = \dfrac{5.76}{5} = 1.152\,\text{A}$$

**Step 5: Compute output voltage ripple $\Delta V_{OUT}$:**

$$\Delta V_{OUT} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{8 \cdot f_{sw} \cdot C} = \dfrac{1.152}{8 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 47 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.152}{18.8} = 0.0613\,\text{V}$$

**Step 6: Compute efficiency (assuming ideal components, $\eta = 100\%$):**

In a **real buck converter**, the dominant losses are:
- MOSFET conduction loss: $I^2 R_{DS,on}$
- Diode forward drop loss (or synchronous FET loss)
- Inductor ESR loss
- Switching loss: $\dfrac{1}{2} V_{IN} I_{OUT} (t_r + t_f) f_{sw}$

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{OUT}}{P_{OUT} + P_{losses}} \approx 92\text{–}95\%$$

**Step 7: KTU exam verification check (boundary condition):**

If $D = 0$ → $V_{OUT} = 0$ (switch always OFF, no power).
If $D = 1$ → $V_{OUT} = V_{IN}$ (switch always ON, direct connection).
This confirms physical intuition.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2: Side-by-side regulator comparison for exam answers**

| Parameter | Series (Linear) | Shunt (Zener) | IC (LM317) | Switching (Buck) |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------|------------|------------------|
| **Efficiency** | 40–60% | 25–40% | 50–65% | **85–95%** |
| **Complexity** | Medium | Low | Very Low | High |
| **Ripple** | Very Low | Medium | Low | Medium-High |
| **Heat Dissipation** | High | High | High | Very Low |
| **Cost** | Low | Lowest | Low | Higher |
| **Noise** | Low | Low | Low | **Higher (EMI)** |
| **Dropout** | $V_{BE}+V_Z$ | N/A | ~2V | None (ideal) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Feedback Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unregulated DC Input VIN] --> B[Pass Element Q1]
    B --> C[Filtered Output VOUT]
    C --> D[Sampling Network R1 R2]
    D --> E[Comparator Op-Amp]
    F[Reference Voltage VREF] --> E
    E -->|Error Signal| B
    C --> G[Load RL]
    H[Feedback Path beta] --> E
```

**Description:** This block diagram illustrates the universal architecture of any linear voltage regulator — error amplifier, reference, sampling, and pass element in a closed feedback loop.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Switching Regulator (Buck) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VIN DC Source] --> B[Switching MOSFET S1]
    B --> C[Inductor L]
    C --> D[Output Capacitor C]
    D --> E[Load RL]
    C --> F[Diode D1 to Ground]
    G[PWM Controller] -->|Gate Drive| B
    H[VOUT Sense] --> G
    I[Reference VR] --> G
```

**Description:** The MOSFET S1 is switched at high frequency. When ON, current ramps up in L; when OFF, D1 freewheels the inductor current. The output capacitor filters the pulsating waveform.

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Voltage Regulator Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Voltage Regulators] --> B[Linear Regulators]
    A --> C[Switching Regulators]
    B --> B1[Series Pass Regulator]
    B --> B2[Shunt Zener Regulator]
    B --> B3[IC Regulators 78xx 79xx LM317]
    B --> B4[LDO Regulators]
    C --> C1[Buck Step-Down]
    C --> C2[Boost Step-Up]
    C --> C3[Buck-Boost Inverting]
    C --> C4[Cuk Converter]
    C --> C5[SEPIC Converter]
```

## 4.4 LM317 Functional Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VIN Pin] --> B[Bandgap Reference 1.25V]
    A --> C[Darlington Pass Transistor]
    C --> D[VOUT Pin]
    B --> E[Error Amplifier]
    F[ADJ Pin] --> E
    E --> C
    G[Thermal Shutdown] --> C
    H[Current Limit] --> C
```

**Description:** Shows the three-terminal pinout of LM317 with internal bandgap reference, error amplifier, Darlington output, and protection circuitry (thermal shutdown + current limit).

## 4.5 Comparison: Series vs Shunt Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SeriesReg[Series Regulator]
        A1[VIN] --> A2[Q1 Pass Transistor]
        A2 --> A3[VOUT]
        A4[DZ Zener] --> A2
    end
    subgraph ShuntReg[Shunt Regulator]
        B1[VIN] --> B2[RS Series Resistor]
        B2 --> B3[VOUT]
        B4[DZ Zener to GND] --> B3
    end
```
