# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Differentiate between series and shunt voltage regulators. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| Position of pass element | In series with load | In parallel with load |
| Variable quantity | Current through transistor | Current through Zener |
| Efficiency | Higher (40–60%) | Lower (25–40%) |
| Short-circuit protection | Poor (needs current limit) | Excellent (inherent) |
| Load variation tolerance | Good | Poor |
| Power dissipation | In pass transistor | In $R_S$ + Zener |

**Advantages of Series Regulators:**
1. Better efficiency for varying loads.
2. Lower quiescent current → suitable for battery applications.

**Advantages of Shunt Regulators:**
1. Inherent short-circuit protection.
2. Simple, cheap, easy to design for fixed loads.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is a switching regulator? Why is its efficiency higher than that of a linear regulator?**

**Model Answer:**

A **switching regulator** is a voltage regulator in which the pass transistor operates as an electronic switch (either fully ON or fully OFF) at high frequency, controlling the duty cycle to regulate the output voltage. Energy is temporarily stored in an inductor or capacitor.

**Higher efficiency reasons:**
1. The pass transistor is either in saturation (low $V_{DS}$) or cutoff (zero current) — power dissipation is minimal in both states.
2. Only switching losses (small) and conduction losses (small) occur — not the full $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ drop of linear regulators.
3. Achieves **85–95% efficiency**, vs **30–60%** for linear regulators.
4. Less heat sinking required → smaller, cheaper designs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **series voltage regulator using op-amp feedback**. Derive the expression for output voltage and discuss the role of foldback current limiting.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a **Buck converter**, derive the expression for output voltage in terms of duty cycle. A buck converter has $V_{IN} = 20\,\text{V}$, $D = 0.6$, $R_L = 5\,\Omega$. Calculate (i) $V_{OUT}$, (ii) $I_{OUT}$, and (iii) the average inductor current.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A **series op-amp voltage regulator** uses a pass transistor $Q_1$ in series with the load, an op-amp as the error amplifier, and a Zener diode for reference.

**Circuit Operation:**
1. Unregulated $V_{IN}$ is applied to the collector of $Q_1$.
2. The emitter of $Q_1$ supplies the load $R_L$ with $V_{OUT}$.
3. A feedback network $R_1, R_2$ samples $V_{OUT}$ and produces $V_{FB} = V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
4. The op-amp compares $V_{FB}$ with reference $V_{REF} = V_Z$.
5. Op-amp output drives the base of $Q_1$, adjusting its conduction to maintain $V_{OUT}$ constant.

**Regulating Action:**
- If $V_{OUT} \uparrow$ → $V_{FB} \uparrow$ → $V_{OUT,opamp} \downarrow$ → $V_{BE} \downarrow$ → $I_C \downarrow$ → $V_{OUT} \downarrow$ (restored).
- If $V_{OUT} \downarrow$ → opposite chain → $V_{OUT}$ restored.

**Output Voltage Expression:**

At equilibrium, $V_{FB} = V_{REF}$ (virtual short principle of op-amp):

$$V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

Solving for $V_{OUT}$:

$$V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Foldback Current Limiting:**
Standard current limiting fixes the max current at a flat value, dissipating high power during short circuits. **Foldback limiting** reduces the current limit as $V_{OUT}$ drops toward zero, dramatically reducing power dissipation under fault conditions.

The current-limit sense resistor $R_{SC}$ develops a voltage across it. When the load current exceeds the threshold, $Q_2$ conducts, shunting base drive away from $Q_1$. As $V_{OUT}$ falls, an additional resistor network lowers the threshold further, "folding back" the limit curve.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Buck Converter Steady-State Derivation:**

A buck converter consists of a switch $S$, diode $D$, inductor $L$, and capacitor $C$ with $V_{IN}$ as source.

**During $T_{ON}$ (Switch Closed):**
The diode is reverse-biased. The inductor sees:
$$V_L = V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$$

**During $T_{OFF}$ (Switch Open):**
The diode conducts (freewheeling). The inductor sees:
$$V_L = -V_{OUT}$$

**Applying Volt-Second Balance** (for steady state, average $V_L = 0$):

$$(V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot T_{ON} = V_{OUT} \cdot T_{OFF}$$

Expanding:

$$V_{IN} \cdot T_{ON} - V_{OUT} \cdot T_{ON} = V_{OUT} \cdot T_{OFF}$$

$$V_{IN} \cdot T_{ON} = V_{OUT}(T_{ON} + T_{OFF}) = V_{OUT} \cdot T$$

Dividing both sides by $T$ and noting $D = T_{ON}/T$:

$$V_{OUT} = D \cdot V_{IN}$$

---

**Numerical Computations:**

Given: $V_{IN} = 20\,\text{V}$, $D = 0.6$, $R_L = 5\,\Omega$.

**(i) Output Voltage:**

$$V_{OUT} = D \cdot V_{IN} = 0.6 \times 20 = 12\,\text{V}$$

**(ii) Output Current:**

$$I_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{R_L} = \dfrac{12}{5} = 2.4\,\text{A}$$

**(iii) Average Inductor Current:**

In a buck converter, average inductor current = load current (assuming lossless components):

$$I_{L,avg} = I_{OUT} = 2.4\,\text{A}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design an **LM317 adjustable voltage regulator** to deliver $V_{OUT} = +12\,\text{V}$ at $I_L = 500\,\text{mA}$. Calculate the value of $R_2$, power dissipated in the LM317, and the efficiency if $V_{IN} = +18\,\text{V}$. State the required heatsink thermal resistance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **linear and switching regulators** in terms of efficiency, noise, complexity, and application. Draw the circuit of a **boost converter** and explain its operation in continuous conduction mode (CCM).

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1: Apply LM317 transfer function:**

$$V_{OUT} = V_{REF} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$$

with $V_{REF} = 1.25\,\text{V}$ and standard $R_1 = 240\,\Omega$.

**Step 2: Solve for $R_2$:**

$$12 = 1.25 \left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{240}\right)$$

$$\dfrac{12}{1.25} = 1 + \dfrac{R_2}{240}$$

$$9.6 = 1 + \dfrac{R_2}{240}$$

$$\dfrac{R_2}{240} = 8.6$$

$$R_2 = 8.6 \times 240 = 2064\,\Omega$$

Select standard value: $R_2 = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$ (gives $V_{OUT} \approx 12.7\,\text{V}$).

**Step 3: Power Dissipated in LM317:**

$$P_D = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L = (18 - 12) \times 0.5 = 6 \times 0.5 = 3\,\text{W}$$

**Step 4: Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{OUT}}{P_{IN}} = \dfrac{V_{OUT} \cdot I_L}{V_{IN} \cdot I_L} = \dfrac{12}{18} = 0.6667 = 66.67\%$$

**Step 5: Heatsink thermal resistance:**

Assume junction-to-ambient maximum $T_{J,max} = 125\,°\text{C}$ and ambient $T_A = 50\,°\text{C}$:

$$\theta_{SA} = \dfrac{T_{J,max} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} = \dfrac{125 - 50}{3} - \theta_{JC}$$

With $\theta_{JC}$ for TO-220 package typically $5\,°\text{C/W}$:

$$\theta_{SA} = 25 - 5 = 20\,°\text{C/W}$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Linear vs Switching Regulator Comparison:**

| Parameter | Linear Regulator | Switching Regulator |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Efficiency | 30–60% | **85–95%** |
| Output noise/ripple | **Very low** | Higher (requires filtering) |
| Circuit complexity | Simple | Complex (inductor, capacitor, controller) |
| EMI | Negligible | High (switching edges) |
| Heat sink | Often required | Minimal |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Transient response | Fast | Slower |
| Applications | RF, audio, sensors | CPUs, batteries, LEDs |

**Boost Converter Circuit and Operation:**

A boost (step-up) converter uses an inductor in series with $V_{IN}$, a switch to ground, a diode from the switch node to $V_{OUT}$, and an output capacitor.

**During $T_{ON}$ (Switch Closed):**
- Current builds up in inductor $L$ — energy stored.
- Diode is reverse-biased, isolating the load.
- Output capacitor supplies load current.

**During $T_{OFF}$ (Switch Open):**
- Inductor voltage reverses (Lenz's law), adding to $V_{IN}$.
- Diode becomes forward-biased, charging $C$ and supplying load.
- $V_{OUT} > V_{IN}$ (boost action).

**Steady-state relation (volt-second balance on $L$):**

$$V_{IN} \cdot T_{ON} = (V_{OUT} - V_{IN}) \cdot T_{OFF}$$

Solving:

$$V_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{IN}}{1 - D}$$

This shows $V_{OUT} \to \infty$ as $D \to 1$, confirming the step-up capability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Voltage Regulator Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting $I_{ADJ}$ term in LM317 derivation** → KTU expects $V_{OUT} = V_{REF}(1 + R_2/R_1) + I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$. Marks lost if you approximate too early.
> 2. **Confusing efficiency formulas** → Linear regulator efficiency = $V_{OUT}/V_{IN}$ (current cancels). Don't write $P_{OUT}/P_{IN}$ as a general formula.
> 3. **Drawing wrong reference direction in op-amp regulator** → The inverting input must go to the sampling network; non-inverting to $V_Z$. Drawing it reversed loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Buck and Boost transfer ratios** → Buck: $V_{OUT} = D V_{IN}$ (less than input). Boost: $V_{OUT} = V_{IN}/(1-D)$ (more than input). This is a 2-mark deduction if swapped.
> 5. **Not mentioning CCM vs DCM** in switching regulator answers → Examiner expects explicit mode of operation in your derivation.
> 6. **Skipping KCL/KVL step in Zener regulator** → The KCL equation $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ is mandatory; skipping it loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Power dissipation not calculated** in IC regulator design → Always compute $P_D$ and check against package rating. KTU often allocates 1 mark for this.
> 8. **No mention of dropout voltage** in linear regulator answers → 0.5 mark reserved for identifying $V_{DO}$ in 78xx vs LM317 vs LDO comparison.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Series regulators** place the pass transistor in series with the load; **shunt regulators** place it in parallel.
- **Series regulators** are more efficient than shunt regulators for varying loads.
- **Zener shunt regulators** use the Zener's reverse breakdown region ($V_Z$) as the reference; $I_S = I_Z + I_L$.
- **78xx/79xx** are **fixed** three-terminal IC regulators (positive/negative); **LM317** is **adjustable** with $V_{OUT} = 1.25(1 + R_2/R_1)$.
- **Dropout voltage** $V_{DO}$ is the minimum $(V_{IN} - V_{OUT})$ for regulation; **LDO regulators** reduce this to 100–500 mV.
- **Switching regulators** achieve high efficiency by operating the pass element as a switch, not a variable resistor.
- **Buck converter**: $V_{OUT} = D \cdot V_{IN}$ (step-down).
- **Boost converter**: $V_{OUT} = V_{IN}/(1-D)$ (step-up).
- **Buck-Boost converter**: $V_{OUT} = -V_{IN} \cdot D/(1-D)$ (inverting, can step up or down).
- **Efficiency of linear regulator**: $\eta = V_{OUT}/V_{IN}$ (always less than 100%).
- **Efficiency of ideal switching regulator**: 100% (lossless L, C, switch); practical **85–95%**.
- **Foldback current limiting** protects pass transistors under short circuits by reducing current limit as $V_{OUT}$ collapses.
- **Bandgap reference** in IC regulators provides temperature-stable $V_{REF}$ (typically 1.25V in LM317).
- **Darlington pair** is used as the pass element in LM317 to achieve high current gain and low dropout.
- **Thermal design**: Always compute $P_D$ and select heatsink with $\theta_{SA} \leq (T_{J,max} - T_A)/P_D - \theta_{JC}$.
- **PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio)** measures regulator's ability to reject input ripple → typically 60–80 dB for IC regulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of a Series Voltage Regulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of a Series Voltage Regulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Block Diagram of a Series Voltage Regulator

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU Syllabus Definition
A **Series Voltage Regulator** is a closed-loop linear voltage regulation circuit in which the **pass element (series pass transistor)** is placed in **series** between the unregulated DC input source and the load. The regulator continuously compares a fraction of the output voltage with an internal **reference voltage** and adjusts the conduction of the series pass transistor to maintain a **constant, ripple-free DC output voltage** irrespective of variations in input line voltage, load current, or temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The block diagram of a series regulator is the foundational building block for understanding IC regulators such as the **78xx / 79xx (fixed)** and **LM317 (adjustable)** series. Mastering this block diagram is mandatory because KTU frequently tests it as a direct 7-mark question in Part B.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Water Tap" Analogy)
Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline (unregulated DC input) feeding a garden tap that must deliver a *constant trickle of water* (regulated DC output) to a sensitive plant, even though the city supply pressure keeps fluctuating throughout the day.

- The **ball valve (pass transistor)** is connected *inline* with the pipe — not in a bypass path. When the input pressure rises, the valve pinches harder; when it falls, the valve opens more.
- A **pressure gauge (sampling network)** constantly measures the output pressure.
- A **spring-loaded controller (error amplifier)** compares this measured pressure to a *fixed reference pressure* (reference voltage) and automatically adjusts the valve opening.
- The **reference pressure tank (Zener/Bandgap reference)** is the unchanging benchmark.

This inline (series) placement is what gives the regulator its name — **Series Voltage Regulator**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Voltage Regulation:**
> - **Line Regulation** $\left(\frac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta V_{in}}\right)$ — measured in **V/V** or **%**.
> - **Load Regulation** $\left(\frac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta I_L}\right)$ — measured in **V/A** or **%**.
> - **Dropout Voltage** $V_{DO}$ — minimum $V_{in} - V_o$ for regulation; typically **2 V to 3 V** for standard series regulators.
> - **Power Dissipation** $P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L$ — critical thermal design parameter.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Functional Decomposition

A series voltage regulator is built from **four mandatory functional blocks** plus one optional block. Each block has a specific, non-overlapping responsibility:

### Block 1 — Reference Voltage Source ($V_{REF}$)
- Provides an **internally generated, temperature-stable DC voltage** independent of the unregulated input.
- Implemented using a **reverse-biased Zener diode** (basic design) or a **bandgap reference** (precision ICs).
- Acts as the **gold-standard benchmark** for comparison.

### Block 2 — Sampling Network (Feedback Divider)
- A **resistive voltage divider** $R_1$–$R_2$ connected across the output terminals.
- Taps off a **scaled replica** of $V_o$ and feeds it to the error amplifier.
- Sampling ratio: $V_{FB} = V_o \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$

### Block 3 — Error Amplifier (Comparator / Difference Amplifier)
- The **brain** of the regulator. A high-gain differential amplifier.
- Computes the **error voltage**: $V_{error} = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{REF} - V_{FB})$
- The high open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ (typically $10^3$ to $10^5$) forces $V_{FB} \to V_{REF}$ via **negative feedback**, which is the secret behind tight regulation.

### Block 4 — Pass Transistor (Series Pass Element)
- Operates in the **active (linear) region**, not in saturation or cutoff.
- For positive outputs, an **NPN Darlington pair** is used (high $\beta$, high current handling).
- For negative outputs, a **PNP transistor** is used.
- Acts as a **variable resistor** whose value $r_{CE}$ is dynamically controlled by $V_{error}$:
$$r_{CE} \approx \dfrac{V_{error}}{I_B} \cdot \beta$$

### Block 5 (Optional) — Current Limiting / Short-Circuit Protection
- A small **sense resistor** $R_S$ in series with the pass transistor's emitter.
- When load current exceeds the safe limit, the voltage across $R_S$ forward-biases a protection transistor that **steals base drive** from the pass transistor, clamping $I_L$.

## 2.2 The Closed-Loop Regulation Principle (Why It Works)

The system is a **negative-feedback closed loop**. The transfer function is:

$$V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{A_{OL}\beta}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$$

where the **feedback factor** is $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Infinite Gain Approximation" Trick (Key for KTU derivations):**
> When $A_{OL}\beta \gg 1$ (always true in practice), the transfer function collapses elegantly to:
> 
> $$V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{REF}}{\beta} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$
> 
> This means **$V_o$ depends ONLY on $V_{REF}$ and the resistor ratio** — NOT on the input voltage, transistor $\beta$, temperature, or load current. This is the entire reason regulators work.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Typical Value | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Feedback factor | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | dimensionless, $0 < \beta < 1$ | Set by sampling network |
| 2 | Output Voltage (closed loop) | $V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Volts (V) | KTU's most-tested equation |
| 3 | Error voltage | $V_{error} = A_{OL}(V_{REF} - V_{FB})$ | Volts (V) | $A_{OL}$ = open-loop gain |
| 4 | Pass transistor power | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L$ | Watts (W) | Determines heatsink size |
| 5 | Line regulation | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta V_{in}}$ | V/V or mV/V | Ideally $\to 0$ |
| 6 | Load regulation | $LD = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta I_L}$ | V/A or $\Omega$ | Ideally $\to 0$ |
| 7 | Dropout voltage | $V_{DO} = V_{in(min)} - V_o$ | V (typ. 2–3 V) | Min headroom for regulation |
| 8 | Current limit | $I_{L(max)} = \dfrac{0.7\text{ V}}{R_S}$ | Amperes (A) | $R_S$ = sense resistor |
| 9 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % (max 70–80%) | Series reg. is *not* a switcher |
| 10 | Ripple rejection | $PSRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{ripple(in)}}{V_{ripple(out)}}\right)$ | dB (typ. 40–80 dB) | Higher = better |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Series regulators are the heart of every **on-board voltage rail** in a desktop/laptop motherboard, mobile phone PMIC, Arduino Uno's 5 V rail, and laboratory bench power supplies. They are preferred over shunt regulators whenever the load current exceeds ~100 mA, because the pass transistor handles the current without burning it across a parallel resistor.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of a Series Voltage Regulator

**Given:** A series voltage regulator with reference voltage $V_{REF}$, sampling resistors $R_1$ (top, connected to $V_o$) and $R_2$ (bottom, connected to ground), and an error amplifier with open-loop gain $A_{OL}$.

**Goal:** Derive the closed-loop output voltage $V_o$.

### Step 1 — Identify the feedback voltage at the divider tap
The sampling network is a simple resistive divider. By the voltage divider rule:

$$V_{FB} = V_o \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \beta \cdot V_o$$

where we define the **feedback factor** $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ for compactness.

### Step 2 — Apply the differential amplifier equation
The error amplifier subtracts the feedback voltage from the reference and multiplies by the open-loop gain:

$$V_{error} = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{REF} - V_{FB})$$

### Step 3 — Express $V_o$ in terms of $V_{error}$ via the pass transistor stage
The pass transistor (NPN emitter follower) drops the difference $V_{in} - V_o$. In the active region, the small-signal transfer is approximately unity, so the control loop enforces:

$$V_o \approx V_{error} + V_{dropout}$$

For the idealized KTU derivation, $V_{dropout}$ is neglected, and we treat the pass stage as a unity-gain buffer driven by $V_{error}$:

$$V_o \approx V_{error} = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{REF} - V_{FB})$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $V_{FB} = \beta V_o$ from Step 1
Substituting the feedback voltage expression:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot \left(V_{REF} - \beta V_o\right)$$

### Step 5 — Expand and collect $V_o$ terms
Expanding the right-hand side:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot V_{REF} - A_{OL} \cdot \beta \cdot V_o$$

Bringing the $V_o$ term on the right to the left-hand side:

$$V_o + A_{OL} \cdot \beta \cdot V_o = A_{OL} \cdot V_{REF}$$

$$V_o \cdot (1 + A_{OL}\beta) = A_{OL} \cdot V_{REF}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $V_o$
Dividing both sides by $(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$:

$$V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$$

This is the **exact closed-loop transfer function**.

### Step 7 — Apply the high-gain approximation $A_{OL}\beta \gg 1$
For a practical error amplifier ($A_{OL} \approx 10^4$ to $10^5$) and typical $\beta \approx 0.1$ to $0.5$, the product $A_{OL}\beta$ is in the range $10^3$ to $10^5$, which is overwhelmingly greater than 1.

Therefore, in the denominator, $1 + A_{OL}\beta \approx A_{OL}\beta$, and the $A_{OL}$ in the numerator cancels:

$$V_o \approx V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{A_{OL}}{A_{OL}\beta} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{\beta}$$

### Step 8 — Substitute back $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$

$$\boxed{V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** that you must memorize and be able to derive from first principles.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Block-by-Block Functional Analysis

| Block | Input | Output | Mathematical Model | Failure Mode if Removed |
|-------|-------|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Reference Source | $V_{in}$ (raw) | $V_{REF}$ (stable) | $V_{REF} = f(\text{Zener } V_Z)$ or bandgap $V_{BG} \approx 1.25$ V | Output drifts with temp; no regulation possible |
| Sampling Network | $V_o$ | $V_{FB} = \beta V_o$ | $V_{FB} = V_o \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Loop cannot sense output; output floats |
| Error Amplifier | $V_{REF}, V_{FB}$ | $V_{error}$ | $V_{error} = A_{OL}(V_{REF} - V_{FB})$ | No error signal → pass transistor cannot be controlled |
| Pass Transistor | $V_{error}, V_{in}$ | $V_o$ (delivers $I_L$) | $V_o \approx V_{in} - V_{CE(sat)}$ or $V_{in} - V_{dropout}$ | No current delivery to load; open circuit |
| Current Limiter | $I_L$ | Steals base drive if $I_L > I_{max}$ | $I_{L(max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_S} = \dfrac{0.7}{R_S}$ | Regulator burns out on short circuit |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A series voltage regulator uses a Zener reference of $V_{REF} = 5.6$ V. The sampling resistors are $R_1 = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 1.0\text{ k}\Omega$. The unregulated input is $V_{in} = 15$ V and the load current is $I_L = 100$ mA. Calculate: (a) the regulated output voltage, (b) the power dissipated by the pass transistor, and (c) the efficiency.

**Solution:**

**(a) Output Voltage:**
$$V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = 5.6 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2.2}{1.0}\right) = 5.6 \cdot 3.2 = 17.92 \text{ V}$$

Wait — this exceeds the input! This means the regulator is **out of headroom**; the input must be raised above 18 V for regulation. KTU students often make this mistake. Let's correct by using $R_1 = 1.0\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$V_o = 5.6 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1.0}{2.2}\right) = 5.6 \cdot 1.4545 = 8.145 \text{ V}$$

**(b) Power Dissipated by Pass Transistor:**
$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L = (15 - 8.145) \cdot 0.1 = 6.855 \cdot 0.1 = 0.6855 \text{ W} \approx 686 \text{ mW}$$

**(c) Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{8.145}{15} \times 100\% = 54.3\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Notice that the efficiency is only ~54%. This is the **fundamental weakness of linear series regulators** — they are inherently inefficient when the input-to-output voltage drop is large. This is why **switch-mode power supplies (SMPS)** are used for high-current, high-drop applications.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Series Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN[Unregulated DC Input V_in] --> PASS
    PASS{{Series Pass Transistor NPN Darlington}}
    PASS --> VOUT[Regulated Output V_out]
    VOUT --> LOAD[Load R_L]
    LOAD --> GND1[(Ground)]
    VOUT --> SAMP
    SAMP[Sampling Network R1 and R2 Divider] --> FB[Feedback Voltage V_FB]
    FB --> ERRAMP
    REF[Reference Source V_REF Zener or Bandgap] --> ERRAMP
    ERRAMP[Error Amplifier High Gain Differential] --> CTRL[Control Signal V_error]
    CTRL --> PASS
    VIN --> REF
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram with All Internal Sub-Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Unregulated Input Stage]
        VIN1[DC Input V_in from Rectifier]
    end
    subgraph REG[Regulator Core]
        direction TB
        PASS1[Series Pass Element Q1 NPN Darlington]
        EA1[Error Amplifier A1 Op-Amp]
        SAMP1[Sampling Network R1 R2]
        REF1[Reference V_REF Zener D1]
        PROT[Current Limit and Thermal Shutdown Q2 and R_S]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Regulated Output]
        VOUT1[Clean DC Output V_out]
        LOAD1[Load R_L]
    end
    VIN1 --> PASS1
    PASS1 --> VOUT1
    VOUT1 --> LOAD1
    LOAD1 --> GND2[(Ground)]
    VOUT1 --> SAMP1
    SAMP1 -->|V_FB| EA1
    REF1 -->|V_REF| EA1
    EA1 -->|V_error| PASS1
    PROT -.->|Steals base drive| PASS1
    VIN1 -.->|Powers| REF1
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Signal Flow Diagram (Control Theory View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setpoint V_REF] --> SUM{{Summing Junction}}
    SUM -->|Error e| G1[Error Amplifier Gain A_OL]
    G1 --> G2[Pass Transistor Stage Gain approx 1]
    G2 --> B[Plant Output V_out]
    B --> H1[Sampling Network Beta]
    H1 -->|V_FB| SUM
    B -.->|Disturbance Delta V_in| SUM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The summing junction subtracts the feedback voltage from the reference. The error is amplified by $A_{OL}$, drives the pass stage, and produces the output. The closed-loop transfer function is the classic $V_o = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta} \cdot V_{REF}$ — derived in Section 3.1.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Signal-Flow Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Reference V_REF] --> CMP[Comparator Error Amp]
    DIV[Fb Divider R1 R2] --> CMP
    CMP --> CTRL[Base Drive]
    CTRL --> QPASS[Pass Transistor Q1]
    SRC2[Unreg Input V_in] --> QPASS
    QPASS --> OUT[Regulated V_out]
    OUT --> DIV
    OUT --> RL[Load R_L]
    RL --> GNDX[(GND)]
    SRC3[Sense R_S] --> QPROT[Protection Q2]
    QPROT --> CTRL
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Q1. List any **four** essential functional blocks of a series voltage regulator. Briefly state the role of each.
*(3 Marks — Module 4, CO2, Bloom Level: Remember)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Reference Voltage Source** — Provides a stable, temperature-independent DC voltage ($V_{REF}$) that serves as the comparison benchmark for the regulator.
2. **Sampling Network** — A resistive divider ($R_1, R_2$) that taps off a scaled fraction $\beta V_o$ of the output voltage and feeds it back to the error amplifier.
3. **Error Amplifier** — A high-gain differential amplifier that compares $V_{FB}$ with $V_{REF}$ and generates an error signal $V_{error} = A_{OL}(V_{REF} - V_{FB})$ to drive the pass transistor.
4. **Series Pass Transistor** — The active current-handling element connected in series between $V_{in}$ and the load; it dynamically adjusts its collector-emitter voltage to maintain a constant $V_o$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Define **line regulation** and **load regulation**. Why are they important parameters for a series voltage regulator?
*(3 Marks — Module 4, CO2, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Line Regulation** is defined as the change in regulated output voltage per unit change in the unregulated input voltage:
  $$LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta V_{in}} \quad \text{(units: V/V or \%/V)}$$
- **Load Regulation** is the change in output voltage per unit change in load current:
  $$LD = \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\Delta I_L} \quad \text{(units: V/A or mV/A)}$$
- **Importance:** Both parameters quantify how well the regulator rejects disturbances. A good series voltage regulator should ideally have $LR \to 0$ and $LD \to 0$, meaning the output remains **constant** regardless of fluctuations in the input line or the load demand. In IC regulator datasheets (e.g., 7805), these are specified in **mV** for a given $\Delta V_{in}$ or $\Delta I_L$ range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Module 4, CO2, Bloom Levels: Apply + Analyze*

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the operation of a **series voltage regulator**. Clearly identify all functional blocks and describe how negative feedback keeps the output voltage constant.
*(7 Marks — Bloom: Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

A series voltage regulator is a closed-loop linear regulator in which the regulating transistor (pass element) is connected in **series** between the unregulated DC input and the load. The complete block diagram is shown in Section 4.1 above. The operation is as follows:

1. **Unregulated DC input** $V_{in}$ (typically from a rectifier + filter) is applied to the **series pass transistor** $Q_1$.
2. The **reference source** (Zener diode or bandgap circuit) generates a stable $V_{REF}$ independent of $V_{in}$.
3. The **sampling network** $R_1$–$R_2$ senses the output and produces a feedback voltage:
   $$V_{FB} = V_o \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$
4. The **error amplifier** continuously compares $V_{FB}$ with $V_{REF}$ and produces:
   $$V_{error} = A_{OL} (V_{REF} - V_{FB})$$
5. This $V_{error}$ drives the base of the pass transistor, adjusting its $V_{CE}$.

**Negative Feedback Action:**
- If $V_o$ **increases** (e.g., due to a rise in $V_{in}$ or drop in $I_L$), then $V_{FB}$ rises, so $V_{REF} - V_{FB}$ becomes more negative, $V_{error}$ falls, the pass transistor conducts **less**, $V_{CE}$ increases, and $V_o$ is pulled back down.
- If $V_o$ **decreases**, the opposite sequence occurs — the pass transistor conducts more, restoring $V_o$.
- This continuous correction forces $V_{FB} \to V_{REF}$, giving the canonical result:
   $$V_o = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

Thus, $V_o$ depends **only on $V_{REF}$ and the resistor ratio**, making the output highly stable against line and load variations.

**Functional Blocks Summary (as required by KTU):**
| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Reference source | Stable comparison voltage |
| Sampling network | Output voltage sensing |
| Error amplifier | Error detection & amplification |
| Pass transistor | Series current control |
| Current limiter (optional) | Overload protection |

---

**Q. (b)** A series voltage regulator uses a Zener reference of $V_{REF} = 5.6$ V, $R_1 = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$. The unregulated input is $V_{in} = 18$ V and load current is $I_L = 150$ mA. Calculate: (i) regulated output voltage, (ii) power dissipated by the pass transistor, (iii) efficiency, and (iv) the minimum input voltage for which the regulator can maintain regulation (assume $V_{DO} = 2$ V).
*(7 Marks — Bloom: Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Output Voltage:**
$$V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = 5.6 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1.2}{2.2}\right) = 5.6 \cdot (1 + 0.5455)$$
$$V_o = 5.6 \cdot 1.5455 = 8.655 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Power Dissipated by Pass Transistor:**
$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L = (18 - 8.655) \cdot 0.150 = 9.345 \cdot 0.150 = 1.402 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{8.655}{18} \times 100\% = 48.08\%$$

**(iv) Minimum Input Voltage (Headroom Check):**
$$V_{in(min)} = V_o + V_{DO} = 8.655 + 2 = 10.655 \text{ V}$$

Since the actual $V_{in} = 18$ V $> 10.655$ V, the regulator operates safely in the active region with adequate headroom.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Alternative Pattern]**

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Derive the expression for the **regulated output voltage** $V_o$ of a series voltage regulator in terms of the reference voltage $V_{REF}$ and the sampling resistors $R_1, R_2$. State clearly the assumption used in the simplification.
*(7 Marks — Bloom: Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the exhaustive step-by-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes (Steps 1 → 8).

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$

**Assumption Used:** The product of the open-loop gain of the error amplifier and the feedback factor is much greater than unity:
$$A_{OL} \cdot \beta \gg 1$$
This is the **"infinite gain approximation"** (also called the "virtual short" between $V_{REF}$ and $V_{FB}$ at the input of the error amplifier). Under this condition, the exact transfer function
$$V_o = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$$
collapses to the simple closed-form result above.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **role of the pass transistor** in a series voltage regulator. Why is a **Darlington pair** often used in place of a single pass transistor? Derive the **power dissipation** in the pass transistor.
*(7 Marks — Bloom: Understand + Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**Role of the Pass Transistor:**
The series pass transistor is the **heart** of the regulator. It is placed in **series** between $V_{in}$ and the load, and acts as a **variable resistor** (or, more precisely, a voltage-controlled current source in the active region). It absorbs the difference between the unregulated input and the regulated output:
$$V_{CE}(Q_1) = V_{in} - V_o$$
By varying its base current $I_B$ in response to $V_{error}$ from the error amplifier, it modulates $I_C = \beta_{DC} I_B$ and therefore $V_{CE}$, keeping $V_o$ constant. The pass transistor **must** always operate in the **active (linear) region** — never in saturation or cutoff — for proper regulation.

**Why a Darlington Pair is Used:**
A single pass transistor has a finite current gain $\beta_{DC}$ (typically 50–200). At high load currents (e.g., $I_L = 1$ A), the required base current $I_B = I_L / \beta_{DC} \approx 10$ mA, which the error amplifier cannot source directly (op-amps typically source only 5–20 mA).

A **Darlington pair** (two cascaded transistors) provides a **super-multiplied current gain**:
$$\beta_{Darlington} = \beta_1 \cdot \beta_2 + \beta_1 + \beta_2 \approx \beta_1 \beta_2$$

So if $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = 100$, the effective gain is ~10,000. This means the required base drive from the error amplifier is reduced to microamperes, allowing the use of a low-power op-amp. The trade-off is a higher $V_{BE}$ drop (1.4 V instead of 0.7 V), which **increases dropout voltage**.

**Derivation of Power Dissipation:**
The pass transistor drops $V_{CE} = V_{in} - V_o$ and carries the full load current $I_C \approx I_L$. By the fundamental power equation:
$$P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L$$

This is the **heat** that the pass transistor must dissipate. For a TO-220 package rated at 1.5 W without heatsink and ~15 W with a proper heatsink, the designer must verify $P_D < P_{max}$ for the chosen package.

**Example:** For $V_{in} = 15$ V, $V_o = 5$ V, $I_L = 500$ mA:
$$P_D = (15 - 5) \cdot 0.5 = 5 \text{ W}$$ — requires a heatsink.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "high-gain approximation"** in $V_o$ derivations → lose 1–2 marks. Always explicitly state $A_{OL}\beta \gg 1$.
> 2. **Confusing Series vs Shunt regulators** — KTU frequently tests this distinction. In **series**, the pass element carries the load current *inline*; in **shunt**, the pass element is in *parallel* with the load and burns excess current.
> 3. **Missing the "headroom condition"** — if the calculated $V_o > V_{in} - V_{DO}$, the regulator **cannot regulate**; the output will simply be $V_{in} - V_{dropout}$. Always check $V_o < V_{in} - V_{DO}$.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw arrowheads** in the block diagram's signal flow → lose 0.5–1 mark for "neatness".
> 5. **Wrong sign convention** in error amplifier equation — write $V_{error} = A_{OL}(V_{REF} - V_{FB})$, not $(V_{FB} - V_{REF})$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Stick on Wall)**
> - [x] **Series regulator = inline pass transistor** between $V_{in}$ and load.
> - [x] **Four core blocks:** Reference → Sampling → Error Amplifier → Pass Transistor.
> - [x] **Master formula:** $V_o = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ — derived under the assumption $A_{OL}\beta \gg 1$.
> - [x] **Negative feedback** is the *only* reason the output stays constant; open-loop has zero regulation.
> - [x] **Darlington pair** is used at the pass transistor for high current gain ($\beta_1 \beta_2$).
> - [x] **Power dissipation** $P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_L$ — dictates heatsink requirement.
> - [x] **Dropout voltage** $V_{DO} \approx 2$–$3$ V is the minimum headroom; below this, regulation fails.
> - [x] **Efficiency** $\eta = V_o / V_{in}$ — series regulators are inherently inefficient (typically 40–70%).
> - [x] **Line regulation** $\Delta V_o / \Delta V_{in}$ — ideally zero.
> - [x] **Load regulation** $\Delta V_o / \Delta I_L$ — ideally zero.
> - [x] **Current limiting** via sense resistor $R_S$ prevents burn-out on short circuit.
> - [x] **Real-world ICs** based on this topology: **LM7805/LM7812** (fixed +ve), **LM7905** (fixed −ve), **LM317** (adjustable).
> - [x] **Always check** $V_o < V_{in} - V_{DO}$ before claiming the regulator works in your numerical.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series Voltage Regulator (Working Principle and Design for a Specific Output Voltage and Current)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Series Voltage Regulator (Working Principle and Design for a Specific Output Voltage and Current)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Series Voltage Regulator** is a linear, closed-loop voltage regulation topology in which a **pass transistor** (also called the *series control element*) is placed in **series** between the unregulated DC input supply and the regulated load. The pass transistor acts as a *variable resistance* whose instantaneous dynamic resistance is continuously modulated by a **negative feedback error amplifier** (often built using a discrete transistor pair or an op-amp). The amplifier compares a scaled sample of the output voltage with an internal **reference voltage** (usually generated by a Zener diode) and automatically adjusts the base/gate drive of the series element so that the load voltage is held constant despite variations in input voltage, load current, and temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag:** This topic is a cornerstone of Module 4 — *Voltage Regulators and Power Supplies*. KTU expects students to derive the output voltage, design component values, calculate power dissipation in the pass transistor, and add a current-limit / foldback protection network. Expect derivations and numerical design problems in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Linear Regulators by Topology:**
> - **Series Regulator** (pass element in series with load) — high efficiency, current flows continuously through the transistor.
> - **Shunt Regulator** (pass element in parallel with load) — current is diverted away from the load; older, low-power topology.
> - The *series* form dominates modern linear regulator ICs (LM317, LM7805, LM7812) because the pass transistor drops only the *excess* voltage, not the *full* current, yielding much higher efficiency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Float-Valve Water Tank"

Imagine a water storage tank supplying homes. A **float valve** sits at the inlet pipe:
- When the water level (load demand) drops, the float falls, the valve opens, and more water flows in.
- When the level rises, the float lifts, the valve throttles, and inflow reduces.
- A **reference level** (the pivot of the float arm) sets the desired height.

In a **series voltage regulator**:
- The **float valve** is the **pass transistor** (it controls the flow of current).
- The **water level** is the **output voltage** $V_O$.
- The **reference level** is the **Zener reference voltage** $V_Z$.
- The **float arm + linkage** is the **error amplifier + feedback network**.

If a downstream home suddenly opens many taps (load current increases), $V_O$ tries to drop. The error amplifier senses this, increases the base drive of the pass transistor, raises the collector–emitter conduction, and restores $V_O$ — exactly as the float valve opens wider when the tank level falls.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Silicon BJT base-emitter voltage in regulation:** $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ (at 25 °C)
- **Temperature coefficient of $V_{BE}$:** $-2\ \text{mV/°C}$ (used in $V_{BE}$-referenced designs)
- **Zener temperature coefficient:** typically $-2\ \text{mV/°C}$ for $V_Z > 5.6\ \text{V}$ (positive above knee), negative below.
- **Standard Zener voltages:** **3.3 V, 5.1 V, 5.6 V, 6.2 V, 9.1 V, 12 V**
- **Line regulation metric:** $\Delta V_O / \Delta V_{IN}$ — measured in **mV/V** or as a **percentage**.
- **Load regulation metric:** $\Delta V_O / \Delta I_L$ — measured in **mV/A** or **%/A**.
- **Ripple Rejection (PSRR):** expressed in **dB** (typical discrete series regulator: 40–60 dB).
- **Dropout voltage $V_{DO}$:** the minimum $V_{IN} - V_O$ required to keep the pass transistor in active region — typically **2 V to 3 V** for Darlington, **0.5 V to 1 V** for LDO designs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A *very common* 3-mark question is: *"Differentiate between series and shunt regulators."* Memorise the trade-off table — series is preferred above **~100 mA** because in a shunt regulator the pass transistor must carry the *full* $I_L + I_{shunt}$ current continuously, wasting power.

---

## 1.4 Why "Series" and Not "Shunt"? — Topology Decision

In a series regulator, the controlling element drops only the **excess voltage** $V_{IN} - V_O$. The current through it is exactly the load current $I_L$ (plus a small bias current). The power dissipation is:

$$P_{pass} = (V_{IN} - V_O) \cdot I_L$$

In a shunt regulator, the controlling element is in parallel with the load and must drop the **full** $V_O$ while carrying the *difference* between supply current and load current. The *wasted* power is always $V_O \cdot I_{shunt}$. For a varying load, $I_{shunt}$ is large when the load is light, wasting power. Hence for high-current or battery-powered designs, **series regulators win** on efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Series Voltage Regulator

A complete discrete series voltage regulator contains **four functional blocks**:

1. **Reference source** — Zener diode $D_Z$ biased through resistor $R_S$ from $V_{IN}$. Establishes $V_Z$ across itself.
2. **Sampling network** — Resistive divider $R_1$, $R_2$ that produces a feedback voltage $V_F$ proportional to $V_O$.
3. **Error amplifier / comparator** — A BJT (e.g., $Q_2$) or op-amp that compares $V_F$ with $V_Z$ and produces an error current/voltage.
4. **Series pass transistor** — $Q_1$ (often a power BJT or Darlington pair) whose collector connects to $V_{IN}$, emitter to $V_O$, and base is driven by the error amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> In the op-amp implementation, the error amplifier has very high gain (≥ 10⁵), giving extremely tight regulation. In the discrete two-transistor implementation, the gain is lower (β of $Q_2$ times $R_C$), but the design is cheap, rugged, and excellent for KTU design problems.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

Consider the classical **two-transistor series regulator** with op-amp error amplifier (the variant KTU most frequently tests):

1. **Input perturbation** — $V_{IN}$ increases (or load current $I_L$ decreases). Without regulation, $V_O$ would also rise.
2. **Sampling** — The divider $R_1$–$R_2$ senses this rise: $V_F = V_O \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ increases proportionally.
3. **Error generation** — The op-amp's differential input sees $V^+ - V^- = V_F - V_Z > 0$. Its output swings positive.
4. **Control action** — The op-amp output drives the *base* of $Q_1$ more positive, which (since $Q_1$ is an NPN emitter follower) *reduces* its base–emitter voltage $V_{BE1}$.
5. **Conduction reduction** — A smaller $V_{BE1}$ means $Q_1$ conducts less. The voltage drop $(V_{IN} - V_O)$ across $Q_1$ increases, *absorbing* the surplus of $V_{IN}$.
6. **Restoration** — $V_O$ is forced back to its nominal value. The output is now *clamped* to a value that makes the feedback voltage exactly equal the reference:

$$V_F = V_Z \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

The same logic in reverse (with inverted signs) handles a *decrease* in $V_{IN}$ or an *increase* in $I_L$ — the pass transistor simply conducts more.

## 2.3 The Core "Why" — Negative Feedback Forces Equality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative feedback forces the feedback signal to *equal* the reference at DC, within the limits set by the open-loop gain.** This single statement is the *philosophical* heart of every series regulator. At equilibrium (steady state), the error amplifier output is whatever base voltage is required to bias $Q_1$ correctly. The output voltage is *self-adjusting* until $V_F = V_Z$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table.** Every KTU Part B question on series regulators is solved by combining 2–3 rows of this table. The delimiter inside each cell uses the LaTeX `\vert` (or `\mid`) command — **never** the literal pipe `|`, which would break the markdown table.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | Feedback sample voltage | $V_F = V_O \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Volts; $R_1$ is the *upper* resistor (from $V_O$ to op-amp $V^+$) | Standard non-inverting sampling |
| 2 | **Regulated output voltage** | $V_O = V_Z \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = V_Z \left( 1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2} \right)$ | Volts; holds when $V_{IN} \geq V_O + V_{CE1(sat)}$ | **The most tested formula** |
| 3 | Op-amp / error output | $V_{err} = V_O + V_{BE1}$ | Volts | If $Q_1$ is single NPN |
| 4 | Op-amp / error output (Darlington) | $V_{err} = V_O + V_{BE1} + V_{BE2}$ | Volts | Add one $V_{BE}$ per extra stage |
| 5 | Pass transistor power | $P_{Q_1} = (V_{IN} - V_O) \cdot I_L$ | Watts | Worst case = max $V_{IN}$ × max $I_L$ |
| 6 | Total efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_O}{V_{IN}} \cdot 100\ \%$ | Percent | Series linear regulator *never* exceeds $\eta = V_O / V_{IN}$ |
| 7 | Zener bias resistor | $R_S = \dfrac{V_{IN(\min)} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_{L(\text{bias to op-amp})}}$ | Ohms | $I_Z$ chosen at $0.25 \cdot I_{Z(\max)}$ |
| 8 | Current-limit sense resistor | $R_{sense} = \dfrac{V_{BE(Q_3)}}{I_{L(\max)}}$ | Ohms; $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V | Where $Q_3$ is the limit transistor |
| 9 | Current-limit threshold | $I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE(Q_3)}}{R_{sense}}$ | Amperes | Adjust $R_{sense}$ to set $I_{L(\max)}$ |
| 10 | Foldback sense resistor | $R_a = \dfrac{V_{O(\text{sc})} + V_{BE3}}{I_{L(\max)}}$ | Ohms | $V_{O(\text{sc})}$ is the short-circuit output |
| 11 | Foldback top resistor | $R_b = \dfrac{R_a \cdot (V_O - V_{O(\text{sc})})}{V_{BE3}}$ | Ohms | Sets the "knee" of foldback curve |
| 12 | Line regulation | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}} \bigg\vert_{\Delta I_L = 0}$ | V/V or %/V | Should be very small |
| 13 | Load regulation | $LD = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta I_L} \bigg\vert_{\Delta V_{IN} = 0}$ | V/A or %/A | Should be very small |
| 14 | Minimum input (dropout) | $V_{IN(\min)} = V_O + V_{CE1(\text{sat})} \approx V_O + 0.3$ V | Volts | For LDO use $\approx V_O + 0.1$ V |
| 15 | Ripple rejection (approx.) | $PSRR \approx A_{OL} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{out(\text{open})}}{Z_{out(\text{closed})}}$ | dB | High open-loop gain ⇒ high PSRR |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **On-board DC regulation** in nearly every consumer electronic device: the LM78xx series, LM317 (adjustable), and modern LDO chips (LP2985, TPS7A47) are all series regulators.
- **Battery management**: Low-dropout (LDO) regulators use a P-channel MOSFET as the series element to extend battery life by operating with $V_{IN}$ only ~100 mV above $V_O$.
- **Instrumentation & ADC reference rails**: When a clean, low-noise reference is required (e.g., for a 24-bit ADC), a series regulator post-filtered by an LC network is preferred over a switching supply.
- **Lab bench power supplies**: Almost all linear bench supplies use a series pass transistor (often a power Darlington or MOSFET) preceded by a toroidal transformer and rectifier.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Output Voltage Equation

**Setup.** The op-amp negative input is tied to the Zener reference $V_Z$. The positive input is the mid-point of the divider $R_1$–$R_2$ connected across the output $V_O$. The op-amp output drives the base of the NPN pass transistor $Q_1$, whose emitter is the regulated output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working assumption:** The op-amp is ideal — infinite input impedance, zero input current, infinite open-loop gain. With infinite gain, the differential input voltage in the closed loop tends to zero: $V^+ - V^- = 0$, i.e. $V^+ = V^-$. This is the **virtual short** principle.

**Step 1.** Write the voltage at the op-amp's inverting input (the reference node):

$$V^- = V_Z$$

**Step 2.** Write the voltage at the op-amp's non-inverting input (the divider mid-point). Treating the op-amp's input as drawing no current, the divider carries only $I_{div} = V_O / (R_1 + R_2)$:

$$V^+ = V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the virtual short — at DC equilibrium:

$$V^+ = V^- \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $V_O$ by multiplying both sides by $(R_1 + R_2)/R_2$:

$$V_O = V_Z \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = V_Z \left( 1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2} \right)$$

This is the **canonical output voltage expression** for a series voltage regulator with an op-amp error amplifier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** $V_O$ is set *only* by the Zener reference and the resistor ratio. It is *independent* of $V_{IN}$, $I_L$, temperature, and transistor β. This is the magic of negative feedback.

**Step 5.** (Optional) Solve for $R_1$ in terms of a chosen $R_2$:

$$R_1 = R_2 \left( \frac{V_O}{V_Z} - 1 \right)$$

This is the *design-friendly* form: pick $R_2$ first (a standard value, e.g., 1 kΩ or 10 kΩ), then compute $R_1$. A fine-tuning potentiometer is often placed in series with $R_1$ to allow precise setting of $V_O$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Current-Limit Threshold

**Setup.** Add a third transistor $Q_3$ (NPN) whose base is tied to the *opposite* side of a small sense resistor $R_{sense}$ that sits in series with the load. $Q_3$'s emitter connects to $V_O$, and its collector ties to the base of $Q_1$. When the load current $I_L$ is excessive, the IR drop across $R_{sense}$ exceeds $V_{BE3(\text{on})} \approx 0.7$ V, switching $Q_3$ on and *robbing* base drive from $Q_1$.

**Step 1.** Write the voltage at the right end of $R_{sense}$ (= the load side):

$$V_{R_{sense},\, R} = V_O$$

**Step 2.** Write the voltage at the left end of $R_{sense}$ (= the $Q_3$ base side):

$$V_{R_{sense},\, L} = V_O + I_L \cdot R_{sense}$$

**Step 3.** The voltage at $Q_3$'s base is therefore:

$$V_{B3} = V_O + I_L \cdot R_{sense}$$

**Step 4.** The voltage at $Q_3$'s emitter is $V_O$, so:

$$V_{BE3} = V_{B3} - V_{E3} = (V_O + I_L \cdot R_{sense}) - V_O = I_L \cdot R_{sense}$$

**Step 5.** $Q_3$ begins to conduct when $V_{BE3}$ reaches the cut-in voltage, taken as **0.7 V**. At this instant, the load current is *limited*:

$$V_{BE3(\text{on})} = I_{L(\max)} \cdot R_{sense}$$

$$\boxed{\; I_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{BE3(\text{on})}}{R_{sense}} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{R_{sense}} \;}$$

**Step 6.** Solving for the design value of $R_{sense}$ given a desired $I_{L(\max)}$:

$$R_{sense} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{I_{L(\max)}}$$

For example, to limit at **1 A**: $R_{sense} = 0.7 / 1 = 0.7\ \Omega$ (with adequate power rating $P = I^2 R = 1 \times 0.7 = 0.7$ W, derate to 2 W).

> [!WARNING]
> **Foldback vs. Constant-Current Limit:** A simple current limit *holds* $I_L$ at $I_{L(\max)}$ indefinitely even under short-circuit. The pass transistor then dissipates $P = V_{IN} \cdot I_{L(\max)}$, which can destroy it. **Foldback limiting** (Section 3.4) reduces the current further as $V_O$ collapses, drastically reducing short-circuit dissipation.

## 3.3 Pass Transistor Power Dissipation — Worst Case Design

**Step 1.** The instantaneous power in the pass transistor is:

$$P_{Q_1} = (V_{IN} - V_O) \cdot I_L$$

**Step 2.** Worst-case occurs at *maximum* input voltage and *maximum* continuous load current:

$$P_{Q_1(\max)} = (V_{IN(\max)} - V_O) \cdot I_{L(\max)}$$

**Step 3.** The transistor's safe operating area (SOA) requires the chosen device to have a $P_D$ rating **at least 1.5× to 2×** the calculated $P_{Q_1(\max)}$, with a proper heatsink sized to keep the junction below 100 °C (or the manufacturer's $T_J(\max) = 150$ °C).

**Step 4.** Required thermal resistance of the heatsink:

$$\theta_{JA} \leq \frac{T_{J(\max)} - T_A}{P_{Q_1(\max)}}$$

For example: $T_{J(\max)} = 150$ °C, $T_A = 50$ °C, $P = 10$ W:

$$\theta_{JA} \leq \frac{150 - 50}{10} = 10\ \text{°C/W}$$

This is the *total* thermal budget; subtract the transistor's $\theta_{JC}$ (junction-to-case) and the insulator's $\theta_{CS}$ (case-to-sink) to find the required sink resistance.

## 3.4 Foldback Current Limiting — Analytical Derivation

Foldback uses two sensing resistors and a feedback divider to make $I_L(\max)$ itself a *function* of $V_O$. As $V_O$ collapses toward 0 V (toward a short), the *allowed* $I_L$ also collapses — hence the name *foldback*.

**Step 1.** The foldback transistor $Q_3$ has its base fed by the divider formed by $R_a$ (in series with load) and $R_b$ (from base to ground). The base voltage is:

$$V_{B3} = V_O + I_L \cdot R_a \cdot \frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}$$

**Step 2.** Equating $V_{BE3} = V_{B3} - V_O$ to 0.7 V at the limit:

$$V_{BE3(\text{on})} = I_L \cdot \frac{R_a \cdot R_b}{R_a + R_b} = 0.7\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3.** Setting $I_L = I_{L(\max)}$ at *full* output $V_O$, and at *short-circuit* $V_{O(\text{sc})}$ for the foldback knee:

- At full $V_O$: $\dfrac{R_a \cdot R_b}{R_a + R_b} = \dfrac{0.7}{I_{L(\max)}}$
- At $V_{O(\text{sc})}$: $\dfrac{R_a \cdot R_b}{R_a + R_b} = \dfrac{0.7}{I_{SC}}$ where $I_{SC} \ll I_{L(\max)}$

Choosing $I_{SC}$ (e.g., 20 % of $I_{L(\max)}$) sets the ratio $R_b / (R_a + R_b) = I_{L(\max)} / I_{SC}$.

**Step 4.** Choose $R_a$ for the appropriate power dissipation in normal operation, then compute $R_b$:

$$R_b = R_a \cdot \frac{I_{L(\max)} - I_{SC}}{I_{SC}}$$

This is a classic KTU design task — expect it in Part B (14-mark question).

## 3.5 Worked Example — Complete Design Walkthrough

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Style Numerical:** Try to solve this *before* reading the solution. Then check your work step by step.

**Specification:** Design a series voltage regulator with the following requirements.

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Nominal input $V_{IN}$ | **15 V DC** (ripple ±2 V) |
| Regulated output $V_O$ | **9 V** |
| Maximum load current $I_{L(\max)}$ | **500 mA** |
| Reference Zener $V_Z$ | **5.6 V** (at $I_Z = 10$ mA) |
| Pass transistor | 2N3055 (NPN, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $h_{FE} \approx 50$, $P_D = 115$ W) |
| Current limit required | Yes, with foldback ratio $I_{SC}/I_{L(\max)} = 0.2$ |

**Step 1 — Output voltage resistor ratio.** From $V_O = V_Z(1 + R_1/R_2)$:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{V_O}{V_Z} - 1 = \frac{9}{5.6} - 1 = 1.6071 - 1 = 0.6071$$

Choose $R_2 = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$ (standard). Then:

$$R_1 = 0.6071 \times 1200 = 728.6\ \Omega \approx 750\ \Omega \ \text{(use 680}\ \Omega \text{ + 100}\ \Omega\ \text{trimmer in series)}$$

**Step 2 — Zener bias resistor $R_S$.** Minimum $V_{IN} = 13$ V. Allow 10 mA Zener current and 1 mA op-amp bias:

$$R_S = \frac{V_{IN(\min)} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_{bias}} = \frac{13 - 5.6}{10\ \text{mA} + 1\ \text{mA}} = \frac{7.4}{0.011} = 672.7\ \Omega \approx 680\ \Omega$$

Power in $R_S$: $P = (V_{IN} - V_Z)^2 / R_S = (9.4)^2 / 680 = 0.130$ W → use a **0.25 W** resistor.

**Step 3 — Pass transistor base current.** At $I_L = 500$ mA and $h_{FE} = 50$:

$$I_{B1} = \frac{I_L}{h_{FE}} = \frac{500}{50} = 10\ \text{mA}$$

The op-amp must source/sink this. Standard 741 op-amp handles ±20 mA, so this is feasible.

**Step 4 — Worst-case power dissipation in $Q_1$.**

$$P_{Q_1(\max)} = (V_{IN(\max)} - V_O) \cdot I_{L(\max)} = (17 - 9) \times 0.5 = 4\ \text{W}$$

The 2N3055 in TO-3 package with a small clip-on heatsink ($\theta_{JA} \approx 25$ °C/W) is more than adequate.

**Step 5 — Current-limit sense resistor.**

$$R_{sense} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{I_{L(\max)}} = \frac{0.7}{0.5} = 1.4\ \Omega$$

Power: $P = I^2 R = 0.25 \times 1.4 = 0.35$ W → use a **1 W** wirewound.

**Step 6 — Foldback resistors.** Choose $I_{SC} = 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.1$ A.

Let $R_a = 1.4\ \Omega$ (same as $R_{sense}$, for simplicity). Then:

$$R_b = R_a \cdot \frac{I_{L(\max)} - I_{SC}}{I_{SC}} = 1.4 \times \frac{0.5 - 0.1}{0.1} = 1.4 \times 4 = 5.6\ \Omega$$

(Rounded to standard **5.6 Ω**, 0.5 W.)

**Step 7 — Verify foldback at short circuit.** At $V_O = 0$:

$$V_{BE3(\text{on})} = I_{SC} \cdot \frac{R_a \cdot R_b}{R_a + R_b} = 0.1 \times \frac{1.4 \times 5.6}{1.4 + 5.6} = 0.1 \times 1.12 = 0.112\ \text{V}$$

This is *below* the 0.7 V turn-on, so $Q_3$ stays off — wait, this is a problem! The foldback is too aggressive. **Correction:** At $V_O = 0$, we need $V_{BE3} = 0.7$ V, so we must increase the divider. Recompute with $R_b = 5.6\ \Omega$ replaced with the correct condition:

$$\frac{R_a \cdot R_b}{R_a + R_b} = \frac{0.7}{0.1} = 7\ \Omega$$

With $R_a = 1.4\ \Omega$:

$$\frac{1.4 R_b}{1.4 + R_b} = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.4 R_b = 7 R_b + 9.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_b = -\frac{9.8}{5.6} \quad (\text{negative!})$$

This means we need a *larger* $R_a$. Let $R_a = 10\ \Omega$ (it will dissipate $I^2 R = 0.25 \times 10 = 2.5$ W; use 5 W).

$$\frac{10 R_b}{10 + R_b} = 7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10 R_b = 70 + 7 R_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_b = \frac{70}{3} = 23.3\ \Omega$$

Use **$R_a = 10\ \Omega$ (5 W)** and **$R_b = 24\ \Omega$ (0.5 W)**. This achieves proper foldback: at full $V_O = 9$ V, $I_L$ is allowed up to **500 mA**; at short circuit, $I_L$ folds back to **100 mA**, drastically reducing the 17 V × 0.1 A = 1.7 W dissipation under fault.

**Step 8 — Final Component Summary.**

| Component | Value | Rating |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| $R_1$ | 750 Ω (or 680 + 100 trimmer) | 0.25 W |
| $R_2$ | 1.2 kΩ | 0.25 W |
| $R_S$ | 680 Ω | 0.25 W |
| $R_a$ | 10 Ω | 5 W |
| $R_b$ | 24 Ω | 0.5 W |
| $R_{sense}$ (alternative) | 1.4 Ω | 1 W |
| $D_Z$ | 5.6 V Zener (1N4734) | 1 W |
| $Q_1$ | 2N3055 | $P_D$ ≥ 10 W with heatsink |
| $Q_2$ (error BJT) | BC547 | Small signal |
| $Q_3$ (limit BJT) | BC547 | Small signal |
| Op-amp | µA741 | Single supply ok |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Series Voltage Regulator

The Mermaid diagram below is a **block-level functional architecture** showing all four functional blocks and their signal interconnections. It uses only alphanumeric node IDs (prefixed with letters) and clean uppercase text inside node labels — strictly following the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeVIN["UNREGULATED DC INPUT V_IN"] --> nodeBLK1
    nodeBLK1["BLOCK 1 REFERENCE SOURCE ZENER DZ AND BIAS RS"] --> nodeREF["REFERENCE VOLTAGE V_Z"]
    nodeBLK1 --> nodeBLK2["BLOCK 4 PASS TRANSISTOR Q1 SERIES ELEMENT"]
    nodeBLK2 --> nodeVOUT["REGULATED DC OUTPUT V_O TO LOAD"]
    nodeVOUT --> nodeBLK3A["BLOCK 3 SAMPLING NETWORK R1 R2 DIVIDER"]
    nodeBLK3A --> nodeFB["FEEDBACK VOLTAGE V_F PROPORTIONAL TO V_O"]
    nodeFB --> nodeBLK3B["BLOCK 2 ERROR AMPLIFIER COMPARES V_F WITH V_Z"]
    nodeREF --> nodeBLK3B
    nodeBLK3B --> nodeCTRL["CONTROL SIGNAL BASE DRIVE TO Q1"]
    nodeCTRL --> nodeBLK2
    nodeVOUT --> nodeBLK5["BLOCK 5 CURRENT LIMIT AND FOLDBACK Q3 RSENSE"]
    nodeBLK5 --> nodeCTRL
    nodeLOAD["LOAD R_L DEMANDS I_L"] -.-> nodeVOUT
```

## 4.2 Complete Circuit Schematic (ASCII-Art Mermaid)

The following detailed schematic shows the resistor–transistor–op-amp interconnections, with every node labelled using safe alphanumeric identifiers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["V_IN 15V DC"] --> A2["R_S 680 OHM"]
    A2 --> A3["NODE A V_Z 5.6V ZENER DZ TO GROUND"]
    A3 --> A4["OPAMP INVERTING INPUT V_MINUS"]
    A1 --> A5["Q1 2N3055 COLLECTOR"]
    A5 --> A6["Q1 EMITTER V_O 9V OUTPUT"]
    A4 --> A7["OPAMP OUTPUT"]
    A7 --> A8["Q1 BASE"]
    A6 --> A9["R_1 750 OHM"]

    A9 --> A12["OPAMP NON INVERTING INPUT V_PLUS"]
    A6 --> B1["R_SENSE 1.4 OHM"]

    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> B4["Q3 COLLECTOR TIES TO Q1 BASE STEALING DRIVE"]

    C1 --> C2["GROUND"]
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow / Control-Loop Diagram

This diagram emphasises the **negative feedback loop** — note how the arrows form a closed cycle, with the comparator output *inverting* the error.

```mermaid

    S1["V_IN DISTURBANCE OR LOAD CHANGE"] --> S2["PASS TRANSISTOR Q1"]

    S4 --> S5["FEEDBACK V_F"]
    S5 --> S6["COMPARATOR V_F MINUS V_Z"]

    S3 --> S8["CURRENT SENSE R_SENSE"]

    S9 --> S7
```

## 4.4 Load-Current vs. Output-Voltage Characteristic (Foldback Curve)

The Mermaid graph below plots the **foldback characteristic** — note how $I_L$ is allowed up to 500 mA at full $V_O = 9$ V, but folds back to 100 mA at $V_O = 0$ (short circuit). This is implemented by proper selection of $R_a$ and $R_b$ as derived in Section 3.4.

