# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Answering Strategy (3-mark questions):** State the formula, define each symbol, then either sketch the response or give a one-line explanation. Avoid essays — KTU examiners want **3 crisp lines**.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**State and explain the working principle of a series voltage regulator with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A series voltage regulator is a closed-loop linear regulator in which the **pass transistor** $Q_1$ is connected in **series** between the unregulated input $V_{IN}$ and the regulated output $V_O$. The four functional blocks are: **reference source** (Zener $D_Z$ with bias $R_S$), **sampling network** ($R_1$–$R_2$ divider), **error amplifier** (op-amp or BJT), and **series pass element** $Q_1$.

**Working principle:** Any increase in $V_{IN}$ or decrease in $I_L$ tends to raise $V_O$. The divider samples this rise, the op-amp compares the sample $V_F$ with the reference $V_Z$, and the resulting *error* signal *reduces* the base drive of $Q_1$. The pass transistor's conduction drops, increasing the voltage drop $(V_{IN} - V_O)$ across it, *absorbing* the surplus. $V_O$ is restored to its nominal value. The steady-state condition is $V_F = V_Z$, giving the canonical relation $V_O = V_Z(1 + R_1/R_2)$. [Block diagram to be drawn — see Section 4.1.]

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the importance of a current-limit circuit in a series voltage regulator. How is it implemented using a sense resistor and a BJT?**

**Model Answer:**

A **current-limit circuit** protects the pass transistor and the load from **overcurrent faults** (e.g., load short circuit). Without it, the pass transistor would dissipate $P = (V_{IN} - V_O) \times I_L$ which can rapidly exceed the SOA and destroy the device.

**Implementation:** A small **sense resistor** $R_{sense}$ is inserted in series with the load. The IR drop $I_L R_{sense}$ is applied across the base–emitter junction of a **current-sense BJT** $Q_3$. When $I_L R_{sense} \geq V_{BE3(\text{on})} \approx 0.7$ V, $Q_3$ turns ON and *steals* base drive from the pass transistor $Q_1$, throttling it. The current-limit threshold is:

$$I_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{BE3(\text{on})}}{R_{sense}} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{R_{sense}}$$

In **foldback** versions, a divider $R_a$–$R_b$ makes the limit current itself a function of $V_O$, reducing short-circuit dissipation to a small fraction of the full-load value.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks with sub-parts of (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. There is *internal choice* — students answer either Q-A OR Q-B. Both choices are given below. Each sub-question must be answered with a *diagram*, the *formula*, the *substitution*, and a *one-line conclusion*.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the regulated output voltage of a series voltage regulator using an op-amp error amplifier. State all assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Op-amp is ideal — infinite open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance.
2. Zener diode $D_Z$ is ideal — constant $V_Z$ over the operating current range.
3. Pass transistor $Q_1$ is in the active region — $V_{CE1} > V_{CE(sat)}$.
4. The system is in DC steady state — no AC ripple analysis here.

**Derivation:**

Apply KCL at the op-amp's two inputs. Since the op-amp draws *no* input current, the current through $R_1$ and $R_2$ is the same:

$$I_{div} = \frac{V_O}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The voltage at the *non-inverting* input is:

$$V^+ = I_{div} \cdot R_2 = V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The voltage at the *inverting* input is the Zener reference:

$$V^- = V_Z$$

By the **virtual short** principle (since $A_{OL} \to \infty$ forces the differential input to zero at DC):

$$V^+ = V^- \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

Solving for $V_O$:

$$\boxed{\; V_O = V_Z \left( 1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2} \right) \;}$$

**Conclusion:** The output voltage is set entirely by the Zener reference and the resistor ratio — *independent* of input voltage, load current, and transistor parameters. This is the strength of negative feedback.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Design a series voltage regulator with the following specifications: $V_{IN} = 15 \pm 3$ V, $V_O = 12$ V, $I_{L(\max)} = 1$ A, $V_Z = 5.6$ V. Use an op-amp error amplifier. Calculate the worst-case pass-transistor power dissipation and the value of the current-sense resistor for a simple current limit. Verify that the pass transistor's SOA is not exceeded if a 2N3055 ($P_D = 115$ W with infinite heatsink, $T_J(\max) = 200$ °C, $\theta_{JC} = 1.5$ °C/W) is used with a heatsink of $\theta_{SA} = 5$ °C/W and ambient $T_A = 50$ °C.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Resistor ratio.** With $V_O = 12$ V, $V_Z = 5.6$ V:

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{V_O}{V_Z} - 1 = \frac{12}{5.6} - 1 = 2.143 - 1 = 1.143$$

Choose $R_2 = 1.0$ kΩ (standard). Then $R_1 = 1.143 \times 1000 = 1143\ \Omega \approx 1.2$ kΩ (use 1 kΩ + 200 Ω trimmer in series, or 1.1 kΩ + 47 Ω fixed).

**Step 2 — Zener bias resistor.** $V_{IN(\min)} = 12$ V, $I_Z = 10$ mA, $I_{bias} = 1$ mA:

$$R_S = \frac{12 - 5.6}{11\ \text{mA}} = \frac{6.4}{0.011} = 581.8\ \Omega \approx 560\ \Omega \quad (0.25\ \text{W})$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case pass-transistor power.** $V_{IN(\max)} = 18$ V, $I_{L(\max)} = 1$ A:

$$P_{Q_1(\max)} = (V_{IN(\max)} - V_O) \cdot I_{L(\max)} = (18 - 12) \times 1 = 6\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Current-sense resistor.**

$$R_{sense} = \frac{V_{BE3(\text{on})}}{I_{L(\max)}} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{1\ \text{A}} = 0.7\ \Omega$$

Power rating: $P = I^2 R = 1 \times 0.7 = 0.7$ W → use **1.5 W** wirewound.

**Step 5 — Verify the 2N3055 SOA.** Total thermal resistance:

$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA} = 1.5 + 0.5\ (\text{mica washer}) + 5 = 7\ \text{°C/W}$$

Maximum junction temperature at $P = 6$ W and $T_A = 50$ °C:

$$T_J = T_A + P \cdot \theta_{JA} = 50 + 6 \times 7 = 50 + 42 = 92\ \text{°C}$$

Since $T_J = 92$ °C $ \ll T_{J(\max)} = 200$ °C, the design is **safe with a wide margin**. The transistor is comfortably within its SOA.

**Conclusion:** The series voltage regulator meets the specification. The output is fixed at 12 V by the feedback equation, the pass transistor dissipates a worst-case 6 W (well within the 2N3055's capability), and the current limit trips at 1 A with a 0.7 Ω sense resistor.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a series voltage regulator with **foldback current limiting**. Derive the expression for the current-limit knee.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** The basic series regulator is augmented with a third transistor $Q_3$ whose base is connected to the *junction* of a voltage divider formed by $R_a$ (in series with the load return) and $R_b$ (to ground). $Q_3$'s collector ties to the base of $Q_1$.

**Operation:**

1. In normal operation, $I_L \cdot R_a \cdot R_b/(R_a + R_b) < 0.7$ V, so $Q_3$ is OFF. The regulator functions as a standard series regulator.
2. As $I_L$ increases (or $V_O$ begins to drop due to overload), the voltage at $Q_3$'s base *rises*. When it reaches $V_{BE3(\text{on})}$, $Q_3$ turns ON.
3. $Q_3$ robs base drive from $Q_1$, reducing $I_L$ and *lowering* $V_O$ further.
4. Lower $V_O$ means a *higher* fraction of $V_{B3}$ is determined by $I_L R_a$ (since the divider effect of $R_b$ is reduced as $V_O$ falls), so an even smaller $I_L$ is needed to keep $V_{BE3}$ at 0.7 V.
5. The net effect: as $V_O$ collapses from 12 V toward 0 V (short circuit), the *allowed* $I_L$ *folds back* from 1 A to perhaps 0.2 A.

**Derivation of the knee current $I_{Knee}$:**

The base voltage of $Q_3$ is:

$$V_{B3} = V_O + I_L \cdot R_a - I_{B3} \cdot R_a \quad (\text{with } I_{B3} \ll I_L,\ \text{simplifies to})$$

$$V_{B3} \approx V_O + I_L \cdot R_a \cdot \frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}$$

The voltage at $Q_3$'s emitter is $V_O$, so:

$$V_{BE3} = V_{B3} - V_O = I_L \cdot \frac{R_a R_b}{R_a + R_b}$$

At the *knee* (limit onset at full output voltage $V_O = 12$ V), $V_{BE3} = 0.7$ V and $I_L = I_{Knee}$:

$$I_{Knee} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V} \cdot (R_a + R_b)}{R_a R_b}$$

For the short-circuit current $I_{SC}$ at $V_O = 0$ V, the base voltage is purely $I_{SC} R_a$ (the $R_b$ branch has no voltage across it), so:

$$V_{BE3} = I_{SC} \cdot R_a = 0.7\ \text{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{SC} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{R_a}$$

**Conclusion:** Foldback current limiting drastically reduces the dissipation in the pass transistor under short-circuit conditions, from $V_{IN} \times I_{Knee}$ down to $V_{IN} \times I_{SC}$, improving long-term reliability.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A series regulator uses a 7805-type IC (dropout 2 V) to deliver 5 V at 1 A from a 12 V nominal input with ±10 % tolerance. Calculate: (i) worst-case pass-transistor power dissipation, (ii) maximum efficiency, (iii) the minimum heatsink thermal resistance if $T_{J(\max)} = 125$ °C and $T_A = 55$ °C. Assume $\theta_{JC} = 5$ °C/W for the IC package.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_O = 5$ V, $I_{L(\max)} = 1$ A, $V_{IN(\text{nom})} = 12$ V, tolerance ±10 %.

**Step 1 — Maximum input voltage.**

$$V_{IN(\max)} = 12 \times 1.10 = 13.2\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the regulator is in regulation.** Dropout condition:

$$V_{IN(\min)} = 12 \times 0.90 = 10.8\ \text{V} > V_O + V_{DO} = 5 + 2 = 7\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case pass-transistor power.**

$$P_{pass(\max)} = (V_{IN(\max)} - V_O) \cdot I_{L(\max)} = (13.2 - 5) \times 1 = 8.2\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum efficiency (at nominal input).**

$$\eta_{(\max)} = \frac{V_O}{V_{IN(\text{nom})}} \times 100\ \% = \frac{5}{12} \times 100 = 41.67\ \%$$

This is the *theoretical ceiling* for a linear series regulator — at the worst input (13.2 V), efficiency drops further to $5/13.2 = 37.88\ \%$.

**Step 5 — Heatsink thermal resistance.** The total $\theta_{JA}$ must satisfy:

$$T_{J(\max)} \geq T_A + P \cdot \theta_{JA} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_{JA} \leq \frac{T_{J(\max)} - T_A}{P}$$

$$\theta_{JA} \leq \frac{125 - 55}{8.2} = \frac{70}{8.2} = 8.54\ \text{°C/W}$$

Subtracting the IC's $\theta_{JC} = 5$ °C/W and the insulator's $\theta_{CS} \approx 0.5$ °C/W:

$$\theta_{SA} \leq 8.54 - 5 - 0.5 = 3.04\ \text{°C/W}$$

**Conclusion:** Choose a heatsink with $\theta_{SA} \leq 3$ °C/W (e.g., a 50 mm × 50 mm finned aluminium extrusion). This keeps the junction at $T_J \approx 125$ °C exactly at worst case, with no safety margin — *so a 2 °C/W heatsink is recommended in practice for derating*.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Series Regulator Design Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the virtual short justification** (–1 mark): Students write $V^+ = V^-$ directly *without* invoking $A_{OL} \to \infty$ or the negative-feedback concept. The examiner *requires* this justification. Always state the assumption explicitly.
>
> 2. **Power-rating oversight** (–2 marks): The pass transistor's power rating $P_D$ is quoted at 25 °C; it *de-rates* linearly to 0 at $T_{J(\max)}$. Failing to apply the derating or to compute the required $\theta_{JA}$ loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing R1 and R2 positions** (–1 mark): KTU uses $R_1$ as the *upper* resistor (from $V_O$ to op-amp $V^+$) and $R_2$ as the *lower* (from $V^+$ to ground). Some textbooks reverse this. Verify against your class notes — consistency matters.
>
> 4. **Missing the dropout condition** (–1 mark): A regulator designed with $V_{IN(\min)} < V_O + V_{DO}$ will *not* regulate. Always verify the dropout condition before finalising the design.
>
> 5. **Current limit without base-current compensation** (–1 mark): A BJT pass transistor's base current is $I_L / h_{FE}$. If the op-amp cannot source/sink this, the regulation will fail. Always check $I_{opamp(\max)} > I_{B1(\max)} = I_{L(\max)} / h_{FE(\min)}$.
>
> 6. **Foldback algebra sign error** (–2 marks): When $V_O$ collapses, $R_b$'s contribution vanishes. Many students compute the short-circuit current as if the divider is *still* active, getting $I_{SC}$ wrong by a factor of $(R_a + R_b)/R_b$. Re-derive at $V_O = 0$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before walking into the exam hall.**

- ☐ The **canonical output equation** is $V_O = V_Z(1 + R_1/R_2)$. Memorise it.
- ☐ The op-amp's two inputs are at the **same voltage at DC** (virtual short). The non-inverting input gets the *sample*; the inverting input gets the *reference* (or vice versa, depending on the topology).
- ☐ The pass transistor must always remain in the **active region**: $V_{CE1} > V_{CE(\text{sat})}$. Verify dropout: $V_{IN(\min)} > V_O + V_{DO}$.
- ☐ **Pass-transistor power**: $P_{Q_1} = (V_{IN} - V_O) \cdot I_L$. Worst case at *max* $V_{IN}$ and *max* $I_L$.
- ☐ **Efficiency ceiling**: $\eta \leq V_O / V_{IN}$. For large $(V_{IN} - V_O)$, use a **switching regulator** instead.
- ☐ **Current limit (simple)**: $I_{L(\max)} = 0.7 / R_{sense}$.
- ☐ **Foldback limit** at short circuit: $I_{SC} = 0.7 / R_a$ (use a *larger* $R_a$ than the simple $R_{sense}$ to achieve foldback).
- ☐ **Heatsink sizing**: $\theta_{JA} \leq (T_{J(\max)} - T_A) / P_{Q_1(\max)}$. Derate by 1.5× to 2× in practice.
- ☐ **Zener bias**: $R_S = (V_{IN} - V_Z) / I_Z$; ensure $I_Z$ remains above the Zener's *knee current* even at $V_{IN(\min)}$.
- ☐ **BJT pass transistor requires base current** $I_{B1} = I_L / h_{FE}$. Either use a Darlington (low base current) or ensure the error amplifier can source it.
- ☐ **Negative feedback** is the *philosophy* — at DC equilibrium, the feedback signal equals the reference within the error amplifier's resolution.
- ☐ **Discrete op-amp implementation** gives best regulation; **discrete BJT-pair implementation** is cheaper and more rugged; **Darlington pass element** boosts current capacity and reduces op-amp loading.
- ☐ The **four functional blocks** (Reference, Sampling, Error Amplifier, Pass Element) are the examiner's checklist. Always draw and label all four.
- ☐ **Compare with shunt regulators**: series is preferred above 100 mA; shunt is preferred for very low noise / very low current / reference-voltage generation.
- ☐ **Dropout voltage** is the single biggest weakness of a series regulator — overcome it with **LDO (Low-Dropout) regulators** using P-MOSFET pass elements.
- ☐ **PSRR (ripple rejection)** depends on the loop gain — high-gain op-amp → high PSRR (40–60 dB typical).
- ☐ **Thermal design** is non-optional — calculate the heatsink thermal resistance, not just the transistor's $P_D$ rating.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Circuit Protection and Fold Back Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Protection and Fold Back Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Short Circuit Protection (SCP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Short Circuit Protection* is a current-limiting mechanism embedded within a voltage regulator (series or shunt) that restricts the maximum output current delivered to the load when a low-resistance fault (short circuit) occurs at the output terminals. It prevents the pass transistor from exceeding its Safe Operating Area (SOA) and undergoing thermal runaway or secondary breakdown.

In regulated power supplies, the pass transistor handles the difference between the unregulated input and the regulated output. If the load is accidentally shorted, the full input voltage drops across the pass transistor, causing destructive current flow. Short circuit protection "clamps" this fault current to a safe predetermined value $I_{SC}$.

**Standard Metric (KTU 2024):** The popular three-terminal regulator **LM 7805** has a built-in short-circuit current limit of approximately **$I_{SC} = 750 \text{ mA}$** (typical) and internal thermal shutdown at **$T_J = 150^\circ\text{C}$**.

## 1.2 Fold-Back Protection (Fold-Back Current Limiting)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Fold-Back Protection* is an advanced form of overcurrent protection where the regulated output current is *not constant* under fault conditions. Instead, as the output voltage collapses due to a short circuit, the current limit value **decreases (folds back)** along a sloped line, drastically reducing the power dissipation in the pass transistor once a short circuit is detected.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Kitchen Tap

Imagine a kitchen water tap connected to a high-pressure pipeline:

- **Constant Current Limit** is like a tap that, no matter how much you turn it, releases only a *fixed* maximum flow. Safe, but the pipe still gets warm under full blockage.
- **Fold-Back Protection** is like a *smart pressure sensor* tap. The moment someone pinches the pipe (short circuit), the tap *automatically closes further*, reducing the flow almost to a trickle to protect the pipe from bursting. The "fold-back" curve represents this intelligent throttling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fold-Back is superior to Constant Current Limiting:**
> - $P_{D(\text{pass})} = V_{CE} \times I_C$. Under a hard short, $V_{out} \to 0$, so $V_{CE} \approx V_{in}$.
> - With *constant* limiting, $I_C$ stays high ⇒ $P_D$ stays high ⇒ **risk of thermal failure**.
> - With *fold-back*, $I_C$ is reduced as $V_{out}$ falls ⇒ $P_D$ is dramatically reduced ⇒ **pass transistor survives**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constant Current Short-Circuit Protection

This is the simplest form. A **current-sense resistor** $R_{sense}$ (also called $R_{sc}$) is placed in series with the load path. The voltage developed across it is used to drive a **BJT current-limit transistor** $Q_{sc}$ into conduction when the threshold is exceeded.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):

- The unregulated input $V_{in}$ is applied to the pass transistor $Q_1$ (Darlington in high-current regulators).
- The load current $I_L$ flows through $R_{sense}$ to the output.
- The voltage at the emitter of $Q_1$ is $V_{out}$. The voltage at the base of the limit transistor $Q_{sc}$ is derived from $V_{out}$ (or a divider across $R_{sense}$).
- The base-emitter voltage of the limit transistor is:

$$V_{BE(sc)} = V_{R_{sense}} - V_{R_{divider}}$$

- When $V_{BE(sc)} \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$, $Q_{sc}$ turns ON and **steals base drive** from $Q_1$, preventing $I_L$ from increasing further.
- The current at which this occurs is the **Short-Circuit Current** $I_{SC}$.

### Key Design Equations:

The current limit is set when $V_{BE(sc)} = V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7 \text{ V}$. For the simplest case where the divider is omitted and $Q_{sc}$ senses $R_{sense}$ directly relative to $V_{out}$:

$$I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_{sense}} = \frac{0.7 \text{ V}}{R_{sense}}$$

## 2.2 Fold-Back Current Limiting — The Superior Method

In fold-back, a **resistive voltage divider** ($R_a$ and $R_b$) is added so that the reference voltage seen by $Q_{sc}$ *changes* with $V_{out}$.

### Operational Logic:

- When the regulator operates normally: $V_{out} = V_{\text{nominal}}$, the current $I_L$ is below the **knee current** $I_K$. No limiting action.
- As $I_L$ increases and approaches the knee: $V_{R_{sense}} = I_K \cdot R_{sense}$ reaches the threshold. $Q_{sc}$ begins to conduct.
- Once limiting begins, $V_{out}$ starts to drop (load is being starved). The divider voltage decreases, which means $R_{sense}$ only needs to develop *less* voltage to keep $Q_{sc}$ on ⇒ the current limit **decreases** as $V_{out}$ falls.
- At a hard short ($V_{out} = 0$), the current settles at the minimum value $I_{SC} < I_K$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short-circuit current (Constant limit) | $I_{SC}$ | $\dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_{sense}}$ | Fixed max current at full short |
| Knee current (Fold-back) | $I_K$ | $\dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_{sense}} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_b}{R_a}\right)$ | Current at which limiting begins ($V_{out}$ nominal) |
| Short-circuit current (Fold-back) | $I_{SC}$ | $\dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_{sense}}$ | Reduced current at $V_{out} = 0$ |
| Fold-back ratio | $K_f$ | $\dfrac{I_K}{I_{SC}} = 1 + \dfrac{R_b}{R_a}$ | Always $\ge 1$; higher ⇒ more aggressive fold-back |
| Pass transistor dissipation at short | $P_{D(\text{short})}$ | $V_{in} \times I_{SC}$ | Must be $\le P_{D(\text{max})}$ rating |
| Safe Operating Area (dc) | $SOA$ | $V_{CE} \cdot I_C \le P_{D(\text{max})}$ | Boundary for pass transistor survival |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> Fold-back protection is the industry standard in **bench power supplies**, **LM 723-based regulators**, and **high-current linear regulators** used in telecom base stations and aircraft avionics, where reliability under fault is non-negotiable. The fold-back curve is typically plotted on the **$V_{out}$ vs. $I_{out}$** plane.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Constant Short-Circuit Current Limit

**Given:** Pass transistor $Q_1$ in series with the load. Sense resistor $R_{sense}$ in series with $Q_1$'s emitter. Limit transistor $Q_{sc}$ whose base is connected to the top of $R_{sense}$ and emitter connected to $V_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the trigger condition.**
$Q_{sc}$ turns ON when its base-emitter voltage reaches the cut-in voltage:

$$V_{BE(sc)} = V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Express the base-emitter voltage of $Q_{sc}$ in terms of circuit variables.**
The base of $Q_{sc}$ sits at potential $V_B = V_{out} + I_L \cdot R_{sense}$ and the emitter sits at $V_E = V_{out}$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{BE(sc)} &= V_B - V_E \\
&= (V_{out} + I_L \cdot R_{sense}) - V_{out} \\
&= I_L \cdot R_{sense}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate to the trigger condition to find $I_{SC}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SC} \cdot R_{sense} &= 0.7 \\
I_{SC} &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This shows that the current limit is **constant** — it does not depend on $V_{out}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Fold-Back Current Limit (Knee and Short-Circuit Currents)

**Given:** Same circuit as above, but now a divider $R_a$ (top, between $V_{out}$ node and $Q_{sc}$ base) and $R_b$ (bottom, between $Q_{sc}$ base and ground... actually between base and $R_{sense}$ top junction) is inserted so that the base voltage of $Q_{sc}$ is referenced differently.

**Circuit topology (standard KTU form):**
- $V_{out}$ node → $R_a$ → Node $X$ (base of $Q_{sc}$) → $R_b$ → top of $R_{sense}$.
- Emitter of $Q_{sc}$ is connected to $V_{out}$ (or referenced to a fixed potential $V_{ref}$ depending on design).

For the standard derivation using $V_{out}$ as the reference and $Q_{sc}$'s emitter at the top of $R_{sense}$ (i.e., emitter at node after $R_b$):

**Step 1 — Define node voltages.**
Let $V_E$ = voltage at the emitter of $Q_{sc}$ = $V_{out} + I_L \cdot R_{sense}$ (top of $R_{sense}$).
Let $V_B$ = base voltage of $Q_{sc}$ = voltage at the junction of $R_a$ and $R_b$.

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider.**
The divider is fed by the difference $(V_{out} - V_E)$ across the series combination, or in the more common KTU formulation, by $(V_{out} + I_L R_{sense} - V_{out}) = I_L R_{sense}$ across the $R_a$–$R_b$ chain, with the divider tap at $V_B$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_B - V_E &= I_L \cdot R_{sense} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right) \\
\text{or equivalently: } \quad V_{BE(sc)} &= V_B - V_E = I_L \cdot R_{sense} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right) + V_{\text{offset}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **cleanest KTU 2024 derivation**, consider the case where the divider is referenced such that:

$$V_{BE(sc)} = I_L \cdot R_{sense} - V_{out} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)$$

Wait — using the more standard textbook form (Sedra/Smith & Millman): let us define the divider as $R_1$ (top, from $V_{in}$-side rail) and $R_2$ (bottom). The fold-back derivation is most cleanly expressed as:

**Step 1 — Trigger condition (general).**
$Q_{sc}$ activates when $V_{BE(sc)} = 0.7 \text{ V}$. At this point, the relationship between $V_{out}$, $I_L$, and the divider is:

$$I_L \cdot R_{sense} = V_{BE(\text{on})} + V_{out} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the limiting current $I_L$ as a function of $V_{out}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_{sense}} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_{sense}} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right) \\
I_L &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_{sense}} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **linear equation in $V_{out}$**, which geometrically produces a sloped straight line on the $V_{out}$–$I_L$ plane — the **fold-back line**.

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $V_{out} = V_{nom}$ to get the Knee Current $I_K$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_K &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} + \frac{V_{nom}}{R_{sense}} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right) \\
I_K &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} \left[1 + \frac{V_{nom}}{0.7} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> The term in the brackets is the **fold-back ratio** $K_f$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate at $V_{out} = 0$ (dead short) to get $I_{SC}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SC} &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} + 0 \\
I_{SC} &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Fold-back ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_f &= \frac{I_K}{I_{SC}} = 1 + \frac{V_{nom}}{0.7} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** $K_f$ is typically chosen between **1.5 and 3.0** in commercial designs. If $K_f$ is too high, fold-back becomes too aggressive and the regulator may fail to *recover* automatically when the short is removed (latch-up). If $K_f$ is too close to 1, the protection degenerates to constant current limiting.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A fold-back regulator has $V_{nom} = 12 \text{ V}$, $R_{sense} = 0.5 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_a = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_b = 100 \text{ }\Omega$. Find $I_{SC}$, $I_K$, and $K_f$. Verify pass-transistor dissipation is within safe limits if $V_{in} = 18 \text{ V}$ and the pass transistor is rated $P_{D(\text{max})} = 30 \text{ W}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $I_{SC}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SC} &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} = \frac{0.7}{0.5} = 1.4 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_K$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_K &= \frac{0.7}{0.5} + \frac{12}{0.5} \cdot \left(\frac{100}{1000 + 100}\right) \\
&= 1.4 + 24 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{1100}\right) \\
&= 1.4 + 24 \cdot 0.0909 \\
&= 1.4 + 2.18 \\
&= 3.58 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K_f$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_f &= \frac{I_K}{I_{SC}} = \frac{3.58}{1.4} \approx 2.56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify dissipation at short circuit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D(\text{short})} &= V_{in} \times I_{SC} = 18 \times 1.4 = 25.2 \text{ W} \\
\text{Since } 25.2 \text{ W} &< 30 \text{ W}, \quad \text{the pass transistor SURVIVES.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> If we had used *constant* current limiting with $I_{SC} = 3.58 \text{ A}$ (same knee), then $P_D = 18 \times 3.58 = 64.4 \text{ W}$ — **catastrophic failure** would occur. This is the entire engineering justification for fold-back.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Fold-Back Protected Regulator

```mermaid
graph TD
    Vin["V_IN Unregulated DC"] --> Q1["Pass Transistor Q1 Darlington"]
    Q1 --> NodeA["Output Node V_OUT"]
    Rsense["R_sense"] --> NodeA
    Q1 --> Rsense
    NodeA --> Ra["R_a Divider Top"]
    Ra --> NodeB["Base of Q_sc Node X"]
    NodeB --> Rb["R_b Divider Bottom"]
    Rb --> NodeC["Top of R_sense"]
    NodeB --> Qsc["Current Limit Transistor Q_sc"]
    Qsc --> NodeC
    NodeC --> Q1
    NodeA --> Load["R_LOAD"]
    Load --> Gnd["Ground"]
    Qsc -.->|Steals base drive| Q1
```

## 4.2 Fold-Back I-V Characteristic Curve (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Normal Operation Region: V_out = V_nom, I_L < I_K"] --> B["Knee Point: I_L = I_K, V_out = V_nom"]
    B --> C["Fold-Back Line: V_out drops, I_L decreases linearly"]
    C --> D["Short Circuit: V_out = 0, I_L = I_SC"]
    D -.->|Fault cleared| A
    style A fill:#90EE90
    style B fill:#FFD700
    style C fill:#FFA500
    style D fill:#FF6347
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Description (Architecture Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input["V_IN"] --> Step1["Step 1: Pass Transistor Q1 regulates V_OUT"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Load current I_L flows through R_sense"]
    Step2 --> Step3{"Step 3: V_BE of Q_sc reaches 0.7 V?"}
    Step3 -->|No, I_L less than I_K| Step4["Normal regulation continues"]
    Step3 -->|Yes, I_L reaches I_K| Step5["Step 4: Q_sc conducts, steals base drive from Q1"]
    Step5 --> Step6["Step 5: V_OUT begins to drop"]
    Step6 --> Step7["Step 6: Divider voltage falls, less R_sense voltage needed to keep Q_sc on"]
    Step7 --> Step8["Step 7: I_L folds back along sloped line to I_SC at V_OUT = 0"]
    Step4 --> Step9["Steady State Regulated Output"]
    Step8 --> Step9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "explain the fold-back characteristic," always draw a **two-quadrant graph** with $V_{out}$ on the y-axis and $I_L$ on the x-axis. Mark four points: **(0, 0) → (I_SC, 0) → (I_K, V_nom) → (0, V_nom)**. Connect (I_K, V_nom) to (I_SC, 0) with a **straight sloped line** — this *is* the fold-back line.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between constant current limiting and fold-back current limiting in voltage regulators.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Constant Current Limit** | **Fold-Back Current Limit** |
|---|---|---|
| $I_{SC}$ behavior | Remains constant at all $V_{out}$ | Decreases as $V_{out}$ falls |
| $V_{out}$–$I_L$ curve | Vertical drop at $I_{SC}$ | Sloped line from $(I_K, V_{nom})$ to $(I_{SC}, 0)$ |
| Power dissipation at short | High ($V_{in} \cdot I_{SC}$) | Drastically reduced |
| Circuit complexity | Simple (one $Q_{sc}$, one $R_{sense}$) | Complex (adds $R_a$, $R_b$ divider) |
| Recovery from short | Smooth | Risk of latch-up if fold-back ratio too high |

*(Examiner awards 3 marks for: definition of each + one distinguishing point + recovery behavior.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of $R_{sense}$ in a short-circuit-protected regulator? Derive the expression for short-circuit current in a constant current limiter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$R_{sense}$ is a low-value resistor (typically **0.1 Ω to 1 Ω**) placed in series with the load to develop a voltage proportional to the load current. This voltage is sensed by the base-emitter junction of the limit transistor $Q_{sc}$. When $V_{R_{sense}}}$ exceeds the cut-in voltage of $Q_{sc}$ (≈ 0.7 V), $Q_{sc}$ conducts and limits the current.

$$I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_{sense}} = \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}}$$

*(3 marks: 1 for function + 1 for derivation setup + 1 for final expression with units.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **fold-back current-limited series regulator**. Derive the expressions for knee current $I_K$ and short-circuit current $I_{SC}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fold-back regulator is designed with $V_{nom} = 15 \text{ V}$, $V_{in} = 20 \text{ V}$, $R_{sense} = 0.25 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_a = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_b = 200 \text{ }\Omega$. Calculate $I_K$, $I_{SC}$, the fold-back ratio $K_f$, and the maximum power dissipation in the pass transistor under short-circuit condition. Comment on the safety of the design if the pass transistor is rated $P_{D(\text{max})} = 20 \text{ W}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks

**Circuit Operation (3 marks):**
The regulator consists of:
- Pass transistor $Q_1$ (Darlington pair for high current gain) in series between $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$.
- Sense resistor $R_{sense}$ in the emitter path of $Q_1$.
- Voltage divider $R_a$–$R_b$ sensing $V_{out}$.
- Current-limit transistor $Q_{sc}$ whose base is at the divider tap and emitter at the top of $R_{sense}$.

**Operation phases:**
- **Phase 1 — Normal:** $I_L < I_K$, $V_{BE(sc)} < 0.7 \text{ V}$, $Q_{sc}$ is OFF. $Q_1$ regulates $V_{out} = V_{nom}$ as set by the reference/error amplifier.
- **Phase 2 — Knee:** $I_L = I_K$, $V_{BE(sc)} = 0.7 \text{ V}$, $Q_{sc}$ just begins to conduct.
- **Phase 3 — Fold-back:** $Q_{sc}$ steals base drive from $Q_1$, $V_{out}$ starts falling. Divider voltage drops, so less $V_{R_{sense}}$ is required to keep $Q_{sc}$ ON ⇒ $I_L$ decreases along the sloped fold-back line.
- **Phase 4 — Hard short:** $V_{out} = 0$, $I_L = I_{SC}$ (minimum).

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Applying KVL around the $Q_{sc}$ base-emitter loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{BE(sc)} &= I_L \cdot R_{sense} - V_{out} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $V_{BE(sc)} = 0.7 \text{ V}$ at the limit boundary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L \cdot R_{sense} &= 0.7 + V_{out} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right) \\
I_L &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_{sense}} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

At $V_{out} = 0$ (short):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SC} = \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $V_{out} = V_{nom}$ (knee):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_K &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} + \frac{V_{nom}}{R_{sense}} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $V_{nom} = 15 \text{ V}$, $V_{in} = 20 \text{ V}$, $R_{sense} = 0.25 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_a = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_b = 200 \text{ }\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Short-circuit current $I_{SC}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{SC} &= \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} = \frac{0.7}{0.25} = 2.8 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Knee current $I_K$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_K &= \frac{0.7}{0.25} + \frac{15}{0.25} \cdot \left(\frac{200}{2000 + 200}\right) \\
&= 2.8 + 60 \cdot \left(\frac{200}{2200}\right) \\
&= 2.8 + 60 \cdot 0.0909 \\
&= 2.8 + 5.45 \\
&= 8.25 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fold-back ratio $K_f$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_f &= \frac{I_K}{I_{SC}} = \frac{8.25}{2.8} \approx 2.95
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipation at short circuit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{D(\text{short})} &= V_{in} \times I_{SC} = 20 \times 2.8 = 56 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Safety Comment (1 mark):**
$P_{D(\text{short})} = 56 \text{ W} \gg P_{D(\text{max})} = 20 \text{ W}$. **The pass transistor WILL FAIL** under sustained short circuit. Either (i) use a heat sink to upgrade $P_{D(\text{max})}$, (ii) increase $R_{sense}$ to lower $I_{SC}$, or (iii) use a pass transistor with a higher $P_{D(\text{max})}$ rating.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the **short-circuit protection scheme** in the three-terminal regulator **IC 7805**. State typical values of $I_{SC}$ and the thermal shutdown junction temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **constant current** and **fold-back** protection schemes with reference to (i) circuit complexity, (ii) recovery from short circuit, (iii) power dissipation under fault, and (iv) load regulation during current limiting. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**IC 7805 Internal Protection (3 marks):**
The LM 7805 monolithic regulator contains four internal protection mechanisms:
1. **Current limiting** — A dedicated sense transistor monitors the output current. When $I_L$ exceeds the internal limit (≈ 750 mA typical), the sense transistor conducts and reduces the base drive to the Darlington pass transistor.
2. **Thermal shutdown** — A temperature-sensing transistor embedded near the die turns off the regulator when $T_J \geq 150^\circ\text{C}$ (typical).
3. **Safe Operating Area (SOA) protection** — Limits the $V_{CE}$–$I_C$ combination to prevent secondary breakdown.
4. **Input voltage transient protection** — Internal clamp diodes.

**Block diagram of internal current limit (4 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["V_IN Pin 1"] --> B["Darlington Pass Q1 Q2"]
    B --> C["Current Sense Junction Node M"]
    C --> D["V_OUT Pin 3"]
    C --> E["Sense Transistor Q_sc internal"]
    E --> F["Thermal Shutdown Sensor Q_ts at 150 C"]
    E -->|Steals drive| B
    F -->|Shuts down regulator| B
    D --> G["Pin 2 GND"]
    style F fill:#FF6347
    style E fill:#FFA500
```

**Typical values (from datasheet):**
- $I_{SC} \approx 750 \text{ mA}$ (minimum guaranteed $I_{SC} \approx 200 \text{ mA}$)
- $T_{J(\text{shutdown})} \approx 150^\circ\text{C}$ (hysteresis $T_{J(\text{reset})} \approx 125^\circ\text{C}$)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.5 each + 1 for overall conclusion):**

| **Parameter** | **Constant Current Limit** | **Fold-Back Limit** |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Circuit complexity** | Simple: $Q_{sc} + R_{sense}$ | Complex: $Q_{sc} + R_{sense} + R_a + R_b$ |
| **(ii) Recovery from short** | Smooth, automatic | May latch if $K_f$ too high; needs proper design |
| **(iii) Power dissipation at fault** | High: $V_{in} \cdot I_{SC}$ constant | Low: $I_L$ falls with $V_{out}$ |
| **(iv) Load regulation during limit** | Output voltage collapses hard | Output collapses gradually along fold-back line |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Fold-back is preferred for high-current regulators where $V_{in}$ is large and pass-transistor SOA is tight; constant current limiting suffices for low-current monolithic regulators (e.g., 78xx series) where SOA is already generous.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners explicitly deduct **0.5 mark** per missing SI unit.
> 2. **Confusing $I_{SC}$ with $I_K$** — Many students compute only one and call it the answer. KTU expects *both* values, since the *difference* is the entire point of fold-back.
> 3. **Sign error in the divider expression** — The equation $I_L = \frac{0.7}{R_{sense}} + \frac{V_{out}}{R_{sense}} \cdot \left(\frac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}\right)$ is **additive**, not subtractive. Mixing up the polarity is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Not stating the condition $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7 \text{ V}$** — This must be *declared* before the derivation, not assumed silently.
> 5. **Confusing fold-back with current-foldback of op-amps** — The fold-back *current limiter* is a regulator topic (this module), NOT an op-amp internal topology.
> 6. **Skipping the slope logic** — Examiners want the *linear* relationship $I_L \propto V_{out}$ on the fold-back line explicitly stated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **Short Circuit Protection** = safety mechanism that clamps $I_L$ to a safe max value $I_{SC}$ when load is shorted.
- **Constant current limit** = $I_{SC} = \dfrac{0.7}{R_{sense}}$; simple but $P_D$ at short = $V_{in} \cdot I_{SC}$ (high).
- **Fold-back limit** = $I_L$ *decreases* as $V_{out}$ falls; protects pass transistor better.
- **Knee current $I_K$** = current at which limiting *starts* (at $V_{out} = V_{nom}$).
- **Short-circuit current $I_{SC}$** = current at $V_{out} = 0$ (always $I_{SC} = 0.7 / R_{sense}$).
- **Fold-back ratio** $K_f = I_K / I_{SC} = 1 + \dfrac{V_{nom}}{0.7} \cdot \dfrac{R_b}{R_a + R_b}$.
- **Typical $K_f$** = 1.5 to 3.0 (higher ⇒ more fold-back, but latch-up risk).
- **LM 7805** has built-in: $I_{SC} \approx 750 \text{ mA}$, thermal shutdown at $T_J = 150^\circ\text{C}$.
- **IC 723** is the canonical *discrete* fold-back regulator chip (mentioned frequently in KTU).
- **I–V curve** has four key points: (0, 0), ($I_{SC}$, 0), ($I_K$, $V_{nom}$), (0, $V_{nom}$) connected by the sloped fold-back line.
- **Power dissipation check:** $V_{in} \cdot I_{SC} \le P_{D(\text{max})}$ is the *hard design constraint*.
- **Recovery latch-up** is the key *disadvantage* of fold-back — design $K_f$ carefully.
- **Graph direction:** Always plot $V_{out}$ (y) vs. $I_L$ (x); the fold-back line is **negatively sloped** (top-right to bottom-left).
- **Keyword for KTU theory:** "fold-back protection reduces the short-circuit current to a value *below* the knee current, minimizing SOA stress on the pass transistor."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Output Current Boosting Using Darlington Pair Transistor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT413 - ANALOG CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Output Current Boosting Using Darlington Pair Transistor # 📘 MODULE 4 — OUTPUT STAGES & CURRENT BOOSTING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Darlington Pair (Darlington Transistor):** A composite transistor configuration consisting of **two bipolar junction transistors (BJTs)** cascaded such that the emitter of the first (driver) transistor feeds the base of the second (output) transistor, with both collectors tied together. The overall structure behaves as a single super-beta transistor whose effective current gain is the **product of the individual current gains** of the two stages. Mathematically, the composite current gain is expressed as: $$ \beta_{D} \;=\; \beta_{1}\,\beta_{2} \;+\; \beta_{1} \;+\; \beta_{2} \;\approx\; \beta_{1}\,\beta_{2} $$ where $\beta_{1}$ and $\beta_{2}$ are the common-emitter current gains of the driver and output transistors, respectively. The pair is most commonly implemented using **two NPN transistors** (NPN Darlington), although **PNP Darlington** and **Complementary Feedback (Sziklai) pairs** are also used for symmetry in push-pull output stages. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade" Imagine two workers standing in series trying to lift a heavy load (the output current). **Worker-1 (Q1)** can lift only a small weight, but he has a *megaphone* (his high $\beta$) that amplifies a tiny shout into a louder instruction. **Worker-2 (Q2)** has immense raw strength but is deaf — he only responds to the amplified shout of Worker-1. The result? A *tiny whisper* at the input lifts a *huge load* at the output. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In a Darlington pair, the base current of Q1 is the only current you supply; Q1's emitter current becomes the base current of Q2. Because $\beta$ is multiplicative, even modest transistors (e.g., $\beta_{1} = \beta_{2} = 100$) yield a composite $\beta_{D} \approx 10{,}000$ — a true "super-beta" effect. ### 1.3 Why "Output Current Boosting"? In analog design, the final stage of a system (a power amplifier, a voltage regulator pass element, a motor driver) must deliver **large currents to a low-impedance load** (speakers, motors, LEDs, solenoids) while being driven by a **weak, high-impedance signal source** (the preceding voltage-gain stage). A single BJT typically cannot simultaneously provide: | Requirement | Single BJT Limitation | |---|---| | High current gain | $\beta \approx 50 - 300$ (insufficient for kΩ loads) | | High input impedance | $r_{\pi}$ is modest (1–10 kΩ) | | Acceptable bandwidth | High $\beta$ ↔ low $f_{T}$ trade-off | | Thermal stability | Power dissipation concentrates in one device | The **Darlington pair overcomes all four** by cascading two devices — *and this is the cornerstone of its use in output stages.* > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Note:** Whenever you see "current boosting," "high-gain output stage," or "composite transistor" in a question, the expected answer framework is the **Darlington configuration** with $\beta_{D} \approx \beta_{1}\beta_{2}$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology of the Darlington Pair The two canonical configurations are: 1. **NPN Darlington (Darlington Connection Proper)** — Q1 (NPN) drives Q2 (NPN). 2. **Sziklai Pair (Complementary Feedback Darlington)** — Q1 (NPN) drives Q2 (PNP), mimicking a single high-power PNP transistor with PNP-like $\beta$ but NPN-like $f_{T}$. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic - **Step 1 — Base Drive:** A small input base current $I_{B1}$ is applied to Q1. - **Step 2 — Q1 Amplification:** Q1 acts as a common-emitter amplifier. Its emitter current is $I_{E1} = (\beta_{1}+1) I_{B1}$. - **Step 3 — Cascaded Drive:** The emitter of Q1 is wired to the base of Q2. Thus $I_{B2} = I_{E1} = (\beta_{1}+1) I_{B1}$. - **Step 4 — Q2 Power Delivery:** Q2's collector current is $I_{C2} = \beta_{2} I_{B2} = \beta_{2}(\beta_{1}+1) I_{B1}$. - **Step 5 — Total Output Current:** The load current $I_{L} \approx I_{C2} + I_{E1} \approx I_{C2}$ (since $I_{C2} \gg I_{E1}$ for large $\beta$). - **Step 6 — Composite Beta:** Dividing $I_{C2}$ by $I_{B1}$ yields the super-beta: $$ \beta_{D} \;=\; \frac{I_{C2}}{I_{B1}} \;=\; \beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2} $$ - **Step 7 — Base–Emitter Voltage Penalty:** Because two BE junctions are in series, the composite $V_{BE(on)} \approx 1.4$ V (vs. 0.7 V for a single BJT). This is a *critical drawback* in low-voltage designs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two BE Junctions in Series ⇒ $V_{BE,Darlington} \approx 2 \times 0.7 = 1.4$ V.** Always remember this — it directly limits the minimum supply voltage and causes higher power dissipation. ## 2.3 Small-Signal Parameters Using the hybrid-π model for each transistor: - Input resistance of the pair: $$ r_{\pi,D} \;=\; r_{\pi 1} \;+\; (\beta_{1}+1)\,r_{\pi 2} $$ - Output resistance (looking into the collector of Q2 with Q1's emitter degenerated): $$ r_{o,D} \;\approx\; r_{o2}\,\bigl[1 + g_{m2}\,r_{\pi 1}\bigr] $$ - Transconductance of the composite device: $$ g_{m,D} \;\approx\; \frac{g_{m1}\,g_{m2}\,r_{\pi 1}}{1 + g_{m2}\,r_{\pi 1}} \;\approx\; \frac{g_{m1}}{2} \quad \text{(for matched } g_{m1} \approx g_{m2}\text{)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $g_{m,D}$ is roughly halved:** The emitter-follower action of Q1 inside the pair means the effective transconductance seen at the input is reduced. This is the price paid for the enormous $\beta$ boost. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Composite current gain | $\beta_{D} = \beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2}$ | dimensionless | $\approx \beta_{1}\beta_{2}$ for $\beta_{1},\beta_{2} \gg 1$ | | 2 | Composite base–emitter voltage | $V_{BE,D} = V_{BE1} + V_{BE2} \approx 1.4$ V | V | Two junctions in series | | 3 | Composite base current | $I_{B1} = I_{L} / \beta_{D}$ | A | Drive requirement | | 4 | Composite input resistance | $r_{\pi,D} = r_{\pi 1} + (\beta_{1}+1)r_{\pi 2}$ | Ω | High-Z input | | 5 | Composite output resistance | $r_{o,D} \approx r_{o2}\bigl[1 + g_{m2}r_{\pi 1}\bigr]$ | Ω | High-Z output | | 6 | Effective transconductance | $g_{m,D} \approx g_{m1}/2$ | S | Reduced by half | | 7 | Load current (output) | $I_{L} \approx \beta_{D}\,I_{B1}$ | A | Boosted current | | 8 | Power dissipation per device | $P_{D} = V_{CE}\,I_{C}$ | W | Heat sinking required | | 9 | Sziklai (CFP) effective beta | $\beta_{CFP} = \beta_{NPN}\beta_{PNP}$ | — | Behaves like complementary PNP | | 10 | Saturation voltage (Darlington) | $V_{CE(sat),D} = V_{CE(sat),Q2} + V_{BE(sat),Q1}$ | V | $\approx 0.7 - 1.0$ V | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown table integrity note:** In all rows above, the absolute value / conditioning expressions use parentheses $\bigl(\cdot\bigr)$ or vertical bars rendered through LaTeX spacing — never the raw `\|` character — to preserve the KTU-rendered table layout. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World - **Audio Power Amplifiers:** Complementary Darlington (NPN + Sziklai pseudo-PNP) output stages in 2N6284 / 2N6287 or TIP31/TIP32 pairs. - **Linear Voltage Regulators:** LM317, LM350, and 78xx series use Darlington pass transistors to deliver 1–3 A with only mA-level quiescent current. - **Motor Driver ICs:** L293D, ULN2003A, and discrete H-bridges use Darlington arrays for high-side/low-side switching. - **Display Drivers:** Relay coils, solenoid valves, large LED matrices — ULN2803 (8-Darlington array) is a household name in industrial electronics. - **Audio Mixer Summing Buses:** High input impedance prevents loading the preceding op-amp stage. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Application Focus:** When answering design questions, always connect Darlington to **(a) high $\beta$, (b) high input impedance, (c) output current boosting**, and **(d) the $V_{BE}$ penalty**. Examiners award marks for explicitly stating these four pillars. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Composite Current Gain $\beta_{D}$ **Starting point:** Apply KCL at the emitter node of Q1 (which equals the base of Q2). By definition of BJT operation: $$ I_{C1} = \beta_{1}\,I_{B1} $$ $$ I_{E1} = (\beta_{1} + 1)\,I_{B1} $$ The emitter of Q1 is wired to the base of Q2, so: $$ I_{B2} = I_{E1} = (\beta_{1} + 1)\,I_{B1} $$ Now Q2's collector current is: $$ I_{C2} = \beta_{2}\,I_{B2} = \beta_{2}\,(\beta_{1} + 1)\,I_{B1} $$ The total output (load) current is the sum of the two collector currents (both flow to the load node in the standard Darlington output stage configuration): $$ I_{L} = I_{C1} + I_{C2} = \beta_{1} I_{B1} + \beta_{2}(\beta_{1}+1) I_{B1} $$ Expanding: $$ I_{L} = \beta_{1} I_{B1} + \beta_{1}\beta_{2} I_{B1} + \beta_{2} I_{B1} $$ $$ I_{L} = (\beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2})\,I_{B1} $$ Dividing by $I_{B1}$: $$ \boxed{\;\beta_{D} \;=\; \frac{I_{L}}{I_{B1}} \;=\; \beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2}\;} $$ **Approximation for large $\beta$:** If $\beta_{1}, \beta_{2} \geq 50$, then $\beta_{1}\beta_{2} \gg \beta_{1} + \beta_{2}$, so: $$ \beta_{D} \;\approx\; \beta_{1}\,\beta_{2} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical sanity check:** For $\beta_{1} = \beta_{2} = 100$, $\beta_{D} = 100 \times 100 + 100 + 100 = 10{,}200$. The approximation $100 \times 100 = 10{,}000$ underestimates by only 2 % — perfectly acceptable for hand calculations in KTU exams. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Composite Input Resistance $r_{\pi,D}$ Apply a small-signal test voltage $v_{be}$ at the composite base. The small-signal base current of Q1 is $i_{b1}$. The emitter of Q1 sees a load equal to $r_{\pi 2}$ (looking into the base of Q2 from the emitter of Q1). The total input resistance is the resistance looking into the base of Q1 plus the resistance looking from the emitter of Q1 into the base of Q2, **referred back to the base of Q1 by the factor $(\beta_{1}+1)$**: $$ r_{\pi,D} \;=\; r_{\pi 1} \;+\; (\beta_{1}+1)\,r_{\pi 2} $$ Substituting $r_{\pi} = \beta/g_{m}$: $$ r_{\pi,D} = \frac{\beta_{1}}{g_{m1}} + (\beta_{1}+1)\frac{\beta_{2}}{g_{m2}} $$ Assuming $g_{m1} \approx g_{m2} = g_{m}$ and $\beta_{1} \gg 1$: $$ \boxed{\;r_{\pi,D} \;\approx\; \frac{\beta_{1}}{g_{m}} \Bigl(1 + \beta_{2}\Bigr) \;\approx\; \frac{\beta_{1}\beta_{2}}{g_{m}} \;=\; \frac{\beta_{D}}{g_{m}}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implication:** The input resistance is multiplied by $\beta_{D}$ — which is exactly why the Darlington is prized as a **high-input-impedance** buffer. A standard emitter follower with a single BJT has $R_{in} = (\beta+1)R_{E}$; with a Darlington, $R_{in} \approx (\beta_{D}+1)R_{E}$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A Darlington pair uses $\beta_{1} = 80$ and $\beta_{2} = 120$. The driver base current is $I_{B1} = 20\ \mu A$. The load is $R_{L} = 50\ \Omega$ connected from the +12 V rail to the composite collector. Find: (a) composite $\beta_{D}$, (b) load current $I_{L}$, (c) input resistance of the pair, given $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = 0.05$ S. **Solution (Step-by-Step):** **(a) Composite $\beta_{D}$:** $$ \beta_{D} = \beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2} = (80)(120) + 80 + 120 $$ $$ \beta_{D} = 9600 + 80 + 120 = 9800 $$ **(b) Load current:** $$ I_{L} = \beta_{D}\,I_{B1} = 9800 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A} $$ $$ I_{L} = 0.196\ \text{A} = 196\ \text{mA} $$ **(c) Input resistance:** $$ r_{\pi,D} \approx \frac{\beta_{1}\beta_{2}}{g_{m}} = \frac{9600}{0.05} $$ $$ r_{\pi,D} = 192{,}000\ \Omega = 192\ \text{k}\Omega $$ > [!TIP] > **What the numbers reveal:** A 20 μA input base current produces a **196 mA load current** — a current gain of 9,800×. The input resistance of 192 kΩ is so high that the preceding stage barely feels loaded. This is the *defining performance signature* of a Darlington output stage. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Darlington vs. Single BJT vs. Sziklai | Performance Metric | Single BJT (CE) | Darlington (NPN+NPN) | Sziklai (NPN+PNP) | |---|---|---|---| | Current gain $\beta$ | 50–300 | 1,000–20,000 | 1,000–20,000 | | $V_{BE(on)}$ | 0.7 V | 1.4 V | 0.7 V (NPN-like) | | $V_{CE(sat)}$ | 0.2 V | 0.7–1.0 V | 0.2 V (NPN-like) | | Input impedance | Moderate | Very high | High | | Output polarity | NPN ⇒ sourcing | NPN ⇒ sourcing | PNP-equivalent ⇒ sinking | | Bandwidth ($f_{T}$ roll-off) | High $f_{T}$ | Lower (cascaded $C_{bc}$) | Moderate | | Power dissipation split | One device | Two devices (Q2 takes most) | Two devices | | Use in push-pull output | Limited | Yes (with Sziklai for PNP half) | Yes (complementary half) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway for KTU:** The *Sziklai pair* gives PNP-like behavior with NPN-like $V_{BE}$ and saturation voltage. It is the *secret ingredient* in high-fidelity audio amplifiers (e.g., the legendary Hitachi MOSFET-Darlington-Sziklai hybrid output stage) because it overcomes the historical shortage of high-power, high-speed PNP transistors. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Schematic — NPN Darlington Pair (Output Stage) > [!NOTE] > The diagram below is rendered as a **functional schematic** showing the inter-electrode wiring that defines the Darlington connection. This is the canonical pedagogical representation used in KTU textbooks. ```mermaid graph TD IN["Input Signal
Base Drive I_B1"] --> BASE_Q1["Base of Q1
NPN Driver"] BASE_Q1 --> EMITTER_Q1["Emitter of Q1"] EMITTER_Q1 --> BASE_Q2["Base of Q2
NPN Output"] BASE_Q2 --> EMITTER_Q2["Emitter of Q2
= Output Node"] EMITTER_Q2 --> LOAD["Load R_L
To Ground"] COLLECTOR_Q1["Collector of Q1"] --> VCC["V_CC Supply Rail"] COLLECTOR_Q2["Collector of Q2"] --> VCC I_C1["I_C1 = beta1 * I_B1"] -.-> COLLECTOR_Q1 I_C2["I_C2 = beta2 * I_B2"] -.-> COLLECTOR_Q2 I_L["Load Current I_L
= I_C1 + I_C2
= beta_D * I_B1"] -.-> LOAD ``` **Reading the schematic:** Note that **both collectors** connect to $V_{CC}$, and the **emitter of Q1** ties directly to the **base of Q2**. The load $R_{L}$ is driven from the emitter of Q2 to ground — this is a *Darlington emitter-follower* output stage. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Output Current Boosting Architecture ```mermaid graph LR PREAMP["Pre-amplifier
Voltage Gain Stage
High Zout = 10k"] -->|Weak signal| DRIVER["Darlington Driver
Q1 NPN beta1
+ Q2 NPN beta2"] DRIVER -->|Boosted current| OUTPUTNODE["Output Node
Emitter of Q2"] OUTPUTNODE --> LOAD["Low-Z Load
Speaker / Motor / Relay"] OUTPUTNODE -->|Feedback| PREAMP VCC["V_CC Rail"] --> DRIVER VCC["V_CC Rail"] --> LOAD ``` **Functional flow:** The preamp produces a *voltage* signal at high impedance. The Darlington driver converts this voltage into a *current* that is $\beta_{1}\beta_{2}$ times larger — the precise mechanism of *output current boosting*. The load receives the boosted current, and a feedback path may be added for linearity (as in a Class-AB amplifier). --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Darlington vs. Sziklai Push-Pull Half-Cycle ```mermaid graph TD subgraph "Positive Half Cycle (Sourcing)" Q1NPN_A["Q1: NPN Driver"] Q2NPN_A["Q2: NPN Power"] Q1NPN_A -->|Emitter to Base| Q2NPN_A Q2NPN_A -->|Sources current| LOAD_POS["Load (Positive swing)"] end subgraph "Negative Half Cycle (Sinking)" Q1NPN_B["Q1: NPN Driver"] Q2PNP_B["Q2: PNP Power
Sziklai / CFP"] Q1NPN_B -->|Emitter to Base| Q2PNP_B Q2PNP_B -->|Sinks current| LOAD_NEG["Load (Negative swing)"] end ``` **Reading the architecture:** A *complementary Darlington-Sziklai* output stage uses a conventional Darlington for the positive half-cycle and a Sziklai pair for the negative half-cycle. This is the topology of the **LM3886**, **TDA2050**, and other high-fidelity audio amplifier ICs. --- ## 4.4 Mermaid — Current Flow Magnification (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR A["I_B1 = 1 microamp"] -->|"x beta1 = 100x"| B["I_E1 = 100 microamps"] B -->|"x beta2 = 120x"| C["I_C2 = 12 milliamps"] C -->|"Total Load Current I_L = 12 mA"| D["Effective Gain = 12000"] style A fill:#FFE0B2 style D fill:#C8E6C9 ``` > [!TIP] > **Visual Reinforcement:** The progressive widening of the "pipe" in the diagram represents the **current magnification** at each stage. This is the *intuitive picture* of $\beta_{1}\beta_{2}$ gain multiplication that you should sketch in your KTU answer sheets when explaining Darlington action. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define a Darlington pair and state its two key advantages over a single BJT.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > A Darlington pair is a composite configuration of two BJTs (typically NPN) with the emitter of the first connected to the base of the second, and both collectors tied together. It behaves as a single super-beta transistor. > > **Two key advantages:** > 1. **Very high current gain:** $\beta_{D} \approx \beta_{1}\beta_{2}$ (e.g., 10,000), enabling output current boosting from a small base drive. > 2. **Very high input resistance:** $r_{\pi,D} \approx \beta_{1}\beta_{2}/g_{m}$, allowing it to be driven by high-impedance signal sources without loading them. > > *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for the two advantages.)* --- ### **Q2. Why is the composite $V_{BE}$ of a Darlington pair approximately 1.4 V, and what is the practical consequence?** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Model Answer:** > In a Darlington pair, the base–emitter junctions of Q1 and Q2 are in series between the composite base and the composite emitter. Each junction requires $\approx 0.7$ V to turn on, so $V_{BE(\text{composite})} = V_{BE1} + V_{BE2} \approx 1.4\ \text{V}$. > > **Practical consequence:** This higher turn-on voltage (a) restricts use in low-voltage designs (requires supply $> 2$–$3$ V), (b) increases the *headroom loss* in class-AB output stages, and (c) raises quiescent power dissipation because the device idles at a higher voltage drop. > > *(3 marks: 1.5 marks for the why + 1.5 marks for the consequence.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** > > **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and operation of a Darlington pair. Derive the expression for its composite current gain. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A Darlington emitter follower uses $\beta_{1} = 90$ and $\beta_{2} = 150$. It is driven by a source of $i_{b} = 15\ \mu A$ and feeds a load $R_{L} = 25\ \Omega$ from a +15 V supply. Compute the composite current gain, the load current, and the input resistance of the pair. Take $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = 0.04$ S. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Construction, Operation, and Derivation:** The Darlington pair consists of two NPN transistors Q1 (driver) and Q2 (output) connected such that the **emitter of Q1 is tied to the base of Q2**, and **both collectors are connected to $V_{CC}$**. The composite base is at the base of Q1; the composite emitter is at the emitter of Q2. Applying KCL at the emitter of Q1 (= base of Q2): $$ I_{B2} = I_{E1} = (\beta_{1}+1)\,I_{B1} $$ Output current: $$ I_{L} = I_{C1} + I_{C2} = \beta_{1}I_{B1} + \beta_{2}(\beta_{1}+1)I_{B1} $$ $$ I_{L} = (\beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2})\,I_{B1} $$ $$ \therefore \beta_{D} = \frac{I_{L}}{I_{B1}} = \beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2} \approx \beta_{1}\beta_{2} $$ *(4 marks for diagram + 3 marks for derivation.)* **Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** **(i) Composite current gain:** $$ \beta_{D} = (90)(150) + 90 + 150 = 13{,}500 + 90 + 150 = 13{,}740 $$ **(ii) Load current:** $$ I_{L} = \beta_{D}\,I_{B1} = 13{,}740 \times 15 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ I_{L} = 0.2061\ \text{A} = 206.1\ \text{mA} $$ **(iii) Input resistance:** $$ r_{\pi,D} \approx \frac{\beta_{1}\beta_{2}}{g_{m}} = \frac{90 \times 150}{0.04} $$ $$ r_{\pi,D} = \frac{13{,}500}{0.04} = 337{,}500\ \Omega = 337.5\ \text{k}\Omega $$ *(7 marks: 2 marks each for (i) and (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with substitution and unit.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]** > > **(a)** Explain the concept of output current boosting using a Darlington pair. Compare its performance with a single-BJT emitter follower in terms of: (i) current gain, (ii) input impedance, (iii) $V_{BE}$ drop, and (iv) saturation voltage. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** In a complementary push-pull output stage, a Sziklai pair is used to realize a "PNP-equivalent" half. Sketch the Sziklai configuration, derive its composite current gain, and justify why it is preferred over a true PNP Darlington in audio amplifiers. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Output Current Boosting Concept and Comparison:** Output current boosting refers to the technique of using a Darlington pair to multiply the load-driving capability of a small input base current by a factor of $\beta_{1}\beta_{2}$ (typically 5,000–20,000). A single emitter follower has $\beta \approx 100$ and input impedance $R_{in} = (\beta+1)R_{E} \approx 100R_{E}$, whereas a Darlington emitter follower has $R_{in} \approx (\beta_{D}+1)R_{E} \approx 10{,}000R_{E}$. | Parameter | Single BJT | Darlington | |---|---|---| | Current gain | 50–300 | 1,000–20,000 | | Input impedance | Moderate ($\beta R_{E}$) | Very high ($\beta_{1}\beta_{2} R_{E}$) | | $V_{BE}$ drop | 0.7 V | 1.4 V | | $V_{CE(sat)}$ | 0.2 V | 0.7–1.0 V | *(4 marks: 2 for concept + 2 for tabular comparison; 3 marks for prose explanation.)* **Part (b) — Sziklai Pair Sketch and Justification:** The Sziklai (Complementary Feedback Pair, CFP) consists of an NPN driver Q1 whose collector feeds the **base** of a PNP power transistor Q2. The **emitter of Q2** is the output node, and the **emitter of Q1** is grounded (or tied to a reference). The composite device behaves like a high-$\beta$ PNP transistor. **Derivation of composite $\beta_{CFP}$:** For the NPN driver, $I_{C1} = \beta_{1}I_{B1}$. The collector of Q1 is the base of Q2, so $I_{B2} = I_{C1} = \beta_{1}I_{B1}$. The PNP Q2 sources emitter current $I_{E2} = (\beta_{2}+1)I_{B2} = (\beta_{2}+1)\beta_{1}I_{B1}$. $$ \boxed{\;\beta_{CFP} = \frac{I_{E2}}{I_{B1}} = \beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} \approx \beta_{1}\beta_{2}\;} $$ **Why preferred over a true PNP Darlington:** 1. **Lower $V_{BE}$ drop:** $\approx 0.7$ V (one junction) instead of 1.4 V. 2. **Lower saturation voltage:** $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V, identical to a single NPN. 3. **Better high-frequency performance:** PNP power transistors have lower $f_{T}$ than NPNs of equivalent rating; the Sziklai uses an NPN driver, preserving bandwidth. 4. **Cost-effective:** High-power PNP BJTs are scarcer and more expensive than NPN counterparts. *(7 marks: 2 marks for sketch, 2 marks for derivation, 3 marks for justification.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on Darlington questions — and how to avoid it:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $V_{BE} = 1.4$ V penalty.** Many students quote only $\beta_{D} = \beta_{1}\beta_{2}$ and ignore the double junction. Always mention both $V_{BE}$ and $V_{CE(sat)}$ penalties explicitly. > 2. **Confusing Darlington with Cascode.** A cascode is a *common-emitter + common-base* stack for bandwidth, **not** current boosting. Examiners will deduct for this confusion. > 3. **Writing $\beta_{D} = \beta_{1} + \beta_{2}$.** This is the *wrong* formula — it applies to *parallel* transistors, not cascaded. The correct cascaded form is $\beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2}$. > 4. **Forgetting the Q1 base-current drive.** A Darlington is *current-driven* through Q1's base. If the preceding stage cannot supply $I_{B1} = I_{L}/\beta_{D}$, the output stage will not deliver the rated current. > 5. **Ignoring thermal runaway.** Q1 and Q2 share the same heatsink in practice. If Q2 heats up, its $I_{CBO}$ increases → increases Q1's base drive → Q2 conducts more → thermal runaway. Always mention emitter degeneration or a base–emitter resistor across Q1 for stability. > 6. **Mismeasuring $V_{CE(sat)}$.** The composite $V_{CE(sat),D} = V_{CE(sat),Q2} + V_{BE(sat),Q1}$ — students often write just one term. Both must appear. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Examinations** - ✅ A **Darlington pair** = two BJTs cascaded (emitter of Q1 → base of Q2; collectors common). - ✅ **Composite $\beta_{D} = \beta_{1}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1} + \beta_{2} \approx \beta_{1}\beta_{2}$** — write the exact form, then justify the approximation. - ✅ **Composite $V_{BE} \approx 1.4$ V** — two BE junctions in series. - ✅ **Composite $V_{CE(sat)} = V_{CE(sat),Q2} + V_{BE(sat),Q1} \approx 0.7 - 1.0$ V.** - ✅ **Composite $r_{\pi,D} = r_{\pi 1} + (\beta_{1}+1)r_{\pi 2} \approx \beta_{1}\beta_{2}/g_{m}$** — very high input impedance. - ✅ **Composite $g_{m,D} \approx g_{m1}/2$** — reduced by emitter-follower action of Q1. - ✅ **Primary use: output current boosting** in power amplifiers, regulators, motor drivers. - ✅ **Sziklai / CFP pair** = NPN driver + PNP output → PNP-equivalent behavior with NPN-like $V_{BE}$ and $V_{CE(sat)}$. - ✅ **Drawbacks to mention in any design answer:** higher $V_{BE}$, higher $V_{CE(sat)}$, slower $f_{T}$, thermal runaway risk. - ✅ **Complementary Darlington-Sziklai** = the standard push-pull audio output topology (LM3886, TDA2050). - ✅ **Always** show both junction voltages, both $\beta$ values, and the final composite $I_{L}$ in numerical answers. - ✅ **Sample numbers** to memorize: $\beta_{1}=\beta_{2}=100$ ⇒ $\beta_{D} \approx 10{,}000$; $V_{BE} = 1.4$ V; $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.9$ V. > **Final KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question asks *"How is output current achieved in a power amplifier?"* — your **one-line answer opener** must be: *"By using a Darlington pair (or complementary Darlington–Sziklai configuration), which boosts the load current by a factor of $\beta_{1}\beta_{2}$ compared to a single BJT."* This opener alone earns the first 2 marks and sets the framework for the rest of your answer.
